  - sentence: …However, as we shall see in this chapter, land actually becomes more affordable because land will no longer be hoarded.…
  - why: The essay argues that land contributions (LVT) increase affordability by preventing hoarding.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“property rental market will simply accommodate”**
  - sentence: …In other words, since tenants already pay land contributions to property owners, land contributions are already included in landlords’ profits if they rent their properties out; if property owners try to pass community land contributions on…
  - why: The essay explains that landlords cannot shift land taxes to tenants because market competition prevents double charging.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“issuance of a partial Universal Basic Income”**
  - sentence: …One effective way to do this is through the issuance of a partial Universal Basic Income to all residents, financed entirely from community land contributions.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a Universal Basic Income funded by land rent to share wealth and prevent gentrification.
- **supports** [`problems/homelessness-is-housing-cost-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/homelessness-is-housing-cost-problem/) — anchor: **“cost of providing housing for the homeless”**
  - sentence: …To provide the homeless with free housing also makes sense on a financial basis, since the cost of providing housing for the homeless often tends to be significantly less than the actual welfare costs and societal burdens that are created b…
  - why: The essay frames homelessness as a failure to provide land access and argues housing is financially cheaper than the alternative.

### [Ain't Got No Job](https://www.progress.org/articles/aint-got-no-job/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“Get public revenue from land value”**
  - sentence: …Eliminate all taxes and arbitrary restrictions on labor and enterprise. Get public revenue from land value, pollution charges, and user fees instead.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting the tax burden off labor and onto land value eliminates involuntary unemployment, thereby raising productivity and employment.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Eliminate all taxes and arbitrary restrictions on labor”**
  - sentence: …Eliminate all taxes and arbitrary restrictions on labor and enterprise. Get public revenue from land value, pollution charges, and user fees instead.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the Georgist fiscal shift of removing taxes on work to fund government through land value.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“restrictions and taxes make it more costly”**
  - sentence: …The alternative to being an employee of others is to be self-employed, and here restrictions and taxes make it more costly and difficult to be your own boss.…
  - why: The essay identifies zoning and regulatory barriers—not just land tax policy—as primary causes of unemployment, implying that land value capture alone is insufficient without deregulation.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“slide down the contracting phase of the business cycle”**
  - sentence: …Otherwise, more workers will get laid off and have a hard time finding work as we slide down the contracting phase of the business cycle.…
  - why: The essay documents rising unemployment and job losses as evidence of the recurring business cycle downturn.

### [All Creek, No Paddle](https://www.progress.org/articles/all-creek-no-paddle/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Their donations to colleges create”**
  - sentence: …Their donations to colleges create the departments and scholarships that manufacture conventional wisdom.…
  - why: The essay argues that powerful interests shape economic knowledge and silence land rent analysis through funding and institutional control.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“public agencies no longer collect”**
  - sentence: …Worse still, public agencies no longer collect, collate, and package stats in quasi-intelligible form but have privatized their duty, just as the US military now hires mercenaries, the Congress gave the power to issue new money to a private…
  - why: The essay illustrates the capture of public agencies and data by private interests, supporting the public-choice objection regarding government failure.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“surplus—also known as “economic rent””**
  - sentence: …This article is part of a series by Jeffery J. Smith on the surplus—also known as “economic rent”—that exists in the economy.…
  - why: The essay focuses on the existence and hoarding of economic rent, which is the central theme of the rentier economy narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“once shared, this surplus could”**
  - sentence: …Currently, this surplus is hoarded; yet once shared, this surplus could generate undreamed of possibilities for the entire human population.…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the potential of sharing the surplus (rent) to benefit the population, which is the core of the citizens dividend narrative.

### [Amateurs talk Algorithms, Professionals talk Data Cleaning](https://www.progress.org/articles/amateurs-talk-algorithms-professionals-talk-data-cleaning/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“data quality trumps fancy algorithms”**
  - sentence: …Fancy algorithms can and should be part of a balanced mass appraisal breakfast, but there’s an important lesson for technology boosters to learn (a bitter lesson if you will): data quality trumps fancy algorithms.…
  - why: The essay argues that the primary barrier to accurate land valuation is not the complexity of the model but the quality of the input data, countering the objection that land value is too difficult to 
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“aggressively validating their sales data”**
  - sentence: …Today we’re going to pivot back to modern mass appraisal, starting with one of the single most important ways every assessor can get better results right away: by aggressively validating their sales data.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete methodological response (sales validation) to the practical objection that land values cannot be accurately separated and assessed.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“automates part of the sales scrutiny pipeline”**
  - sentence: …The Center for Land Economics has built a simple script that automates part of the sales scrutiny pipeline; it’s part of OpenAVMKit , our free and open source mass appraisal software.…
  - why: By introducing open-source tools to standardize the assessment process, the essay counters the fear that assessment is inherently subjective or prone to corruption.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“Mass Appraisal for the Masses”**
  - sentence: …Welcome to the third article in the Mass Appraisal for the Masses series.…
  - why: The essay implicitly distinguishes modern mass appraisal techniques (focused on land value) from simple property taxation, supporting the claim that LVT requires distinct, sophisticated methods.

### [America drifts closer to fascism](https://www.progress.org/articles/america-drifts-closer-to-fascism/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“arrogant mentality in the federal government”**
  - sentence: …This ploy reveals a disturbing arrogant mentality in the federal government.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies the public-choice objection by describing government agencies acting to evade accountability and serve their own interests rather than the public.
- **illustrates** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“usurpation by presidential power”**
  - sentence: …Much worse is usurpation by presidential power.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the fear that concentrated power and revenue in the hands of the state lead to authoritarianism and the erosion of constitutional rights.

### [American Exceptionalism](https://www.progress.org/articles/american-exceptionalism/)
- **counters** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“recognition of natural rights”**
  - sentence: …While the typical American belief in liberty in the abstract is not unique, what is unique to the USA, so far as I know, is the constitutional recognition of natural rights in the 9th Amendment and in some of the state constitutions.…
  - why: The essay argues that the US Constitution's recognition of pre-existing natural rights is the true 'American exceptionalism,' countering the narrative that US land titles are uniquely derived from rob
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“individually sovereign”**
  - sentence: …The American exception is the rejection of parliamentary, governmental, and national sovereignty, that “we the people” are recognized as individually sovereign.…
  - why: The essay asserts that the US system uniquely rejects state sovereignty in favor of individual sovereignty, countering the objection that state ownership (nationalization) is a valid solution to land 
- **counters** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“recognition of American Indians”**
  - sentence: …The exceptionalism is the ideal of equal liberty, not the practice, although the ideal has influenced reforms such as the abolition of slavery, the recognition of American Indians as equal citizens, equal voting rights for women, and the mo…
  - why: The essay frames the recognition of Indigenous people as equal citizens as a fulfillment of the American ideal of natural rights, countering narratives that focus on dispossession without this constit

### [American Wealth and Real Estate](https://www.progress.org/articles/american-wealth-and-real-estate/)
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“Much of the rise”**
  - sentence: …Much of the rise in US “wealth” since the Great Recession is the recovery of land values.…
  - why: The essay attributes the post-recession recovery of net worth specifically to land values rather than productive capital.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“land rent could pay”**
  - sentence: …The data indicate that land rent could pay for the public goods provided by government.…
  - why: The author calculates total land rent and compares it to government spending to demonstrate funding feasibility.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“capitalized value of the wages”**
  - sentence: …To a large extend, the price of land is the capitalized value of the wages and capital yields extracted for public goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that land prices represent the capitalization of public goods funded by taxes on labor and capital.
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“more than half is real estate”**
  - sentence: …Per capita net worth is about $300,000, of which more than half is real estate value.…
  - why: By showing that real estate constitutes the majority of household wealth, the essay counters the claim that land is economically insignificant.

### [America’s Royalist Coin Set](https://www.progress.org/articles/americas-royalist-coin-set/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“mental disease of leader-worship”**
  - sentence: …Like the current series of quarters that depict symbols of the states, the president coins might be popular, but if so, that only shows the prevalence of the mental disease of leader-worship in the U.S.…
  - why: The essay critiques the cultural tendency to revere political leaders as royalty, illustrating the ideological mindset that Georgists argue corrupts political economy.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“The United States Mint”**
  - sentence: …The United States Mint has begun a new series of dollar coins.…
  - why: The essay frames the state's monopoly on currency and its use to glorify executive power as part of a broader critique of state privilege and centralized authority.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“The U.S. state is embodied”**
  - sentence: …The U.S. state is embodied in the Constitution, not in a man who holds the executive office.…
  - why: The essay emphasizes the republican principle that the state belongs to the people and the law, not to a ruler, which aligns with the Georgist view that natural resources belong to the community.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“American Indian woman”**
  - sentence: …They will be the same size as the previous issue that depicts Sacagawea, the American Indian woman who helped the Lewis and Clarke expedition that explored the American Northwest.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the replacement of Sacagawea with presidents, implicitly critiquing the marginalization of indigenous representation in favor of executive power.

### [An Integration of Ideas](https://www.progress.org/articles/an-integration-of-ideas/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“shared equally in cash”**
  - sentence: …The universal ethic is implemented in the economy with a pure free market, in which there is no restriction nor imposed cost on acts which do not coercively harm others, and in which the benefits of nature, as measured by land rent, are sha…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for distributing the benefits of nature (land rent) equally in cash, which is the core definition of a citizens' dividend.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“benefits of nature”**
  - sentence: …The universal ethic is implemented in the economy with a pure free market, in which there is no restriction nor imposed cost on acts which do not coercively harm others, and in which the benefits of nature, as measured by land rent, are sha…
  - why: The essay identifies land rent as the primary ethical source of public revenue to replace taxes on labor and enterprise.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“inducing efficient land use”**
  - sentence: …As ethics and economics are in harmony, the policies that are ethical also generate maximum prosperity, as taxes on enterprise and labor impose losses, while revenues from rent reduce inequality while inducing efficient land use and prevent…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting revenue to land rent induces efficient land use and prevents economic depressions, supporting the claim that taxing land raises productivity.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“West-Bank settlement leaseholds”**
  - sentence: …The conflict between Israel and the Palestinians can be resolved with West-Bank settlement leaseholds that pay rent to Palestine.…
  - why: The essay uses the Israel-Palestine conflict to illustrate how land rent capture and leaseholds can resolve territorial disputes, intersecting with themes of land justice.

### [An Open Letter to Transition Towns](https://www.progress.org/articles/an-open-letter-to-transition-towns/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value of its land increases”**
  - sentence: …Every time we make a community more livable—such as through charity, better social services, technological innovation, or business enterprise—a community becomes more desirable to live in; as a result, the value of its land increases.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that community improvements create land value, which is the core definition of this narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Wall Street banks get most of their money from land”**
  - sentence: …It’s easy to blame Wall Street, but few people stop to think about where Wall Street gets all its money from: Wall Street banks get most of their money from land, via mortgages.…
  - why: The essay supports the claim that modern banking growth is tied to land by stating banks derive most of their money from land via mortgages.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“redistributes one part of its rental income back to the community”**
  - sentence: …The community land trust then redistributes one part of its rental income back to the community via a basic income, and the other part to the city government for community services.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the citizens dividend narrative by proposing a model where land rent is collected and redistributed to the community as a basic income.
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“I’m not proposing that “the government should own the land””**
  - sentence: …I’m not proposing that “the government should own the land”.…
  - why: The essay directly counters the objection that land reform requires state ownership by distinguishing its proposal from government ownership.

### [An Uncharitable Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/an-uncharitable-tax/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“make the affected land more attractive”**
  - sentence: …Public goods such as streets, highways, transit, parks, schools, fire protection, security, and water all make the affected land more attractive and productive.…
  - why: The essay explicitly lists public goods that increase land value, illustrating the capitalization effect.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“landowners would have to pay to have their sites serviced”**
  - sentence: …Polluters would have to compensate for their dumping, and landowners would have to pay to have their sites serviced.…
  - why: The essay argues that landowners should pay for the services that create their land's value, supporting the community-creation narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“polluter gets subsidized while the donor gets penalized”**
  - sentence: …But no, the polluter gets subsidized while the donor gets penalized.…
  - why: The essay critiques the lack of full polluter compensation, implicitly supporting the case for proper carbon pricing.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“how can the economic gurus of the government could come up with something as absurd”**
  - sentence: …If you ever wonder how the world has gotten into such a mess, ponder how can the economic gurus of the government could come up with something as absurd as taxing charity for the poor.…
  - why: The essay expresses bewilderment at policy absurdity, illustrating the narrative that economic policy is fundamentally misguided.

### [Another Job Killer: Section 385](https://www.progress.org/articles/another-job-killer-section-385/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“reduce marginal costs”**
  - sentence: …The idea is to reduce marginal costs without changing average costs.…
  - why: The essay argues that replacing marginal taxes on profits with a fixed-cost land tax reduces the burden on employment and investment.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“re-classify some debt as equity”**
  - sentence: …Thus on 4 April 2016, the US Treasury Department and the Internal Revenue Service proposed new regulations, Section 385, to stop the inversions... the rule would re-classify some debt as equity in financial transactions.…
  - why: The essay critiques Section 385's attempt to curb debt-based tax avoidance, implicitly supporting the Georgist alternative of taxing rent to eliminate the incentive for debt manipulation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“levy a tax on the economic land rent”**
  - sentence: …If the marginal-tax-rate reduction is to be revenue neutral, it would be efficient and equitable for the US government to levy a tax on the economic land rent of commercial and rented-out real estate...…
  - why: The essay proposes land value taxation as a viable solution to regulatory and tax burdens without preconditions like zoning reform.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“replaces what would otherwise be paid”**
  - sentence: …After the transition, a tax on rent, by reducing the purchase price of land, replaces what would otherwise be paid for mortgage interest, so that there is no burden even on future landowners.…
  - why: The essay explains that a land tax lowers purchase prices and offsets mortgage costs, illustrating the mechanism by which the tax burden is capitalized rather than passed on.

### [Anti-Stimulus for Las Vegas](https://www.progress.org/articles/anti-stimulus-for-las-vegas/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“crash created by government”**
  - sentence: …It is that much worse at making management decisions for the millions of firms that are struggling to survive in the crash created by government in the first place.…
  - why: The essay attributes the recession to government policy, specifically blaming subsidized real estate for the boom-bust cycle.
- **counters** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“subsidized real estate”**
  - sentence: …They did it to themselves, as Nevada could have prevented its boom-bust if it had not subsidized real estate, but now when they are down is the worst time for anti-stimulus.…
  - why: The essay argues that public subsidies for real estate caused the boom-bust cycle, countering the idea that public investment always creates net value.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“making management decisions”**
  - sentence: …The government should avoid seeking to micro-manage the economy such as telling firms where to meet, but stick to macro-economic policy.…
  - why: The essay disputes the effectiveness of government intervention, arguing that government interference exacerbates economic problems rather than solving them.

### [Apparel in Peril](https://www.progress.org/articles/apparel-in-peril/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“taxes on the wages”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on the wages of workers and on the profits of companies are passed on as higher prices of goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing wages and profits drives businesses away, implying that shifting to a tax base that cannot flee (land) would prevent this economic loss.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“taxes removed from labor”**
  - sentence: …It is impossible to forecast what the wage would be in a pure rent-sharing market economy, but the law of demand indicates that, with taxes removed from labor and investment, economic growth would be dazzling.…
  - why: The essay explicitly claims that removing taxes from labor and investment (and funding government via land rent instead) would lead to significant economic growth.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax only what can not hide”**
  - sentence: …Really, the only lasting solution to job shrinkage and production exodus is to tax only what can not hide, flee, or shrink: land value.…
  - why: The essay concludes with the core Georgist argument that shifting the tax burden from mobile labor/capital to immobile land is the solution to economic exodus.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed directly to the people”**
  - sentence: …Part of the public revenue pays for public goods, and part is distributed directly to the people in equal shares of money.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a system where land rent funds public goods and is also distributed as a citizen dividend to ensure a proper initial distribution of income.

### [Are Pessimists Right? Or is Optimism Warranted?](https://www.progress.org/articles/are-pessimists-right-or-is-optimism-warranted/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“trade not hindered by taxes”**
  - sentence: …How to develop economically, while not widely known by activists or politicians, is well known in the literature—mainly widespread ownership of land, plus trade not hindered by taxes, tariffs, licenses, and subsidies to insiders (corruption…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for free trade untaxed by tariffs as a key component of economic development, aligning with the Single Tax narrative of removing barriers to production.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“solutions that openminded people have worked out”**
  - sentence: …People could investigate the solutions that openminded people have worked out and that mainstream media ignore.…
  - why: The essay argues that effective solutions exist but are ignored by mainstream media and institutions, echoing the narrative that economic truth is suppressed or obscured.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“widespread ownership of land”**
  - sentence: …How to develop economically, while not widely known by activists or politicians, is well known in the literature—mainly widespread ownership of land, plus trade not hindered by taxes, tariffs, licenses, and subsidies to insiders (corruption…
  - why: The essay posits that widespread land ownership is a fundamental solution to poverty and oppression, supporting the justice-oriented narrative of equitable land distribution.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“subsidies to insiders (corruption)”**
  - sentence: …How to develop economically, while not widely known by activists or politicians, is well known in the literature—mainly widespread ownership of land, plus trade not hindered by taxes, tariffs, licenses, and subsidies to insiders (corruption…
  - why: By identifying subsidies and corruption as barriers to economic stability, the essay supports the Georgist view that rent-seeking and privilege distort the economy.

### [Are Tax Credits for Home Buying Good or Bad?](https://www.progress.org/articles/are-tax-credits-for-home-buying-good-or-bad/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“unsustainable speculative economic boom”**
  - sentence: …As it is, real estate is highly subsidized, because governmental services such as transit make locations more productive and attractive, raising rents and site values.…
  - why: The essay argues that subsidizing real estate caused the speculative boom and subsequent crash.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“governmental services such as transit”**
  - sentence: …As it is, real estate is highly subsidized, because governmental services such as transit make locations more productive and attractive, raising rents and site values.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that public services like transit raise land values.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“shift taxes off of income”**
  - sentence: …Second, to stimulate supply, shift taxes off of income and goods and into pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting the tax burden from income to land value.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“shift taxes off of income”**
  - sentence: …Second, to stimulate supply, shift taxes off of income and goods and into pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes to land value stimulates supply, countering the objection that it is useless without zoning reform.

### [At an Impasse](https://www.progress.org/articles/at-an-impasse/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“the worth of Earth”**
  - sentence: …In writing this book, we’re trying to discover something new—the worth of Earth, a datum either unknown or as thoroughly buried as eagerly I try to unearth it (pun intended).…
  - why: The essay frames the 'worth of Earth' (total land rent) as a critical, currently unknown datum that is essential to understanding the economy's surplus.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“data keepers”**
  - sentence: …What’s holding up this investigation into the standard procedures of business, government, and academia, to wit, their treatment of the total for rents, for society’s spending on all the land and resources that all its members use?…
  - why: The author describes institutional resistance from 'data keepers' in academia and government that obscures the true magnitude of land rent.
- **illustrates** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“precise answer of the worth of Earth”**
  - sentence: …Well, if I can’t beat them, maybe I can join them . Perhaps some bastion of obscured data has need for an apostate investigator who’s an expert in all the reasons why a precise answer of the worth of Earth is impossible to ever know.…
  - why: The essay highlights the practical difficulty and institutional obstruction involved in assessing the total value of land rent.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“surplus—also known as “economic rent””**
  - sentence: …This article is part of a series by Jeffery J. Smith on the surplus—also known as “economic rent”—that exists in the economy.…
  - why: The essay introduces the concept of economic rent as a surplus that, if shared rather than hoarded, could generate vast public benefits.

### [At This Tipping Point, Can We Back Off?](https://www.progress.org/articles/at-this-tipping-point-can-we-back-off/)
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“value of the underneath location”**
  - sentence: …What makes this crisis ironic is that it’s not the housing—which ages and wears out—going ballistic.…
  - why: The essay argues that the housing crisis is driven by land values rather than structures, directly supporting the claim.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“most of mortgage is land value”**
  - sentence: …Most don’t realize that most of that debt is mortgage , and most of mortgage is land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly links private debt and mortgage lending to land value, evidencing the claim.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“spending for the nature we use”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …You hear feverish talk of a coming recession . You always hear such talk. And every now and then the talkers are correct without knowing why, without knowing the role of the money society spends for the nature it uses.…
  - why: The essay suggests that recognizing and capturing the surplus spent on nature (rent) is key to solving economic crises.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“system of rent-winning”**
  - sentence: …The role of rent in all this is both as the sustenance of the 1% and as the system of rent-winning that the lucky recipients are determined to maintain around the world.…
  - why: The essay describes how the elite sustain their power through hoarding rent, illustrating the rentier economy narrative.

### [Austrian Economics Explained](https://www.progress.org/articles/austrian-economics-explained/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“some Austrian economists”**
  - sentence: …The complementarity of Austrian and Georgist thought has been recognized by some Austrian economists such as Leland Yeager, and by some Georgist economists such as Mason Gaffney, but some Austrian economists have let their anarchist ideolog…
  - why: The essay explicitly addresses the Austrian critique of land value collection, arguing that while Austrian and Georgist theories are complementary, some Austrian economists mistakenly reject land rent
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“malspeculation in land value”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Just as an artificial reduction in interest rates by governmental intervention generates unsustainable investments in buildings, which Austrians call “malinvestments,” cheap credit also induces “malspeculation” in land value.…
  - why: The essay provides an Austrian-theory perspective that supports the Georgist narrative, arguing that cheap credit induces speculative bubbles in land value which contribute to economic cycles.
- **supports** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Austrian theory that emphasizes”**
  - sentence: …The Georgist theory of the business cycle, which emphasizes land, and the Austrian theory that emphasizes money, interest, and capital goods, are complimentary, and their integration provides a more complete Austrian theory of economic cycl…
  - why: The essay outlines the Austrian view that business cycles are driven by money, interest, and capital goods (credit), positioning it as a complementary but distinct explanation to the land-focused Geor
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“non-distorting sources of public revenue”**
  - sentence: …The policy consequence is that public goods are better provided by markets, such as with voluntary private communities, or from non-distorting sources of public revenue, namely pollution levies and land rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that Austrian theory supports the use of land rent as a non-distorting source of revenue to replace taxes that distort market signals like wages and investment returns.

### [Ballad of the Housing Crunch](https://www.progress.org/articles/ballad-of-the-housing-crunch/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“Speculation is the game”**
  - sentence: …Speculation is the game.…
  - why: The essay depicts the speculative frenzy and subsequent crash as a cycle driven by land speculation.
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“You’re a fool if you don’t invest”**
  - sentence: …You’re a fool if you don’t invest.…
  - why: The narrative portrays the speculative investment as a reckless gamble that leads to ruin, countering the idea that it performs a useful economic function.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“bought a lot of land”**
  - sentence: …Once upon a time you were a man who bought a lot of land!…
  - why: The essay frames the housing crunch and financial ruin around the acquisition and loss of land.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Mortgage brokers came and said”**
  - sentence: …Mortgage brokers came and said, “You can’t go wrong!…
  - why: While credit is present, the essay attributes the boom and bust to the land value itself rising, countering a purely credit-based explanation.

### [Banks, Land, and the Institution of Private Landownership](https://www.progress.org/articles/banks-land-and-the-institution-of-private-landownership/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“banks will increasingly become more powerful”**
  - sentence: …Based on the economic patterns I’ve been observing, I’ll make an educated prediction: As long as land is not in the commons, but instead privately owned — thus requiring financing—banks will increasingly become more powerful until one day, …
  - why: argues that private land ownership necessitates financing, driving the growth and power of banks
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“any money that’s being made from land”**
  - sentence: …Owning land in common doesn’t mean it can’t be privately used; of course it can . But once land is owned in common, any money that’s being made from land by certain banks or individuals goes to everyone, equally.…
  - why: advocates for distributing land rents equally to the community as a solution to economic inequality
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“banks profit from our human need”**
  - sentence: …So the real kicker is this: because banks make money from land, and because land is more expensive the closer we live together, banks profit from our human need to be close to one another .…
  - why: illustrates how financial institutions extract wealth from the community-created value of land
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“land existed long before anyone”**
  - sentence: …The trouble, however, is that land existed long before anyone has ever lived on it, and it’ll still be there long after we’ll all be gone.…
  - why: supports the moral argument that land is a common inheritance rather than a product of individual labor

### [Basic Income as a Strategy to Promote the Georgist Movement](https://www.progress.org/articles/basic-income-as-a-strategy-to-promote-the-georgist-movement/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“redistribution in cash without paternalism”**
  - sentence: …Basic Income is generating excitement with the idea of a fundamentally different kind of social spending: redistribution in cash without paternalism or judgment.…
  - why: The essay frames Basic Income as a Citizens Dividend that provides emancipation and the power to say no.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“people who own the resources”**
  - sentence: …Until the people who own the resources start compensating the propertyless, our labor market will contain an unspoken element of coercion.…
  - why: The essay argues that resource control creates coercive labor markets, aligning with the rent-seeking critique.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“money will bring human emancipation”**
  - sentence: …Second, this money will bring human emancipation, bestowing on workers the one thing they’ve always needed to enter the market as free individuals: the power to say no.…
  - why: The essay claims that compensating the propertyless via rent dividends empowers workers and reduces coercion.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“people who have grabbed the earth’s resources”**
  - sentence: …First, the people who have grabbed the earth’s resources owe you money.…
  - why: The essay invokes a justice narrative that those who monopolize resources owe restitution to the community.

### [Basic Income Explained: Guaranteed Basic Income (GBI) vs. Universal Basic Income (UBI)](https://www.progress.org/articles/gbi-vs-ubi/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“dividends from common wealth”**
  - sentence: …We already have basic income models that work around the world: guaranteed basic income to buy people time by keeping them out of poverty, and UBI dividends from common wealth.…
  - why: The essay argues that distributing rent from common resources as a dividend reduces poverty and inequality, citing Alaska's Permanent Fund as evidence.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“Alaska's oil dividend”**
  - sentence: …Universal basic income (UBI) , when given to everyone regardless of income, is most politically viable as a dividend from a common fund or resource — like Alaska's oil dividend , Norway's sovereign wealth fund , or proposed AI dividends.…
  - why: The essay uses Alaska's oil dividend as a primary example of a durable, working resource-rent dividend program.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“dividends from wealth we own together”**
  - sentence: …All persons have a right to income from wealth we inherit or create together… paying dividends from wealth we own together is a practical, market-based way to assure the survival of a large middle class.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative that common wealth belongs to citizens and should be distributed via dividends.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“Norway's sovereign wealth fund”**
  - sentence: …Universal basic income (UBI) , when given to everyone regardless of income, is most politically viable as a dividend from a common fund or resource — like Alaska's oil dividend , Norway's sovereign wealth fund , or proposed AI dividends.…
  - why: The essay cites Norway's sovereign wealth fund as evidence of successful high-rate capture of natural-resource rent.

### [Basic Income’s Third Wave](https://www.progress.org/articles/basic-incomes-third-wave/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Alaska Dividend”**
  - sentence: …Both countries conducted five implementation trials, and the United States created or expanded several more limited programmes, like the Earned Income Tax Credit and the Alaska Dividend.…
  - why: Cites the Alaska Dividend as a successful policy outcome of the second wave, evidencing the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“proceeds be distributed in cash”**
  - sentence: …Some supporters of Henry George’s land tax suggested that proceeds be distributed in cash.…
  - why: Notes early Georgist support for cash distributions of land rent, illustrating the historical roots of the citizens dividend narrative.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“national dividend”**
  - sentence: …Major C. H. Douglas called it a national dividend and included it in his ‘social credit’ programme.…
  - why: Documents the historical use of the 'national dividend' concept, illustrating the narrative's evolution.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“share the wealth”**
  - sentence: …In 1934, the Louisiana senator Huey Long debuted his ‘share the wealth’ programme: he seems to have come up with the idea on his own, as there’s no evidence he was influenced by the ideas spreading around the United Kingdom in those years.…
  - why: Describes Huey Long's 'share the wealth' program as a historical instance of the dividend narrative.

### [Book Review: The Land Trap by Mike Bird](https://www.progress.org/articles/book-review-the-land-trap-by-mike-bird/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Fiat currency isn’t backed by nothing”**
  - sentence: …The fun fact is this: Fiat currency isn’t backed by nothing, as commonly supposed, but by land.…
  - why: The essay argues that modern banking and currency creation are fundamentally backed by land as collateral.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“sucks capital out of the actually productive”**
  - sentence: …This not only bids up the price of land, but also sucks capital out of the actually productive part of the economy.…
  - why: The essay details how land speculation diverts investment from productive enterprise, slowing economic growth.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“inflating a dangerous real estate bubble”**
  - sentence: …The Land Trap is when land slowly sucks up all your economy’s productivity, inflating a dangerous real estate bubble that eventually pops, leaving disaster in its wake.…
  - why: The essay outlines a specific mechanism where financialized land speculation creates boom-bust cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“Land outcompetes other assets as an investment”**
  - sentence: …Land outcompetes other assets as an investment Traditional investments like stocks, bonds, and going into business for yourself all contribute to the real economy by directing money towards tools, materials, research, and workers.…
  - why: The essay describes how land becomes the dominant asset class, crowding out productive investment.

### [BOOM: LVT Candidates Get Elected - Landscape #5](https://www.progress.org/articles/boom-lvt-candidates-get-elected-landscape-5/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“incentivize development”**
  - sentence: …On her campaign website, Wilson endorsed shifts to land value taxes as a property tax reform to incentivize development.…
  - why: The essay cites electoral victories for candidates who support LVT specifically to encourage development, validating the claim that shifting taxes onto land spurs construction.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“tax base naturally shifts”**
  - sentence: …With an exemption in place, the building portion is excluded, so the tax base naturally shifts onto land value while keeping total revenue constant.…
  - why: The detailed explanation of Spokane's 'universal building exemption' illustrates the mechanism by which split-rate taxation encourages density and discourages land hoarding.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Realigning property tax incentives”**
  - sentence: …Realigning property tax incentives is a necessary part of that work.…
  - why: The essay argues that even in Minneapolis, which has already eliminated single-family zoning, LVT remains a necessary tool to address housing shortages, countering the objection that zoning reform mus
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“needs more development”**
  - sentence: …Even so, Minneapolis continues to face a shortage of housing and needs more development to bring costs down and strengthen the regional economy.…
  - why: The essay uses the housing shortage in Minneapolis and the push for LVT in postindustrial cities like Buffalo to exemplify the narrative that solving the housing crisis requires addressing land costs.

### [BOOM: Unanimous Vote Puts Land Value Tax Enablement on Governor’s Desk](https://www.progress.org/articles/boom-unanimous-vote-puts-land-value-tax-enablement-on-governors-desk/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“reward the construction”**
  - sentence: …PPI have also published similar analyses for Fairfax City and Charlottesville (although the latter study now relies on outdated assessment data), both of which found that a revenue-neutral LVT shift tends to reward the construction of much-…
  - why: Cites studies showing split-rate taxes reward multifamily construction and penalize underutilized land.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“revenue-neutral LVT shift would be broadly progressive”**
  - sentence: …Analysis on behalf of the City found that a revenue-neutral LVT shift would be broadly progressive, tending to reduce the tax burden in lower-income neighborhoods.…
  - why: Provides evidence from Richmond analysis that LVT shifts can be progressive and reduce burdens on lower-income areas.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“pad the pockets of land speculators”**
  - sentence: …“Should these gains translate into affordable housing and better public transit, or should they merely pad the pockets of land speculators?…
  - why: Illustrates the argument that LVT prevents socially created value from merely enriching speculators.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“highest and best use”**
  - sentence: …“We will also explore new opportunities such as a land value tax to incentivize the highest and best use of property for economic growth”, Mayor Owens stated.…
  - why: Quotes a mayor endorsing LVT to incentivize the highest and best use of property, countering low-density sprawl.

### [Build-to-Rent Isn’t the Problem](https://www.progress.org/articles/build-to-rent-isnt-the-problem/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“America has a land crisis”**
  - sentence: …The concern of build-to-rent development exists because America has a land crisis as public institutions have systematically failed to capture the value their own investments create, leaving private actors to step into the gap and extract i…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the build-to-rent issue as a symptom of a deeper land crisis where public value is privatized.
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“That freeway didn’t just move traffic”**
  - sentence: …That freeway didn’t just move traffic. It created land value.…
  - why: The Cadence at Gateway example demonstrates how public infrastructure (freeways) directly creates private land value.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“no pressure on the owners of that land”**
  - sentence: …One of the reasons cities fail to develop their interior land — the vacant lots, the surface parking, the underutilized parcels next to transit stations — is that there’s no pressure on the owners of that land to do anything with it.…
  - why: The essay explains that landowners hold idle land because they capture unearned appreciation without building, causing sprawl.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“the surrounding community’s investment in itself”**
  - sentence: …They just wait, and the surrounding community’s investment in itself — in schools, transit, parks, public safety — flows into private land values as an unearned windfall.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value increases are generated by community investment, not owner effort.

### [Buildings Waste Energy. But We Tax’em!](https://www.progress.org/articles/buildings-waste-energy-but-we-taxem/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“jurisdictions enjoyed 50% more built value”**
  - sentence: …The Lincoln Institute found the benefactor behind the lovely suburbs around Melbourne Australia . Those jurisdictions enjoyed 50% more built value per acre compared to the bordering jurisdictions imposing the conventional property tax.…
  - why: provides empirical evidence that taxing land rather than buildings increases construction density
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“spurs owners who were leaving parcels idle”**
  - sentence: …Conversely, public recovery of location value—whether via tax, fee, lease, or dues—spurs owners who were leaving parcels idle to put up something beautiful.…
  - why: argues that recovering land value eliminates the financial incentive to hold land vacant
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“taxes land and pays citizens a dividend”**
  - sentence: …Further, that world-class city taxes land and pays citizens a dividend .…
  - why: cites Singapore as a successful example of combining land value capture with a citizen's dividend
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“Shift the property tax off buildings, onto land”**
  - sentence: …One reform lets owners, investors, and architects put up high quality buildings and site them next to each other. Shift the property tax off buildings, onto land.…
  - why: distinguishes land value taxation from conventional property taxes by showing the different incentives created for building improvements

### [By buying mortgages, the state subsidizes speculators](https://www.progress.org/articles/by-buying-mortgages-the-state-subsidizes-speculators/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“the secondary trade-medium for mortgages”**
  - sentence: …Then in the ozo decade (2000 to 2009), the vast expansion of computer power and electronic communications made the secondary trade-medium for mortgages much greater.…
  - why: The essay describes the expansion of mortgage-backed securities and government-sponsored enterprises as the primary drivers of the credit system, supporting the claim that modern banking growth is mor
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“did not understand the real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …Somehow the financial wizards of Fannie and Freddie did not understand the real estate cycle and that their own institutions would make the downturn that much more severe.…
  - why: The essay attributes the crash to ignorance of the real estate cycle and government-subsidized speculation, directly supporting the narrative that land speculation causes boom and bust.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“government intervention ultimately fails”**
  - sentence: …But people, even economists, are not learning the lesson that government intervention ultimately fails, and can be catastrophic.…
  - why: The essay argues that government intervention (via Fannie/Freddie) exacerbates rather than prevents economic instability, countering the objection that government can tame the cycle.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“refused to publish articles”**
  - sentence: …That is why “free market” publications have refused to publish articles about the real estate cycle.…
  - why: The essay claims that free-market publications ignore the real estate cycle and land subsidies, supporting the narrative that economic discourse is corrupted to avoid addressing land issues.

### [California’s Deforestation Project](https://www.progress.org/articles/californias-deforestation-project/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-causes-overdevelopment`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-causes-overdevelopment/) — anchor: **“The project is being promoted”**
  - sentence: …The project is being promoted by “nativists” who seek to obliterate introduced species of trees, on the claim that the clearing would reduce the fire danger.…
  - why: The essay disputes the rationale for clearing established forests, countering the idea that maximizing land development or modification (like removing trees) is always beneficial or safe.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“capture fog, create shade”**
  - sentence: …He points out the many benefits of trees: they capture fog, create shade, cool the land, sequester carbon, provide homes to thousands of animals and birds, and are more resilient to wildfire than the grasses and shrubs that grow in their pl…
  - why: The essay highlights the ecosystem services (carbon sequestration, cooling) provided by natural resources, supporting the narrative that nature produces value.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Governments are too often”**
  - sentence: …Governments are too often a destructive force, contrary to their claimed mission to be the protector of our rights and property.…
  - why: The essay illustrates government failure—specifically how agencies are pursuing a destructive project despite expert warnings—supporting the public-choice critique.

### [Call in the Cavalry—the Moneybags](https://www.progress.org/articles/call-in-the-cavalrythe-moneybags/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“how much money society spends”**
  - sentence: …So while foundations do make grants, typically it’s on programs that address symptoms rather than correct the system Money, the Master What’s needed is a funder who specializes in all things geonomic (Earth-focused economics).…
  - why: The essay argues for the necessity of calculating the total value of land and natural resources ('the worth of Earth') to determine the potential scale of public funding, which is the core premise of 
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“mimicking the Alaska model”**
  - sentence: …And, if mimicking the Alaska model, how much does the grand sum work out to be per capita?…
  - why: The essay explicitly references the Alaska Permanent Fund model as a benchmark for calculating what a per-capita dividend based on total land rent would look like.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“scholars who’ve conformed sufficiently”**
  - sentence: …Yet the scholars who’ve conformed sufficiently to garner degrees in economics are not the same scholars who wonder about such things as how much money society spends on the nature it uses.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the idea that mainstream academic economics ignores or marginalizes the study of land and natural resource rents, leaving the research to 'lone researchers' outside the establish
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“well-cloaked total amount”**
  - sentence: …There are a few firms that offer to sell the well-cloaked total amount of US residential real estate value, and property values in general, firms like CoStar and Case-Shiller .…
  - why: The essay supports the premise that accurately assessing land value is difficult and expensive ('well-cloaked'), requiring paid expert data rather than simple amateur estimation.

### [Can competing groups find a way to share land?](https://www.progress.org/articles/can-competing-groups-find-a-way-to-share-land/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“share the land equally”**
  - sentence: …Instead of fighting, why don’t we all share the land equally.…
  - why: The story illustrates the Georgist principle that land value is created by the community presence and productivity, not the individual owner, justifying shared rent.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“rent went up even more”**
  - sentence: …My output doubled, and I did get higher wages, but the rent went up even more, so most of the gain went to pay Ungachku the higher rent!…
  - why: The parable demonstrates how technological progress and population growth are captured by landlords as rent, suppressing wages.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“collect the rent”**
  - sentence: …Let us collect the rent that is soaking up the output and share it equally.…
  - why: The dialogue explicitly proposes the Single Tax solution—collecting rent to fund public benefit—as the alternative to conflict.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“first-come first-served”**
  - sentence: …We were here first, so this better land is ours.…
  - why: The story critiques the 'first-come first-served' justification for land monopoly, framing it as theft from the community.

### [Can the U.S. Constitution Force Americans to Have Medical Insurance?](https://www.progress.org/articles/can-the-us-constitution-force-americans-to-have-medical-insurance/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax on personhood”**
  - sentence: …But it is a tax on personhood rather than on a privilege or an activity or on property.…
  - why: The essay contrasts a tax on mere existence with taxes on privileges or property, illustrating the Georgist principle of taxing land (property) rather than labor (personhood).
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“slaves of the government”**
  - sentence: …Tyrants who are in favor of making U.S. citizens slaves of the government argue that drivers are required to obtain liability insurance, and thus the same applies to medical insurance.…
  - why: The essay explicitly rejects the characterization of government mandates as making citizens 'slaves,' countering the objection that Georgist or government-led reforms equate to socialism or communism.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“destroying the spirit of American liberty”**
  - sentence: …If some folks are getting angry about the coming medical legislation, it is because such mandates are what is destroying the spirit of American liberty.…
  - why: The essay frames the expansion of federal power as a corruption of constitutional liberty, aligning with the narrative that established interests distort governance against the public good.

### [Can there be Recession Insurance?](https://www.progress.org/articles/can-there-be-recession-insurance/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate boom-bust cycles”**
  - sentence: …National governments can prevent recessions by eliminating their real estate boom-bust cycles and by avoiding the manipulation of their money and interest rates.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that eliminating real estate boom-bust cycles is a key to preventing recessions.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“capitalized in higher rents”**
  - sentence: …Much of the gain from economic expansion is capitalized in higher rents and land values.…
  - why: The essay describes how economic gains are captured by landowners, mirroring the claim about public investment capitalizing into land values.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“fuel the land speculation”**
  - sentence: …The gains from public services and economic expansion would no longer fuel the land speculation that creates the real estate bubble.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting revenue to land rent stops the gains that fuel land speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“price of land would fall”**
  - sentence: …The price of land would fall to near zero, so mortgages would be only for buildings, not land.…
  - why: The essay claims that the proposed tax shift would cause land prices to fall, directly supporting the affordability claim.

### [Canada Commits Economic Suicide](https://www.progress.org/articles/canada-commits-economic-suicide/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“tap only the economic rent”**
  - sentence: …The policy that would be friendly to entrepreneurship as well as investment and labor would be to tap only the economic rent of land, including all natural resources, and avoid taxing labor and enterprise.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing natural resource rent specifically is the efficient alternative to the destructive corporate income tax.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“tap only the rent of natural resources”**
  - sentence: …Indeed, if the finance minister proposed to tap only the rent of natural resources and not the overall income from the trusts, the share prices of many of the companies would have fallen.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a tax base that targets resource rents rather than total corporate income, aligning with the ACE/cash-flow principle of exempting normal returns.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Abolish the general tax on corporate”**
  - sentence: …Abolish the general tax on corporate and personal income, and abolish the GST, the sales tax on goods and services.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting the tax base to land/rents is beneficial even without other structural reforms, countering the objection that LVT is useless without zoning changes.

### [Candidates Seek Small Change](https://www.progress.org/articles/candidates-seek-small-change/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tapping of land rent”**
  - sentence: …The tapping of land rent for public revenue has no excess burden and prevents the subsidy of land by taking back the value generated by beneficial public works and civic services.…
  - why: The essay explicitly contrasts the inefficiency of income and sales taxes with the efficiency of land value taxation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“subsidize landowners”**
  - sentence: …The advocates call it a “fair tax,” but never explain why it is fair to subsidize landowners and to punish consumption.…
  - why: The essay critiques a national sales tax for exempting land, implicitly arguing that land must be taxed to avoid subsidizing it.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“green tax shift”**
  - sentence: …But the US medical system is already half socialized, and is corrupted by massive fraud... instead of the big change of the green tax shift, replacing regulations and permit trading with taxes or charges on pollution and other environmental…
  - why: The essay advocates for replacing command-and-control regulations with pollution taxes as a superior environmental policy.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“replacing income and sales taxes”**
  - sentence: …So really, a national sales tax seems big but is ridiculously small change compared to the really big green tax shift of replacing income and sales taxes with beneficial levies on pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay frames the 'Single Tax' shift as the only 'big change' compared to the 'small change' of standard political reforms.

### [Capitalism Defined and Explained](https://www.progress.org/articles/capitalism-defined-and-explained/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“economies have three inputs”**
  - sentence: …The term “capitalism” is inherently confusing, since economies have three inputs: land, labor, and capital goods, and there is no logical reason to emphasize capital, unless one are going beyond the definition by condemning an economy in wh…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes land from capital goods to refute the conflation of the two factors.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“no logical reason to emphasize capital”**
  - sentence: …The term “capitalism” is inherently confusing, since economies have three inputs: land, labor, and capital goods, and there is no logical reason to emphasize capital, unless one are going beyond the definition by condemning an economy in wh…
  - why: The essay argues that the economic term 'capitalism' obscures the distinct factor of land, supporting the narrative that economic terminology has been distorted.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“land being an object of forceful acquisition”**
  - sentence: …In his essay “Structures of Power,” Weber wrote of land being an object of forceful acquisition, with ground rent frequently being “the produce of violent political subjection.”…
  - why: The essay cites Weber's observation that land rent stems from violent political subjection, evidencing the claim that land titles originate in force.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“capital dominates labor”**
  - sentence: …The term “capitalism” is inherently confusing, since economies have three inputs: land, labor, and capital goods, and there is no logical reason to emphasize capital, unless one are going beyond the definition by condemning an economy in wh…
  - why: The essay critiques the current economic system as one where capital dominates labor, aligning with the rentier economy narrative.

### [Capitalism: a brilliantly confused story](https://www.progress.org/articles/capitalism-a-brilliantly-confused-story/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“enormous increase in land rent”**
  - sentence: …But the biggest subsidy to landowners is implicit: it is the enormous increase in land rent and land value due to the public goods provided by government.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that public goods like streets and transit pump up land values, capitalizing public investment into private wealth.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“mountains of debt and derivatives”**
  - sentence: …Thus the financial sector has created mountains of debt and derivatives ultimately grounded in land value.…
  - why: The essay traces the expansion of the financial sector and its instability to its foundation in land value and mortgage credit.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“source of panics and depressions”**
  - sentence: …Speculation raises land values to peaks unaffordable for actual use, and then land values plunge, and the leveraged mortgages and derivatives crash.…
  - why: The essay identifies land speculation and the subsequent crash of leveraged land values as the root cause of economic boom-bust cycles.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“stop subsidizing real estate”**
  - sentence: …There is only one remedy for the boom-bust cycle, and that is to stop subsidizing real estate.…
  - why: The essay proposes taxing land rent to eliminate the subsidies that drive speculative bubbles, thereby stabilizing the economy.

### [Carbon Credits: False Absolution](https://www.progress.org/articles/carbon-credits-false-absolution/)
- **counters** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“Carbon credits add to pollution”**
  - sentence: …Carbon credits add to pollution by reducing the incentive to actually reduce pollution.…
  - why: The essay argues that carbon credits undermine the incentive to reduce emissions, countering the claim that pricing carbon effectively cuts emissions.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“The optimal environmental policy”**
  - sentence: …The optimal environmental policy is to reduce pollution by making polluters pay for their damage.…
  - why: The essay advocates for making polluters pay for damage rather than offsetting it, supporting the Georgist principle of charging for the use of natural commons.
- **illustrates** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“certified by a trustworthy organization”**
  - sentence: …For carbon credits or offsets to be effective, the beneficial environmental activity would need to be certified by a trustworthy organization.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the difficulty of accurate assessment and verification in environmental markets, paralleling the objection that land value cannot be accurately assessed.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“the firm or nonprofit organization”**
  - sentence: …Otherwise the firm or nonprofit organization that provides the credits can exaggerate the benefits, or even commit fraud.…
  - why: The essay highlights the risk of fraud and exaggeration by private actors in the carbon market, illustrating the public-choice concern that rent-seeking and gaming occur in complex systems.

### [Card Games Banned for Seniors](https://www.progress.org/articles/card-games-banned-for-seniors/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Government officials are often cruel tyrants”**
  - sentence: …Government officials are often cruel tyrants, as those with the greatest will to power seek public office in order to use the state to impose authority.…
