  - why: The essay exemplifies the public-choice critique that officials seek power to impose authority rather than serve the public.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Government is often not a servant of the people”**
  - sentence: …Government is often not a servant of the people, but a cruel master.…
  - why: The essay disputes the assumption that government intervention is benign or efficient, countering arguments that rely on state capacity to implement complex reforms.
- **illustrates** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“use the state to impose authority”**
  - sentence: …Government officials are often cruel tyrants, as those with the greatest will to power seek public office in order to use the state to impose authority.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the fear that state power attracts those who wish to dominate, supporting the objection that large government revenues invite abuse.

### [Cato on the Financial Crisis](https://www.progress.org/articles/cato-on-the-financial-crisis/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“fundamental cause is massive subsidies”**
  - sentence: …None of the financial regulations, going back to the Great Depression, confront the causes of the boom and bust.…
  - why: The essay argues that the boom-bust cycle is driven by subsidized land values and speculation, not financial regulation.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“generate greater land rent”**
  - sentence: …The provision of public works, welfare to the poor and elderly, and artificially cheap credit, all generate greater land rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that public works and services increase land rent and value, capitalizing into a wealth transfer to landowners.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“invisible to the public”**
  - sentence: …After the public domain was disposed of, the government continued the subsidy of the large landed interests with implicit policies that are invisible to the public and to most economists.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative that economic understanding is obscured by 'invisible' policies favoring landed interests.
- **illustrates** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“favor it even at the price”**
  - sentence: …None of the financial regulations touch this basic cause, and land-value seeking is so deeply ingrained in American culture that people favor it even at the price of high taxes, high unemployment, and other economic deprivation.…
  - why: The essay provides a vivid illustration of the political resistance to land value taxation, describing a cultural preference for land speculation over economic well-being.

### [Chicago, Vienna, and San Francisco](https://www.progress.org/articles/chicago-vienna-and-san-francisco/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“theory of the business cycle”**
  - sentence: …The San Francisco school has a theory of the business cycle based on land values, which rise during a boom, when speculation carries land prices so high that investment gets choked off, resulting in a recession.…
  - why: The essay explicitly outlines the Georgist business cycle theory, attributing recessions to land speculation choking off investment.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Join together the Vienna and San Francisco”**
  - sentence: …Join together the Vienna and San Francisco theories of the business cycle to get a more complete picture.…
  - why: The essay argues for a synthesis of the Austrian (credit-based) and Georgist (land-based) cycle theories, countering the view that they are mutually exclusive rivals.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“tax on land not affecting the rent”**
  - sentence: …Equilibrium shows that since market rent is based on the fixed supply of land and the demand to rent space, the tax on land not affecting the rent.…
  - why: The essay provides a concise equilibrium argument that a tax on land does not impact the market rent, supporting the claim that the tax cannot be passed to tenants.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“public revenue from land rent replacing taxes”**
  - sentence: …George analyzed the cause of poverty in land tenure and taxation, the remedy being a free-market with public revenue from land rent replacing taxes on wages, business profits, goods, and value added.…
  - why: The essay defines the core Georgist remedy as shifting the tax burden from labor and capital to land rent.

### [City Density—Friend of Trip Efficiency](https://www.progress.org/articles/city-densityfriend-of-trip-efficiency/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shift the property tax off buildings”**
  - sentence: …To reform local taxes, residents can perform their civic duty. Join a group, or form one, or persuade one they belong to to help shift taxes to spur residents to resettle on land more economically.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes off buildings and onto land incentivizes owners to develop vacant lots and redevelop dilapidated buildings, directly supporting the claim that split-rate taxation 
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“absorbed new development that otherwise would have become sprawl”**
  - sentence: …When Scranton shifted its property tax off buildings, onto locations, the city absorbed new development that otherwise would have become sprawl.…
  - why: The essay cites Scranton as a case study where a land value tax shift prevented suburban expansion, evidencing the claim that LVT reduces urban sprawl.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“drew up surrounding location values by more than the cost”**
  - sentence: …A subway, an amphitheater, in the old days a post office, all those stamps on the land drew up surrounding location values by more than the cost of building and operating the project.…
  - why: The essay explicitly references the Henry George Theorem and the economic principle that public investments increase nearby land values, supporting the claim that public investment capitalizes into la
- **illustrates** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“pay residents dividends from the ground rents”**
  - sentence: …The embarrassment of riches enables jurisdictions to pay residents dividends from the ground rents— a la Aspen Colorado .…
  - why: The essay proposes using recovered land rents to fund citizen dividends, citing Aspen and Singapore as models, which illustrates the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty and inequality.

### [Climate Change from a Georgist Perspective](https://www.progress.org/articles/climate-change-from-a-georgist-perspective/)
- **counters** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“Geoist taxes can’t possibly affect”**
  - sentence: …Geoist taxes can’t possibly affect a reduction of CO2 emissions.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that economic rent capture alone cannot reduce emissions, countering the claim that carbon pricing effectively cuts emissions.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“removing the super profits from the industry”**
  - sentence: …However, having said the above, value capture should be applied to fossil fuels — not that doing so will reduce the amount burned, but because removing the super profits from the industry will offer renewables and conservation a level playi…
  - why: The essay supports the efficacy of resource rent capture by arguing it strips super-profits from fossil fuels to create a level playing field for renewables.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“Green Georgists who want to slap”**
  - sentence: …Green Georgists who want to slap economic rent capture on fossil fuels to spike their price to discourage their use to help avoid climate change.…
  - why: The passage illustrates the internal debate within Georgism regarding the application of rent capture to environmental issues, which is the core of the Green Georgism narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/taxing-quasi-rents-kills-innovation`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-quasi-rents-kills-innovation/) — anchor: **“rent capture allows companies a roughly”**
  - sentence: …Even full economic rent capture won’t reduce emissions since, by definition, rent capture allows companies a roughly 5% 'operating profit' so as not to damage any industry.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that rent capture damages industries, countering the objection that taxing quasi-rents (or super-profits) kills innovation or harms the sector.

### [Co-existing with Rent-seekers](https://www.progress.org/articles/co-existing-with-rent-seekers/)
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“regarded as economic rent”**
  - sentence: …Corporate profits, dividends, and capital gains from share buybacks all need to be regarded as economic rent or unearned income, and heavily taxed.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that corporate profits and capital gains constitute economic rent that should be reclaimed by the government.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“unearned income he generates”**
  - sentence: …The answer is: only to the point that the rent-seeker is allowed a (sometimes generous) wage, but not to the point that he is allowed to keep most of the unearned income he generates…
  - why: It defines rent-seeking as generating unearned income and argues that allowing privatization of this income is parasitic, aligning with the claim that rent-seeking drags growth.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“unearned income he generates”**
  - sentence: …The answer is: only to the point that the rent-seeker is allowed a (sometimes generous) wage, but not to the point that he is allowed to keep most of the unearned income he generates…
  - why: The essay identifies landlords, lenders, employers, and speculators as rent-seekers extracting unearned income, illustrating the dynamics of a rentier economy.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Corporations totally owe their existence”**
  - sentence: …Corporations totally owe their existence to the people-owned state and federal governments that created and maintain them.…
  - why: It counters the Marxist view that Georgism merely saves capitalism by asserting that corporations are public creations and their rents should be reclaimed for the public benefit.

### [Conditions for Peace between Israel and Palestine](https://www.progress.org/articles/conditions-for-peace-between-israel-and-palestine/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“equal ownership of the land”**
  - sentence: …With the State of Israel at war once again with Arabs, this time with Hamas in Gaza and Hezbollah in Lebanon, it is time to revisit the only peace plan that can end the conflict: an equal ownership of the land by all inhabitants, under a co…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific model of land justice—equal ownership via rent sharing—as a solution to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“pay for the expenses of the Confederation”**
  - sentence: …Half the rent would pay for the expenses of the Confederation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that collecting land rent is sufficient to fund the operations of the proposed confederation government.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed to the residents”**
  - sentence: …The other half would be periodically distributed to the residents.…
  - why: The essay advocates distributing half the collected land rent directly to residents as a concrete benefit of equal ownership.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“the rent reflects the benefit”**
  - sentence: …The only practical implementation of equality is for each permanent resident to have an equal share of the land rent, as the rent reflects the benefit from the land.…
  - why: The essay justifies equal rent shares by asserting that rent reflects the benefit derived from the land, implying a social or communal source of value.

### [Contra Argumentation Ethics](https://www.progress.org/articles/contra-argumentation-ethics/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“Libertarians have no consensus”**
  - sentence: …Libertarians have no consensus on issues such as abortion, capital punishment, land value subsidies, the use of the military, and the justification of imposed government, but argumentation does not resolve such issues either.…
  - why: The essay critiques the internal consistency of libertarian ethics, noting that libertarians lack consensus on issues like land value subsidies, thereby undermining the absolutist claims often associa
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“justification of imposed government”**
  - sentence: …Libertarians have no consensus on issues such as abortion, capital punishment, land value subsidies, the use of the military, and the justification of imposed government, but argumentation does not resolve such issues either.…
  - why: By highlighting the lack of libertarian consensus on the justification of government, the essay challenges the rigid dichotomy often used to dismiss land value taxation as socialism, suggesting the et
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“argumentation does not resolve”**
  - sentence: …Libertarians have no consensus on issues such as abortion, capital punishment, land value subsidies, the use of the military, and the justification of imposed government, but argumentation does not resolve such issues either.…
  - why: The essay argues that abstract ethical argumentation fails to resolve practical policy disputes, countering the objection that Georgist policy must wait for other reforms like zoning to be theoretical
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“land value subsidies”**
  - sentence: …Libertarians have no consensus on issues such as abortion, capital punishment, land value subsidies, the use of the military, and the justification of imposed government, but argumentation does not resolve such issues either.…
  - why: The essay explicitly lists 'land value subsidies' as a point of disagreement among libertarians, countering the objection that land value taxation is merely a standard property tax by acknowledging it

### [Control and Management of Monopolies](https://www.progress.org/articles/control-and-management-of-monopolies/)
- **counters** [`objections/taxing-quasi-rents-kills-innovation`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-quasi-rents-kills-innovation/) — anchor: **“artificially high “quasi-rents””** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …I provide a link to Seligman’s book below, but in short, he adds to George’s concerns about monopolistic industries and explains why, unless closely scrutinized, the wages of certain professionals should not be regarded as the personal prop…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the Schumpeterian view that quasi-rents are necessary rewards for innovation, arguing instead that professional wages in monopolistic fields are often unearned monopoly r
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“The shareholders of these corporations cried “socialism,””**
  - sentence: …In short, banking, utility and railroad corporations did not want Congress managing their “monopolies other than the monopoly in land” like, for example, Congress was then managing the postal service or military.…
  - why: The essay provides a historical example of the 'socialism' objection being levied against Georgist proposals for public management of monopolies, illustrating the political resistance to such policies
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Platform of the Single Tax League”**
  - sentence: …When Henry George spelled out his official agenda in the first Georgist national Platform of the Single Tax League of the United States in 1890, a huge controversy erupted over his use of the simple words “and managed” in the 11th and final…
  - why: The essay details a specific historical controversy within the Single Tax movement regarding the scope of government intervention, illustrating the complexities of the Single Tax narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“monopolies other than the monopoly in land”**
  - sentence: …In short, banking, utility and railroad corporations did not want Congress managing their “monopolies other than the monopoly in land” like, for example, Congress was then managing the postal service or military.…
  - why: The essay argues that Georgism advocates for public control of non-land monopolies, countering the Marxist critique that Georgism is merely a facade for capitalist domination.

### [Coping with Iran](https://www.progress.org/articles/coping-with-iran/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“oil revenues of Iran”**
  - sentence: …The oil revenues of Iran, $50 billion per year, tap that natural resource, but much of the money goes to the military and Iran’s nuclear program.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the capture of resource rent (oil) but argues it is misappropriated rather than used for public benefit.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“untaxing labor and investment”**
  - sentence: …Iran would be best off untaxing labor and investment, and replacing these taxes with a levy on the rental value of all its land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that shifting taxes from labor/capital to land value would improve Iran's economic freedom and prosperity.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“land-reform program”**
  - sentence: …In 1963, the Shah, under American influence, initiated a “White Revolution” land-reform program.…
  - why: The essay discusses the failure of land reform and the persistence of unequal land tenure as a root cause of economic injustice.
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“socialism could not bring prosperity”**
  - sentence: …The poor of Iran hoped that the president’s program of “Islamic socialism” would bring them prosperity, but socialism could not bring prosperity to Russia or eastern Europe, and it cannot do so in Iran either.…
  - why: The essay argues that state ownership/socialism (nationalization) fails to solve economic problems, countering the idea that nationalization alone fixes land issues.

### [Corporations Aren’t Dysfunctional—the System Is](https://www.progress.org/articles/corporations-arent-dysfunctional-the-system-is/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Wall Street banks are massive”**
  - sentence: …Wall Street banks are massive; their power is nearly unrivaled.…
  - why: The essay argues that banks are huge specifically because they profit from land via mortgages, supporting the claim that banking growth is land-credit driven.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“profit from common wealth”**
  - sentence: …Most corporations are only so large because they profit from common wealth—from wealth that belongs to the people.…
  - why: The essay contends that corporate size and wealth derive from extracting value from the commons (land, nature) rather than productive activity, aligning with the claim that profits reflect economic re
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“forces people to look for jobs”**
  - sentence: …This forces people to look for jobs in order to pay the rent instead of allowing them (with more available cash and less risk) to engage in activities that create jobs for other people.…
  - why: The essay explains that land hoarding and rent burdens force workers into wage labor rather than entrepreneurial job creation, illustrating how high land rents suppress productivity and opportunity.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“financially rewards corporations with millions”**
  - sentence: …We the people are consenting to live in a system that financially rewards corporations with millions of dollars when they don’t add value to society.…
  - why: The essay describes a systemic flaw where the economy rewards corporations for extracting common wealth (rents) rather than adding value, which is the core definition of a rentier economy.

### [Correcting the Misconceptions](https://www.progress.org/articles/correcting-the-misconceptions-about-henry-georges-mistakes/)
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“the rich are growing richer”**
  - sentence: …Mr. George has devoted a large portion of his book, Progress and Poverty, to the assertion and illustration of his belief that, all over the civilized world, the rich are growing richer and the poor relatively poorer.…
  - why: Shearman defends George's thesis that wealth concentration is increasing, a core component of the claim that land costs drive poverty.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“ground rent would never be sufficient”**
  - sentence: …For they all, with one voice, maintain that ground rent would never be sufficient to meet the existing taxes; and so this question, if any of Mr. George’s critics are correct, could never arise.…
  - why: Shearman explicitly identifies and mocks the objection that ground rent is insufficient to fund government, framing it as a critic's error.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“concentration of all taxes upon ground rent”**
  - sentence: …He insists, with admitted eloquence and earnestness, that privately ownership of land must be abolished; but he proposes one remedy and only one, the concentration of all taxes upon ground rent alone.…
  - why: The essay summarizes the central mechanism of the Single Tax narrative—shifting all taxes to land rent.

### [Corruption in Tapping the Oil Rent](https://www.progress.org/articles/corruption-in-tapping-the-oil-rent/)
- **counters** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“MMS has been lax”**
  - sentence: …In the US, the Minerals Management Service (MMS), a bureau in the U.S. Department of the Interior, is responsible for monitoring the oil companies that operate in federal territory and collecting the revenue.…
  - why: The essay details institutional failures and corruption that prevented effective rent capture, challenging the claim that it works where institutions are strong.
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“incentives for effective royalty collection have been lacking”**
  - sentence: …Evidently, incentives for effective royalty collection have been lacking for many years, and the incentives have been reduced in recent years.…
  - why: The essay argues that large rent revenues invite political influence and regulatory capture, illustrating the resource-curse objection.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“The very structure of mass democracy is inherently corrupting”**
  - sentence: …The very structure of mass democracy is inherently corrupting.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the structure of government creates incentives for politicians to accept special-interest money, validating the public-choice critique.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Energy companies are big contributors”**
  - sentence: …Energy companies are big contributors to politicians who run or stand for office.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of how industry money influences government oversight, paralleling the narrative of corruption by landed interests.

### [Could a 137 Year-Old Equation Explain the Financial Collapse?](https://www.progress.org/articles/could-a-137-year-old-equation-explain-the-financial-collapse/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“tax systems rewarding private rent-seeking”**
  - sentence: …Land prices, representing the private capitalization of uncollected rent, have risen astronomically as a result of tax systems rewarding private rent-seeking at the expense of productive activity;…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing labor and capital while allowing rent-seeking depresses wages and profits, causing economic distress.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“mortgage-inflated profits of banks”**
  - sentence: …That the mortgage-inflated profits of banks would be reduced, as is necessary, is also a matter of concern to some.…
  - why: The essay links the financial collapse and banking profits to the treatment of land as a traded commodity rather than a revenue base.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“capturing natural resource rents for public revenue”**
  - sentence: …Indeed, capturing natural resource rents for public revenue might leave the rewards to labour and capital totally undiminished by taxation, and economic life greatly enhanced.…
  - why: The essay uses George's equation to argue that shifting taxes to land rent could fully fund government without harming wages or capital returns.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“P – R = W + I”**
  - sentence: …The transposition by Henry George of the distribution equation to P – R = W + I encapsulates his 1879 tour de force, Progress and Poverty , which became the world's best-seller in economics.…
  - why: The essay centers on Henry George's equation as the explanatory mechanism for wealth differentials and the rationale for the Single Tax.

### [Creating Change from the Ground Up](https://www.progress.org/articles/creating-change-from-the-ground-up/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“how wealth is created”**
  - sentence: …In this webinar hosted by North Atlantic Books , publisher of my book Land: A New Paradigm for a Thriving World , I explain in simple language how wealth is created, why money is scarce for most, and who receives what and why.…
  - why: The essay frames its explanation of wealth creation and distribution around the premise that land value is a social product.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“live in harmony with nature”**
  - sentence: …If we are to ever to create a world in which we can all have enough and still live in harmony with nature, I believe it’s vital that we understand these economic dynamics.…
  - why: The essay links the proposed economic model to the goal of ecological sustainability and harmony with nature.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“a radically new economic model”**
  - sentence: …In this webinar, I talk about a radically new economic model that promises a sustainable and abundant world for all.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a systemic shift in economic thinking, describing the Georgist land reform paradigm as a transformative model.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“land reform can create meaningful work”**
  - sentence: …For more information about how land reform can create meaningful work, restore our ecology, and bring more wealth into our local communities, I invite you to read my book Land: A New Paradigm for a Thriving World .…
  - why: The essay posits land reform as the solution to economic scarcity and the mechanism for restoring community wealth.

### [Creation and Confiscation of Currency](https://www.progress.org/articles/creation-and-confiscation-of-currency/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“confiscate property without any conviction”**
  - sentence: …With asset forfeiture, a government can confiscate property without any conviction or trial.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of government failure—asset forfeiture without trial—that illustrates the public-choice objection regarding state power and rent-seeking.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Federal Reserve and the Mint fear competition”**
  - sentence: …As monopolists..., the Federal Reserve and the Mint fear competition, and would rather force competitors out using the federal court system and the threat of asset forfeiture than compete in the market.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that entrenched interests (the Fed) use state power to suppress competition and protect their monopoly, aligning with the 'corruption' theme.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“government can confiscate any asset at any time”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Any government in the USA can confiscate any asset at any time if government officials merely suspect that the property has been involved in a crime.…
  - why: While about currency, the essay's argument that government officials can arbitrarily seize assets under color of law substantively illustrates the broader objection that tax systems can be corrupted f

### [Critics Blind to Land](https://www.progress.org/articles/critics-blind-to-land/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“elimination of taxes on production”**
  - sentence: …Henry George posited that the elimination of taxes on production and goods would bring forth a gusher of growth while providing greater equality, a proposition that has never been refuted by theory or facts.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing taxes on production (capital) in favor of land value taxation would spur growth, directly supporting the claim that shifting taxes to land improves welfare.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Taxes would be fixed cost per year”**
  - sentence: …Taxes would be fixed cost per year, based on a resource that does not hide from the tax collector, nor flee to other countries, nor shrink down when taxed.…
  - why: This passage outlines the efficiency and administrative advantages of taxing land over mobile factors like labor or capital, which is the core of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“critics such as Brin generally ignore land”**
  - sentence: …Unlimited property in land can be a problem, but critics such as Brin generally ignore land.…
  - why: The essay critiques critics (like Brin and Marx) for focusing solely on capital and ignoring the distinct economic role of land, countering the objection that land is just a form of capital.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“aristocratic golf buddies”**
  - sentence: …Here is the problem of today’s economies, according to Brin: “5,000 or so aristocratic golf buddies, who appoint each other to company boards in order to vote each other titanic ‘compensation packages’ while trading insider information and …
  - why: The essay cites Brin's description of corporate governance failures and excessive executive compensation as evidence that modern corporate profits increasingly reflect unearned rents rather than compe

### [Critics Want Other Stats: Why Not Rents?](https://www.progress.org/articles/critics-want-other-stats-why-not-rents/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“how much we’re all spending”**
  - sentence: …While our public agencies do not tell us how much we’re all spending for the land and nature we use in total, let’s not feel singled out.…
  - why: The essay argues that official statistics fail to measure the total economic rent available, implying that this unmeasured surplus is a potential funding source.
- **illustrates** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“determining the size of all locational value”**
  - sentence: …The OECD has produced useful reports before on the link between land value and economic growth , so maybe they could become a standard bearer for determining the size of all locational value.…
  - why: The essay highlights the difficulty in obtaining accurate statistics for land value, illustrating the practical challenges of assessment.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“incentives to governments to compile figures”**
  - sentence: …It’s in “ Government Finance Indicators: Truth and Myth ”, by, of all people, the (1) OECD ! ... it has also created great incentives to governments to compile figures on deficit and debt that look good, instead of them being good from an e…
  - why: The essay cites evidence that governments manipulate economic statistics to hide the truth, supporting the narrative that economic data and reporting are corrupted.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“surplus—also known as “economic rent””**
  - sentence: …This article is part of a series by Jeffery J. Smith on the surplus—also known as “economic rent”—that exists in the economy.…
  - why: The essay focuses on the existence and magnitude of economic rent, a core concept of the rentier economy narrative.

### [Curbing Climate Change Justly](https://www.progress.org/articles/curbing-climate-change-justly/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“Auction off Emission Permits”**
  - sentence: …complexity-loving wonks might warm up to four more, non-bossy policies : Auction off Emission Permits.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for auctioning emission permits as a key non-bossy policy to curb pollution.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“share some land value”**
  - sentence: …Some places do share some land value—Aspen CO and Singapore—and Alaska shares oil value.…
  - why: The essay argues that distributing land value (rent dividends) provides financial security, allowing people to make wiser environmental choices.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“in-fills cities”**
  - sentence: …However, where owners pay land dues (or land taxes), without any tax on buildings, they develop their lots, which in-fills cities.…
  - why: The essay claims that land taxes encourage development and infill, which reduces sprawl and promotes walking/cycling.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“pay the full annual rental value”**
  - sentence: …When extractors pay the full annual rental value of, say, coal to society, they’d be far less eager to go mining.…
  - why: The essay argues that charging extractors the full rental value of resources discourages environmentally harmful extraction.

### [Curious About Earth’s Worth? Pay Up!](https://www.progress.org/articles/curious-about-earths-worth-pay-up/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“surplus—also known as”**
  - sentence: …This article is part of a series by Jeffery J. Smith on the surplus—also known as “economic rent”—that exists in the economy.…
  - why: The essay introduces the concept of economic rent as a vast surplus that, if shared, could fund public possibilities.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“no longer providing research”**
  - sentence: …Surely some of the elite want even more wealth and power, even at the expense of democracy and broad prosperity.…
  - why: The essay argues that elites manipulate public institutions and data access to obscure economic realities and hoard wealth.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“state and the elite”**
  - sentence: …One huge blind spot in the worldview of anti-governmentarians is their belief that the state and the elite are separate entities.…
  - why: The essay critiques the public-choice view that government and elite interests are separate, arguing they are intertwined.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“once shared, this surplus”**
  - sentence: …Currently, this surplus is hoarded; yet once shared, this surplus could generate undreamed of possibilities for the entire human population.…
  - why: The essay frames the sharing of economic rent as a means to generate broad public benefit.

### [Decades of Wisdom from an LVT Advocate: Josh Vincent](https://www.progress.org/articles/decades-of-wisdom-from-an-lvt-advocate-josh-vincent/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“fill up these vacant lots”**
  - sentence: …He said, look, you’ve got to fill up these vacant lots to pay the bills.…
  - why: Illustrates the practical mechanism where LVT incentivizes development to generate revenue.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“working not in the best interest”**
  - sentence: …It was voted in in 1996 by the electorate of Allentown because it had been vetoed six times by city council who were working not in the best interest of the city but in the best interest of their golfing partners, if you will.…
  - why: Provides a concrete example of political interests blocking efficient tax reform.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“creating this land skyrocketed”**
  - sentence: …And it turns out that just creating this land skyrocketed the land value.…
  - why: Evidences that public creation of land (fill) captures immense value.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“reduction of even more corrosive”**
  - sentence: …Most of my time in the center’s time is spent exploring the feasibility of administering and implementing the land value based system of taxation, be it through the process of property tax or reduction of even more corrosive forms of tax, l…
  - why: Frames LVT as a tool to replace regressive taxes that burden labor and business.

### [Deplorable Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/deplorable-taxes/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Taxes artificially make labor”**
  - sentence: …Taxes artificially make labor more expensive while wreaking the balance of a worker between his desire for leisure and for goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing labor distorts market prices and reduces well-being, contrasting this with the commendable alternative of taxing land.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“Taxes stifle entrepreneurship and the investment”**
  - sentence: …Taxes stifle entrepreneurship and the investment that generates growth.…
  - why: The essay claims that taxes on enterprise and capital hinder growth, implying that shifting these taxes to land would remove this distortion and raise productivity.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“providing each person with an equal share”**
  - sentence: …A free market would provide a higher minimum income by maximizing labor productivity, letting workers keep their higher wage, and providing each person with an equal share of the community’s land rent.…
  - why: The essay explicitly proposes that community land rent can be distributed to provide income, supporting the claim that land rent is a viable funding source.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“belongs to the members of the relevant communities”**
  - sentence: …A levy on the occupation of land is a payment of the land rent that properly belongs to the members of the relevant communities.…
  - why: The essay asserts that land rent is properly owned by the community, which is the core moral argument of this narrative.

### [DeSantis is Half-Right About Property Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/desantis-is-half-right-about-property-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“Cities in Pennsylvania that shifted”**
  - sentence: …Cities in Pennsylvania that shifted taxes off buildings and onto land saw significant increases in development and decreases in vacancy, providing powerful evidence for this effect.…
  - why: The essay cites Pennsylvania cities as empirical evidence that shifting taxes off buildings and onto land boosts development.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“land tax prevents the corporate”**
  - sentence: …The land tax prevents the corporate land speculator from profiting on vacant or underutilized land and instead incentivizes the market to put the most desirable urban land to good use, encouraging development where it's most needed.…
  - why: The essay argues that a land tax stops speculators from profiting on vacant land and encourages productive use.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“land tax decreases the upfront”**
  - sentence: …But wouldn’t a land tax raise the price of land for new buyers? Counterintuitively, a land tax decreases the upfront purchase price.…
  - why: The essay explains that a land tax lowers the purchase price because buyers capitalize future tax payments into a lower offer.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“property tax is that it’s”**
  - sentence: …The trouble with the property tax is that it’s two taxes in one: a tax on buildings, and a tax on land.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes the 'bad' building tax from the 'good' land tax to refute the idea that property taxes are a single, uniform entity.

### [Design Mechanisms for Democracy, Prosperity, and Liberty](https://www.progress.org/articles/design-mechanisms-for-democracy-prosperity-and-liberty/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“shifting taxation to land value”**
  - sentence: …We can redesign the public finance mechanisms to the ultimate tax reform, shifting taxation to land value or land rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes to land value eliminates the excess burden caused by taxes on wages and capital.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“creates the incentives for”**
  - sentence: …Shifting to land value taxation creates the incentives for the efficient use of land and eliminates the disincentives of punitive taxes.…
  - why: The essay claims that land value taxation promotes efficient land use and removes economic disincentives.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“land rent reflects the benefits”**
  - sentence: …Since land is fixed in supply, and land rent reflects the benefits from civic services, tapping site values for public revenue is efficient and equitable.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that land rent reflects the value of civic services, justifying its use for public revenue.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Taxes on wages, business profits”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on wages, business profits, buildings, and sales create an excess burden on the economy, a waste of resources caused by the tax costs.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the waste of taxing labor and capital with the efficiency of taxing land.

### [Does a Tax on Land Imply Not Owning the Land?](https://www.progress.org/articles/does-a-tax-on-land-imply-not-owning-the-land/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“does not imply that the title holder”**
  - sentence: …A land-value tax does not imply that the title holder does not own the land, but only that one does not own the rent of the land.…
  - why: The essay directly refutes the objection that land value tax equates to renting from the state or constitutes socialism by clarifying the nature of ownership rights.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“public goods pump up land values”**
  - sentence: …But since public goods pump up land values, and landowners claw back the taxes they pay by receiving higher rent and land value from the public goods, ATCOR is not true...…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that public investment creates land value, using this mechanism to argue against the ATCOR theory.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Government imposes restrictions such as zoning”**
  - sentence: …In all countries, no private title holder owners all the rights to land. Government imposes restrictions such as zoning; it taxes the property, and may even take the land by asset forfeiture.…
  - why: The essay counters the idea that LVT is uniquely destructive of ownership by noting that government restrictions like zoning already limit ownership rights.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“who is the proper owner of the benefits”**
  - sentence: …Therefore those who complain that paying a tax on land implies that one is renting the land are engaging in semantics, and are also not confronting the basic moral question, who is the proper owner of the benefits from the land that nobody …
  - why: The essay supports the justice narrative by framing the moral question of who owns the unearned benefits of land.

### [Does Taxing Land Damage Land?](https://www.progress.org/articles/does-taxing-land-damage-land/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-causes-overdevelopment`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-causes-overdevelopment/) — anchor: **“imposing land dues”**
  - sentence: …And exemptions for housing does not create more affordable housing but less.…
  - why: The essay argues that charging land dues cannot force development because owners must still find projects profitable to attract capital.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“like those poor Indians”**
  - sentence: …They’re like those poor Indians who believed in treaties with the US.…
  - why: The essay uses the failure of treaties with Indigenous peoples to illustrate the precariousness of relying on political protections for land.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“get much more efficient”**
  - sentence: …So you’d get much more efficient urban land use.…
  - why: The essay argues that land dues would push development onto under-used downtown sites, reducing sprawl and motorized transport.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“residential dividend or”**
  - sentence: …The residential dividend or Citizens Dividend turns land value into commonwealth.…
  - why: The essay advocates for sharing recovered rents as a dividend to align public interests with environmental preservation.

### [Don’t Reject Data Centers. Negotiate Harder.](https://www.progress.org/articles/dont-reject-data-centers-negotiate-harder/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“one plot of land”**
  - sentence: …The data center industry in Loudoun County is so strong that one plot of land recently sold for $6 million per acre.…
  - why: Illustrates how private industry demand capitalizes into extreme land values, driving the price of land up to $6 million per acre.
- **counters** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“data centers should be developed”**
  - sentence: …Data centers should be developed.…
  - why: Counters the view that rent-seeking always drags growth, arguing that data centers—though rent-heavy—provide necessary compute infrastructure.
- **supports** [`objections/planning-restrictions-cause-high-prices`](https://progress.org/wiki/planning-restrictions-cause-high-prices/) — anchor: **“rejected a $2.5b data center”**
  - sentence: …In December of last year, residents in Chandler, Arizona cheered as the city council rejected a $2.5b data center development.…
  - why: Supports the claim that planning restrictions (rejection) cause high costs by blocking development that would expand fiscal capacity.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“generate significant revenue”**
  - sentence: …Instead they are large, nondescript buildings that generate significant revenue per employee.…
  - why: Supports the claim that capturing rent works, noting data centers generate massive revenue that can fund government without raising other taxes.

### [Down All Your Markets](https://www.progress.org/articles/down-all-your-markets/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate speculative boom”**
  - sentence: …What we should be worried about is the unsustainability of debt and the next real estate speculative boom.…
  - why: The essay explicitly predicts the next economic disaster based on the recurring real estate cycle.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“stop the land speculation”**
  - sentence: …A tax on most of the rent or land value would stop the land speculation that has made a few people rich at the expense of the public.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing land value would halt the speculation driving the boom-bust cycle.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“would not add to the cost of goods”**
  - sentence: …The Chinese leaders could have instead enacted LVT, land-value taxation, which would not add to the cost of goods.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the distortionary VAT with LVT, arguing LVT avoids increasing consumer prices.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“interventions into financial markets often backfire”**
  - sentence: …The government of China has yet to learn that interventions into financial markets often backfire.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that government intervention can successfully manage or prevent market cycles.

### [Downtowns: the Interior Frontier](https://www.progress.org/articles/downtowns-the-interior-frontier/)
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“wounds cities suffer from speculation”**
  - sentence: …Vacant lots are the wounds cities suffer from speculation in land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that vacant lots are a direct result of land speculation, causing negative outcomes like crime and unemployment.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“Shifting that tax from buildings to land”**
  - sentence: …Each owner would pay in land dues, which would replace the property tax. Shifting that tax from buildings to land is what worked in Pittsburgh and Harrisburg.…
  - why: The essay cites Pittsburgh and Harrisburg as successful examples where shifting taxes off buildings and onto land ended the waste of urban land.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“push behind sprawl would be redirected”**
  - sentence: …Like a good Buddhist, the push behind sprawl would be redirected inward.…
  - why: The essay argues that utilizing vacant lots within cities absorbs development demand, thereby preventing outward expansion into woods and farms.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value of a parcel of land does not belong”**
  - sentence: …However, the value of a parcel of land does not belong to the owner alone.…
  - why: The essay asserts that land value is created by the populace's demand, not the owner, justifying rent collection as compensation to the community.

### [Dropped by Gatekeepers](https://www.progress.org/articles/dropped-by-gatekeepers/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“how much is the worth”**
  - sentence: …If those sources (who should know but …) can not tell the public how much is the worth of Earth in America, could they at least tell us the price?…
  - why: The essay argues that the total value of land and natural resources is a massive, yet obscure, economic flow that could fund public needs.
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“range between official measurements”**
  - sentence: …Right now, the range between official measurements for seemingly the same is in the many trillions — not off by a few percentage points but by whole factors!…
  - why: The essay illustrates the practical difficulty of assessing land value by highlighting massive inconsistencies in government data.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“how the economy actually operates”**
  - sentence: …They may see that their number-crunching is not just well-paid busywork but that it also contributes to our understanding of how the economy actually operates, and how much surplus it generates.…
  - why: The essay suggests that bureaucratic obfuscation and poor data prevent the public from understanding economic surplus and land's role.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“spending for things not produced”**
  - sentence: …The thing to do is to how society’s spending for things not produced (land, etc) impacts society’s output of things we do produce (goods and services).…
  - why: The essay frames the investigation around analyzing the economic impact of spending on non-produced assets like land.

### [Earned Income and Unearned Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/earned-income-and-unearned-income/)
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“earned income is wages”**
  - sentence: …At the roots, earned income is wages, and unearned income is land rent.…
  - why: The essay explicitly defines the core Georgist distinction that land rent is unearned income while wages are earned.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“originating in labor and capital goods”**
  - sentence: …After having paid the land rent to the relevant community, none of the dividends include land rent. Therefore, originating in labor and capital goods, dividends are earned income.…
  - why: The essay argues that returns on capital are earned once land rent is separated, countering the claim that land is indistinguishable from capital.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“proper owners of the land rent”**
  - sentence: …If the people are equally the proper owners of the land rent, they deserve to receive it.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that the community, not the individual owner, is the proper recipient of land rent.
- **counters** [`objections/marshall-single-tax-objection`](https://progress.org/wiki/marshall-single-tax-objection/) — anchor: **“origin of the saved funds is wages”**
  - sentence: …The origin of the saved funds is wages, and the origin of the interest payment is wages. Therefore the interest income is wages.…
  - why: The essay refutes the idea that all asset returns are rent by distinguishing interest derived from wages (earned) from interest derived from rent (unearned).

### [Earth Sharing for a New Generation](https://www.progress.org/articles/earth-sharing-for-a-new-generation/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Citizen's Dividend Universal Basic Income”**
  - sentence: …Compensating for exclusive ownership is another policy we will likely see in the form of a Citizen's Dividend Universal Basic Income.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a Citizen's Dividend as a policy to compensate for exclusive ownership of natural resources.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“right to the land”**
  - sentence: …Like the sun, air, and water, we all have a right to the land and natural resources of nature.…
  - why: The essay argues that access to land and natural resources is an equal human right, framing exclusive ownership as unjust.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“eliminate poverty”**
  - sentence: …Sharing the natural resources of Earth inclusively as a collective would also help to curb pollution, eliminate poverty, and allow prosperity for everyone on a more leveled economic playing field.…
  - why: The essay claims that inclusive sharing of natural resources (rent capture) would eliminate poverty and level the economic playing field.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“help to curb pollution”**
  - sentence: …Sharing the natural resources of Earth inclusively as a collective would also help to curb pollution, eliminate poverty, and allow prosperity for everyone on a more leveled economic playing field.…
  - why: The essay links the sharing of natural resources to environmental benefits like curbing pollution.

### [Ecocide](https://www.progress.org/articles/ecocide/)
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“Millions upon millions of privately owned acres”**
  - sentence: …Millions upon millions of privately owned acres contribute to an artificially created scarcity in a world of plenty.…
  - why: The essay argues that private landholding for speculative gain creates artificial scarcity and underuse, directly supporting the claim that land speculation leads to waste.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“encourages a sprawl of human civilization”**
  - sentence: …And third, our current model of land ownership encourages a sprawl of human civilization as populations seek out land that’s still available at a lesser cost.…
  - why: The text explicitly links the current model of land ownership (speculative holding) to urban sprawl and the destruction of nature, supporting the Georgist argument that land value taxation reduces spr
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“destruction of nature is the direct result”**
  - sentence: …The destruction of nature is the direct result of a severely dysfunctional economic system, a system we have created ourselves over millennia.…
  - why: The essay frames environmental destruction as a consequence of an economic system that allows private profiting from nature, which is the core thesis of the 'Green Georgism' narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“incentivizes the destruction of our own habitat”**
  - sentence: …It doesn’t take too much imagination to realize that as long as people and institutions are allowed to profit from land at the expense of other people, we’re enabling a system that incentivizes the destruction of our own habitat.…
  - why: The essay argues that allowing profit from land (rent-seeking) incentivizes ecological destruction rather than productive growth, illustrating the negative externalities of rent-seeking behavior.

### [Economic Capitalization](https://www.progress.org/articles/economic-capitalization/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“capitalization of territorial benefits”**
  - sentence: …One of the most important occurrences in an economy is the economic capitalization of territorial benefits into land values.…
  - why: The essay explains the mechanism by which public amenities raise ground rent and land value.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“net benefits of the location”**
  - sentence: …As land has no cost of production, the market price of land is the capitalization of the net benefits of the location and the material natural resources, including the climate: rainfall, sunlight, and temperature.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value derives from location benefits created by the community, not production costs.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base/) — anchor: **“tax on the land rent”**
  - sentence: …A tax on the land rent or value, such as by a property tax or an income tax on the rental income, gets capitalized down into lower purchase prices for land.…
  - why: The essay explains that taxing land rent lowers purchase prices but does not destroy the rental flow, refuting the idea that the tax base vanishes.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“ignoring land for both mathematical convenience”**
  - sentence: …It goes back to the mathematization of academic economics into models of K and L, capital and labor, ignoring land for both mathematical convenience and the influence of landed interests who turned grounded classical theory into ethereal ne…
  - why: The essay attributes the exclusion of land from economic models to the influence of landed interests.

### [Economic Depressions: What Causes Them, and How to Prevent Them](https://www.progress.org/articles/economic-depressions-what-causes-them-and-how-to-prevent-them/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“property prices—driven by increases in underlying land values”**
  - sentence: …In 1983, British economist Fred Harrison published his seminal book The Power in the Land , in which he analyzed the economic history of Great Britain since 1701 and noted that property prices—driven by increases in underlying land values—t…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land-value cycles drive the business cycle, citing Fred Harrison's 18-year cycle theory.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“high cost of land restricts the optimal functioning”**
  - sentence: …Thus, could it be that the high cost of land restricts the optimal functioning of the economy?…
  - why: The essay argues that high land costs restrict the ability to produce wealth, causing economic contraction.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“creation of wealth must always precede the availability of money”**
  - sentence: …But this conclusion is the economic equivalent of putting the cart before the horse because the creation of wealth must always precede the availability of money, since money only functions as a medium in the exchange of wealth.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that money supply or credit is the root cause of depressions, arguing instead that wealth production (constrained by land) is primary.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“accurately predict not only the timing of the major depression”**
  - sentence: …Harrison’s observations were so on point that he went on record to accurately predict not only the timing of the major depression of 1992, but also the global 2008–2010 depression in…
  - why: The essay cites Fred Harrison's successful predictions of major depressions to counter the claim that bubbles cannot be identified in advance.

### [Economic Forecast 2008](https://www.progress.org/articles/economic-forecast-2008/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“18-year real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …The 18-year real estate cycle continues to march on right on schedule.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for the existence and predictive power of the 18-year land cycle.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“geo-Austrian synthesis”**
  - sentence: …On July 2007, I published a booklet entitled The Depression of 2008, in which I explain the business cycle based on a synthesis of the Austrian-school and the Henry-George cycle theories, a geo-Austrian synthesis.…
  - why: The author integrates Austrian credit theory with Georgist land theory to refute the idea that credit alone drives cycles.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“secondary mortgage market”**
  - sentence: …I also include the role played by the secondary mortgage market in spreading the pain of the real estate crash to the whole financial industry.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how mortgage credit secured against land transmits a real estate bust into a broader financial crisis.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“government chiefs are basically”**
  - sentence: …The government chiefs are basically putting duct tape on a collapsing economic structure.…
  - why: The essay dismisses monetary and fiscal interventions as ineffective fixes for the structural land-driven cycle.

### [Economic Indicators Point You Away From What Matters](https://www.progress.org/articles/economic-indicators-point-you-away-from-what-matters/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“land is cosmos-made”**
  - sentence: …Of the two, only buildings are manmade capital; land is cosmos-made and so not actually “capital” at all.…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes land from manmade capital to argue that land should not be counted as fixed capital in GDP.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“obeisance to FIRE”**
  - sentence: …And thus the official obeisance to FIRE (Finance, Insurance, & Real Estate)—now the biggest category in the GDP—was complete.…
  - why: The essay argues that official statistics have been manipulated to inflate the perceived contribution of finance and real estate to the economy.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“massaging the data”**
  - sentence: …Back up to officialdom redefining their chosen phenomena—which amounts to massaging the data.…
  - why: The essay claims that economic indicators are intentionally redefined by powerful interests to obscure economic reality.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“spending for things never produced”**
  - sentence: …Truly inexcusable is the official failure to measure basic economic reality, like our spending for things never produced, namely land.…
  - why: The essay critiques the failure to measure spending on land, highlighting the economy's focus on unproductive rent-seeking.

### [Economics Olympics](https://www.progress.org/articles/economics-olympics/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“replacing punitive taxes with”**
  - sentence: …Greatest reformer: Henry George, who proposed replacing punitive taxes with public revenue from land rent.…
  - why: Endorses George's proposal to shift the tax base to land rent.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“quantifying the dead weight loss”**
  - sentence: …Best on quantifying the dead weight loss of taxation: T. Nicolaus Tideman.…
  - why: Highlights Tideman's work on tax inefficiency, which supports the case for shifting to efficient land taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“predicted the 1990 and 2008-10 downturns”**
  - sentence: …Best economic forecaster: Fred Harrison, who predicted the 1990 and 2008-10 downturns.…
  - why: Cites Fred Harrison's successful predictions of land-driven crashes.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“downward jump in production”**
  - sentence: …Best original thought: Mason Gaffney, for the 'quantum leap effect,' the downward jump in production when a firm cannot pass on a tax.…
  - why: References Gaffney's argument that tax burdens on production cause output drops, countering the view that land taxes are insufficient without zoning reform.

### [Editor's Note](https://www.progress.org/articles/editors-note/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“closely tied to the Geoism movement”**
  - sentence: …In the past, Progress has been closely tied to the Geoism movement, which will remain and with an academic and professionally minded approach.…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies the publication's historical and ongoing connection to the Geoist movement, which is the subject of this narrative page.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“Early settlers migrated to escape”**
  - sentence: …Early settlers migrated to escape political tensions, religious persecution, and wealth gap disparities, among other socioeconomic injustices, many of which we still see today.…
  - why: This sentence references the historical context of settlement and the socioeconomic injustices of that era, which are central to the discussion of land justice and dispossession.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“wealth gap disparities”**
  - sentence: …Early settlers migrated to escape political tensions, religious persecution, and wealth gap disparities, among other socioeconomic injustices, many of which we still see today.…
  - why: The essay highlights the persistence of wealth gap disparities stemming from historical injustices, which aligns with the narrative that land theft and enclosure drive modern inequality.

### [Efficiency Taxes vs. Inefficiency Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/efficiency-taxes-vs-inefficiency-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“polluter pays method”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The "polluter pays" method is more efficient than command-and-control regulation, since it does not force everyone to use a specific method, but allows each person and company to adjust according to its own costs and benefits.…
  - why: The essay argues that charging for pollution is more efficient than regulation, supporting the claim that carbon pricing cuts emissions without wrecking growth.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“charges that compensate for social costs”**
  - sentence: …The main categories of efficient taxes are user fees, charges based on land value or rent, and charges that compensate for social costs such as pollution and congestion.…
  - why: The essay lists congestion charges as a primary category of efficient taxes that reduce economic harm.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“tapping of land value”**
  - sentence: …A simultaneous shift that decreases taxes on sales, income, and buildings while increasing levies on land value, pollution, and congestion would reduce pollution while increasing economic efficiency, raising profits and wages.…
  - why: The essay advocates replacing taxes on buildings and income with land value levies to eliminate deadweight loss and increase productivity.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“increase productivity and wages”**
  - sentence: …In fact, efficiency taxes would substantially increase productivity and wages.…
  - why: The essay contends that shifting to efficiency taxes, specifically land value taxes, raises productivity and wages by removing the excess burden of current taxes.

### [Electing Geoist Candidates in the United States](https://www.progress.org/articles/electing-geoist-candidates-in-the-united-states/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“run candidates for office”**
