  - sentence: …One of the ways to promote LVT and other Geoism principles is to run candidates for office.…
  - why: The essay proposes a political strategy to overcome the lack of adoption by organizing caucuses and running candidates.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“propose and support LVT legislation”**
  - sentence: …If a candidate wins, then that person can propose and support LVT legislation.…
  - why: The essay outlines a practical path for enacting the Single Tax narrative through electoral politics.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“advocates LVT”**
  - sentence: …Within the Democratic Party, there is the Democratic Freedom Caucus, which advocates LVT and has endorsed Democratic candidates whose platform includes LVT.…
  - why: The essay highlights political groups actively promoting the shift of taxation onto land.

### [Eliminate Electronic Voting](https://www.progress.org/articles/eliminate-electronic-voting/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“foundations of government are on quicksand”**
  - sentence: …With electronic voting, we no longer have a genuine democracy; the foundations of government are on quicksand.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice concern that government failure and corruption undermine legitimacy, specifically via the vulnerability of electronic voting to theft.
- **counters** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“return to our origins”**
  - sentence: …Human beings evolved from small bands, and we need to return to our origins.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a return to small-scale, direct governance as a remedy for modern dysfunction, countering the Georgist focus on systemic economic reform within the modern state.
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“How much corruption will we tolerate”**
  - sentence: …How much corruption will we tolerate until society stumbles into the effect solution?…
  - why: The essay reinforces the objection that concentrated power—whether in voting machines or rent revenue—breeds corruption that society struggles to prevent.

### [Empires and Human Behavior](https://www.progress.org/articles/empires-and-human-behavior/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“sample set of human behavior”**
  - sentence: …If your entire sample set of human behavior was observing them playing the game of monopoly , you might come away with the impression that humans are irredeemably ruthless and greedy, incapable of even the most rudimentary level of compassi…
  - why: The essay argues that observed human nature is a product of the economic system's rules, supporting the Georgist critique that mainstream economics ignores systemic incentives in favor of fixed behavi
- **illustrates** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“reshaping the landscape with enormous”**
  - sentence: …They engaged in some of the first grand scale agricultural projects, reshaping the landscape with enormous irrigation systems, some which took over 100 years to complete, utilizing enormous amounts of labor, all managed by the state.…
  - why: The description of state-managed irrigation creating long-term value illustrates the principle that land value is generated by public investment and community planning.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“amalgam primarily pieced together out of”**
  - sentence: …We live now in a global system, and it is the system bequeathed to us as an amalgam primarily pieced together out of cultural elements of those societies throughout history that were most adept at conquest, subjugation, and genocide.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that current land titles and economic systems are rooted in historical conquest and dispossession rather than just production.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“mass redistribution being a fairly normal”**
  - sentence: …The emperor personally 'owned' enormous amounts of agricultural land, and taxed even more, and we have records of mass redistribution being a fairly normal activity the government engaged in.…
  - why: The historical example of state-organized redistribution serves as an illustration of the feasibility and historical precedent for capturing common wealth for public benefit.

### [Empty Sovereignty](https://www.progress.org/articles/empty-sovereignty/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“worth of land is created”**
  - sentence: …The worth of land is created either by nature itself, or by the activity of the surrounding community: people producing wealth, governments providing public services and infrastructure.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that land value derives from nature and community activity, which is the core thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“That's the big robbery”**
  - sentence: …When it gives that value up to private owners (whether or not they're foreign), it has already yielded up its sovereignty.…
  - why: The essay frames the private appropriation of socially created land value as a 'big robbery' and a loss of sovereignty, echoing the justice argument that land titles represent an enclosure of common w
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“value of its land”**
  - sentence: …A nation's most important asset is the value of its land.…
  - why: By identifying land value as the nation's primary asset, the essay implies that capturing this rent is essential for national sovereignty and fiscal capacity.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“collect that rent”**
  - sentence: …Yet private land 'owners' are allowed to collect that value.…
  - why: The essay critiques the private collection of unearned rent, supporting the narrative that the economy is increasingly dominated by rent-seeking asset owners rather than producers.

### [Enacting Land Value Return in your hometown](https://www.progress.org/articles/enacting-land-value-return-in-your-hometown/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“taxing buildings discourages developing”**
  - sentence: …We point out that taxing buildings discourages developing downtown to its full potential, while under-taxing downtown’s tremendous land value implicitly shifts property taxes onto everybody else in the city.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing improvements discourages development, implying that removing this tax via a split-rate would encourage construction.
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“valuable undeveloped and underdeveloped land”**
  - sentence: …First, we open by framing the conversation around how much valuable undeveloped and underdeveloped land is just sitting there:…
  - why: The essay uses data on underutilized land to illustrate the problem of speculative vacancy and wasted urban potential.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“taxing buildings discourages developing”**
  - sentence: …We point out that taxing buildings discourages developing downtown to its full potential, while under-taxing downtown’s tremendous land value implicitly shifts property taxes onto everybody else in the city.…
  - why: By contrasting the disincentives of taxing buildings versus land, the essay counters the claim that LVT is functionally identical to standard property taxes.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“rewards speculation”**
  - sentence: …We then move to concrete, common-sense examples of how the current property tax system punishes building and investment, and rewards speculation:…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that the current system rewards speculation, supporting the claim that shifting to LVT would dampen it.

### [Equifax Moving Forward](https://www.progress.org/articles/equifax-moving-forward/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“data is a financial life story”**
  - sentence: …The data is a financial life story, so to speak, of citizen consumers.…
  - why: The essay describes how corporations like Equifax and Facebook monetize personal data generated by users, exemplifying the rentier economy's shift toward profiting from asset ownership and information
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“insiders have been dumping company stocks”**
  - sentence: …Furthermore, days after the breach and weeks before it was made public, insiders have been dumping company stocks, which only makes the company image look worse in an already dim public relations fiasco.…
  - why: The insider trading following the breach serves as a concrete example of the public-choice critique, showing how agents within powerful entities prioritize private gain over public duty.
- **illustrates** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“nationalize or reorganize the industry”**
  - sentence: …Some pundits are saying that organizations like Equifax as well as the whole credit bureau needs to be overhauled.…
  - why: The essay explicitly raises the debate over whether nationalizing or reorganizing the credit bureau industry is the solution to its failures, directly engaging with the objection's premise.

### [Essay Contest 2nd Place: The Sum of All Desires](https://www.progress.org/articles/essay-contest-2nd-place-the-sum-of-all-desires/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“profit-incentive will be to eliminate”**
  - sentence: …Consequently, the profit-incentive will be to eliminate public spending on education, healthcare, and the social safety net, as workers will no longer be necessary for production or consumption and, therefore, might as well not exist from t…
  - why: The essay describes a dystopian scenario where rent-seekers, capturing returns from capital and land, displace labor and dismantle public goods, illustrating the drag on social welfare caused by rent-
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“Henry George theorem creates a virtuous cycle”**
  - sentence: …Moreover, the Henry George theorem creates a virtuous cycle whereby public spending on hospitals, schools, and other local public goods increases the value of land by more than the cost to build improvements and infrastructure.…
  - why: The essay explicitly invokes the Henry George Theorem to argue that public investment increases land value enough to fund its own costs, supporting the claim that public goods can be funded from the l
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“beauty of the single tax is it will make greed good”**
  - sentence: …The beauty of the single tax is it will make greed good in fact.…
  - why: The essay argues that the Single Tax harnesses self-interest (greed) for the common good by channeling private efforts into increasing land values that fund public services, supporting the narrative o
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“larger and larger universal basic income”**
  - sentence: …Snowballing land values will fund more and more public spending, as well as a larger and larger universal basic income, which in turn will raise land values further.…
  - why: The essay proposes that rising land values should fund a universal basic income, supporting the narrative of distributing common wealth as a citizen's dividend.

### [Essay Contest 3rd Place: The Georgist Jubilee](https://www.progress.org/articles/essay-contest-3rd-place-the-georgist-jubilee/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“mass displacement of America's indigenous”**
  - sentence: …Our land's history has been far from just; the very existence of the United States required the mass displacement of America's indigenous people from their land.…
  - why: The essay cites the dispossession of indigenous peoples as evidence that land titles descend from conquest rather than just production.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“reservations located in regions”**
  - sentence: …Today, many reside on reservations located in regions with the most minimal access to economic opportunity anywhere in the…
  - why: The essay highlights the economic marginalization of indigenous peoples on reservations, illustrating the intersection of land justice and economic outcomes.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“land ownership becomes a kind”**
  - sentence: …The philosophy of individual land ownership has always sat uneasily in the American tradition since land ownership becomes a kind of de facto monopoly over that economic resource.…
  - why: The essay argues that land ownership functions as a monopoly, distinguishing it from other forms of capital and challenging the view that land is merely capital.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land was a creation of God”**
  - sentence: …Israel's land was not earned by their hard work or conquest; the land was a creation of God, gifted to them by God.…
  - why: The essay uses the theological concept of land as a divine gift to illustrate the principle that land value is not created solely by individual effort.

### [Essay Contest Honorable Mention: Serfs No More](https://www.progress.org/articles/essay-contest-honorable-mention-serfs-no-more/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“lost opportunities”**
  - sentence: …We will never know, but that number is not insignificant and represents the most far-reaching cost of BS jobs - the lost opportunities.…
  - why: The essay argues that talent flowing into unproductive 'bullshit jobs' constitutes a massive unseen cost to society, directly illustrating the Georgist concept that rent-seeking drags growth by misall
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Without a broader suite”**
  - sentence: …Without a broader suite of Georgist policies, a UBI is unlikely to provide anything more than temporary relief for tenants everywhere.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that a single reform (UBI) is sufficient, arguing instead that a 'broader suite' of Georgist policies is necessary to address structural issues, countering the objection th
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“ballooning of administrative staff”**
  - sentence: …Graeber acknowledges that, while the number of professors in universities has followed the increase in the number of students, the ballooning of administrative staff has not, despite the dubious utility of all these roles.…
  - why: The essay uses the growth of non-productive administrative roles in universities and other sectors as a prime example of the 'rentier economy,' where income is derived from position rather than produc
- **counters** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“UBI is unlikely to provide”**
  - sentence: …Without a broader suite of Georgist policies, a UBI is unlikely to provide anything more than temporary relief for tenants everywhere.…
  - why: The essay critiques the standard UBI solution (often analogous to rent dividends) as insufficient without structural tax reform, suggesting that dividends alone cannot solve the underlying economic fu

### [Essay Contest Winner: A Common Heritage](https://www.progress.org/articles/essay-contest-winner-a-common-heritage/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“right of conquest”**
  - sentence: …What would the world look like if we refused to lay down before the right of conquest (where this right to privatize the common heritage of all mankind ultimately stems from) if we took the advice of economists like Henry George, of foundin…
  - why: The essay grounds the injustice of private land ownership in the historical 'right of conquest' and enclosure, arguing that titles trace back to theft rather than production.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value is generated collectively”**
  - sentence: …The value is generated collectively — the landowner collects the economic rent privately.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value arises from community investments like subways and businesses, which landowners then privately capture.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“housing prices to fall”**
  - sentence: …Economic incentives would shift, causing housing prices to fall as speculators are turned into developers.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing the ability to collect unearned rent would force speculators to develop land, increasing supply and lowering housing costs.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed to all members”**
  - sentence: …Under a Georgist framework, value generated collectively through community improvements and public investment is distributed to all members of the community while value generated through private investment remains private.…
  - why: The essay advocates for distributing collectively generated land value to the community via a basic income or negative income tax, framing it as a return of common heritage.

### [Every Day Is Earth Day](https://www.progress.org/articles/every-day-is-earth-day/)
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“efficient ways to steward”**
  - sentence: …There are efficient ways to steward the earth and resources for the benefit of everyone.…
  - why: The essay frames environmental stewardship as a matter of justice and efficiency, introducing Henry George's tax on land values as the solution for managing resources for the common benefit.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“creation of the community”**
  - sentence: …It is the taking by the community, for the use of the community, of that value which is the creation of the community.…
  - why: The essay quotes Henry George directly to argue that land value is created by the community and should be used for the community's benefit.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“selfish men or greedy interests”**
  - sentence: …Do not let selfish men or greedy interests skin your country of its beauty, its riches or its romance.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the unearned extraction of natural wealth by greedy interests with the moral imperative to treat the earth as a shared heritage.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal rights to the elements”**
  - sentence: …They must all have the same equal rights to the elements which Nature has provided for the sustaining of life—to air, to water, and to land.…
  - why: The essay asserts a universal birthright to natural resources, framing access to land and nature as a fundamental equal right.

### [Everything You Suspected About U.S. Gov’t Corruption is True](https://www.progress.org/articles/everything-you-suspected-about-govt-corruption-is-true/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“scientific proof of corruption”**
  - sentence: …Data presented in this video is based on a Princeton University study that reveals scientific proof of corruption at the highest levels of Congress.…
  - why: The essay provides empirical evidence for the public-choice critique that government policy is shaped by elites rather than the general public.
- **counters** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“scientific proof of corruption”**
  - sentence: …Data presented in this video is based on a Princeton University study that reveals scientific proof of corruption at the highest levels of Congress.…
  - why: While the objection warns that public rent collection breeds corruption, this essay illustrates that the existing political system is already corrupt, undermining the argument against capturing rent.

### [Evidence for a Uniform National Single Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/evidence-for-a-uniform-national-single-tax/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“uniform nationally-enforceable single tax”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …There should be no doubt that Henry George was seeking a uniform nationally-enforceable "single tax" when he established the Platform of the Single Tax League of the United States (copied below) on Sept. 3, 1890 in Coopers Union, New York, …
  - why: provides historical evidence of George's specific push for a national, uniform single tax platform
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“George abandoned the state”**
  - sentence: …But skimming through the back pages of George’s weekly newspaper, The Standard (1887-1892), reveals that George abandoned the state and local government approach in September 1890, that George was willing to lose well over half his “single …
  - why: argues that the lack of adoption was due to strategic shifts and historical contingencies rather than the policy's merit
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“raising all public revenues”**
  - sentence: …The national committee is circulating a petition asking the United States house of representatives to appoint a special committee to make inquiry into and report upon the expediency of raising all public revenues by a single tax upon the va…
  - why: cites the official 1890 platform demanding that land rent alone fund all government revenues
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Supreme Court struck down”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The Supreme Court struck down his landowner income taxes in Pollock v. Farmers' Loan (1895), and he died in 1897, and his lawyer Thomas Shearman died a few years later in 1900, so neither was around to remind their followers that the nation…
  - why: highlights the legal setbacks and loss of leadership that hindered the movement's historical progress

### [Experience and the Presidency](https://www.progress.org/articles/experience-and-the-presidency/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“public revenue should come from”**
  - sentence: …In economics, the chief would understand that public revenue should come from the land rent, not from punitive taxes on labor and capital.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the proper economic understanding for a government chief is that public revenue should be derived from land rent rather than taxes on labor or capital.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“not from punitive taxes on labor”**
  - sentence: …In economics, the chief would understand that public revenue should come from the land rent, not from punitive taxes on labor and capital.…
  - why: The essay advocates for the Georgist principle of shifting the tax burden off labor and capital onto land rent as a matter of economic wisdom.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“public revenue should come from the land rent”**
  - sentence: …In economics, the chief would understand that public revenue should come from the land rent, not from punitive taxes on labor and capital.…
  - why: The essay endorses the core Single Tax narrative—that land rent is the proper source of public revenue—by framing it as essential wisdom for governance.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“honor self-ownership by individuals”**
  - sentence: …In ethics, a government chief would understand natural moral law and honor self-ownership by individuals.…
  - why: By defining proper governance as honoring individual self-ownership and natural moral law, the essay implicitly counters the objection that land value capture is a form of socialism or collectivism.

### [Exporting the Alaska Model](https://www.progress.org/articles/exporting-the-alaska-model/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“Alaska’s decades-long Permanent Fund Dividend”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Alaska’s decades-long Permanent Fund Dividend shows that capturing natural-resource rent and distributing it as a citizen’s dividend is administratively and politically durable — a…
  - why: The essay details the history, popularity, and durability of the Alaska Permanent Fund Dividend as a successful example of resource rent distribution.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“helped Alaska attain the highest economic equality”**
  - sentence: …It has helped Alaska attain the highest economic equality of any state in the United States.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that the dividend helped Alaska achieve the highest economic equality of any state and provided unconditional cash assistance to needy residents.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“ensure that the state will continue to benefit”**
  - sentence: …As a sovereign wealth fund, it helps to ensure that the state will continue to benefit from its oil long after its reserves are depleted.…
  - why: The essay describes the Alaska Permanent Fund as a mechanism for capturing resource rents to ensure long-term state benefits, validating the feasibility of high-rate capture.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“the oil, by right, belonged to all Alaskans”**
  - sentence: …The argument was simple: the oil, by right, belonged to all Alaskans.…
  - why: The essay uses the Alaska model to illustrate the narrative that natural resource wealth is common property and should be distributed as a dividend to citizens.

### [Fannie & Freddie Get Bailed Out Again](https://www.progress.org/articles/fannie-and-freddie-get-bailed-out-again/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“perpetual mortgage machine”**
  - sentence: …The government has thus created a perpetual mortgage machine.…
  - why: The essay details how government-backed enterprises and subsidies inflate credit specifically to prop up land values.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“public goods provided by government”**
  - sentence: …The biggest subsidy is implicit: the public goods provided by government generate greater land rent and higher land values.…
  - why: The essay argues that government spending on public goods increases land rent, which is then captured by private landowners rather than the public.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“speculators jump in”**
  - sentence: …Government thus redistributes wealth from workers to landowners. The government generates a boom-bust cycle as land rent absorbs the gains from an economic expansion, and then speculators jump in to ride the surge in land values.…
  - why: The essay explicitly links government subsidies to land speculation that drives boom-bust cycles.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“not sustainable”**
  - sentence: …A big problem with the government subsidy of land values is that it is not sustainable, as it has always created a boom followed by a bust.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that government intervention can prevent cycles, arguing instead that subsidies inevitably cause busts.

### [Fannie has been a Bad Girl](https://www.progress.org/articles/fannie-has-been-a-bad-girl/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“huge increase in mortgages”**
  - sentence: …The promotion of mortgage-backed securities by Freddie and Fannie in the 1970s led to a huge increase in mortgages.…
  - why: The essay argues that government-sponsored enterprises fueled the expansion of mortgage credit, driving the speculative rise in real estate prices.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“land values get pumped up”**
  - sentence: …Meanwhile, land values get pumped up by governmental services and works that landowners don't pay for…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that public services increase land values, creating unearned gains for landowners.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“fuel the speculative rise of real estate prices”**
  - sentence: …The subsidized secondary market combined with high inflation to fuel the speculative rise of real estate prices that ended in the recession of 1980.…
  - why: The essay links government-subsidized credit to land speculation and the resulting economic bust, illustrating the Georgist land-cycle theory.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“eliminate the taxation of human action”**
  - sentence: …But to have a truly free market in real estate, the government has to eliminate the taxation of human action and its products, and stop its fiscal subsidy of landowners.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing taxes on labor and capital while capturing land value is the prerequisite for a free market, countering the view that zoning reform must precede land value capture.

### [Fate of the Union 2015: More Bad Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/fate-of-the-union-2015-more-bad-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“tax induces excessive borrowing”**
  - sentence: …So the tax induces excessive borrowing, making the firm vulnerable to bankruptcy if business declines.…
  - why: The essay argues that the tax preference for debt over equity (deductible interest vs non-deductible dividends) creates a debt bias, which is the core problem this claim addresses.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“shift all taxation to income that is completely unearned”**
  - sentence: …If the President and Congress seek more productivity and more economic justice, they should remove all taxes on earned income and shift all taxation to income that is completely unearned, including income that comes from subsidies.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for shifting the tax burden off labor and onto unearned income, which is the central thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“rent generated by the community”**
  - sentence: …The money one pays for land is a subsidy to previous owners, and the rent one receives now, either explicitly in rental money, or implicitly in the rental value, is a subsidy from the rent generated by the community and its public goods.…
  - why: The essay directly states that land rent is generated by the community and public goods, which is the foundational argument of this claim.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“high tax rate on wages or investment income stifles”**
  - sentence: …On the other hand, a high tax rate on wages or investment income stifles work effort, entrepreneurship, and investment.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing capital and labor harms economic productivity, implying that shifting to a base that does not stifle these factors (land) would improve welfare.

### [Favors for a Group or Dividends for All?](https://www.progress.org/articles/favors-for-a-group-or-shares-for-all/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“vastly pumped up land values”**
  - sentence: …Build It & We Will Gain Projects like the Eire Canal vastly shortened shipping times.…
  - why: The essay uses the Erie Canal and other public projects as historical examples of infrastructure raising nearby land values.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“disburse a Citizen’s Dividend”**
  - sentence: …Now that we know better, we can abolish favoritism for any group and disburse a Citizen’s Dividend to us all.…
  - why: The essay concludes by advocating for the replacement of special favors with a universal dividend derived from land value.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“The modern separation of gentry”**
  - sentence: …The modern separation of gentry and ruler is merely superficial.…
  - why: The essay argues that government institutions, from the Supreme Court to legislatures, historically and currently favor landowners over the public good.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“Vacant lots and under-used parcels”**
  - sentence: …Vacant lots and under-used parcels cause unemployment, poverty, crime, and ugliness of behavior and the manmade environment and curb supply.…
  - why: The essay explicitly links the hoarding of land for speculation to negative social and economic outcomes like unemployment and reduced supply.

### [Federal Government Continues Its Money Monopoly](https://www.progress.org/articles/federal-government-continues-its-money-monopoly/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“distortions that end up in recessions”**
  - sentence: …The Federal Reserve money monopoly does not just inflate the currency, but causes distortions that end up in recessions such as the current one.…
  - why: The essay argues that monetary expansion distorts relative prices—specifically inflating land values—which creates boom-bust cycles, directly supporting the narrative that land speculation drives rece
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Monetary policy along with government’s subsidy”**
  - sentence: …Monetary policy along with government’s subsidy to land values created the boom that ended with the Crash of 2008.…
  - why: The essay explicitly attributes the 2008 crash to the combination of monetary policy and land value subsidies, countering the objection that credit cycles alone are responsible.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“distortions that end up in recessions”**
  - sentence: …The Federal Reserve money monopoly does not just inflate the currency, but causes distortions that end up in recessions such as the current one.…
  - why: By asserting that the Federal Reserve's actions inevitably cause distortions and recessions, the essay counters the claim that government intervention can successfully tame the economic cycle.

### [Fencing Someone In](https://www.progress.org/articles/fencing-someone-in/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“fenced out of access”**
  - sentence: …But at some point, the more a person is fenced out of access to land, the more that resembles being fenced in.…
  - why: The essay argues that exclusive land ownership effectively imprisons those excluded, supporting the narrative that land titles constitute a theft of common liberty.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“paying him land rent”**
  - sentence: …For example, they could provide him with compensation by paying him land rent for excluding him from having as much access to land as they do.…
  - why: It argues that landowners must compensate the excluded for the value of access denied, grounding the moral claim that land value is socially created and owed to the community.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value of the land”**
  - sentence: …Preferably, compensation should be based on the value of the land, not the acreage, because different parts of the island have different access to natural resources, and different access to other valuable locations.…
  - why: It illustrates that land value derives from location and access to resources (community context), justifying compensation based on value rather than mere physical area.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“provide him with equivalent compensation”**
  - sentence: …The only consistent response is that the other nine should be obligated to either allow him to have as much right to move around as they have, or at least provide him with equivalent compensation.…
  - why: The essay's conclusion that exclusion requires compensation aligns with the Single Tax narrative of recovering rent for public use to rectify unequal access to the earth.

### [Finland’s Basic Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/finlands-basic-income/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“source of public revenue”**
  - sentence: …The three-dimensional spatial land we live in was provided by nature, and in effect, nature says, “Here, humans, is a free source of public revenue for your public goods and basic incomes.”…
  - why: The essay argues that land rent is a sufficient natural revenue source to fund public goods and basic income.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“guaranteed income”**
  - sentence: …By decreasing the current transfer payments and providing all persons with a guaranteed income, the basic income would at first increase land rent even more, as people could afford to bid higher for housing, and tax-free enterprise would bi…
  - why: The essay advocates for a basic income funded by land rent as a path to economic justice and prosperity.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“penalize labor and enterprise”**
  - sentence: …But people and their governments reply, “No thank you, O nature. We shall give this surplus to landowners as a special privilege. To pay for public goods, we will penalize labor and enterprise with high taxes.”…
  - why: The essay contrasts the inefficiency of taxing labor with the efficiency of taxing land.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“eliminating its anti-enterprise taxes”**
  - sentence: …If Finland departed from the EU, it could negotiate a free-trade agreement, but even if Finland faces import tariffs with other countries, the boost to its prosperity from eliminating its anti-enterprise taxes would generate high growth and…
  - why: The essay claims that replacing distortionary taxes with a land value tax would generate high growth and eliminate unemployment.

### [Five Forms of Unearned Corporate Net Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/five-forms-of-unearned-corporate-net-income/)
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“pure unearned income”**
  - sentence: …Under this form, any net profits which remain are pure unearned income, or what Henry George used to call “economic rent” or unearned “land value.”…
  - why: The essay explicitly defines corporate net income as economic rent, supporting the claim that profits are increasingly unearned rents.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“fiduciary duty hierarchy”**
  - sentence: …The fiduciary duty hierarchy of corporations is first to those work for it, second to the state and federal governments which created and protected them, and lastly to shareholders.…
  - why: The essay challenges the primacy of shareholder profit, countering the Marxist critique that Georgism merely preserves capitalist domination.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“Gains Derived from Share Buyback Scheme”**
  - sentence: …(5) Gains Derived from Share Buyback Scheme This form is similar to a stock dividend (#4), but with an added element of stock value manipulation.…
  - why: The essay details mechanisms like buybacks and stock manipulation that allow corporations to extract unearned wealth, illustrating the rentier economy.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“supply of gullible people”**
  - sentence: …In economics jargon, the supply of gullible people is highly elastic.…
  - why: The essay argues that the public is easily manipulated by corporate interests, supporting the public-choice critique regarding voter rationality.

### [Five Stages of the Georgist Movement](https://www.progress.org/articles/five-stages-of-the-georgist-movement/)
- **supports** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“Georgist movement, to Stage 3”**
  - sentence: …The last 10-20 seconds of this 4-minute O'Donnell clip provides some background about the transition from Stage 2 (the clarification stage) of the Georgist movement, to Stage 3 (the national appeal stage) mentioned above:…
  - why: The essay details the political history and legislative strategies of the Georgist movement from 1879 to 1913, directly supporting the narrative of Georgism's influence during the Progressive Era.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“tax on imputed land incomes”**
  - sentence: …Both income tax appeals being rejected, Shearman and George propose a tax on imputed land incomes using state and local property tax laws.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes the Georgist proposal for a tax on imputed land incomes from general income taxes, countering the objection that LVT is merely a standard property tax.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“conflating land (the natural universe)”**
  - sentence: …The much greater problem, which threw the entire movement into confusion, was conflating land (the natural universe) and bank bills (Federal Reserve notes) with "capital," neither of which would have been remotely acceptable to George.…
  - why: The essay explicitly critiques the conflation of land with capital, arguing that this confusion undermined the Georgist movement and counters the claim that land is just a form of capital.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“single tax” label”**
  - sentence: …1. Start-up (1879-1886) Henry George publishes Progress and Poverty in 1879, it becomes a great success, and it catches the eye of attorney Thomas Shearman, who suggests the “single tax” label.…
  - why: The essay provides historical context for the origin and evolution of the 'single tax' concept, illustrating the narrative surrounding this central Georgist idea.

### [Flooding in Houston: No Real Surprise](https://www.progress.org/articles/flooding-in-houston-no-real-surprise/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“anti-sprawl efficiencies”**
  - sentence: …It might not be an exaggeration to say that in this day and age, the anti-sprawl efficiencies of land value taxation are not just a tool for urban revitalization, but a key to urban survival.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value taxation is a necessary solution to the unsustainable and dangerous sprawl epitomized by Houston.
- **illustrates** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“miles and miles of impermeable pavement”**
  - sentence: …If you aren’t worried about egregious waste of land and resources, and miles and miles of impermeable pavement shunting water off onto lower-lying (and lower-income) neighborhoods, then Houston is doing pretty well.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how land-use decisions (sprawl and paving over absorbent land) create negative externalities that harm lower-income neighborhoods, exemplifying the misallocation and suppression 
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Each new loop has, of course, raised land values”**
  - sentence: …Each new loop has, of course, raised land values further out from the city, and led to new waves of sprawl development.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of public infrastructure (beltways) raising land values and driving development, which is the core mechanism of public investment capitalizing into land.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“recklessly sprawled out, paving over absorbent grassland”**
  - sentence: …Population has grown rapidly, and Houston has recklessly sprawled out, paving over absorbent grassland.…
  - why: The essay supports the Green Georgism narrative by highlighting the environmental damage—specifically the loss of natural water absorption due to paving over land—that results from treating land as a 

### [Florida Going Tax-Backwards](https://www.progress.org/articles/florida-going-tax-backwards/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“tax on structures adds”**
  - sentence: …Buildings and other improvements have a cost of production, so the property tax on structures adds to the cost of having these.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing improvements (capital) is harmful because it adds to production costs, implying that shifting taxes off buildings and onto land would avoid this inefficiency.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“lower tax is offset”**
  - sentence: …For a new buyer, the lower tax is offset by higher mortgage interest.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the economic incidence of land value cuts, explaining that tax reductions on land are capitalized into higher purchase prices rather than benefiting consumers, which is the inver
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“real-estate property tax is really”**
  - sentence: …So the real-estate property tax is really a tax on two quite different types of assets: the land, and the improvements.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the idea that property tax is a single levy, arguing instead that it is a composite tax on two distinct economic goods (land and improvements) with different supply chara
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“exempt the entire value”**
  - sentence: …The more effective remedy is to eliminate grandfathering and exempt the entire value of all the improvements.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a fundamental tax reform that removes the tax burden on buildings entirely, which is the defining feature of a split-rate or land-only tax system known to encourage development

### [Food, Clothing, and Shelter](https://www.progress.org/articles/food-clothing-and-shelter/)
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“Shelter, alas, is not”**
  - sentence: …Shelter, alas, is not.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the falling costs of food and clothing with the rising cost of shelter, supporting the claim that housing is uniquely unaffordable.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“housing costs for the lower third”**
  - sentence: …And it's intensifying: over the last 19 years, housing costs for the lower third of US income earners have increased by over 50% .…
  - why: The essay cites data showing housing costs consuming a growing share of income for the poor, evidencing the claim that rising housing costs drive poverty.
- **supports** [`problems/homelessness-is-housing-cost-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/homelessness-is-housing-cost-problem/) — anchor: **“The lower one's income”**
  - sentence: …The lower one's income, the likelier one is to rent, rather than own.…
  - why: The essay highlights the disproportionate burden of housing costs on low-income renters, supporting the link between housing costs and economic insecurity.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“why does shelter demand more”**
  - sentence: …So why does shelter demand more of what we earn every year?…
  - why: The essay frames the unique failure of the housing market—contrasted with food and clothing—as a central economic mystery, aligning with the narrative that the housing crisis is a distinct land crisis

### [Foundations of Earth Sharing](https://www.progress.org/articles/foundations-of-earth-sharing/)
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“Man did not make the earth”**
  - sentence: …Man did not make the earth, and, though he had a natural right to occupy it, he had no right to locate as his property in perpetuity any part of it; neither did the Creator of the earth open a land-office, from whence the first title-deeds …
  - why: The essay quotes Paine to argue that land is a common gift and private ownership is an artificial construct, supporting the moral narrative that land value increments belong to the community.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“The first accepted tax”**
  - sentence: …Ground rents are, therefore, perhaps a species of revenue which can best bear to have a peculiar tax imposed upon them.…
  - why: The essay cites historical precedent and Adam Smith to argue that land rent is a viable and superior source of government revenue.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“encourage people to invest”**
  - sentence: …But a tax on land...rents would actually address some of the underlying problems... because you encourage people to invest in productive capital rather than into rent generating wealth and the result of that shift in the composition of savi…
  - why: The essay quotes Joseph Stiglitz to argue that taxing land shifts investment from rent-seeking to productive capital, thereby raising economic productivity.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“birth right to the land”**
  - sentence: …Like the shining sun and clean breathable air, we all have a birth right to the land and resources of the Earth.…
  - why: The essay frames access to land and natural resources as a universal birthright, which is the foundational moral argument for distributing rent as a citizen's dividend.

### [Founding Startup Societies](https://www.progress.org/articles/founding-startup-societies/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Land near Startup Societies becomes more expensive”**
  - sentence: …Land near Startup Societies becomes more expensive as governance improves.…
  - why: The essay explicitly notes that governance improvements in these zones cause land values to rise, illustrating the capitalization of public value into land.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“long-time residents inherit the asset value created”**
  - sentence: …With the inclusive approach, localities commit to deep reforms and offer the Venture prime leasehold sites at affordable costs... With the above approaches, long-time residents inherit the asset value created by Startup Society ventures.…
  - why: The essay argues that the value created by the community and governance reforms should be captured and returned to the residents, aligning with the narrative that the community creates land value.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Residents receiving dividends from ground lease income”**
  - sentence: …Cornerstones of this approach include: ... Residents receiving dividends from ground lease income as the full-scale project develops.…
  - why: The essay proposes distributing ground lease income to residents as a mechanism for inclusive growth, evidencing the claim that rent dividends can reduce poverty and inequality.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“turn over Startup Society asset gains, in full, to the community”**
  - sentence: …After an agreed-upon period to reward private funders, the authors recommend that ventures turn over Startup Society asset gains, in full, to the community.…
  - why: The essay's proposal to return asset gains to the host community addresses concerns about neo-colonialism and dispossession, intersecting with land justice principles.

### [Freddie's Big Loss](https://www.progress.org/articles/freddies-big-loss/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“boom-bust cycle in real estate”**
  - sentence: …Everybody should have known that the subprime real estate loans were risky, and that there has been a boom-bust cycle in real estate for the last two hundred years.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that real estate speculation drives a recurring 18-year business cycle, citing historical timing and predicting the 2008 crash.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“portfolio of mortgages is worth”**
  - sentence: …Its portfolio of mortgages is worth about $700 billion.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the dominance of mortgage credit in the financial system through the massive scale of Freddie Mac's holdings and the systemic risk they posed.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Government public works pump up”**
  - sentence: …Government public works pump up land values, and fuel speculative purchases.…
  - why: The essay identifies public investment as a driver of land value increases that fuels speculation, supporting the claim that public goods capitalize into land.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“remedy is to have landowners pay”**
  - sentence: …The remedy is to have landowners pay their way, by tapping land rent or land value for government revenue.…
  - why: The essay argues that capturing land rent removes the subsidy from landownership and calms the speculative cycle, directly supporting the benefit of LVT.

### [Free Banking Explained](https://www.progress.org/articles/free-banking-explained/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land speculators also borrow funds”**
  - sentence: …With the artificial rate of interest below the natural free-market rate, land speculators also borrow funds.…
  - why: The essay argues that artificially low interest rates from central banking induce land speculation, which creates unsustainable real estate bubbles that inevitably burst and cause recessions.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“real estate bubble caused by money expansion”**
  - sentence: …The economy gets a real estate bubble caused by money expansion and land-value subsidy.…
  - why: The essay explicitly attributes the business cycle to money expansion and land speculation rather than treating credit and land as separate causes, countering the objection that credit alone drives cy
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“make neighborhoods more productive”**
  - sentence: …Public goods such as streets, transit, schooling, security, and various subsidies all make neighborhoods more productive and attractive.…
  - why: The essay explains that public goods and services increase land values, effectively redistributing wealth from workers to landowners.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“implicit redistribution of wealth”**
  - sentence: …This is a huge implicit redistribution of wealth from workers and entrepreneurs to landowners.…
  - why: The essay describes how the current system, funded by taxes on labor, transfers wealth to landowners who capture the value generated by public investment.

### [From Capitalism to Rentierism?](https://www.progress.org/articles/from-capitalism-to-rentierism/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“economic performance of the city”**
  - sentence: …Many municipalities made the change to SV rating, and there was not one instance where the economic performance of the city did not improve immediately after the change to SV in comparison with cities that had remained on the NAV system.…
  - why: Provides empirical evidence that shifting to site value (land) rating improves municipal economic outcomes.
- **illustrates** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“using “the poor widow” as the stooge”**
  - sentence: …There is little doubt that the property lobby led this putsch, often using “the poor widow” as the stooge to promote their cause.…
  - why: Illustrates the political tactic of using sympathetic figures (the 'little old lady') to block land taxation.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“larger and larger speculative land booms”**
  - sentence: …So, the green light had been given to larger and larger speculative land booms and busts from which it has become increasingly difficult for nations to extract themselves.…
  - why: Argues that reducing property taxes enabled damaging land speculation cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“capitalism has been permitted to morph”**
  - sentence: …With nary a whimper, capitalism has been permitted to morph into rentierism and is suffering consequences warned by Adam Smith and Henry George.…
  - why: Frames the shift from productive capitalism to rent-seeking as a negative economic transformation.

### [From LVT to MMT and back again](https://www.progress.org/articles/from-lvt-to-mmt-and-back-again/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“tax base which people cannot generally avoid”**
  - sentence: …As Milton Friedman presciently noted in 1999 crypto may create increased opportunity for tax evasion, but as long as governments can find a tax base which people cannot generally avoid (ie -land, his and our favoured base), they can maintai…
  - why: The essay cites Milton Friedman to argue that land is the unique tax base that cannot be avoided, which is the central thesis of this claim page.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“line with the Harrison/Hoyt 18 year model”**
  - sentence: …Whilst the global economy slowed in 2019, notably in line with the Harrison/Hoyt 18 year model projections (not considered by MMT) and some economies entered a technical recession it looked as if this might be the mildest 'mid cycle' in liv…
  - why: The essay explicitly references the Harrison/Hoyt 18-year land cycle model to explain economic timing, supporting the narrative that land speculation drives cycles.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“remedies of Georgism fully achievable within the paradigm”**
  - sentence: …That is to say my understanding of the remedies of Georgism fully achievable within the paradigm where use of free floating fiat money is not only the norm but considered the ideal.…
  - why: The author argues that Georgist remedies are fully achievable within a fiat money paradigm, countering the objection that LVT is useless without other structural reforms like zoning.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“taxes effectively create unemployment in the private sector”**
  - sentence: …Along with chartalism before it, MMT maintains the reason the currency has value is taxes effectively create unemployment in the private sector which the government then directs to the public purpose via spending.…
  - why: The essay discusses the functional necessity of taxes to drive currency demand, which relates to the Georgist narrative of replacing distortionary taxes with a single tax on land.

### [Fuel For Hope](https://www.progress.org/articles/fuel-for-hope/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“key role that land”**
  - sentence: …There is a way-of-thinking that understands the key role that land -- the earth, the opportunities of nature -- plays in economic and political life.…
  - why: The essay introduces the Georgist perspective as a vital framework for understanding economic and political systems.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“supply a remedy”**
  - sentence: …These ideas, when understood and applied, supply a remedy for society's deepest economic problems.…
  - why: The essay asserts that these specific ideas offer a solution to deep societal issues, echoing the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“isn't culturally fashionable”**
  - sentence: …It isn't culturally fashionable, or politically popular; it's not on the tip of very many tongues.…
  - why: The essay notes the obscurity and lack of popularity of these ideas, supporting the narrative of their suppression or marginalization.

### [GDP First Quarter 2008](https://www.progress.org/articles/gdp-first-quarter-2008/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“shifting taxes to land value”**
  - sentence: …These could be offset by shifting taxes to land value, since land does not run away when taxed.…
  - why: The essay argues that stimulating the economy requires cutting taxes on labor and investment, which can be offset by shifting the tax burden to land value.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“Residential construction plunged”**
  - sentence: …This includes a 9.7 percent fall in “fixed investment,” i.e. real estate construction. Residential construction plunged by 26.7 percent.…
  - why: The essay documents the collapse in residential construction and unsold inventories in early 2008, illustrating the bust phase of the land cycle.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“federal government can keep GDP”**
  - sentence: …The federal government can keep GDP from falling by massively borrowing from abroad and spending the money for war.…
  - why: The essay counters the idea that government intervention prevents economic downturns, arguing that military spending merely delays the inevitable recession.

### [Geo-Capitalism](https://www.progress.org/articles/geo-capitalism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Henry George’s 1890 Platform”**
  - sentence: …A “Geo-capitalist” would support Henry George’s 1890 Platform , adjusted for modern interpretation, and also would agree with the 1893 tax plan of George’s lawyer, Thomas G. Shearman, who proposed a special income tax that targeted only une…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for Henry George's platform and the single tax principle on unearned income.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“tax any unearned gains”**
  - sentence: …LAND is capable of being owned “fee simple” by individuals, the individual has the right to exclusively possess the land, is able to sell, develop, and even bequeath land after death, but the state at all times retains right to tax any unea…
  - why: The essay argues that the state should retain the right to tax unearned gains derived from land, aligning with the narrative that land value increments belong to the public.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“should not be compromised by a tax”**
  - sentence: …In short, the wages of natural persons (unlike the unearned income, dividends, interest, etc. generated by corporations) are legally capable of being one’s personal property, and such a property right should not be compromised by a tax on s…
  - why: The essay argues for exempting wages from taxation while taxing unearned income, directly supporting the shift of tax burden from labor to land/privilege.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“tax “as near as possible” all unearned income”**
  - sentence: …Those who study Henry George know that he referred to himself as an “unlimited single taxer,” and reluctantly accepted his lawyer Thomas Shearman’s identification as a “limited single taxer.”…
  - why: The essay references the debate over whether land rent should fund all government needs (unlimited) or just necessary expenses (limited), bearing on the claim's revenue potential.

### [Geo-Libertarianism Gets Criticized](https://www.progress.org/articles/geo-libertarianism-gets-criticized/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“mixing labor and capital”**
  - sentence: …Cuneo then makes the common error in mixing labor and capital goods into land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the conflation of land with labor and capital goods, a core tenet of the objection.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“create a class of people”**
  - sentence: …He then argues that geo-libertarianism has the potential to create a class of people who become “Land Value Tax assessors.”…
  - why: The essay addresses the fear of a corrupt assessor class by proposing appeals to juries and democratic accountability.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“what happens if a title holder”**
  - sentence: …Cuneo asks a good question, what happens if a title holder cannot pay.…
  - why: The essay outlines specific remedies (moving, deferral, loss of services) for owners unable to pay the tax.
- **counters** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“absolute ownership of land”**
  - sentence: …An allodial libertarian, Mike Cuneo, recently wrote an article opposing geo-libertarianism.…
  - why: The essay critiques the allodial view of absolute land ownership derived from conquest or first claim, contrasting it with the equal-benefit view.

### [Geoism As Part of The Left-Libertarian Tradition](https://www.progress.org/articles/geoism-as-part-of-the-left-libertarian-tradition/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“unite the truth perceived”**
  - sentence: …What I have done in this book, if I have correctly solved the great problem I have sought to investigate, is, to unite the truth perceived by the school of Smith and Ricardo to the truth perceived by the school of Proudhon and Lasalle; to s…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames George's project as synthesizing classical liberalism and socialism to achieve egalitarian ends via the market.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“opens the way to a realization”**
  - sentence: …What I have done in this book, if I have correctly solved the great problem I have sought to investigate, is, to unite the truth perceived by the school of Smith and Ricardo to the truth perceived by the school of Proudhon and Lasalle; to s…
  - why: The essay argues that George's 'single tax' approach uses free markets to achieve socialist goals, distinguishing it from state socialism.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“privileges that benefit the wealthy”**
  - sentence: …According to these left-libertarians, existing social problems such as systematic inequalities, social domination, and wage slavery should not be attributed to free market interactions, but rather to economic privileges that benefit the wea…
  - why: The essay supports the claim that systemic inequality stems from rent-seeking privileges (like land monopoly) rather than productive market activity.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“forcefully take it from its original producers”**
  - sentence: …One can either make valuable goods and services in free and voluntary cooperation with others or one can forcefully take it from its original producers.…
  - why: The essay grounds the justice narrative in a class analysis where wealth accumulation via land ownership is defined as 'taking' rather than 'making'.

### [Georgism and Globalization](https://www.progress.org/articles/georgism-and-globalization/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“eradicating taxes on goods”**
  - sentence: …George used the word “true free trade,” which focused to exclude of domestic barriers to trade by eradicating taxes on goods and services other than the land tax.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for removing taxes on goods and services in favor of a land tax, which is the core definition of this narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“replacing taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …Developing economies could reduce pollution and improve their economies by replacing taxes on wages and goods with a tax on pollution, in addition to land value.…
  - why: The essay argues for taxing pollution (a form of natural resource use) and land value instead of labor, directly supporting the Green Georgism narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“denser population generates increasing returns”**
  - sentence: …A denser population generates increasing returns due to a finer division of labor, with greater specialization.…
  - why: The essay cites Henry George's argument that population density increases productivity through specialization, a key mechanism for why land taxes (which encourage density) raise productivity.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“comparative advantage in oil”**
  - sentence: …As a result, for example, Kuwait, Iran, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela, having a comparative advantage in oil and gas or other natural resources for production, compete well with U.S. chemical production firms.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the economic efficiency of countries specializing in natural resource extraction, which underpins the Georgist argument for capturing resource rents.

### [Georgism in Interwar Europe](https://www.progress.org/articles/georgism-in-interwar-europe/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“greatest real success of Georgism”**
  - sentence: …By no means the Georgist ideal (something which would continue to elude Danish Georgists, constituted in the Justice Party , to this day), it still unfortunately represents perhaps the greatest real success of Georgism in Europe.…
  - why: The essay details the failure of Georgist policies to take root or survive in interwar Europe despite political opportunities, illustrating the objection's premise.
- **supports** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“reliance of the elected government”**
  - sentence: …The power of landowners to influence the course of democratic politics was even clearer in Germany, where the old Prussian nobility (the Junkers) held incredible sway.…
  - why: The text describes how the political power of landowners (Junkers) successfully blocked land taxation in Germany, supporting the claim that landowners block LVT.
- **supports** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“New Liberals – here represented”**
  - sentence: …In Great Britain – after causing a tremendous parliamentary battle and a constitutional crisis that stripped the unelected House of Lords of its ability to block legislation indefinitely – a national survey of land values, ordered to preced…
  - why: The essay recounts the significant political impact and legislative battles of Georgists in the UK and Europe during this era, supporting the narrative.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“bail out the bankrupt estates”**
  - sentence: …The culmination of this was the Osthilfe (“Eastern Aid”), a policy enacted to bail out the bankrupt estates of the Junkers in the aftermath of the Great Depression.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the political resistance to land value capture by showing how governments instead subsidized landowners to protect their asset values during crises.
