### [Georgism through Land Leasing](https://www.progress.org/articles/georgism-through-land-leasing/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“revenue it could generate post-development”**
  - sentence: …Because the BPCA lacked direct access to tax revenue — public-benefit corporations don’t get taxing authority — it structured its bonds around the revenue it could generate post-development; revenue generated, in no small part, by long-term…
  - why: Shows how public investment (land reclamation) creates value captured via lease agreements.
- **supports** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“electorate of owner-occupier landowners”**
  - sentence: …In the U.S., we’ve created an electorate of owner-occupier landowners.…
  - why: Explains the political durability of leasing over taxation due to homevoter resistance.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“appropriation of land value for the public benefit”**
  - sentence: …Georgism isn’t about land value taxation, per se. It’s about the appropriation of land value for the public benefit.…
  - why: Frames the essay's core thesis that public capture of value is the defining Georgist principle.
- **illustrates** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“remitted back to the City of New York”**
  - sentence: …The other half it remitted back to the City of New York.…
  - why: Demonstrates that captured land rent can fund substantial public revenue.

### [Georgism... In Space!](https://www.progress.org/articles/georgism-in-space/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Georgist framework would suggest”**
  - sentence: …In this case, the Georgist framework would suggest a severance tax , where individuals who extract energy from natural sources must pay a fee that goes into the citizens dividend.…
  - why: The essay applies the Single Tax principle to new domains like energy and matter, illustrating the framework's extensibility.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“rights to different bands”**
  - sentence: …Today, the rights to different bands of the spectrum are auctioned off in exchange for exclusive rights to the frequency band.…
  - why: The essay cites spectrum auctions as a successful example of capturing economic rent for public benefit.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“goes into the citizens dividend”**
  - sentence: …In this case, the Georgist framework would suggest a severance tax , where individuals who extract energy from natural sources must pay a fee that goes into the citizens dividend.…
  - why: The essay argues that capturing space resource rents should fund a dividend to share the gains of colonization.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“incentives to search for”**
  - sentence: …It’s important not to set taxes too high to ensure that there are incentives to search for new sources of energy, matter and land.…
  - why: The essay argues that while taxes must be balanced to avoid slowing growth, they do not require prior zoning reform to function.

### [Georgist Globalization](https://www.progress.org/articles/georgist-globalization/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“single tax system”**
  - sentence: …The 19th-century economist Henry George analyzed and promoted the “single tax system” of using land rent or land value for public revenue.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the Georgist proposal to replace other taxes with a land value tax.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“abolition of all taxes other than on land”**
  - sentence: …The contribution of Georgism is to advance the case for free trade to the "true free trade" of the abolition of all taxes other than on land, so that as productivity increases land rent, the benefits are shared by the whole population.…
  - why: The essay argues for shifting the tax burden off labor and goods onto land to ensure shared prosperity.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“green tax shift”**
  - sentence: …The developing countries would also benefit with a "green tax shift," a replacement of their taxes on income and sales with levies on pollution.…
  - why: The essay advocates for taxing environmental externalities (pollution) while reducing distortionary taxes on income and sales.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“argued against the Malthusian doctrine”**
  - sentence: …Henry George argued against the Malthusian doctrine that food supply would not keep up with population.…
  - why: The essay cites Henry George's rejection of Malthusian limits to support a pro-immigration stance.

### [German State to Recover Society's Land Value](https://www.progress.org/articles/german-state-to-recover-societys-land-value/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“tax that actually does”**
  - sentence: …Amazingly, there is one tax that actually does clean up politics, economies, and the environment.…
  - why: The essay introduces the land value tax as a uniquely beneficial levy that improves governance and efficiency, echoing the narrative of a tax that cannot be avoided and fixes systemic issues.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“tax is actually very progressive”**
  - sentence: …The tax is actually very progressive, since the rich own the most valuable sites both residential and downtown commercial, which in cities popular with the rich are hundreds of times more pricey than the land beneath suburban homes.…
  - why: The text explicitly argues that the tax burden falls disproportionately on the wealthy due to their ownership of high-value land, countering the objection that it hurts the middle class.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“disburse the monies to residents”**
  - sentence: …Then, not only will residents not mind paying, but they will also welcome rapidly rising location values, since the higher they go, the bigger the share for residents.…
  - why: The essay proposes distributing land value revenue as a citizen dividend to ensure political durability and public support, mirroring the Alaska Permanent Fund model.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“abolish the other, counterproductive taxes”**
  - sentence: …If Germany were to raise that rate, it could abolish the other, counterproductive taxes.…
  - why: The author argues that shifting the tax burden onto land values allows for the elimination of harmful taxes on wages and production, supporting the efficiency claim.

### [Germany Fiscally Invades Liechtenstein](https://www.progress.org/articles/germany-fiscally-invades-liechtenstein/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxation of labor”**
  - sentence: …The taxation of labor, including the products of labor, transactions of labor, and investments of wages, is a violation of liberty and natural rights.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing wages violates natural rights and that land value is the only just source of public revenue.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Plenty of tax revenue”**
  - sentence: …Plenty of tax revenue is available from land rent or land value, which does not take from labor, as human action did not create land.…
  - why: The essay asserts that land rent is a sufficient source of revenue to replace taxes on labor and capital.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pump up land rent”**
  - sentence: …If land rent is not tapped for public revenue, then landowners get subsidized, as public works and civic services pump up land rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay explains that public investment increases land values, creating a subsidy for landowners if that rent is not captured.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“reduces incomes and economic growth”**
  - sentence: …The taxation of wages, interest, dividends, business profits, and trade reduces incomes and economic growth and violates freedom.…
  - why: The essay claims that taxing labor and capital harms the economy, implying that shifting the burden to land would avoid this damage.

### [Give me your tired, your poor...](https://www.progress.org/articles/give-me-your-tired-your-poor/)
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“Why is the opportunity to make a living”**
  - sentence: …Why is the opportunity to make a living so scarce that we horde it, turning away newcomers who only want a chance to work?…
  - why: The essay frames the scarcity of economic opportunity and the resulting hostility toward immigrants as a consequence of artificial scarcity, which aligns with the claim that high land rents suppress p
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“Give me your tired, your poor”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …In 'The New Colossus,' Emma drew a vivid contrast -- between a monument to power and conquest, and America's emblem of radical welcome:…
  - why: The essay invokes the moral narrative of radical welcome and shared opportunity associated with the Statue of Liberty, contrasting it with modern exclusionism to illustrate the ethical dimension of la
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“We have plenty of room”**
  - sentence: …We have plenty of room.…
  - why: The essay cites density statistics to argue that physical space is abundant, countering the scarcity narrative used to justify exclusion and supporting the view that the crisis is one of artificial la

### [Gold, Interest, and Land](https://www.progress.org/articles/gold-interest-and-land/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“unsustainable land boom”**
  - sentence: …An unsustainable land boom financed by artificial credit has to collapse, and the financial crash then further brings down the economy.…
  - why: The essay argues that the business cycle is driven by credit-fueled land speculation, which is the core claim of this page.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“land values are grossly inflated”**
  - sentence: …In today’s economy, land values are grossly inflated by subsidies, mostly implicit.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the objection that credit alone drives cycles, arguing instead that credit acts primarily to inflate land values.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“central bankers will often create instability”**
  - sentence: …Since the correct money supply is unknowable and can only be determined by a pure free market, the central bankers will often create instability in their attempt to either stimulate or “cool off” an economy.…
  - why: The essay rejects the idea that government intervention can prevent cycles, arguing that central planning of money causes instability.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Why are they blind to the interventions?”**
  - sentence: …Why are they blind to the interventions?…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative that economists and commentators are blind to the true causes of crises due to a 'cult of statism' and educational control.

### [Government Debt — Who Will Pay?](https://www.progress.org/articles/government-debt-who-will-pay/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“destruction of the tax base”**
  - sentence: …Excessive taxation becomes self-defeating, as the deadweight loss exceeds the tax revenue.…
  - why: The essay argues that high taxes on income destroy the tax base via deadweight loss, contrasting this with land value taxation which avoids this problem.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“recognize a bubble”**
  - sentence: …Those who say that we cannot recognize a bubble that we are in are wrong.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the Greenspan doctrine that bubbles cannot be identified in advance.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“bail out failed real estate loans”**
  - sentence: …But the voiding of the debt is a last resort, likely only during a financial crisis in which government is all tapped out and can no longer borrow to bail out failed real estate loans.…
  - why: The essay predicts a financial crisis triggered by government debt that will ultimately require intervention in real estate loans, illustrating the link between land speculation and systemic busts.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“probability of the US federal government”**
  - sentence: …This destruction of the tax base does not occur if land value is taxed, but the probability of the US federal government shifting towards a tax on land rent or land value is microscopically close to zero.…
  - why: The essay acknowledges the economic superiority of land value taxation but notes its near-zero probability of political adoption.

### [Government Financing of Political Campaigns](https://www.progress.org/articles/government-financing-of-political-campaigns/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Government financing of elections creates”**
  - sentence: …Government financing of elections creates a self-perpetuating elite who use tax money to get elected and then impose taxes and restrictions on the public.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice failure where officials use public funds to entrench themselves, validating the objection that government failure won't stop at the assessor's door.
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“greatest special interest of all”**
  - sentence: …This money is not free of special interests, as it comes from the greatest special interest of all, the ruling powers.…
  - why: The essay argues that concentrating rent revenue in government hands empowers the ruling elite, supporting the objection that public rent collection breeds corruption.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax on corporate profits acts”**
  - sentence: …But a tax on corporate profits acts like an addition to the costs of production, and some of the tax is passed on to those who buy the products.…
  - why: By highlighting the inefficiency and incidence of corporate taxes, the essay implicitly supports the narrative of shifting taxes onto inelastic bases like land.

### [Government Regulation of Sneezing](https://www.progress.org/articles/government-regulation-of-sneezing/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“The legislation passed by Congress”**
  - sentence: …The legislation passed by Congress prohibits any consecutive sneezing (also referred to as "sternutation") with more than three hapchoos.…
  - why: satirizes government overreach and the failure to consider unintended consequences
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Employers can increase productivity”**
  - sentence: …Employers can increase productivity by stifling employee sneezing.…
  - why: ridicules the simplistic argument that regulating behavior automatically boosts economic output
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“The war on sneezing will make”**
  - sentence: …The war on sneezing will make America more competitive internationally, said the Chief Doctor.…
  - why: mocks the conflation of social control with economic competitiveness

### [Government’s War on All-Ages Clubs](https://www.progress.org/articles/governments-war-on-all-ages-clubs/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“beneficiaries of complex rules”**
  - sentence: …This is not an accident, since besides the bureaucrats, the beneficiaries of complex rules and tyrannical enforcement are lawyers.…
  - why: The essay describes how the legal lobby creates complex regulations to force demand for their services, exemplifying rent-seeking that harms the economy.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“bureaucrats they pay for become”**
  - sentence: …So Californians will have to sit there watching helplessly as the bureaucrats they pay for become the enemy of the people rather than agents hired to serve the people.…
  - why: The essay depicts state bureaucrats acting in their own self-interest to protect wages and punish the public, illustrating the public-choice critique of government failure.

### [Green Tax Cuts](https://www.progress.org/articles/green-tax-cuts/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“levy a charge directly on the pollution”**
  - sentence: …The more effective way to reduce emissions is to levy a charge directly on the pollution.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing pollution directly is more effective than tax cuts on green goods.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“replace taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …A green tax shift would replace taxes on wages, goods, and enterprise profits with pollution levies, and for a complete shift, also tap land values.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting taxes off wages and onto pollution and land to reduce economic waste.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“tap land values”**
  - sentence: …A green tax shift would replace taxes on wages, goods, and enterprise profits with pollution levies, and for a complete shift, also tap land values.…
  - why: The essay explicitly includes capturing land values as part of a comprehensive green tax shift.

### [Groundly's Ground-Rent Grift](https://www.progress.org/articles/groundlys-ground-rent-grift/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“A landowner appropriates value”**
  - sentence: …A landowner appropriates value that wasn’t created by them.…
  - why: The essay uses Groundly's business model to exemplify the extraction of unearned income from land.
- **supports** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“desperately needing to buy a home”**
  - sentence: …As great as this may sound to someone desperately needing to buy a home, it might be yet another short-term affordable long-term unaffordable setup.…
  - why: The essay critiques a financial product targeting those priced out of traditional ownership, highlighting the structural barriers to land access.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“We will rent you the land”**
  - sentence: …We currently own the land and the house, so we will sell you just-the-house.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates how separating land value from housing value creates financial complexity and affordability issues.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“take advantage of people”**
  - sentence: …With its “innovative” startup branding, the company is all set to take advantage of people unaware of the downsides.…
  - why: The essay describes a firm extracting rent without providing corresponding value, illustrating the concept of rent-seeking.

### [Henry George and Sun Yat-sen: A Global Legacy of Land Reform](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-and-sun-yat-sen-a-global-legacy-of-land-reform/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“Land value is created socially”**
  - sentence: …Land value is created socially, thus society (as the source of the spillover captured by landlords) has the right to harvest it.…
  - why: Sun Yat-sen explicitly argues that land value derives from the surrounding community, justifying social collection.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“The teachings of your single-taxer”**
  - sentence: …The teachings of your single-taxer, Henry George, will be the basis of our program of reform.…
  - why: Sun Yat-sen's declaration to American journalists illustrates the global adoption and political ambition of the Single Tax narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“This policy would lead to industrialization”**
  - sentence: …This policy would lead to industrialization as the government coordinated railroad corridors and centers of industry.…
  - why: Sun argues that land value taxation itself drives industrialization and development, countering the view that it is useless without prior zoning reform.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“he will strike a mean”**
  - sentence: …he will strike a mean and report the true market price to the government.…
  - why: Sun proposes a self-assessment mechanism where owners set prices to avoid buyouts, countering the objection that land value cannot be accurately assessed.

### [Henry George and the Communist Manifesto](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-henry-george-might-have-corrected-the-marxian-communist-manifesto/)
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“Abolition of property in land”**
  - sentence: …Plank #1: Abolition of property in land and application of all rents of land to public purposes.…
  - why: The essay contrasts Marx's state ownership with George's preference for private possession and public taxation.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Marx’s abolishment of private property”**
  - sentence: …In a nutshell, although there was some agreement between Marx and George, Marx’s abolishment of private property was too extreme for George, who essentially accused Marx of throwing out the baby with the bath water.…
  - why: The essay explicitly addresses the Marxist critique by rejecting Marx's total abolition of private property.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“taxation powers over the unearned gains”**
  - sentence: …The state was to act as trustee for the people, and would only have taxation powers over the unearned gains derived from land, gains that we today call “capital gains” or “net rental income.”…
  - why: The essay illustrates the Single Tax mechanism of taxing unearned land gains rather than abolishing ownership.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Marx’s complete government takeover”**
  - sentence: …But again, I think Marx’s complete government takeover of private industry was generally too extreme for George.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes Georgism from socialism by rejecting the complete government takeover of industry.

### [Henry George and the Single Tax Documentary and Interview](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-and-the-single-tax-documentary-and-interview/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“The Single Tax attempted in theory”**
  - sentence: …But, I think The Single Tax attempted in theory to do the same by providing support for the poorest people in a society, and do so by keeping capitalism and industry well intact.…
  - why: The interviewee describes the Single Tax's theoretical goal of supporting the poor while preserving capitalism.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“socio-political events influenced by the movement”**
  - sentence: …The Arden, Delaware, community is highlighted, as are some of the relevant socio-political events influenced by the movement leading to the Progressive Era.…
  - why: The essay explicitly references the movement's influence on events leading to the Progressive Era.
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“assessing land value”**
  - sentence: …However, I understand one of the most difficult aspects of The Single Tax would have been assessing land value.…
  - why: The filmmaker identifies assessment as a historical difficulty for the Single Tax, acknowledging the practical challenge.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Georgist UBI called a Citizen's Dividend”**
  - sentence: …Are you familiar with the concept of a Georgist UBI called a Citizen's Dividend?…
  - why: The interview explicitly connects Georgist policy to the concept of a Citizen's Dividend.

### [Henry George Google Doodle Tribute](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-google-doodle-tribute/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“use of the land value tax”**
  - sentence: …His most famous work, Progress and Poverty (1879), which sold millions of copies worldwide and probably more than any other American book before that time, is a treatise on inequality, the cyclic nature of industrialized economies, and the …
  - why: The essay identifies George's core proposal as the land value tax remedy for inequality and cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Citizen’s Dividend”**
  - sentence: …Georgists also advocate for a form of Universal Basic Income, called a Citizen’s Dividend, to be established with the revenue raised by the state through leasing or taxing land and natural resources for private use, similar to the Alaska Pe…
  - why: The essay explicitly connects Georgism to the Citizen's Dividend funded by land rents.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“cyclic nature of industrialized economies”**
  - sentence: …His most famous work, Progress and Poverty (1879), which sold millions of copies worldwide and probably more than any other American book before that time, is a treatise on inequality, the cyclic nature of industrialized economies, and the …
  - why: The essay cites George's analysis of the boom-bust cycle as a central theme of his work.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal right to the use of land”**
  - sentence: …In opposition to land monopoly as well as social injustice, he stated that people have equal right to the use of land as they have equal right to breathe the air.…
  - why: The essay quotes George's moral argument that land value is a common right.

### [Henry George in Favor of a Basic Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-in-favor-of-a-basic-income/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“share and share alike”**
  - sentence: …We could divide this, if we wanted to, among the whole community, share and share alike.…
  - why: George explicitly advocates distributing the surplus land rent equally among citizens, which is the core definition of a citizen's dividend.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“yield revenues even larger”**
  - sentence: …The taking for the use of the community of that value of privilege which attaches to the possession of land, would, wherever social development has advanced beyond a certain stage, yield revenues even larger than those now raised by taxatio…
  - why: George argues that capturing land rent would generate sufficient revenue to exceed current government needs, creating a surplus.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“secure from want”**
  - sentence: …And out of the fund which would thus accrue from the common property, we might, without degradation to anybody, provide enough to actually secure from want all who were deprived of their natural protectors or met with accident, or any man w…
  - why: George proposes using the land rent fund to provide a basic income that would prevent poverty and want among the vulnerable.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“value created by the community”**
  - sentence: …To take land values for public purposes is not really to impose a tax, but to take for public purposes a value created by the community.…
  - why: George justifies the dividend by asserting that land value is socially created, not earned by the individual owner.

### [Henry George Knew How to End Economic Inequality](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-knew-how-to-end-economic-inequality/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“advocating a "single tax"”**
  - sentence: …George thus became known as advocating a "single tax" on land.…
  - why: The essay recounts George's proposal to replace all other taxes with a tax on land value.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“value contributed by natural resource scarcity”**
  - sentence: …Rather than confiscating land from current owners, George proposed instead a high enough property tax on land — but not on improvements — to capture all of the value contributed to production by natural resource scarcities.…
  - why: The essay argues that value derived from nature (land) is distinct from labor and should be captured by the public.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“distributed equally — substantially reducing economic inequality”**
  - sentence: …Trust fund money would be distributed equally — substantially reducing economic inequality — to all individual members of the public as an Alaska-like social dividend, rather than flowing to people collectively as services.…
  - why: The author proposes distributing captured land rents as a citizen's dividend to reduce inequality.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“finance all government operations”**
  - sentence: …Since he felt that the captured "rents" could finance all government operations, he advocated abolishing all other taxes.…
  - why: The essay notes George's belief that land rent is sufficient to fund all government functions.

### [Henry George, Marx, and the 16th Amendment](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-marx-and-the-16th-amendment/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Marx's actual words from the letter”**
  - sentence: …Marx's actual words from the letter (where he criticizes Henry George, or, more accurately, criticizes his own misunderstanding of Henry George) were: "All these 'socialists' since Colins have this much in common that they leave wage labor …
  - why: The essay quotes Marx's critique directly and contextualizes it as a misunderstanding of George's position on private ownership.
- **supports** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“Henry George national period”**
  - sentence: …Thanks to the Henry George national period, which began in September 1890 and ended with the decision in Pollock v. Farmers' Loan (1895), we got the power to tax unearned income derived from land under the 16th Amendment (1913).…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative of Georgism's influence in the Progressive Era by linking the 16th Amendment to the Georgist political period.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“privatization of 'factory rents'”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …I would also like to add that the current form of Georgism, which is complicit in the privatization of 'factory rents' (corporate net income and dividends), has given rise to such a virulent form of capitalism, it may very well destroy the …
  - why: The essay argues that failing to capture corporate rents (factory rents) exacerbates economic problems, aligning with the claim that corporate profits increasingly reflect economic rents.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“believed in a kind of qualified private ownership”**
  - sentence: …George definitely objected to private ownership to the extent that the landlord or bank would be allowed to keep the unearned income derived from land, but otherwise firmly believed in the right to exclusively possess land, or believed in a…
  - why: The essay counters the conflation of land with capital by distinguishing George's support for exclusive possession from his objection to the private appropriation of unearned income.

### [Henry George: The Prophet of San Francisco (Part 1)](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-the-prophet-of-san-francisco-part-1/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“booms and busts”**
  - sentence: …George’s insight that day articulated one of the root causes not only of economic inequality, but of a great number of social ailments that still plague society today, from booms and busts to widespread unemployment, environmental destructi…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies land speculation as the root cause of boom-and-bust cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“the single tax”**
  - sentence: …They even used the term l’impot unique—the single tax— which George’s American disciples arrived at independently, and which George accepted.…
  - why: The essay traces the historical origins and acceptance of the Single Tax concept central to Georgism.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“theft of land”**
  - sentence: …He was not the only economist who realized that the root cause of economic injustice was to be found in the theft of land: others before him had independently arrived at similar conclusions...…
  - why: The essay frames the appropriation of land value as a form of theft or injustice, supporting the moral narrative of the unearned increment.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“root cause of economic injustice”**
  - sentence: …He was not the only economist who realized that the root cause of economic injustice was to be found in the theft of land: others before him had independently arrived at similar conclusions...…
  - why: The essay lists historical economists (Physiocrats, Paine, Spence) who anticipated George, illustrating the lineage of thought that Gaffney argues was later obscured or corrupted.

### [Henry George: The Prophet of San Francisco (Part 2)](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-george-the-prophet-of-san-francisco-part-2/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“renown diminished soon after his death”**
  - sentence: …And yet, his renown diminished soon after his death; today, Henry George — once known as “the prophet of San Francisco” — is no longer well known to the general conscience of the public, despite the significance of his discoveries.…
  - why: provides historical context for the decline of Georgism's political prominence
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“devote the rest of his life to sharing”**
  - sentence: …His book Progress and Poverty had by then sold into the millions, and he was traveling around the world, deftly expounding upon his visionary insights with the fierce passion of a man touched by a transcendent realization that he would devo…
  - why: depicts the historical spread and passion of the Single Tax movement
- **illustrates** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“considerable powers arrayed against him”**
  - sentence: …Because of his fierce opposition to the land monopoly, he had considerable powers arrayed against him, and because he did not pander to the short-term interests of the masses either, he appeased neither political side.…
  - why: demonstrates the entrenched political opposition that blocked land reform
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“leaving him only the uncertain and momentary prestige”**
  - sentence: …After this defeat, he and his writings became discredited in the eyes of many: with this, according to Nock, vanished “whatever credit he may have had in America as an economist and philosopher, leaving him only the uncertain and momentary …
  - why: exemplifies the marginalization and discrediting of Georgist thought by the establishment

### [Henry Plottre and The Bridge of Supreme Choice](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-plottre-and-the-bridge-of-supreme-choice/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“Bridge of Private Enterprise”**
  - sentence: …One had the sign, "The Bridge of Private Enterprise" and the other had the sign, "The Bridge of Government."…
  - why: The parable distinguishes a privately funded, insured toll bridge from a deficient public bridge to illustrate the difference between user fees and general public services.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“The government budget is limited”**
  - sentence: …"The government budget is limited.…
  - why: The story depicts government infrastructure as structurally deficient due to budget constraints, illustrating the public-choice argument that government provision often fails due to misallocation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Bridge of Private Enterprise”**
  - sentence: …Runpal noticed there was a sign on the Bridge of Private Enterprise.…
  - why: The narrative shows private enterprise maintaining high safety standards through insurance and inspection without zoning, countering the idea that regulation is required for quality.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“The Land Wizard is a socialist”**
  - sentence: …"But I think you will be wasting your time.…
  - why: The essay juxtaposes the Master of Private Enterprise (Mises) with the Land Wizard, suggesting that land value capture is compatible with or distinct from socialism.

### [Henry Plottre and the Democratic Candidates' Search for Magic](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-plottre-and-the-democratic-candidates-search-for-magic/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“most wars are conflicts over land”**
  - sentence: …He also explained to me that most wars are conflicts over land.…
  - why: The essay uses the 'land wizard's' statement to illustrate the Georgist narrative that land disputes are the root cause of conflict and war.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“To function at its best and promote social peace”**
  - sentence: …To function at its best and promote social peace, the invisible hand has to be well grounded.…
  - why: The essay supports the claim that grounding the economy in land economics (implying land value capture) is necessary for social peace and optimal market function.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Markets are good, but only if guided and corrected”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Markets are good, but only if guided and corrected by the strong visible hand of government.…
  - why: The candidates' insistence on a 'visible hand' illustrates the public-choice objection that political actors reject spontaneous market order in favor of government intervention.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“We don’t believe in the invisible hand”**
  - sentence: …We don’t believe in the invisible hand,” said Omama.…
  - why: The candidates' rejection of the 'invisible hand' illustrates the narrative that modern political and economic thought has abandoned the classical insights necessary to understand land value.

### [Henry Plottre and the Magically Expanding Land](https://www.progress.org/articles/henry-plottre-and-the-magically-expanding-land/)
- **illustrates** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“those holding land idle”**
  - sentence: …When you collect the land rent like a bear collects honey, then those holding land idle will put it to good use so that it is worth paying the rent.…
  - why: The wizard's explanation that taxing land forces idle holders to use it illustrates the mechanism by which LVT dampens speculation.
- **illustrates** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“put it to good use”**
  - sentence: …When you collect the land rent like a bear collects honey, then those holding land idle will put it to good use so that it is worth paying the rent.…
  - why: The story equates putting idle land to good use with 'growing more land,' illustrating the Georgist claim that taxing land raises effective supply and productivity.
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“Land is fixed in supply”**
  - sentence: …“Land is fixed in supply,” said Ricardo.…
  - why: The essay sets up Ricardo's statement as a challenge the wizard ultimately overcomes, countering the objection that land's fixed supply limits economic solutions.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Tax the land”**
  - sentence: …“Tax the land,” said the wizard.…
  - why: The wizard's final prescription to 'Tax the land' as the secret to saving the world supports the narrative of shifting burdens to land.

### [Highlights from the 2018 CGO Conference](https://www.progress.org/articles/highlights-from-the-2018-cgo-conference/)
- **supports** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“Baltimore Then and Now”**

  - sentence: …Ethics, Morality and the Land Question by Charles Avila…

- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Politics of Reason”**
  - sentence: …Politics of Reason by Dan Sullivan…

### [History is Just Another Word for Land Speculation](https://www.progress.org/articles/history-is-just-another-word-for-land-speculation/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Corruption of Economics”**
  - sentence: …Instead, for more on the link between speculators and universities, see Dr. Mason Gaffney’s Corruption of Economics.…
  - why: The essay explicitly references Gaffney's thesis that land speculators funded universities to corrupt economic thought.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“Speculators Are Us”**
  - sentence: …Speculators Are Us. This is a brief, partial History of the United States from the POV of those who benefited the most from it.…
  - why: The essay provides historical examples of how US history was driven by land speculation and the dispossession of settlers.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifted their property tax”**
  - sentence: …When Johannesburg South Africa was dying after the nearby mines played out, the city fathers shifted their property tax to fall only on land, not on buildings.…
  - why: The essay cites Johannesburg's shift to land-value taxation as the cause of its development and growth.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“counterproductive power of land speculation”**
  - sentence: …Today, Cheyenne remains a town while Denver is a major American city. Such is the counterproductive power of land speculation.…
  - why: The essay argues that land speculation hindered the growth of cities like Portland and Cheyenne compared to their rivals.

### [Hobbes and Kaldor on Taxing Expenditure](https://www.progress.org/articles/hobbes-and-kaldor-on-taxing-expenditure/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax on consumption”**
  - sentence: …If one insists on taxing consumption, then the best source is consumption which comes from unearned sources, and consumption that does not deplete its source.…
  - why: The essay argues that while consumption taxes are generally preferable to income taxes, the optimal target is specifically the consumption of land services (unearned income) rather than labor-derived 
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“tax on spending”**
  - sentence: …Thomas Hobbes wrote, in chapter 30, that a tax on spending is more equitable than a tax on income.…
  - why: The essay validates the Georgist preference for taxing unearned increments (land) over earned income (labor) by analyzing the economic distortions of income taxes versus the efficiency of taxing land 
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“public revenues would be more than sufficient”**
  - sentence: …As this would best be an annual (or monthly) tapping of land value, and since the public revenues would be more than sufficient for the public goods desired by the people (per empirical studies as well as the Henry George theorem of public …
  - why: The essay explicitly references the Henry George theorem and empirical studies to argue that capturing land rent can fully fund desired public goods.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“annual tax on capital wealth”**
  - sentence: …Nicholas Kaldor (p. 33) also argued in favor of “an annual tax on capital wealth.”…
  - why: The essay clarifies that Kaldor's proposal for a tax on capital wealth is best understood as a tax on land value, distinguishing it from general property or capital taxes.

### [How Appraisers Value Land](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-appraisers-value-land/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“everything is separated out”**
  - sentence: …Ultimately, when you break down what we do in a tax office, everything is separated out between the improvements and the land.…
  - why: The essay details the standard administrative procedure (cost approach) used to separate land value from improvement value, directly countering the objection that land cannot be accurately assessed.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“everything is separated out”**
  - sentence: …Ultimately, when you break down what we do in a tax office, everything is separated out between the improvements and the land.…
  - why: By explaining that tax offices explicitly distinguish between land value and improvement value, the essay illustrates that LVT is fundamentally different from a standard property tax.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“cost schedules are frozen”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …So in North Carolina, they’ve decided as a matter of law, that they go get the best evidence they can, gather all this construction information, then freeze it so they’re limiting the assessor’s degrees of freedom?…
  - why: The assessor describes how cost schedules are publicly set and frozen by law to limit discretion, addressing the concern that assessors have arbitrary power to manipulate valuations.

### [How Baltimore Assessments Accidentally Subsidize Blight—And How We Can Fix It](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-baltimore-assessments-accidentally-subsidize-blight-and-how-we-can-fix-it/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“fundamental flaw in how”**
  - sentence: …In our new report, published by the Center for Land Economics (CLE), we uncovered a fundamental flaw in how Baltimore’s vacant land is valued for tax purposes.…
  - why: The essay identifies systematic undervaluation by assessors, directly contradicting the objection that land value cannot be accurately assessed.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“actively encourages blight”**
  - sentence: …This flawed methodology not only actively encourages blight, but also punishes development and unfairly shifts the tax burden onto ordinary homeowners and businesses.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that underassessed vacant lots create a tax advantage for holding land idle, thereby fueling speculative vacancy and blight.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“keeping land vacant is cheap”**
  - sentence: …This consistent undervaluation sends a powerful economic signal: keeping land vacant is cheap.…
  - why: The essay evidences that low taxes on vacant land incentivize speculation, implying that correcting assessments (or implementing LVT) would remove this subsidy.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“systematically underassessed to a degree”**
  - sentence: …Baltimore’s vacant lots are systematically underassessed to a degree that may amount to as much as half a billion dollars in undervaluation.…
  - why: The essay documents massive assessment errors that benefit speculators, illustrating the risk that flawed assessments can be gamed or fail to prevent inequity.

### [How Bitcoin Is Thriving in Venezuela](https://www.progress.org/articles/bitcoin-thriving-venezuela/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“The condition for the average”**

  - sentence: …The irony of this, of course, is that cryptocurrencies, as yet, are anything but stable.…
  - why: Illustrates the failure of state-controlled currency and economic policy, serving as a modern example of how government mismanagement can destroy a monetary system, relevant to narratives about system

  - sentence: …The Venezuelan government’s decision to curtail circulation of the bolivar and allow US dollar transactions over the last two years has only fueled this move by making it increasingly difficult for Venezuelans to even obtain domestic curren…
  - why: Demonstrates the practical difficulties of government intervention in currency valuation and assessment, paralleling the complexity of assessing value in a volatile or manipulated economic environment

### [How Georgists Valued land in the 1900's](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-georgists-valued-land-in-the-1900s/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“valuing land was no great trouble”**
  - sentence: …While we moderns look to computerized geospatial analysis to solve the thornier aspects of land valuation, Georgist s writing one hundred years ago, lacking all our modern innovations, claimed that valuing land was no great trouble at all.…
  - why: The essay directly counters the objection that land cannot be assessed by detailing a historical system that successfully separated land value from building value without modern technology.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“universal adoption of Euclidean zoning”**
  - sentence: …Finally, the book is written by the president of the company that invented the method, which means that while we may still treat the book as an earnest educational resource, we must also understand it in part as marketing material.…
  - why: The author notes that the Somers system functioned before the universal adoption of zoning, implying that land valuation is possible without zoning reform, countering the objection that LVT requires z
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“get them to collectively color”**
  - sentence: …If I had to describe the Somers system in one sentence, I’d put it like this: Summon a bunch of locals, get them to collectively color in a local land value map, then feed that map as the input to a boring algorithm that spits out fair and …
  - why: The essay describes a transparent, community-driven assessment process that relies on public consensus rather than opaque assessor discretion, countering fears of corrupt assessments.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“value for every single parcel of land, entirely separate”**
  - sentence: …How did its assessors establish a fair, mathematically uniform, and easily explainable value for every single parcel of land, entirely separate from the value of the buildings sitting on top, without the aid of so much as a pocket calculato…
  - why: The essay illustrates a method specifically designed to isolate land value from building value, demonstrating that land value tax is distinct from standard property taxation.

### [How Greece Can Save Itself](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-greece-can-save-itself/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replace the income tax”**
  - sentence: …Immediately replace the income tax, the value-added tax, and all other taxes with a tax on land value and a pollution tax.…

- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“lease it back to the owner”**
  - sentence: …Enable citizens to sue polluting firms that are not paying a pollution tax based on the damage. Allow real estate owners to self-assess their land value with the condition that the state could buy their land at their assessment plus 25 perc…
  - why: The proposal to lease land back at market rentals implies the tax burden falls on the landowner, not the building user.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“self-assess their land value”**
  - sentence: …Allow real estate owners to self-assess their land value with the condition that the state could buy their land at their assessment plus 25 percent, and lease it back to the owner of the building at current market rentals.…
  - why: The essay proposes a self-assessment mechanism with a buyout clause to prevent owners from under-assessing to avoid taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax on land value”**
  - sentence: …Immediately replace the income tax, the value-added tax, and all other taxes with a tax on land value and a pollution tax.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates shifting the tax base from labor and transactions to land value.

### [How important is Understanding Economics?](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-important-is-understanding-economics/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“perverse incentives behind SPRAWL”**
  - sentence: …The perverse incentives behind SPRAWL are one of the many economic mysteries that our course, Understanding Economics , makes sense of.…
  - why: The essay identifies sprawl as a major problem driven by economic incentives, which the course (and the wiki page) explains how to fix.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“new way of thinking about labor”**
  - sentence: …We simply offer a new way of thinking about labor, wealth and the Earth : one that harmonizes seemingly irreconcilable conflicts.…
  - why: The essay promotes the Georgist framework of distinguishing labor from land (Earth) to resolve economic conflicts.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“Sprawling development wastes everything”**
  - sentence: …Sprawling development wastes everything: land, resources, energy, time and effort.…

- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“cities tax themselves out of existence”**
  - sentence: …Meanwhile, cities tax themselves out of existence, struggling to pay for basic services.…
  - why: The essay implies a disconnect between public needs and current revenue systems, supporting the narrative that community value should be captured to fund services.

### [How Land Barons, Industrialists, and Bankers Corrupted Economics](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-land-barons-industrialists-and-bankers-corrupted-economics/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“The Corruption of Economics”**
  - sentence: …The Corruption of Economics by Mason Gaffney and Fred Harrison, while free online, is hardly known; as of December 2015 only three New Zealand university libraries and the Auckland Public Library held copies.…
  - why: The essay explicitly summarizes and endorses Gaffney and Harrison's thesis that wealthy interests corrupted economics to suppress Georgist ideas.
- **supports** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“subsume land under capital”**
  - sentence: …The story explains how, for their wealthy paymasters, academics corrupted the language to subsume land under capital as a factor of production.…
  - why: The essay argues that the conflation of land with capital was a deliberate act of corruption to obscure land's unique economic role.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“a single tax is needed”**
  - sentence: …Fred Harrison describes the phenomenon of Henry George , the San Francisco journalist who took the world by storm with his book Progress and Poverty in 1879, in which he argues that the benefits of land ownership must be shared by all and t…
  - why: The essay recounts the historical rise and global influence of Henry George's Single Tax proposal.
- **illustrates** [`objections/marshall-single-tax-objection`](https://progress.org/wiki/marshall-single-tax-objection/) — anchor: **“Alfred Marshall”**
  - sentence: …These early neoclassical economists were J B Clark, Philip Wicksteed, Alfred Marshall, ERA Seligman and Francis A Walker, who each contributed something to ‘addle, baffle, boggle and dazzle the laity’.…
  - why: The essay identifies Alfred Marshall as part of the group of neoclassical economists who actively worked to undermine Henry George's theories.

### [How much does UBI cost?](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-much-does-ubi-cost/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“drop the official poverty rate”**
  - sentence: …This $539 billion UBI would drop the official poverty rate from 13.5% to 0%, lifting 43.1 million people (including about 14.5 million children) out of poverty.…
  - why: provides quantitative evidence that a universal dividend eliminates poverty
- **illustrates** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“net financial benefit”**
  - sentence: …This UBI will be a net financial benefit to most families with incomes up to $55,000, making it an effective wage subsidy (or tax cut) for tens of millions of working families.…
  - why: demonstrates the distributional impact and net benefit mechanism of a dividend
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“net cost... is $539 billion”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The net cost—the real cost—of a roughly poverty-level UBI is $539 billion per year, less than 16% of its often-mentioned but not-very-meaningful gross cost ($4.15 trillion), less than 25% of the cost of current U.S. entitlement spending, le…
  - why: establishes a feasible revenue benchmark that land rent could potentially fund
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Cost of Basic Income”**
  - sentence: …I recently completed some simple, “back-of-the-envelope” estimates the net cost of a UBI set at about the official poverty line: $12,000 per adult and $6,000 per child with a 50% “marginal tax rate.”…
  - why: provides a concrete fiscal model for implementing a citizen's dividend

### [How Obama could be the Greatest President Ever](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-obama-could-be-the-greatest-president-ever/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“make the economy more productive”**
  - sentence: …A land value tax will make the economy more productive, as the land-value subsidy promotes inefficient uses of land and the excessive speculation that results in the boom-bust sequence we are suffering from now.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that a land value tax increases productivity by eliminating the subsidy that causes inefficient land use.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“excessive speculation that results in the boom-bust”**
  - sentence: …A land value tax will make the economy more productive, as the land-value subsidy promotes inefficient uses of land and the excessive speculation that results in the boom-bust sequence we are suffering from now.…
  - why: The essay identifies land speculation driven by subsidies as the direct cause of the boom-bust economic cycle.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“increase in their rent and land value due to public works”**
  - sentence: …Landowners are subsidized from the increase in their rent and land value due to public works and civic services.…
  - why: The essay explains that public works and civic services are the source of increased land rent and value, justifying the capture of this subsidy.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Stop taxing labor, goods, production, and investment”**
  - sentence: …Stop taxing labor, goods, production, and investment.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a radical tax shift that removes burdens from labor and production in favor of land and resource rents.

### [How Ron Paul could Win the Presidency in 2008](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-ron-paul-could-win-the-presidency-in-2008/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“pollution levies that apply”**
  - sentence: …If libertarians are serious about eliminating subsidies, they should favor pollution levies that apply the polluter-pays principle to emissions that have millions of victims and that replace punitive taxes on wages and entrepreneurship.…
  - why: argues that shifting taxes to pollution charges is consistent with free-market principles
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“eliminate the excess burden”**
  - sentence: …The green tax shift would eliminate the excess burden and also replace intrusive and costly regulations such as gasoline additives and engine requirements.…
  - why: claims that shifting taxes from income to pollution removes economic deadweight loss
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“turn global environmental policy”**
  - sentence: …Ron Paul! You have the opportunity not just to be elected president but also to turn global environmental policy away from regulations, deception-laden carbon credits, and problematic cap-and-trade policies, to market-friendly pollution lev…
  - why: advocates for market-friendly pollution levies as the solution to environmental issues
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replacing income taxes with pollution charges”**
  - sentence: …Although Ron Paul opposes new taxes, it would be consistent with his free-market opposition to subsidies and corporate welfare to favor a "green tax shift," replacing income taxes with pollution charges.…
  - why: promotes shifting the tax burden off labor and onto resource use/pollution

### [How the Free Market Is Providing the Solution to Global Warming](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-the-free-market-is-providing-the-solution-to-global-warming/)
- **counters** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“the free market is beginning”**

- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“the free market is beginning”**
  - sentence: …As often happens, the free market is beginning to find solutions where politicians can’t.…
  - why: The essay disputes the need for government assessment or intervention, claiming the free market provides the solution.
- **counters** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“the free market is beginning”**
  - sentence: …As often happens, the free market is beginning to find solutions where politicians can’t.…

### [How the Republican Party can be Resurrected](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-the-republican-party-can-be-resurrected/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“ride the 18-year boom-bust cycle”**
  - sentence: …That’s too bad for we the people, since we will have to once again ride the 18-year boom-bust cycle, next time to the even greater Crash of 2026, as by that time the federal government will be all tapped out.…
  - why: The essay explicitly predicts a recurring 18-year boom-bust cycle culminating in a crash, supporting the narrative that land speculation drives cyclical economic crises.

  - sentence: …Replace these with public revenues that promote efficiency: levies on land value, pollution, congestion, as well as user fees.…

  - sentence: …The abolition of taxes on wages would be the radical change that would provide labor with the wealth and clout it deserves.…
  - why: The essay argues for the abolition of taxes on wages as a fundamental reform, aligning with the narrative to shift taxation from labor to land.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“levies on land value, pollution, congestion”**
  - sentence: …Replace these with public revenues that promote efficiency: levies on land value, pollution, congestion, as well as user fees.…

### [How the U.S. Government Collapsed the Economy, and How to Build It Back Much Better](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-the-u-s-government-collapsed-the-economy-and-how-to-build-it-back-much-better/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“speculators jump in”**
  - sentence: …As real estate prices rise, helped by cheap credit and government spending, speculators jump in and help raise property values to a height making dwellings unaffordable.…
  - why: The essay explicitly describes the mechanism of the land cycle—cheap credit fueling speculation that drives up prices until the crash—vindicating the narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“increase in land rent”**
  - sentence: …The mother of all subsidies is the increase in land rent and land value caused by the public goods provided by government.…
  - why: The essay argues that public goods create land value, which constitutes a subsidy to owners, directly supporting the claim about public investment capitalizing into land.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“taxes were on land value”**
  - sentence: …There was no income or sales tax back then; taxes were on real estate, and with the buildings destroyed, in effect taxes were on land value.…
  - why: The essay uses the post-1906 San Francisco rapid recovery as historical evidence that taxing land value incentivizes rebuilding and productivity.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxes were on land value”**
  - sentence: …The same would happen today if the taxes were on land value instead of on labor, goods, and enterprise.…
  - why: The essay advocates for shifting the tax burden off labor and enterprise onto land value to improve the economy, which is the core of this narrative.

### [How to Avoid Budget Crises](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-avoid-budget-crises/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“natural source of revenue”**
  - sentence: …Local government was deprived of its natural source of revenue, land value.…
  - why: The essay identifies land value as the natural fiscal base for local government.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“Freed of state income and sales taxes”**
  - sentence: …Freed of state income and sales taxes, enterprise would thrive and the economy of the state would quickly recover, especially in those counties that set their property tax only on land.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes off enterprise and onto land would cause the economy to thrive.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“main source of public revenue would be land rent”**
  - sentence: …Public finance would shift to property taxes, and if the county boards were wise, the main source of public revenue would be land rent or land value.…
  - why: The essay advocates replacing harmful taxes on sales and income with land value.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“enterprise would thrive”**
  - sentence: …Freed of state income and sales taxes, enterprise would thrive and the economy of the state would quickly recover, especially in those counties that set their property tax only on land.…
  - why: The essay implies land taxation alone is sufficient for economic recovery, contrary to the objection that it requires zoning reform first.

### [How to Do Brexit Right](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-do-brexit-right/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“tax on land value”**
  - sentence: …The best policy for the UK would then be to replace its VAT with LVT, a land-value tax.…
  - why: The essay argues that replacing a distortionary tax (VAT) with a land value tax improves economic efficiency by removing the tax burden on production.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“land will not shrink”**
  - sentence: …A tax on land value would have no economic burden, since the land will not shrink, flee, or hide when taxed.…
  - why: The essay uses the classic Georgist argument that land is inelastic to justify LVT as a superior alternative to taxing goods or labor.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replace its VAT with LVT”**
  - sentence: …The best policy for the UK would then be to replace its VAT with LVT, a land-value tax.…
  - why: The essay proposes shifting the tax base from capital (VAT on goods) to land to eliminate deadweight loss and compensate for external trade barriers.

### [How to Eliminate Eminent Domain](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-eliminate-eminent-domain/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land value being a creation”**
  - sentence: …In my judgement, under pure geoism, the philosophy inspired by the thought of Henry George, the title holder should have complete rights of possession so long as the economic rent of the land is distributed to the community, as the land ren…
  - why: The essay explicitly grounds its argument for land rights on the premise that land value is created by the community and nature.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“nasty city chiefs get corrupt assessors”**
  - sentence: …If nasty city chiefs get corrupt assessors to place a high value on the site, this would be public knowledge, as it would be on the books for public inspection.…
  - why: The essay acknowledges the risk of corrupt assessments being used to dispossess owners, illustrating the specific objection about assessment abuse.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“complete rights of possession”**
  - sentence: …In my judgement, under pure geoism, the philosophy inspired by the thought of Henry George, the title holder should have complete rights of possession so long as the economic rent of the land is distributed to the community, as the land ren…
  - why: The essay argues for absolute possession rights conditional on rent payment, supporting the geoist remedy of secure tenure over state seizure.
