- **counters** [`objections/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base/) — anchor: **“title holder always has the choice”**
  - sentence: …As with any increase in site value and rent, under a proper geoist system, the title holder always has the choice, pay the rent or transfer the site to someone who is willing to pay the rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that possession remains secure if rent is paid, countering the objection that a full land tax claim destroys the owner's stake or the tax base.

### [How to End Taxes Immediately](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-end-taxes-immediately/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“abolishing all taxes on income”**
  - sentence: …There would be a constitutional amendment immediately abolishing all taxes on income other than from land, and also immediately abolishing all sales taxes by all jurisdictions, and all taxes on produced goods, including buildings.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the immediate removal of all taxes on labor and production in favor of land revenue.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“revenue would flood to the government”**
  - sentence: …Rent that was suppressed by taxes that lower productivity would now be liberated from distortions, and we would likely see rent clearly as a fifth, possibly a third, of all income, and revenue would flood to the government like a tsunami.…
  - why: The essay argues that liberating rent from distortions would generate enough revenue to fund the government.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“Compensate those who lose”**
  - sentence: …Those with net losses from the great shift would be compensated with special government bonds.…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific mechanism (JAMES bonds) to compensate losers, addressing the objection that LVT inflicts a wealth shock.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“great increase in productivity”**
  - sentence: …At first, the interest paid on JAMES bonds would come from issuing more bonds. But after tax abolition, the great increase in productivity would raise wages and reduce the rationale for transfer payments, and greater productivity would also…
  - why: The essay claims that removing taxes on labor and capital will cause a significant increase in productivity.

### [How to Extirpate Poverty](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-extirpate-poverty/)
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“margin of production moves”**
  - sentence: …As explained by the economist Henry George in his book Progress and Poverty, the margin of production moves out farther and faster to less productive land when people can hold land even if they don’t use it.…
  - why: The essay argues that holding land idle forces labor onto less productive land, lowering wages and aggregate productivity.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“Land is then not worth holding”**
  - sentence: …Land is then not worth holding unless one uses it in its most productive use, since the rent paid to the community is based on the highest and best use of the land.…
  - why: The essay claims that collecting land rent forces land into its most productive use, thereby raising the wage level and economic output.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“spurs land speculation that carries”**
  - sentence: …The capture of economic expansion gains by land rent and land value spurs land speculation that carries the price of land so high it is no longer affordable.…
  - why: The essay explicitly links the capture of rent by landowners to speculative bubbles that cause economic depressions.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxes that come from wages”**
  - sentence: …This would involve a tax shift, in which taxes that come from wages are replaced by public revenue from land rent, or from voluntary payments by folks who receive an equal share of the rent.…
  - why: The essay advocates for an efficiency tax shift that removes levies on work and investment to fund government through land rent.

### [How to Invest Wisely](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-invest-wisely/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“investments are cyclical”**
  - sentence: …Third, be aware that investments are cyclical.…
  - why: The essay explicitly warns that zooming prices indicate coming trouble, supporting the narrative that boom-bust cycles are a predictable feature of markets like real estate.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“prices have been zooming up”**
  - sentence: …If prices have been zooming up, this indicates coming trouble rather than opportunity.…
  - why: The essay argues that rapid price increases are a clear signal of future risk, countering the objection that bubbles cannot be identified before they burst.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“luxury condo-hotel in Baja”**
  - sentence: …An example of investment failure is the luxury condo-hotel in Baja California, Mexico, promoted by real estate developer and owner Donald Trump.…
  - why: The essay uses the collapse of a speculative real estate project to illustrate the volatility and risks inherent in land-based investments during a housing crisis.
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“All they money they had put in is now gone”**
  - sentence: …All they money they had put in is now gone, as the project was abandoned, and all that is left is a big hole.…
  - why: The essay describes a scenario where speculative land investment resulted in total loss and abandonment, countering the claim that such speculation performs a useful economic function.

### [How to Make Baghdad Safe](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-make-baghdad-safe/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“collect ground rent”**
  - sentence: …The gated community would then collect ground rent from every plot of land within the neighborhood, just as private gated communities are financed from the assessments on the real estate owned by the members.…
  - why: The essay proposes funding local governance via ground rent assessments, illustrating the mechanism of land value capture.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“islands of security”**
  - sentence: …As more neighborhoods become secure, there would be an exponential increase in commerce.…
  - why: The essay argues that creating secure communities (public goods) increases commerce and value, supporting the claim that community activity creates land value.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“financed from the assessments”**
  - sentence: …The gated community would then collect ground rent from every plot of land within the neighborhood, just as private gated communities are financed from the assessments on the real estate owned by the members.…
  - why: The essay explicitly models a system where public goods (security, governance) are funded by collecting ground rent.

### [How to Make the New Year Better](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-make-the-new-year-better/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“tax on land value”**
  - sentence: …Aside from pollution taxes, if government chiefs seek more revenue, they can levy a tax on land value.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting revenue sources from income and sales to land value is the 'ultimate in supply-side policy' that avoids the economic distortions of other taxes.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“push the economy towards greater production”**
  - sentence: …The tapping of land rent for public revenue would, contrary to other taxes, push the economy towards greater production, since an underused plot of land would pay taxes based on its most productive use.…
  - why: The essay explicitly claims that taxing land value increases production by forcing underused land to meet its potential, aligning with the productivity claim.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“make the real estate crash even worse”**
  - sentence: …Critics say that since a tax on land value would make the price of land fall, this would make the real estate crash even worse.…
  - why: The essay cites the objection that LVT would exacerbate a real estate crash but argues that the best time to implement it is precisely when values have already fallen.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“eliminating taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …In an efficient tax shift, eliminating taxes on wages, profits, sales, and value added, replacing them with taxes on pollution and land value, most folks would have net gains.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a specific tax shift that removes burdens from labor and capital to place them on land and pollution.

### [How to Outlive Your Mortgage In a Hip City](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-outlive-your-mortgage-in-a-hip-city/)
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“$200,000 bungalow on a $800,000 parcel”**
  - sentence: …That million dollar bungalow? That’s not a million dollar cottage. It’s probably more like a $200,000 bungalow on a $800,000 parcel of land.…
  - why: Decomposes high housing costs into structure vs. land value to show land is the driver.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“product of the presence of society”**
  - sentence: …Rather, it’s the product of the presence of society.…
  - why: Argues that location value derives from social factors like population density and infrastructure.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“paying all San Francisco residents a dividend”**
  - sentence: …San Francisco residents need to recover it and share it by paying all San Francisco residents a dividend from surplus public revenue.…
  - why: Proposes distributing recovered land rent as a citizen dividend to offset housing costs.
- **illustrates** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“It’d take you 150 years to pay”**
  - sentence: …It’d take you 150 years to pay the home off.…
  - why: Illustrates the extreme financial burden locking young adults out of homeownership.

### [How To Prevent the Boom and Bust Cycle](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-prevent-the-boom-and-bust-cycle/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“cause of the boom and bust”**
  - sentence: …There is one fundamental, though mostly unrecognized, cause of the boom and bust economic cycle: subsidies to land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land subsidies are the fundamental cause of economic cycles.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“increase in land value”**
  - sentence: …That fiscal effect, the increase in land value from public goods, is the “mother of all subsidies”.…
  - why: The essay explains how public goods spending increases land rent and value, capitalizing public investment into private land values.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“eliminate the gains from land speculation”**
  - sentence: …A tax on most of the land value would eliminate the gains from land speculation.…
  - why: The essay argues that a land value tax is the specific remedy needed to stop land speculation.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“blaming the financial system”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Most people, including economists, blame the financial system for economic depressions.…
  - why: The essay dismisses the view that the financial system is the root cause, arguing instead that land value subsidies drive the cycle.

### [How to Really Slash Medical Costs](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-really-slash-medical-costs/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“efficiency tax shift”**
  - sentence: …Economically enlightened folks often wonder why the world resists the efficiency tax shift that replaces punitive taxes on wages and goods with levies on land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly endorses the Georgist policy of shifting taxes from wages and goods to land value.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“taxes on wages and goods”**
  - sentence: …Just as taxes on wages and goods impose an excess burden on the economy, eating too much of animal products imposes an excess burden on your body, causing disease.…
  - why: The essay uses the economic principle that taxes on labor and production are inefficient (deadweight loss) to argue for the superior efficiency of a land value tax.

### [How to Restore Economic Growth](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-restore-economic-growth/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“reduce the marginal tax rates”**
  - sentence: …Given the large amount of current taxation and borrowing, the most important policy change governments can do is to reduce the marginal tax rates, replacing them with higher fixed taxes.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting from marginal taxes on labor and investment to fixed taxes on land removes artificial costs and boosts growth.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“pay all our public revenues”**
  - sentence: …Nature’s land is offering to pay all our public revenues, but people reject her offer, and insist on inflicting one another with punitive taxes.…
  - why: The essay asserts that land rent is sufficient to fund government, countering the belief that it is a tiny portion of national income.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“fueled previous asset bubbles”**
  - sentence: …The Federal Reserve has pushed interest rates down to historically low levels, and that policy may have prevented even worse outcomes, but artificially low interest rates fueled previous asset bubbles and has fueled an artificial rise of la…
  - why: The essay links low interest rates to artificial land value increases and warns that land will cause the next global financial disaster.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“compensate landowners for any net loss”**
  - sentence: …Therefore a tax shift that replaces marginal-cost taxation with fixed-cost revenue would compensate landowners for any net loss.…
  - why: The essay proposes compensating landowners with bonds to address the transition shock of shifting to a land-based tax system.

### [How to Revive South Africa](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-revive-south-africa/)
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“Land Nationalisation vs Land Value Tax”**
  - sentence: …Land Nationalisation vs Land Value Tax: There are only two policies to implement if we want to ensure that those that have been excluded can benefit from the land: land nationalisation or a land value tax.…
  - why: The essay contrasts nationalization with LVT, arguing that while nationalization is theoretically possible, it is prone to corruption and economic disaster in South Africa, unlike the safer LVT altern
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“nationalisation goes wrong because”**
  - sentence: …In most cases, nationalisation goes wrong because the process becomes corrupt.…
  - why: The essay argues that state control of land (and its rent) invites corruption, citing the risks of politicians handing out leases to allies, which supports the objection regarding government corruptio
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“confiscated their land for settlers”**
  - sentence: …This vast inequity is the result of the era of colonization, during which first the Dutch and later the British waged continuous wars on native African people and confiscated their land for settlers and government projects.…
  - why: The essay details the historical dispossession of land from native Africans by colonial powers, illustrating the narrative that current land titles stem from theft and conquest.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“leverage the vast labor and capital”**
  - sentence: …Urban land provides access to potential retail customers, workers for factories and offices, tenants for housing developments, and in general the ability to leverage the vast labor and capital of the urban community.…
  - why: The essay explains that urban land value derives from access to the community's labor and capital, supporting the claim that land value is socially created.

### [How to Solve the Credit Crisis](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-solve-the-credit-crisis/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“periodic economic booms and crashes”**
  - sentence: …When we punish labor with taxes and restrictions, and reward land speculators with subsidies, what do you expect other than periodic economic booms and crashes?…
  - why: The essay explicitly attributes the crisis to subsidies and capitalization of land values that fuel speculation, arguing this causes recurring crashes.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“mortgage problem would be gone”**
  - sentence: …The mortgage problem would be gone, as there would be no more mortgages on land.…
  - why: The essay identifies the credit crisis as fundamentally a mortgage problem tied to land, proposing to eliminate land mortgages to solve it.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“complete the deflation the land value bubble”**
  - sentence: …Land prices would fall to one tenth of the previous price. That would complete the deflation the land value bubble in one swoop.…
  - why: The essay argues that a 90% land value tax would immediately pop the land value bubble, thereby dampening speculation.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“compensate for the loss of selling price”**
  - sentence: …To compensate for the loss of selling price, all landowners would be given perpetual US Treasury bills.…
  - why: The essay explicitly addresses the wealth shock of falling land prices by proposing compensation via Treasury bonds.

### [How to Value Land: Korean style!](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-to-value-land-korean-style/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“land can’t be valued separately”**
  - sentence: …One of the most consistent objections to Land Value Tax is that “land can’t be valued separately from improvements,” a complaint I initially addressed in my article series, later published as a chapter in my book, Land is a Big Deal.…
  - why: The essay presents South Korea's annual, parcel-level land valuation system as a direct counter-example to the objection that land cannot be assessed separately from improvements.
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“standard land parcels”**
  - sentence: …Korea’s “one special technique” is a particular focus on standard land parcels.…
  - why: The essay details the specific technical methodology (standard land parcels and adjustment tables) used to overcome the assessment objection.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“each standard parcel must be valued”**
  - sentence: …In fact, it’s more accurate to say that MOLIT organizes the national effort, and crucially, the rules state that in all but exceptional cases each standard parcel must be valued by at least two different appraisal firms.…
  - why: The essay describes institutional safeguards—such as requiring dual appraisals and multi-level oversight—that mitigate the risk of corrupt assessments.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“land values specifically, not just total”**
  - sentence: …South Korea , a country which not only regularly values all its land (and land values specifically, not just total property values), but which does so annually , and not based on broad regional bands, but down to the individual parcel…
  - why: The essay distinguishes the Korean system from standard property taxation by emphasizing its exclusive focus on land values rather than total property values.

### [How Well Can One Know Another? Even Oneself?](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-well-can-we-know-one-another/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“government planning and control”**
  - sentence: …One reason why government planning and control so often fail is that much of the economy is hidden from view.…
  - why: The essay argues government fails because it cannot access the decentralized, tacit knowledge hidden in the economy, echoing the public-choice critique of central planning.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“keep our full wage”**
  - sentence: …If we can keep our full wage and share the rent, we would all be able to choose our own bliss.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for keeping the full product of labor (wages) while sharing land rent, which is the core definition of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“share the rent”**
  - sentence: …If we can keep our full wage and share the rent, we would all be able to choose our own bliss.…
  - why: The essay's proposal to 'share the rent' implies that rent is a common fund to be distributed, supporting the narrative that land value is created by the community and should be returned to it.
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“much of the economy is hidden”**
  - sentence: …One reason why government planning and control so often fail is that much of the economy is hidden from view.…
  - why: The essay's broader theme that economic reality is 'hidden from sight' and difficult to measure supports the objection that accurately assessing specific components like land value is inherently chall

### [How YIMBYism Can Pave The Way For Georgism](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-yimbyism-can-pave-the-way-for-georgism/)
- **supports** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“home owning Americans”**
  - sentence: …Reappropriating land rents remains anathema to most home owning Americans.…
  - why: The essay validates the objection that homeowners are the primary political barrier to LVT, citing the 'tax revolt' and the entrenchment of property as wealth.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“existing land use regimes”**
  - sentence: …As it stands, implementing an LVT is a political non-starter.…
  - why: The essay argues that LVT cannot function as a supply lever where zoning is restrictive, predicting a political backlash rather than development if LVT is enacted without zoning reform.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifting taxes from improvements to land”**
  - sentence: …In a world where homeowners want – and are allowed – to densify their properties, shifting taxes from improvements to land imparts immediate, tangible benefits as well.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the mechanism by which shifting tax burden to land encourages development, specifically noting the synergy with legalized ADUs and density.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“fear of being taxed out”**
  - sentence: …There's also a fear of being taxed out of one's home.…
  - why: The essay identifies the 'asset-rich, cash-poor' fear—specifically the risk of being forced out of one's home—as a core source of political resistance to LVT.

### [Humanity II](https://www.progress.org/articles/humanity-ii/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“special interests feed”**
  - sentence: …Governments world-wide are deep in debt, and politics becomes a battle in which special interests feed at the expense of the public.…
  - why: The essay describes a political economy where special interests capture public wealth, aligning with the claim that rent-seeking hinders economic and social progress.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“voluntary contributions tied”**
  - sentence: …There will also be voluntary contributions tied to the ownership of land value.…
  - why: The essay proposes funding a new governance structure specifically through payments based on land value, illustrating the claim that land rent can support public finance.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“land-based public finance”**
  - sentence: …They may not like the small-group voting, or land-based public finance, or the ethical concept that one be free so long as others are not harmed.…
  - why: The essay explicitly lists 'land-based public finance' as a core constitutional principle of its proposed reform, directly invoking the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“ethical concept that”**
  - sentence: …They may not like the small-group voting, or land-based public finance, or the ethical concept that one be free so long as others are not harmed.…
  - why: The essay grounds its proposed constitution in the ethical principle of non-harm, which is the philosophical foundation for land justice and reconciliation narratives.

### [I'm Lovin' It!](https://www.progress.org/articles/im-lovin-it/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“the city has thrown in”**
  - sentence: …And, the city has thrown in a brand new subway station, to the tune of some $2.4 billion (while the Second Avenue subway line limps along toward its long-awaited completion date of "whenever").…
  - why: The essay details how public infrastructure (the subway station) directly increases the value of nearby private land, which McDonald's then captures.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“world leader in real estate speculation”**
  - sentence: …It's well-known that McDonalds (the world leader in the fast-food business) is, more to the point, a world leader in real estate speculation.…
  - why: The essay highlights McDonald's prioritization of land speculation over productive enterprise (operating the restaurant at a loss) as a prime example of rent-seeking.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“sold this year for $35.6 million”**
  - sentence: …The parcel was sold this year for $35.6 million.…
  - why: The massive profit from selling the land ($35.6 million) versus the tax paid ($174,000) illustrates the capture of unearned increment created by the community.
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“does it make sense to have”**
  - sentence: …Now, you might ask, with Manhattan real estate worth what it's worth, does it make sense to have a drive-thru, with a big parking lot?…
  - why: The essay points out the inefficiency of holding a valuable Manhattan site as a low-value drive-thru with a parking lot, demonstrating land underuse for speculative gain.

### [I.O.U. an Answer](https://www.progress.org/articles/iou-an-answer/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“tax only land value”**
  - sentence: …Congress should have authorized Congress with the power to tax only land value and harms such as pollution.…
  - why: The essay argues that the constitutional power to tax should be limited to land value to solve federal deficits.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …With no taxes on wages, folks can then save much more, and they would have more incentive to save with no taxes on interest and dividends.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting the tax burden off labor and capital to enable savings and eliminate entitlements.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“harms such as pollution”**
  - sentence: …Congress should have authorized Congress with the power to tax only land value and harms such as pollution.…
  - why: The essay includes pollution as a specific target for taxation alongside land value.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“meaningful remedies such as pollution charges”**
  - sentence: …It is a good comparison, because An Inconvenient Truth also lacks meaningful remedies such as pollution charges.…
  - why: The essay identifies pollution charges as the necessary but absent remedy for climate change.

### [Ignorance, Apathy, and Greed](https://www.progress.org/articles/ignorance-apathy-and-greed/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“policy failure that needlessly reduces”**
  - sentence: …However, the government regulates pollution instead, a policy failure that needlessly reduces employment and economic growth.…
  - why: The essay describes how regulatory capture by special interests leads to inefficient policy outcomes, illustrating the concept of rent-seeking.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“special interests pay to legislate it”**
  - sentence: …Because of the tax and land-tenure systems. Why do we have those systems? Because special interests pay to legislate it.…
  - why: The essay identifies the influence of special interests on legislation as a root cause of systemic problems, aligning with the narrative of corruption.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“incentives of the greedy dominate”**
  - sentence: …With the benefits concentrated among a few interests, and the costs spread among the whole population, the incentives of the greedy dominate the incentives of the masses.…
  - why: The essay explains why concentrated interests win over diffuse majorities due to rational apathy, which is the core of the public-choice objection.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“morally undeserved portion of income”**
  - sentence: …The desire of the owners and managers of polluting industries to avoid the social cost of their pollution is greed, a morally undeserved portion of income.…
  - why: The essay defines greed as taking more than one morally deserves, which parallels the Georgist narrative that unearned income is unjust.

### [Implied Consent](https://www.progress.org/articles/implied-consent/)
- **counters** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“implied consent by all”**
  - sentence: …The idea is that a person may not agree with some policies, but benefits from others, so it all evens out, and therefore there is an implied consent by all regarding government policies.…
  - why: The essay disputes the legitimacy of implied consent to justify government authority, challenging the moral foundation of existing land titles derived from state power.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“supported anarchism or voluntary governance”**
  - sentence: …Although Spooner thought that his arguments against a governmental implied consent supported anarchism or voluntary governance under explicit contracts, there is another possibility.…
  - why: The author explicitly distinguishes the proposed governance model (enforcing natural moral law) from anarchism or socialism, countering the objection that Georgist governance is a form of socialism.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“government whose sole function is to protect”**
  - sentence: …Therefore if there is a government whose sole function is to protect people from coercive harm, and that government does not itself commit coercive harm both in its laws and its public finances, then no consent is needed.…
  - why: The essay argues for a minimal state limited to protection from harm, aligning with the Georgist view that government's proper role is to secure rights rather than redistribute wealth.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“no person should be above any other”**
  - sentence: …However, in human reality, there is no perfect governance, and people disagree on the details of law, and so, as a practical matter, if we take human equality to its logical conclusion, no person should be above any other.…
  - why: The essay's emphasis on human equality and voluntary contracts supports the narrative that just land relations must be based on equal standing rather than hierarchical authority.

### [In Memoriam: 125 Years Since Henry George](https://www.progress.org/articles/in-memoriam-125-years-since-henry-george/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“why do these depressions happen”**
  - sentence: …Thus it was that the question occurred to him, why do these depressions happen?…
  - why: The essay recounts Henry George's foundational observation of recurring industrial depressions, which led to his theory that land speculation drives the business cycle.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“shocking contrast between monstrous wealth”**
  - sentence: …Years afterward George said that here “I saw and recognized for the first time the shocking contrast between monstrous wealth and debasing want.”…
  - why: This passage illustrates the extreme inequality and 'monstrous wealth' characteristic of a rentier economy, which motivated George's search for the root cause of poverty amidst progress.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“what is the true fundamental cause”**
  - sentence: …The second question was, what is the true fundamental cause of the enormous inequality in the distribution of wealth?…
  - why: The essay frames George's life work as the quest to answer the two central questions—depressions and inequality—that the Single Tax narrative aims to solve.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“The country was not poor and overpopulated”**
  - sentence: …The country was not poor and overpopulated — far from it.…
  - why: The essay explicitly rejects the Malthusian explanation for poverty, noting the country was rich and sparsely populated yet still suffered from destitution.

### [In Memory of Dr. Fred Foldvary](https://www.progress.org/articles/in-memory-of-dr-fred-foldvary/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“18-year real-estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …Dr. Foldvary was the first economist — due to his in-depth knowledge of the 18-year real-estate cycle — to have correctly forecast the economic crash of 2008 a full 11 years before it happened, back in 1997.…
  - why: The essay cites Foldvary's successful prediction of the 2008 crash as evidence of the predictive power of the 18-year land cycle theory.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“correctly forecast the economic crash”**
  - sentence: …Dr. Foldvary was the first economist — due to his in-depth knowledge of the 18-year real-estate cycle — to have correctly forecast the economic crash of 2008 a full 11 years before it happened, back in 1997.…
  - why: Foldvary's accurate 1997 forecast contradicts the objection that economic bubbles cannot be identified in advance.

### [In the Belly of the Fed](https://www.progress.org/articles/in-the-belly-of-the-fed/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“borrowing to buy real estate”**
  - sentence: …Where our overlooked land and our conspicuous money meet is at the bank —the borrowing to buy real estate, whose value is largely that of the location.…
  - why: The essay argues that banking profits and credit expansion are fundamentally tied to lending against land values.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“make crashes inevitable”**
  - sentence: …When bankers loosen their credit requirements, they make crashes inevitable.…
  - why: The essay attributes the inevitability of economic crashes to bankers loosening credit to fuel land speculation.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“surplus cash goes to bidding up”**
  - sentence: …A lot of the surplus cash goes to bidding up the price of land , which homeowners like when selling, dislike when buying, and realtors always like since their commissioners are fatter even though their daily expenses are too (but who does t…
  - why: The essay describes how inflationary monetary policy channels surplus wealth into driving up land prices, enriching rentiers.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“The Fed Has to Know”**
  - sentence: …The Fed Has to Know If you want to know the worth of Earth, you must consider the price of land, not the rent for land.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative of institutional capture by arguing the Federal Reserve withholds critical land value data to protect banking interests.

### [Independence for Kosovo](https://www.progress.org/articles/independence-for-kosovo/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“historic Serbian churches”**
  - sentence: …There are historic Serbian churches in Kosovo.…
  - why: The essay uses the dispute over Serbian religious sites in Kosovo to illustrate the complexities of land justice and historical claims.
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“Confederation of Serbia and Kosovo”**
  - sentence: …This intertwining of ethnic Serbs and ethnic Albanians can be resolved by a Confederation of Serbia and Kosovo.…
  - why: The essay proposes a confederation model rather than simple nationalization or independence to resolve territorial disputes.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“Serbian and Roma refugees”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Serbian and Roma (Gypsy) refugees who fled from Kosovo after 1998 would be able to return to their home locations.…
  - why: The essay highlights the right of displaced individuals to return to their land as a component of justice.

### [Indigenous Rights](https://www.progress.org/articles/indigenous-rights/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“rights of original inhabitants”**
  - sentence: …The Declaration recognizes the rights of original inhabitants to self-determination, including rights to historic land.…
  - why: The essay details the UN Declaration and the struggle for indigenous land rights, providing the historical context for the intersection of Geoism and Indigenous justice.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“state governments originate in conquest”**
  - sentence: …The historian Franz Oppenheimer showed that state governments originate in conquest.…
  - why: The essay cites Oppenheimer to argue that modern state titles are rooted in the conquest and dispossession of native peoples, supporting the narrative that land titles descend from theft.
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“ownership of land is not a unitary absolute”**
  - sentence: …We should realize that the ownership of land is not a unitary absolute.…
  - why: The essay argues against the binary of state vs private ownership by decomposing land rights into a bundle, countering the idea that state ownership alone solves the land problem.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“rentals generated by public works”**
  - sentence: …The strands of the bundle include the rights of possession, rights to the natural rents, rights to the rentals generated by population and commerce, and rights to the rentals generated by public works and civic services.…
  - why: The essay explicitly decomposes land value into components created by population, commerce, and public services, supporting the claim that community activity creates land value.

### [Individual Sovereignty](https://www.progress.org/articles/individual-sovereignty/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“collecting the economic rent”**
  - sentence: …The premise of human equality implies that all persons have an equal share of the benefits of natural resources, and that can be accomplished by collecting the economic rent of land, its yield when put to optimal use, and distributing that …
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the collection and equal distribution of land rent as a necessary implication of human equality and individual sovereignty.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributing that rent equally”**
  - sentence: …The premise of human equality implies that all persons have an equal share of the benefits of natural resources, and that can be accomplished by collecting the economic rent of land, its yield when put to optimal use, and distributing that …
  - why: The essay argues that equal rights to natural resources require distributing the economic rent of land equally to all persons.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“generated by the local population”**
  - sentence: …The local site rentals, generated by the local population, commerce, and public goods, would be paid to the community’s providers of civic goods.…
  - why: The essay identifies local population, commerce, and public goods as the generators of site rentals, supporting the claim that community creates land value.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“provide the funding needed”**
  - sentence: …The multi-level federations of voluntary communities and associations would implement the collection of land rent and local rentals, and this geo-anarchism would provide the funding needed to implement the voluntary governance.…
  - why: The essay proposes that the collection of land rent is the specific mechanism capable of funding the operations of voluntary governance.

### [Inflation: Prices Up, Our Equilibrium Down](https://www.progress.org/articles/prices-up-our-equilibrium-down/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“value is created by the presence of society”**
  - sentence: …The value of land is not created by the lender or landowner.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value is socially generated, which is the foundational premise for capturing that value.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Society could use a tax, fee, lease”**
  - sentence: …Society could use a tax, fee, lease, dues, whatever, to recover that value of locations which it generates.…
  - why: The essay explicitly proposes recovering socially generated land value to fund public needs and reduce debt.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“That value is created by the presence of society”**
  - sentence: …However, none of that higher land value belongs in a mortgage.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative that location value is produced by the community rather than the owner.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“pay residents a dividend”**
  - sentence: …If society were to share its recovered land value among its members—pay residents a dividend ( Aspen, CO, does something similar , albeit substituting affordable housing for a payout)—then residents would be much better off.…
  - why: The essay advocates for distributing recovered land value as a dividend to improve welfare and reduce reliance on government programs.

### [Intervention Denial](https://www.progress.org/articles/intervention-denial/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“generation of land rent”**
  - sentence: …The biggest subsidy of all is completely ignored by intervention denial: the generation of land rent and land value by government’s public works and civic services, most of which is paid from taxes on wages, enterprise, and goods.…
  - why: The essay identifies the generation of land rent by public works as a massive subsidy that intervention deniers ignore, directly supporting the claim that public investment capitalizes into land value
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“public works and civic services”**
  - sentence: …The biggest subsidy of all is completely ignored by intervention denial: the generation of land rent and land value by government’s public works and civic services, most of which is paid from taxes on wages, enterprise, and goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that government services create land value, which aligns with the narrative that the community (via government) creates land value.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“getting government to do”**
  - sentence: …Thieves are greedy, and those who indirectly steal by getting government to do or protect their forced taking are also greedy.…
  - why: The essay characterizes using government to extract wealth as 'greedy' rent-seeking, supporting the claim that rent-seeking (capturing wealth via intervention rather than production) is economically d
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Intervention denial”**
  - sentence: …There is a meme, an infectious idea, that has spread like a mental plague among advocates of greater governmental intervention.…
  - why: The essay describes 'intervention denial' as a destructive mental plague that blocks effective solutions, illustrating the broader narrative that economic discourse is corrupted by false ideas about i

### [Is a Sales Tax Voluntary?](https://www.progress.org/articles/is-a-sales-tax-voluntary/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“taxing rent indirectly”**
  - sentence: …By taxing rent indirectly, the sales tax imposes a deadweight loss, whereas if the rent had been taxed directly, there is no deadweight loss, since land does not shrink, hide, or flee when taxed.…
  - why: The essay argues that sales taxes create deadweight loss by taxing rent indirectly, supporting the claim that shifting taxes to land avoids this loss.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxation of consumption attacks”**
  - sentence: …The taxation of consumption attacks the purpose of the economy.…
  - why: The essay critiques taxing consumption (sales tax) as harmful to the economy's purpose, implicitly supporting the narrative to tax land instead.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“tax on the gross revenue”**
  - sentence: …The sales tax is equivalent to a tax on the gross revenue of the seller.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes a sales tax (on gross revenue) from land value tax, countering the objection that LVT is just another form of existing taxation like property or sales taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“taxes should be on the consumption of land”**
  - sentence: …if consumption taxes there must be, logically the taxes should be on the consumption of land, valued by its rent.…
  - why: The essay concludes that if consumption must be taxed, it should be on land rent, aligning with the Single Tax narrative of shifting the tax base to land.

### [Is Jury Duty Involuntary Servitude?](https://www.progress.org/articles/is-jury-duty-involuntary-servitude/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxation of labor”**
  - sentence: …The taxation of labor, or its more direct taking as jury duty or a military draft, is said to make us free.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing labor or conscripting it for jury duty violates liberty, implicitly contrasting this with the Georgist principle of taxing land instead of labor.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Arbeit Macht Frei”**
  - sentence: …The Nazi concentration camp slogan, placed on the entrances, was “Arbeit Macht Frei” or “work makes one free.”…
  - why: The author uses the slogan 'work makes one free' to criticize state coercion over labor, countering the objection that land value tax is socialist by highlighting that state control over labor is the 
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“ruled by fiscal illusion”**
  - sentence: …We are ruled by fiscal illusion, and very few are protesting.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice critique by showing how the government hides the true cost of jury duty (an implicit tax) to avoid budgetary visibility, exploiting citizen ignorance.

### [Is Liberalism Dead?](https://www.progress.org/articles/is-liberalism-dead/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“enclosure movement in the United Kingdom”**
  - sentence: …The enclosure movement in the United Kingdom forced farmers off of the village farming commons and into crowded cities, where they became the proletariat compelled to work for low wages and poor conditions.…
  - why: The essay cites the enclosure movement as the historical origin of land monopoly and poverty, supporting the narrative that land titles derive from dispossession.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“subsidized their rent and land value”**
  - sentence: …Taxation hurt the poor the most, as the rich got their tax money back by public works that subsidized their rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that public works funded by taxation increase the wealth of the rich by boosting land values, illustrating the capitalization of public goods into land.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“ideologies of freedom”**
  - sentence: …We now just have libertarianism and geoism (Georgism) as ideologies of freedom, and even there, most libertarians today have a royalist position on land, and many geoists have an illiberal mercantilist position on free trade and monetary po…
  - why: The essay identifies Georgism (geoism) as one of the remaining ideologies of freedom, implicitly endorsing its role in the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“land was monopolized by the aristocracy”**
  - sentence: …Elsewhere the land was monopolized by the aristocracy, while mercantilist policies prevented free trade.…
  - why: The essay highlights the injustice of aristocratic land monopoly, which aligns with the narrative of land justice and the critique of unequal land distribution.

### [Is the US Election Rigged?](https://www.progress.org/articles/is-the-us-election-rigged/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“special-interest influence”**
  - sentence: …So long as we suffer from mass democracy, significant voter fraud will remain a possibility, along with demagogs, special-interest influence, and corruption.…
  - why: The essay's conclusion that mass democracy enables corruption and special-interest influence directly illustrates the public-choice critique that government failure is inevitable.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“In 1886 Henry George”**
  - sentence: …In 1886 Henry George campaigned for mayor of New York City, where he polled second, more than Republican candidate Theodore Roosevelt.…
  - why: The essay provides a historical anecdote about Henry George's 1886 mayoral campaign and the alleged fraud, serving as a specific illustration for the narrative about Georgism in the Progressive Era.

### [Is Undervalued Vacant Land just a Baltimore problem?](https://www.progress.org/articles/is-undervalued-vacant-land-just-a-baltimore-problem/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“Chronic under-valuation of vacant land”**
  - sentence: …Chronic under-valuation of vacant land is specific to Baltimore.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that systematic undervaluation is a localized, correctable error rather than a fundamental impossibility.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“simply require paying some additional attention”**
  - sentence: …The problems we identified are correctable, and simply require paying some additional attention to this issue.…
  - why: Shows that assessment errors are often due to data quality and methodology, not necessarily corruption or malice.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“misapplication of the “allocation” method”**
  - sentence: …Second, a particularly persistent cause of side-by-side discrepancies in otherwise identical vacant vs. improved lots, is a misapplication of the “allocation” method.…
  - why: Explains a technical flaw in standard property tax assessment that a pure land value tax would avoid.

### [Is US Inflation Too Low?](https://www.progress.org/articles/is-us-inflation-too-low/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“tax on value not created”**
  - sentence: …There is only one logical source of public revenue that does not impose a marginal cost: It is a tax on value not created by the actions of the taxpayer: the surplus value of natural resources, or land.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value is the only revenue source that does not impose marginal costs, supporting the claim that shifting taxes to land avoids efficiency losses.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“money into the stock market and real estate”**
  - sentence: …With little interest income from bonds, people instead put their money into the stock market and real estate.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how low interest rates drive capital into real estate, evidencing the claim that modern banking growth is tied to land assets.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“Austrian economist Hayek called a “fatal conceit””**
  - sentence: …The thought that a few officials can best decide on the rate of inflation and interest rates for the whole economy is what the Austrian economist Hayek called a “fatal conceit”.…
  - why: The essay invokes Hayek's critique of central planning to argue against monetary intervention, aligning with the Austrian objection to government economic management.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax on extra income, spending, and wealth”**
  - sentence: …The most important policy variable is the marginal tax rate, the tax on extra income, spending, and wealth.…
  - why: The essay advocates for zero marginal tax rates on labor and capital in favor of land value capture, directly supporting the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.

### [It’s Time to Call the Housing Crisis What It Really Is](https://www.progress.org/articles/its-time-to-call-the-housing-crisis-what-it-really-is-the-largest-transfer-of-wealth-in-living-memory/)
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“increases in house prices rather”**
  - sentence: …The Office for National Statistics explains that this is “largely due to increases in house prices rather than a change in the volume of dwellings.”…
  - why: Essay cites ONS data showing wealth growth is driven by land price inflation, not construction volume.
- **supports** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“takes the same households 20 years”**
  - sentence: …Whereas in the mid-1990s low and middle income households could afford a first time buyer deposit after saving for around 3 years, today it takes the same households 20 years to save for a deposit.…
  - why: Essay documents the drastic increase in time required to save for a deposit, locking out younger generations.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“driving force behind rising house prices”**
  - sentence: …The driving force behind rising house prices — and the UK’s growing wealth — has been rapidly escalating land prices.…
  - why: Essay explicitly argues that the housing crisis is fundamentally a crisis of rising land values.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“gained at the expense of others”**
  - sentence: …Far from the positive sum game capitalism is supposed to be, we have a system where most wealth is gained at the expense of others.…
  - why: Essay frames housing wealth as an unearned increment extracted from non-owners rather than created value.

### [Jeffery J. Smith’s Law of Prime Rent](https://www.progress.org/articles/jeffery-j-smiths-law-of-prime-rent/)
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“vacant lots are a massively”**
  - sentence: …Vacant lots are a massively accurate indicator of unemployment, poverty, and crime.…
  - why: The essay argues that vacant lots held by speculators or government cause unemployment and poverty, evidencing the claim that land underuse wastes cities.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“producers must use sub-prime locations”**
  - sentence: …Then producers must use sub-prime locations, where people can not make the most money possible.…
  - why: The essay explains that withholding prime land forces producers to use less productive locations, driving down wages and suppressing productivity.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“predict the business cycle”**
  - sentence: …And you can’t predict the business cycle while remaining antagonistic toward Ricardo’s Law of Rent.…
  - why: The essay asserts that ignoring Ricardo's Law of Rent (and by extension land speculation dynamics) renders economists unable to predict economic cycles.
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“vast majority now lives in cities”**
  - sentence: …And marketing wise, it matters because a vast majority now lives in cities and simply can not fathom that bad farmland could have anything to do with their paltry paychecks.…
  - why: The essay counters the idea that land is irrelevant in modern economies by arguing that urban land dynamics (Prime Rent) directly determine city wages.

### [Judges Impose Education Monopoly](https://www.progress.org/articles/judges-impose-education-monopoly/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“enforce the dominance of the state”**
  - sentence: …It is bad enough that parents who home school must pay taxes to educate other people’s children while bearing the full expense of their home schools, but to have to get a credential like a government-school teacher has no justification othe…
  - why: The essay exemplifies public-choice theory by arguing that the state enforces credentialing monopolies to benefit the teachers' union rather than students.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“government schools in the U.S.”**
  - sentence: …Government schools in the U.S. have failed to properly educate children.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that government intervention is necessary for quality, arguing that government schools fail while home schooling succeeds.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“parents have a natural right”**
  - sentence: …Parents have a natural right to educate their children in their culture so that their ancestral language and heritage may be perpetuated.…
  - why: The essay champions individual natural rights against state homogenization, countering the charge that Georgist-style reforms are socialist collectivism.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“no justification other than”**
  - sentence: …It is bad enough that parents who home school must pay taxes to educate other people’s children while bearing the full expense of their home schools, but to have to get a credential like a government-school teacher has no justification othe…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that state power is used to protect special interests (teachers' unions) at the expense of the public good.

### [Just Offset Everything](https://www.progress.org/articles/just-offset-everything/)
- **counters** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“we don’t need pollution levies”**
  - sentence: …Now we have environmental indulgences, so that we don’t need pollution levies to reduce emissions.…
  - why: The essay satirizes the idea that voluntary offsets can replace mandatory pollution levies, implicitly arguing that direct pricing is necessary.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“buying their way out with offsets”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …We don’t have to stop polluting and plundering the planet if the perpetrators are able to buy their way out with offsets.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the failure of market mechanisms to protect the commons when polluters can simply pay to continue environmental damage.

### [Just Put It On a Map](https://www.progress.org/articles/just-put-it-on-a-map/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“vacant land analysis feature”**
  - sentence: …We’ve significantly improved the vacant land analysis feature, which should be available for every city on the site.…
  - why: The essay describes a tool that visualizes vacant lots and underused land to show development potential going to waste.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“towering fortress of land value”**
  - sentence: …Here we see a less extreme ratio, but there’s still an unmistakable “towering fortress of land value” in the center of the city…
  - why: The essay provides visual evidence that land values are exponentially concentrated in city centers, driving the housing crisis.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“closer you get to the city center”**
  - sentence: …the same basic pattern persists—the closer you get to the city center, the heart of economic activity, the more land values go exponential.…
  - why: The essay maps land value gradients that peak at the heart of economic activity, illustrating that community proximity creates value.

### [Justice—Coming to a Nation Near You](https://www.progress.org/articles/justice-coming-to-a-nation-near-you/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Citizen’s Dividend, a la Alaska’s”**
  - sentence: …Plus, people need to feel more self-worth, equal to all others, so they feel OK getting an extra income apart from their wages, a rent-share, a Citizen’s Dividend, a la Alaska’s oil dividend.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a rent-share funded by location values, citing Alaska's oil dividend as the model.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“All of us make it valuable”**
  - sentence: …Nobody made a location. All of us make it valuable, since all of us create population density.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value arises from population density created by the community, not the individual owner.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“owner has no more right”**
  - sentence: …In truth, however, an owner has no more right to the rent for “hers” location than does “hers” neighbors.…
  - why: The essay disputes the moral right of landowners to capture location rent, framing it as a common surplus.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“entitled to an equal share”**
  - sentence: …How do we get people to see that they owe rent for the land they claim and are entitled to an equal share of the rent for all the land in their region?…
  - why: The essay proposes that residents are entitled to an equal share of regional land rent, which is the core mechanism of rent dividends.

### [Killing Dolphins and Killing People](https://www.progress.org/articles/killing-dolphins-and-killing-people/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“trained to enforce the law”**
  - sentence: …I understood that had been trained to enforce the law by intimidation, to treat all citizens as enemies and prey.…
  - why: The essay provides a personal anecdote of police intimidation to illustrate the broader claim that state enforcement is inherently violent and corrupt.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“violent cultures have conquered”**
  - sentence: …Perhaps it is a result of thousands of years of cultural evolution, where violent cultures have conquered and massacred peaceful cultures and therefore have become dominant.…
  - why: The essay supports the historical narrative that modern civilization is built on the conquest and dispossession of peaceful peoples.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“No government on earth fully respects”**
  - sentence: …No government on earth fully respects human self-ownership.…
  - why: The essay argues that systemic state violence and lack of self-ownership are global issues, supporting the narrative that justice requires addressing foundational state power.

### [Kinetic Rent and Potential Rent](https://www.progress.org/articles/kinetic-rent-and-potential-rent/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“estimate the total amount”**
  - sentence: …If we seek to estimate the total amount of rent that could be collected for public revenue, we need to include both the potential and the kinetic rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that total collectible rent includes both visible flows and suppressed 'potential' rent, expanding estimates of funding capacity.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“Henry George theorem”**
  - sentence: …There is a theory in public finance called the “Henry George theorem,” which concludes that the cost of the optimal provision of public goods equals the economy’s land rent.…
  - why: The essay invokes the Henry George Theorem to argue that optimal public goods are financed by land rent, contrasting this with the inefficiency of taxing wages or profits.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“removal of stifling taxation”**
  - sentence: …The removal of stifling taxation would convert potential rent into kinetic rent.…
  - why: The essay explains that taxes on production create deadweight loss by converting kinetic rent into potential rent, implying that shifting taxes to land recovers this lost value.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“kinetic rent is no longer”**
  - sentence: …The kinetic rent is no longer there, but has the potential of coming back if the tax is removed.…
  - why: The essay counters the objection that rent is too small by arguing that measured rent is only 'kinetic' and that 'potential' rent—currently suppressed by bad taxation—significantly increases the true 

### [KPFA-FM Radio Interview](https://www.progress.org/articles/kpfa-fm-radio-interview/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“bring more wealth”**
  - sentence: …For more information about how land reform can create meaningful work, restore our ecology, and bring more wealth into our local communities, I invite you to read my book Land: A New Paradigm for a Thriving World.…
  - why: The text posits that land reform can increase community wealth, supporting the narrative that land value is socially created and can be harnessed for local benefit.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“restore our ecology”**
  - sentence: …For more information about how land reform can create meaningful work, restore our ecology, and bring more wealth into our local communities, I invite you to read my book Land: A New Paradigm for a Thriving World.…
  - why: The text explicitly links land reform to ecological restoration, aligning with the Green Georgism narrative of charging for natural resources to protect the environment.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“create meaningful work”**
  - sentence: …For more information about how land reform can create meaningful work, restore our ecology, and bring more wealth into our local communities, I invite you to read my book Land: A New Paradigm for a Thriving World.…
  - why: The promise that land reform can create meaningful work supports the Single Tax narrative that shifting taxes off labor and onto land improves economic opportunity.

### [Labor of Love](https://www.progress.org/articles/labor-of-love/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“natural resources that we all”**
  - sentence: …Sharing the artificial resources openly and the natural resources that we all have as a birthright is a step towards equity and justice in labor.…
  - why: The essay frames natural resources as a common birthright, supporting the narrative that land and resource theft underpins current inequality.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“no one builds anything without”**
  - sentence: …Once realized that we are all on the same team, that no one builds anything without the help of nature and neighbors, that we build upon the work of others in one way or another...…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that individual wealth creation depends on community and nature, the core thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“amass a fortune of hundreds of millions”**
  - sentence: …Eugene Debs, one of the leaders of the labor movement in America, opposed 'a social order in which it is possible for one man who does absolutely nothing that is useful to amass a fortune of hundreds of millions of dollars, while millions o…
  - why: The quote highlights the economic distortion where capturing existing wealth (rent-seeking) outperforms productive labor, supporting the claim that this drags broader welfare.
