- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“excessive wealth at the expense”**
  - sentence: …Working continuously for food, water, healthcare, and shelter when we have the technology and resources to give everyone a better life amounts to economic slavery so a few can live with excessive wealth at the expense of most that live with…
  - why: The essay describes an economy where a few live off the labor of many, illustrating the dynamics of a rentier economy.

### [Labor-saving Robots?](https://www.progress.org/articles/labor-saving-robots/)
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“What keeps labor, land, and capital”**
  - sentence: …What keeps labor, land, and capital from productively coming together, to put every unemployed worker to work and create a prosperous society?…
  - why: The essay frames the central economic mystery as the failure of labor, land, and capital to combine productively due to artificial land scarcity.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“highly valuable land, amply provisioned”**
  - sentence: …We look around in every city and see patches of highly valuable land, amply provisioned with services and infrastructure, sitting idle, or only being put to a fraction of its potential use.…
  - why: The essay explicitly observes that valuable urban land sits idle or underused, directly evidencing the claim about speculative vacancy.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“Yet there is no shortage of land”**
  - sentence: …Yet there is no shortage of land, neither!…
  - why: The essay dismisses the idea that physical land scarcity causes unemployment, countering the Malthusian view that overpopulation or lack of resources is the problem.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“no place for him to work”**
  - sentence: …Why, then, can he not work for himself? Because there's no place for him to work or nothing for him to do.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the rentier economy by describing a scenario where access to land, not lack of labor or capital, prevents people from working.

  - sentence: …For more information about how land reform can create meaningful work, restore our ecology, and bring more wealth into our local communities, I invite you to read my book Land: A New Paradigm for a Thriving World .…

### [Land and Labor in a World of Robots](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-and-labor-in-a-world-of-robots/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed to all the people”**
  - sentence: …The more equitable way to solve the problem is for all the land rent to be distributed to all the people in equal shares.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that distributing land rent equally is the equitable solution to potential displacement by robots.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“flows down to the non-labor factor”**
  - sentence: …But since labor would not get this surplus, and it is not going to the owners of firms and capital goods, it flows down to the non-labor factor, land.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the economic mechanism where surplus value is captured by landowners rather than producers or laborers.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“rent of land would soak up”**
  - sentence: …With lower labor costs, the producer surplus would be that much greater, and so the rent of land would soak up the gains from economic expansion and more productive technology.…
  - why: The essay argues that technological gains ultimately accrue as land rent, implying this rent is a substantial potential revenue source.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“beneficiaries of the displacement”**
  - sentence: …The beneficiaries of the displacement of labor would be either those who have title to land, or to the people in general if the rent is distributed to them.…
  - why: The essay describes a scenario where the primary economic beneficiaries are landowners, characterizing a rentier outcome.

### [Land and Liberty to Build: On Georgism and YIMBYism](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-and-liberty-to-build-on-georgism-and-yimbyism/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“both upzoning and land value tax”**
  - sentence: …In this article, I will argue that both upzoning and land value tax are absolutely necessary if we want to fix our cities.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues against the objection that LVT is useless without zoning, contending instead that they are complementary and necessary together.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“nudge developers back into the business of building”**
  - sentence: …Even better, by placing a price on land banking, it will nudge developers back into the business of building.…
  - why: The essay argues that LVT discourages speculative land banking and delays in construction, supporting the claim that it dampens speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“force cities to sprawl outwards”**
  - sentence: …These force cities to sprawl outwards, undermining the viability of public transit and increasing carbon emissions through car-centric commutes and less energy-efficient dwellings.…
  - why: The essay links zoning regulations to urban sprawl and argues that upzoning (supported by Georgism) enables densification, aligning with the anti-sprawl benefit.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“prevents workers from moving to places”**
  - sentence: …Zoning prevents workers from moving to places where their labor will be more productive and more highly paid.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how high land costs (enforced by zoning) misallocate labor and suppress productivity, vindicating the Georgist claim.

### [Land in Ancient Israel and Early Zionism](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-in-ancient-israel-and-early-zionism/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“appropriating rent to the common benefit”**
  - sentence: …George said “Everything could go on as now,” but that all that needed to be changed was for “ the common right to land to be fully recognized by appropriating rent to the common benefit,” through, as it became known, a land value tax.…
  - why: The essay uses Henry George's definition of the single tax to illustrate the economic philosophy adopted by early Zionists.
- **counters** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“ethnocentric moral dilemma”**
  - sentence: …In retrospect, there is also an ethnocentric moral dilemma in the narrative of both ancient and modern Israel.…
  - why: The essay critiques the exclusionary nature of ancient land distribution and modern Zionism, countering a purely positive narrative of land justice by highlighting the dispossession of prior inhabitan
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“violent acquisition of land”**
  - sentence: …“ Manifest Destiny ”, for example, is founded upon the violent acquisition of land.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that modern land titles often originate in conquest and dispossession, citing Manifest Destiny as a parallel to the Israeli context.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value which is the creation of the community”**
  - sentence: …It is the taking by the community, for the use of the community, of that value which is the creation of the community.…
  - why: The essay quotes Henry George's argument that land value is socially created, which serves as the theoretical basis for the land policies advocated by early Zionists.

### [Land In Thomas Paine’s Lifetime](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-in-thomas-paines-lifetime/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“claiming the land of others”**
  - sentence: …Yet claiming the land of others is the main driver in history.…
  - why: The essay frames historical enclosure and colonization as theft, illustrating the narrative that land titles derive from dispossession.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“inflated the price of land”**
  - sentence: …They not only paid themselves hefty salaries and stock dividends, they also printed so much currency, it inflated the price of land, which made speculators even wealthier.…
  - why: The essay argues that currency expansion fueled land speculation and inflation, supporting the claim that speculation drives economic cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“pay the collected funds as dividends”**
  - sentence: …Our public trustees would then pay the collected funds as dividends to citizens, so all could flourish.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for recovering land value to fund a citizen dividend, directly supporting this narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“Land taxation deters land speculation”**
  - sentence: …Land taxation deters land speculation, removing the motive to become a landlord or absentee owner.…
  - why: The essay states that taxing land removes the incentive for speculation, directly supporting the benefit of LVT dampening speculation.

### [Land Is More Important than Money](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-is-more-important-than-money/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“real estate is, by far”**
  - sentence: …The role of real estate in today’s banking and financial system is acknowledged by just about everyone, but its true extent is often downplayed.…
  - why: The essay argues that real estate is the primary collateral for loans, driving the financial system's expansion.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Fractional reserve banking is not”**
  - sentence: …Fractional reserve banking is not inherently problematic.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the argument that the banking system or credit creation is the fundamental cause of economic instability.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“the private ownership of land”**
  - sentence: …Fractional reserve banking is not inherently problematic. And, the abuses and predations of today’s banking system, real as they are, are not as important, as fundamental, or as damaging as the private ownership of land.…
  - why: The essay identifies private land ownership as the root cause of economic predation, more damaging than financial sector abuses.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“create rent for landowners”**
  - sentence: …Georgists know all about this, of course; they know that as long as land is subject to private ownership (and production is taxed to create the public services that, in turn, create rent for landowners) the market for real estate is replete…
  - why: The essay explains that public services funded by production taxes increase land value, creating market failure.

### [Land Rent as a Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-rent-as-a-tax/)
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“portion of household income”**
  - sentence: …As land values have risen to match previous highs, rent has risen also, and the portion of household income going to housing has risen.…
  - why: The essay cites data showing rising rent consumes a larger share of income, driving poverty.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“speculative rise in land values”**
  - sentence: …This rent increase due to the speculative rise in land values is a tax, as the higher rental is not matched by greater locational services.…
  - why: The essay argues that speculation drives up rents and causes boom-bust cycles.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“implicit subsidy to landowners”**
  - sentence: …The higher rent generated by public works is an implicit subsidy to landowners, which induces even greater land speculation.…
  - why: The essay explains that public works funded by labor taxes increase land rents, subsidizing owners.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“collecting the land rent”**
  - sentence: …The only way to avoid rent as taxation is to avoid the excessive land speculation by collecting the land rent and distributing it as either cash or public goods.…
  - why: The essay concludes that capturing land rent is the solution to prevent rent from acting as a tax.

### [Land Value Reparations for South Africa](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-value-reparations-for-south-africa/)
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“cost of LVT falls only on landowners”**
  - sentence: …As such, the cost of LVT falls only on landowners.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the tax burden cannot be shifted to tenants, citing the inelasticity of supply.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“speculation creates property cycles”**
  - sentence: …This speculation creates property cycles and housing bubbles which LVT smoothes, improving housing market and macroeconomic stability.…
  - why: The essay links land speculation to economic instability and argues that LVT mitigates this by smoothing cycles.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“market value of land cannot be determined”**
  - sentence: …So why don’t we have LVT? Is it because the market value of land cannot be determined separately from the value of the improvements?…
  - why: The essay raises the common objection that land cannot be valued separately from improvements, then refutes it with historical evidence.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“best form of land reparations”**
  - sentence: …Why LVT is the best form of land reparations…
  - why: The essay uses the South African context to illustrate how LVT can serve as a mechanism for restorative justice and land reparations.

### [Land value return is needed, pragmatic, and achievable](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-value-return-is-needed-pragmatic-and-achievable/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“we want more building”**
  - sentence: …What you’re doing there is by decreasing the rate on improvements and increasing the rate on land, you’re saying we want to tax less buildings and we want to tax more land.…
  - why: The speaker explains the mechanism of split-rate taxation—shifting the tax burden from improvements to land—to encourage development and discourage speculation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“land is a special”**
  - sentence: …But as we know, land is a special and uniquely different type of tax.…
  - why: The speaker distinguishes land value taxation from standard property taxes to argue for shifting the tax basis, directly countering the objection that they are effectively the same.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“six states actively considering”**
  - sentence: …There are six states actively considering legislation for land value tax.…
  - why: The speaker cites recent legislative momentum to counter the narrative that LVT is politically impossible or unachievable.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“shift to a full land value tax overnight”**
  - sentence: …We don’t want to shift to a full land value tax overnight because it would destroy our economic system.…
  - why: The speaker acknowledges the economic risks of an immediate transition, validating the objection regarding financial system disruption while advocating for incremental change instead.

### [Land value tax in online games and virtual worlds: A how-to guide](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-value-tax-in-online-games-and-virtual-worlds-a-how-to-guide/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“acquire and hold land not because”**
  - sentence: …Speculation : People acquire and hold land not because they intend to use it, but because they hope that its value will go up over time…
  - why: The essay provides virtual-world examples of speculation and vacancy that mirror real-world inefficiencies.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“make it expensive to simply buy”**
  - sentence: …By placing a heavy tax on the land asset, you make it expensive to simply buy land and hold it for no reason, or for speculative reasons…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing land value specifically discourages holding land for speculative gain.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“Incentivizes adding profitable improvements”**
  - sentence: …Incentivizes adding profitable improvements to land Players should only want to buy land if they plan to build somet…
  - why: The essay explains that untaxing improvements while taxing land encourages development.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“board game you know today as Monopoly”**
  - sentence: …As game designers, we want a blueprint for combating unwanted speculation; ideally one that we can actually communicate to engineers and put in a functioning game.…
  - why: The essay references Monopoly's origin as a Georgist game to frame the discussion of land value taxation.

### [Land Worth = More than Enough](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-worth-more-than-enough/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“value of all land”**
  - sentence: …It's interesting that if you work it out, the value of all land in the U.S. is just about equal to its GDP.…
  - why: The essay provides estimates of total U.S. land value ($11 trillion+) to argue it is sufficient to replace other taxes.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Interest on mortgage loans”**
  - sentence: …Interest on mortgage loans is the new land rent, and banks are, with the exception of rentiers like Donald Trump, the new landowners.…
  - why: The essay argues that modern banking wealth is derived from land rent via mortgage interest.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“public whose demand created”**
  - sentence: …A much fairer system of taxation would be to collect the value of the land for the public good, since it was the public whose demand created the value in the first place.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that land value is created by public demand, justifying public collection.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“get more buildings”**
  - sentence: …We would get more buildings, with just a land-value tax, and more efficient use of the land too.…
  - why: The essay claims that taxing only land value (rather than buildings) would result in increased construction.

### [Land-Value Commissions](https://www.progress.org/articles/land-value-commissions/)
- **illustrates** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“capitalized down as a lower purchase price”**
  - sentence: …The land-value commission would be capitalized down as a lower purchase price.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates the economic principle that a recurring charge on land value is borne by the seller through price capitalization, not shifted to the buyer.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“no commission penalty to improving the property”**
  - sentence: …Those familiar with tapping site values for public revenue will also recognize that with LVC, there would be no commission penalty to improving the property.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting fees to an annual land-value basis removes the disincentive to improve property, a core benefit of land value taxation.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“retain an interest in maintaining a good local environment”**
  - sentence: …The broker would share the increased value of the land, and so would retain an interest in maintaining a good local environment.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how aligning compensation with land value incentivizes stakeholders to support the community investments that generate that value.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“saving interest payments”**
  - sentence: …Land-value commission have the same benefit as land-value public finance in saving interest payments, as the borrower pays the land-value charge rather than mortgage interest.…
  - why: The essay highlights the efficiency gain of substituting a land-based charge for debt-financed lump sums, reducing total interest costs.

### [Land-Value Tax and the Timing of Development](https://www.progress.org/articles/lvt-and-the-timing-of-development/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-causes-overdevelopment`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-causes-overdevelopment/) — anchor: **“LVT has the opposite effect”**
  - sentence: …But LVT has the opposite effect: it promotes an efficient use of land.…
  - why: The essay directly addresses and refutes the objection that LVT forces ecologically valuable land into development.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“LVT promotes more compact cities”**
  - sentence: …As LVT promotes more compact cities, there is less sprawl, so while there may be more overall development, there is also more land for farming and wildlife.…
  - why: The essay argues that LVT reduces sprawl by eliminating subsidies to horizontal development and penalties for vertical infill.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“vertical development is tax-penalized”**
  - sentence: …Land use today is inefficient because vertical development is tax-penalized while horizontal development is subsidized.…
  - why: The essay explains how current tax structures discourage construction and how LVT corrects this inefficiency.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“Landowners often keep their plots underused”**
  - sentence: …Landowners often keep their plots underused when land values are rising, since they hold an appreciating asset that has a low carrying cost, paying little in property taxes.…
  - why: The essay describes how low carrying costs encourage speculation and underuse, which LVT prevents.

### [Landscape 6 – New Year, New Legislative Sessions and Launching the CLE Board](https://www.progress.org/articles/landscape-6-new-year-new-legislative-sessions-and-launching-the-cle-board/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“efficient land use”**
  - sentence: …The bill’s own statement of purpose emphasizes “efficient land use, expanding housing supply, reducing blight/vacancy, and shifting the tax burden away from building investment.”…
  - why: The New Hampshire bill's stated purpose explicitly links the tax shift to expanding housing supply and reducing vacancy, evidencing the claim.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“shifting the tax burden”**
  - sentence: …The bill’s own statement of purpose emphasizes “efficient land use, expanding housing supply, reducing blight/vacancy, and shifting the tax burden away from building investment.”…
  - why: The legislative intent to shift taxes off buildings to encourage efficient land use directly supports the mechanism by which LVT reduces sprawl.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“land value tax legislation”**
  - sentence: …This is the perfect moment for this launch, with land value tax legislation being actively considered across the country.…
  - why: The essay documents active legislative efforts in multiple states (MD, NH, NY, MN, WA, MI), countering the objection that LVT has not been adopted or is not gaining traction.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“shifting the tax burden”**
  - sentence: …The bill’s own statement of purpose emphasizes “efficient land use, expanding housing supply, reducing blight/vacancy, and shifting the tax burden away from building investment.”…
  - why: Shifting the tax burden away from building investment (capital) onto land supports the progressive argument that LVT improves equity by taxing unearned wealth rather than productive improvement.

### [Launching CivicMapper: Visualizing Land Values in 3D](https://www.progress.org/articles/launching-civicmapper-visualizing-land-values-in-3d/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“vacant and underutilized parcels”**
  - sentence: …CivicMapper also includes a second set of maps that highlight vacant and underutilized parcels.…
  - why: The essay uses CivicMapper to visualize and quantify the extent of vacant and underutilized land in cities like Spokane, evidencing the claim that valuable land sits idle.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“less attractive as a long-term speculative asset”**
  - sentence: …It makes extremely valuable underutilized land less attractive as a long-term speculative asset, encouraging speculators to either build on their land now, or sell it to someone who will.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value taxation removes the incentive to hold land idle for speculation, supporting the claim that LVT dampens speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“remove barriers that prevent supply”**
  - sentence: …What tax policy can do is remove barriers that prevent supply from responding to genuine demand.…
  - why: The essay argues that LVT eliminates the penalty for building, thereby encouraging supply to meet demand, which supports the claim that LVT can improve housing affordability.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“assessor’s land valuations”**
  - sentence: …There is one caveat to this work: we are simply visualizing existing official data, which is to say assessor’s land valuations, and there can be considerable variation in accuracy and consistency between different jurisdictions.…
  - why: By acknowledging the existence and variability of assessor's land valuations—and using them to build a functional tool—the essay implicitly counters the objection that land value cannot be assessed.

### [Learned Libertarians Lean Toward Land Rents](https://www.progress.org/articles/learned-libertarians-lean-toward-land-dues/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“Each October, everyone in the state gets a check”**
  - sentence: …Each October, everyone in the state gets a check from the Heritage Fund.…
  - why: The essay cites Alaska's oil dividend as a successful, durable example of rent distribution.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“an extra income apart from their labor”**
  - sentence: …Those Founding Fathers also identified the proper source—the value of resources and locations, of land in general.…
  - why: The essay argues for a citizen's dividend funded by land/resource rents as a right.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“The mere presence of a populace generates the value”**
  - sentence: …The mere presence of a populace generates the value of the locations within the region.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that land value is generated by the community/population.

### [Legal Tender and Illegal Toughness](https://www.progress.org/articles/legal-tender-and-illegal-toughness/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“plain logic does not apply”**
  - sentence: …But then, I'm not an attorney, so perhaps there are legal reasons why plain logic does not apply to federal law.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative of systemic corruption by describing federal agents seizing property and suppressing alternative currencies under logically inconsistent pretexts.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“federal government was more scared”**
  - sentence: …It could be that the federal government was more scared of the Ron Paul dollars than of the other private currency.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies the public-choice critique by showing government agents acting to protect their own power and monopoly on money rather than serving the public interest.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“customers along with the firm”**
  - sentence: …The customers along with the firm have been stripped of property.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the theme of state dispossession by detailing the seizure of gold, silver, and records from the Liberty Dollar firm and its customers.

### [Let the Mandaeans Come In](https://www.progress.org/articles/let-the-mandaeans-come-in/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“property has been stolen”**
  - sentence: …Their property has been stolen and destroyed, their women raped, and the people murdered.…
  - why: Illustrates the violent dispossession of land and property faced by minority groups, relevant to discussions of land justice.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“property has been stolen”**
  - sentence: …Their property has been stolen and destroyed, their women raped, and the people murdered.…
  - why: Provides a modern example of land theft and destruction, supporting the narrative that land titles are often maintained by force.

### [Let's Put the Unemployed to Work!](https://www.progress.org/articles/lets-put-the-unemployed-to-work/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“maximize employment by untaxing labor”**
  - sentence: …We can maximize employment by untaxing labor and shifting the public financing to land rent or land values.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes from labor to land value increases employment incentives and productivity.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“pushes landowners to use their land productively”**
  - sentence: …Taxing land value pushes landowners to use their land productively, while untaxing labor and enterprise pulls up wages as producers and investors have the full incentive to expand.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that taxing land value forces productive use of land, countering speculation.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“eliminate the barriers that governments have placed”**
  - sentence: …The best policy is to eliminate the barriers that governments have placed against employment.…
  - why: The essay identifies taxes on labor and minimum wages as government-imposed barriers that reduce employment and economic efficiency.
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“tremendous amount of work that needs to be done”**
  - sentence: …There is a tremendous amount of work that needs to be done to repair and maintain infrastructure, reduce environmental damage, and enhance public safety.…
  - why: The essay lists infrastructure and public safety projects that, if undertaken, would generate public value and likely increase nearby land values.

### [Letter From a Young Distributist](https://www.progress.org/articles/letter-from-a-young-distributist/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“taxation and equal distribution”**
  - sentence: …In response, George formulated an economic system based on the taxation and equal distribution of land and natural resources, alongside the abolition of taxes on labor and capital- a virtual common ownership of the Earth.…
  - why: The essay explicitly defines George's system as the taxation and equal distribution of land, which is the core definition of the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“citizen’s dividend”**
  - sentence: …Accordingly, widespread ownership of productive property can be readily accomplished through a Georgist land value tax and citizen’s dividend.…
  - why: The essay argues that a citizen's dividend redistributes wealth to the poor and capital-less, directly supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“incentivized to sell it”**
  - sentence: …the people who currently own land would be incentivized to sell it because of the continual draw on their accumulated capital.…
  - why: The essay explains that the tax creates a cost that incentivizes owners to sell land rather than hold it, which supports the mechanism by which LVT dampens speculation.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“monopolization less apparent”**
  - sentence: …In Europe, the frontier was nonexistent, a circumstance which made the economic effect of land monopolization less apparent but made inequality between a poor laborer and the landed gentry much more salient.…
  - why: The essay highlights the historical inequality caused by land monopolization and the landed gentry, which aligns with the narrative that land titles descend from enclosure and conquest.

### [Letter to the California State Legislature](https://www.progress.org/articles/letter-to-the-california-state-legislature/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“ease the crushing burden”**
  - sentence: …By penalizing land speculation and putting a price on exclusionary zoning, LVT can spur the construction of much-needed housing and more efficient land use, which will ensure that houses get built in exactly the locations where they are mos…
  - why: The essay argues LVT spurs construction and efficient land use to lower housing costs.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“LVT does not raise rents”**
  - sentence: …LVT does not raise rents, it cannot be passed on to tenants…
  - why: The essay explicitly asserts that LVT cannot be passed on to tenants.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“LVT discourages land speculation”**
  - sentence: …LVT discourages land speculation and lowers vacancy rates , increases the rate of housing construction , ultimately lowering housing rents .…
  - why: The essay states that LVT discourages land speculation and lowers vacancy rates.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“land value is not created”**
  - sentence: …land value is not created by the land owner, but rather by nature (desirable location, amenities), government (guaranteeing property rights, security, infrastructure investment) and society at large…
  - why: The essay explains that land value is generated by government and society, justifying public capture.

### [Liberalism Ascending](https://www.progress.org/articles/liberalism-ascending/)
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“welfare-state-liberal government typically reacts”**
  - sentence: …As the real estate bubble crumbles and the financial system cracks, there will be greater government controls over those industries, as welfare-state-liberal government typically reacts to control symptoms rather than eliminate causes.…
  - why: The essay argues that government intervention in the housing crisis is a reaction to symptoms rather than a cure for the underlying cycle, countering the idea that government can effectively tame or p
- **illustrates** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“The spirit of the times is green”**
  - sentence: …Ascending liberalism will greatly accelerate movement on climate change.…
  - why: The essay describes a political shift towards environmentalism ('the spirit of the times is green') as part of a new liberal era, illustrating the recurring belief that structural change has ended old
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“control symptoms rather than eliminate causes”**
  - sentence: …As the real estate bubble crumbles and the financial system cracks, there will be greater government controls over those industries, as welfare-state-liberal government typically reacts to control symptoms rather than eliminate causes.…
  - why: The essay criticizes the liberal response to the housing collapse for merely controlling symptoms (regulation) rather than eliminating causes (land speculation), implicitly supporting the Georgist vie
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“infused with Austrian-school Misesian understanding”**
  - sentence: …You have to not only be an economist but one infused with Austrian-school Misesian understanding to appreciate the need for genuine markets to perform economic calculation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly invokes Austrian-school economics (Mises, Hayek) to argue against central planning in medicine, supporting the Austrian critique of Georgist or interventionist policies.

### [Life at the Margin of Production](https://www.progress.org/articles/life-at-the-margin-of-production/)
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“cause of and the solution to poverty”**
  - sentence: …Once one fully grasps these simple economic laws, both the cause of and the solution to poverty in the midst of plenty becomes obvious.…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the law of rent as the key to understanding modern inequality and poverty.
- **illustrates** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“margin of production is mostly visibly”**
  - sentence: …In this case, the margin of production is mostly visibly expressed in the throwing up of great swathes of makeshift dwellings on the outskirts of growing cities in the developing world.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how high urban rents force workers to the margin (slums), suppressing their productivity and welfare.
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“government investment in highways”**
  - sentence: …With significant levels of government investment in highways and access to loans with which to purchase a suburban dwelling, an entire class of people can travel into the city within an hour...…
  - why: The essay describes how government highway spending shifted the margin of production, capitalizing value into suburban land.
- **illustrates** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“unable to afford to rent a location”**
  - sentence: …A remarkable example of life at the margin of production emerges, then, when we observe workers taking advantage of a commute even though they are unable to afford to rent a location either in the city or the suburbs.…
  - why: The essay uses the example of workers living in cars to illustrate that housing unaffordability is a function of location costs.

### [Liquified Tax Dollars](https://www.progress.org/articles/liquified-tax-dollars/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“economists winkingly call “rent seeking””** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …This is an example of what economists winkingly call “rent seeking.”…
  - why: The essay uses the coal subsidy bill as a concrete case study of rent-seeking behavior.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“avoidance of subsidies would require”**
  - sentence: …The avoidance of subsidies would require all polluters to compensate society for the damages.…
  - why: The essay argues for pollution charges over subsidies to address emissions and global warming.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“green tax shift that would simultaneously”**
  - sentence: …The revenues from pollution levies should be a green tax shift that would simultaneously reduce taxes on wages, profits, and goods.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting taxes onto pollution and land rent as an environmental protection strategy.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“shifting public revenues to land rent”**
  - sentence: …A complete green tax shift would eliminate the waste of resources caused by taxes by also shifting public revenues to land rent or land values.…
  - why: The essay promotes capturing land rent as a way to eliminate waste and improve resource efficiency.

### [Macroeconomics in One Lesson](https://www.progress.org/articles/macroeconomics-in-one-lesson/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“unsustainable investment in the higher-order”**
  - sentence: …But when a monetary authority expands the money supply, pushing the interest rate below its natural rate, this induces unsustainable investment in the higher-order capital goods.…
  - why: The essay details the Austrian Business Cycle Theory mechanism where credit expansion fuels land speculation and subsequent recession.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Infrastructure and civic services increase”**
  - sentence: …Infrastructure and civic services increase rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that public spending and services raise land values, capitalizing public goods into private rents.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“taxing it has no deadweight loss”**
  - sentence: …Land is fixed in supply, so taxing it has no deadweight loss.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes to land eliminates the excess burden and inefficiency caused by taxing labor and capital.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“use land rent or land value for public revenue”**
  - sentence: …First, use land rent or land value for public revenue, along with user fees and pollution levies.…
  - why: The essay concludes by advocating for the Georgist policy of using land value for public revenue to achieve a prosperous and efficient economy.

### [Majoritarianism](https://www.progress.org/articles/majoritarianism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“absence of taxes on transactions”**
  - sentence: …The avoidance of imposed costs implies the absence of taxes on transactions and produced goods.…
  - why: The essay advocates for removing taxes on labor and trade, which is the core revenue-replacement argument of the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“absence of taxes on labor”**
  - sentence: …The avoidance of imposed costs implies the absence of taxes on labor, capital, and trade, those who hold title to land would have to pay for civic services from the yield of their land, the rent.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for shifting the tax burden off labor and onto land rent to fund civic services.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“equal benefits from the rent”**
  - sentence: …Ideally, people would understand the logic of equal benefits from the rent generated by nature and community.…
  - why: The essay references the Georgist principle that land rent is generated by both nature and the community, which is the central thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“standing on equal terms”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The deepest equality would consist of both equal self-governance and, as Henry George put it, standing “on equal terms with reference to the bounty of nature.”…
  - why: The essay quotes Henry George's concept of equal rights to nature's bounty, which is the foundational moral argument for capturing the unearned increment.

### [Make Prices Reward “Green” Choices](https://www.progress.org/articles/make-prices-reward-green-choices/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“make prices tell the truth”**
  - sentence: …To change perspectives and behaviors, make prices tell the truth.…
  - why: The essay argues that precise pricing (charging for pollution) changes behavior and reduces emissions, citing Norway's tax on solvents as evidence.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“an eco-bonus, a Citizen’s Dividend”**
  - sentence: …Its centerpiece is an eco-bonus, a Citizen’s Dividend, an extra income drawn from the socially generated value of land and resources.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a Citizen's Dividend funded by land and resource rents to provide income and incentivize environmental stewardship.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“taxed land, not buildings”**
  - sentence: …In Australia, some towns taxed land, not buildings, and averaged 50% more built value per acre than the neighboring towns that taxed buildings and sites at an equal rate.…
  - why: The essay cites Australian towns that shifted taxes to land as evidence that this policy increases development density and construction.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“owners quit speculating and keeping lots vacant”**
  - sentence: …Having to pay a tax, fee, lease, or dues, etc, owners quit speculating and keeping lots vacant or allowing buildings to dilapidate.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing land discourages speculation and vacancy, which forces development inward and reduces urban sprawl.

### [Make the Deep-Data-Depositors De-classify](https://www.progress.org/articles/make-the-deep-data-depositors-de-classify/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“the worth of Earth”**
  - sentence: …It’s the worth of Earth, the value of natural resources and locations in the land called America.…
  - why: The essay argues that this unmeasured statistic is vital for financing public goods, implying it is a substantial potential revenue source.
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“did not keep separate”**
  - sentence: …Those that did track real estate values said they did not keep separate the value of human-made buildings from the value of physics-made land.…
  - why: The author's failed FOIA request evidences the practical difficulty of obtaining distinct land valuations from government agencies.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“keep the public in the dark”**
  - sentence: …Would that explain why some agencies did not reply at all? ... in order to keep the public in the dark.…
  - why: The essay suggests that powerful interests suppress data on land rent to obscure its magnitude, illustrating the narrative of systemic corruption.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“how unresponsive bureaucracies were”**
  - sentence: …John F. Kennedy reportedly expressed amazement upon discovering how little power the president of the United States actually had, how unresponsive bureaucracies were to his executive orders.…
  - why: The anecdote about Kennedy and the subsequent non-compliance with FOIA requests illustrates the public-choice critique that bureaucracies often ignore directives.

### [Making Macroeconomics a More Exact Science](https://www.progress.org/articles/making-macroeconomics-a-more-exact-science/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“these 2 different functions”**
  - sentence: …Regrettably, for political reasons, these 2 different functions were deliberately combined by John Bates Clark and company about 1900, resulting in the later neglect of their different influences on our sociological system-- the terms landl…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the conflation of land and capital, attributing it to John Bates Clark and arguing it obscures the distinct economic roles of landlords and capitalists.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“deliberately combined by John Bates Clark”**
  - sentence: …Regrettably, for political reasons, these 2 different functions were deliberately combined by John Bates Clark and company about 1900, resulting in the later neglect of their different influences on our sociological system-- the terms landl…
  - why: The essay corroborates the narrative that neoclassical economists deliberately obscured the distinction between land and capital, leading to a neglect of land's unique role.
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“include, along with some additional aspects, all the 3 factors of production”**
  - sentence: …The model being described here in this paper is unique, in being the first to include, along with some additional aspects, all the 3 factors of production, in Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations of 1776.…
  - why: By insisting on the necessity of including Land as a distinct factor of production alongside Labor and Capital for a complete macroeconomic model, the essay counters the claim that land is no longer e
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“prescribed independent functions of the land-owners”**
  - sentence: …Economics’ historians will recall, as originally explained by Adam Smith and David Ricardo, that there are prescribed independent functions of the land-owners and the capitalists.…
  - why: The essay supports the distinction between taxing land (rent) and taxing labor (wages) or capital by emphasizing the independent functions and returns of these three factors.

### [March For Our Lives Is About More Than Gun Control](https://www.progress.org/articles/march-for-our-lives-is-about-more-than-gun-control/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“land monopoly, income gaps”**
  - sentence: …The same can be said of issues such as the land monopoly, income gaps, wage stagnations, community disconnect, and you name it.…
  - why: The essay lists the land monopoly alongside other systemic injustices caused by self-serving special interests.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“unrepresentative 1% plutocracy”**
  - sentence: …The older generations are the ones that allowed the government takeover of special interests towards an unrepresentative 1% plutocracy while economic, environmental. and social injustices worsen.…
  - why: The essay describes a takeover by special interests leading to a plutocracy, aligning with the rentier economy narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“growing poverty, violence”**
  - sentence: …Meanwhile we have growing poverty, violence, and strife as a result.…
  - why: The essay links the control of the rich and powerful to worsening poverty and social strife.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“rich and powerful have exercised”**
  - sentence: …The rich and powerful have exercised their control according to their self serving agendas at the expense of everyone else without much of any resistance.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that the wealthy have seized control of resources and government at the expense of the public.

### [Marching Through Georgia](https://www.progress.org/articles/marching-through-georgia/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“greed for land prevails”**
  - sentence: …They could have had joint sovereignty, but greed for land prevails.…
  - why: The essay depicts a military invasion driven by the desire to control territory, illustrating the narrative that land titles are often established by force rather than production.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“They want to be in Russia”**
  - sentence: …They want to be in Russia, not in Georgian coops.…
  - why: The text highlights the conflict over self-determination and territorial sovereignty, touching on the themes of land justice and the right of populations to determine their political status.

### [Marginal-Cost Pricing](https://www.progress.org/articles/marginal-cost-pricing/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“increasing the land rent”**
  - sentence: …The ideal transit makes the city more attractive and productive, increasing the land rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that efficient public services like transit increase land values, which is the core mechanism of public investment capitalization.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“provides the means to efficiently pay”**
  - sentence: …The higher rent provides the means to efficiently pay for the transit.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the Henry George Theorem concept that the land rent generated by public goods can fund their costs.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“ideal price is a fare just high enough”**
  - sentence: …In that case, the marginal cost and ideal price is a fare just high enough to eliminate the congestion.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for congestion pricing (charging the marginal cost of crowding) to manage scarce road or transit capacity.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“Landowners pay back to the city”**
  - sentence: …Landowners pay back to the city the higher rent they get from the public transit.…
  - why: The essay describes the ethical and economic loop where community investment creates value that should be returned to the community.

### [Markets: Getting Past the Debate](https://www.progress.org/articles/markets-getting-past-the-debate/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land was treated as a commons”**
  - sentence: …For example, if land was treated as a commons -- so that, for example, any titles granted for it be in return for annual rent paid to the community – then much more land would exist outside the market, i.e. less land area would be titled.…
  - why: The essay argues for treating land as a commons where titles require paying rent to the community, which is the core definition of this narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“received income from land”**
  - sentence: …But if more people received income from land – in the form of equal shares of the land rent collected by communities – then they could spend less time at work.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for distributing land rent to the community to reduce work hours and improve welfare, supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty and inequality.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“speculators held millions of acres”**
  - sentence: …One reason Europeans settled the interior of the American continent is because speculators held millions of acres out of use that were nearer to the coast, forcing farmers to move farther inland to access farmland.…
  - why: The essay provides historical evidence that speculators holding land out of use forces development outward, illustrating the problem of speculative vacancy.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal shares of the land rent”**
  - sentence: …But if more people received income from land – in the form of equal shares of the land rent collected by communities – then they could spend less time at work.…
  - why: The essay describes a system where land rent is collected and distributed equally to citizens, which is the central narrative of the citizens' dividend.

### [Martin Luther King, Jr.’s Final Words of Advice](https://www.progress.org/articles/martin-luther-king-jrs-final-words-of-advice/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“the guaranteed income”**
  - sentence: …I am now convinced that the simplest approach will prove to be the most effective—the solution to poverty is to abolish it directly by a now widely discussed measure: the guaranteed income.…
  - why: King argues that a direct guaranteed income is the most effective solution to abolish poverty, supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“the guaranteed income”**
  - sentence: …I am now convinced that the simplest approach will prove to be the most effective—the solution to poverty is to abolish it directly by a now widely discussed measure: the guaranteed income.…
  - why: King's advocacy for a guaranteed income serves as a prominent historical illustration of the citizens' dividend narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-and-the-black-white-wealth-gap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-and-the-black-white-wealth-gap/) — anchor: **“two-thirds of them who are white”**
  - sentence: …The program would benefit all the poor, including the two-thirds of them who are white.…
  - why: King emphasizes that poverty affects both white and Black populations, supporting the intersectional analysis of the wealth gap.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“problems of housing and education”**
  - sentence: …We are likely to find that the problems of housing and education, instead of preceding the elimination of poverty, will themselves be affected if poverty is first abolished.…
  - why: King argues that solving poverty directly (via income) is necessary to address housing issues, supporting the link between economic security and housing costs.

### [Mason Gaffney Predicted the China Crash](https://www.progress.org/articles/mason-gaffney-predicted-the-china-crash/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land speculation is the primary cause”**
  - sentence: …Mason Gaffney, Ph.D. , a professor of economics at University of California, Riverside , knows what most economists don’t: That land speculation is the primary cause of economic recessions.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states Gaffney's thesis that land speculation causes recessions.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“undergo boom-and-bust cycles”**
  - sentence: …As a matter of fact, property prices—driven by increases in underlying land values— tend to undergo boom-and-bust cycles about every eighteen years .…
  - why: The essay provides the specific timing evidence for the 18-year land cycle.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“forecast the imminent bursting”**
  - sentence: …In March of this year, Gaffney forecast the imminent bursting of the Chinese economic bubble.…
  - why: The essay uses the successful prediction of the China crash as a case study of the land cycle theory.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“taxing the economic rent of land”**
  - sentence: …“One of the most important but underappreciated ideas in economics is the Henry George principle of taxing the economic rent of land, and more generally, natural resources.…
  - why: The essay quotes Stiglitz endorsing the Henry George principle as a solution to economic ills.

### [Mass Appraisal For The Masses: The Basics](https://www.progress.org/articles/mass-appraisal-for-the-masses-the-basics/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“perform objective tests”**
  - sentence: …I conclude by showing the reader how to perform objective tests that evaluate the accuracy, consistency, and overall fairness of any mass appraisal valuation.…
  - why: The essay directly addresses the objection that land value cannot be accurately assessed by explaining how mass appraisal uses statistics to value parcels objectively and consistently.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“evaluate the accuracy, consistency”**
  - sentence: …I conclude by showing the reader how to perform objective tests that evaluate the accuracy, consistency, and overall fairness of any mass appraisal valuation.…
  - why: By demonstrating that statistical tests can verify the fairness and accuracy of valuations, the essay counters the fear that assessments are purely subjective tools for corruption.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“property tax notice”**
  - sentence: …On the other hand, if you've ever gotten a property tax notice that sports a "valuation" for your home, that was likely the result of a mass appraisal operation.…
  - why: The essay clarifies the technical mechanics of how property tax valuations are currently generated via mass appraisal, illustrating the existing system that LVT would modify.

### [Mass Democracy and its Discontents](https://www.progress.org/articles/mass-democracy-and-its-discontents/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“special interests—corporations, labor unions”**
  - sentence: …Much of the funding comes from special interests—corporations, labor unions, lawyers, and real estate interests.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice failure of mass democracy by detailing how special interests capture the political process through mass media funding.
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“the whole corrupted and unfixable system”**
  - sentence: …In the 2016 US primary elections, many voters are unhappy with the social conditions, and favor candidates who, with bombast, promise to bring back or push forward to greatness, equality, and prosperity.…
  - why: The essay supports the objection that large-scale political systems breed corruption and dysfunction, arguing that mass democracy is inherently 'corrupted and unfixable' due to its structure.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Nobody is talking about systemic change”**
  - sentence: …If the voters are unhappy with politics, the economy, and tangled foreign adventures, they should start thinking about whether our political system is not suited to the 21st century.…
  - why: The essay counters the idea that specific policy fixes are sufficient, arguing instead that the fundamental political system (mass democracy) is the root problem requiring systemic change.

### [Mass Democracy Fails in Gaza](https://www.progress.org/articles/mass-democracy-fails-in-gaza/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“Palestinian National Authority”**
