### [Mass Democracy Fails in Iran](https://www.progress.org/articles/mass-democracy-fails-in-iran/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“If the government is in charge”**
  - sentence: …If the government is in charge of counting the ballots, there is a temptation to skew the results in favor of the ruling party.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice critique by arguing that those in control of the electoral mechanism (the government) have a direct incentive to manipulate outcomes for self-preservation.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“temptation to skew the results”**
  - sentence: …If the government is in charge of counting the ballots, there is a temptation to skew the results in favor of the ruling party.…
  - why: While discussing elections, the essay substantiates the broader public-choice objection that centralized administrative power invites corruption, which is the core mechanism feared in LVT assessment c
- **counters** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“people only elect a neighborhood council”**
  - sentence: …Suppose in Iran the people only voted for a neighborhood council.…
  - why: The essay proposes a decentralized, small-group voting model as a solution to systemic failure, implicitly countering the narrative that large-scale community creation (which necessitates mass democra

### [Massachusetts Gets Medically Socialized](https://www.progress.org/articles/massachusetts-gets-medically-subsidized/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Taxes on wages create poverty”**
  - sentence: …The root cause of the lack of medical coverage and its high cost is that the state has pushed down wages and jacked up costs. Taxes on wages create poverty and deprivation, while subsidies push up the cost of housing and mandates make every…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that taxing wages causes poverty and deprivation, advocating instead for public revenues derived from land.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“state's land should be the commonwealth”**
  - sentence: …The people of Massachusetts are confused about the meaning of 'commonwealth.' The state's land should be the commonwealth, from which their public revenues arise.…
  - why: The essay advocates for the Georgist principle that public revenue should arise from land rather than from labor or personal property.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“public revenues arise”**
  - sentence: …The people of Massachusetts are confused about the meaning of 'commonwealth.' The state's land should be the commonwealth, from which their public revenues arise.…
  - why: The essay suggests that the state's land is the appropriate source for public revenues, implying it could fund government functions.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“subsidies push up the cost of housing”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on wages create poverty and deprivation, while subsidies push up the cost of housing and mandates make everything, especially medicine, more costly.…
  - why: The essay identifies government subsidies as a driver of high housing costs, which contributes to the broader economic burden on residents.

### [Mathias Lefebvre Remembers What Many of Us Have Forgotten](https://www.progress.org/articles/mathias-lefebvre-remembered-what-most-of-us-have-forgotten/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“the land belongs to us all”**
  - sentence: …Mathias has remembered an ancient truth: that the land belongs to us all.…
  - why: The essay explicitly endorses the moral principle that land is a common inheritance.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“live with other people”**
  - sentence: …And then, I got to realize, too, how good it is to have friends to hang out with: I need friends.…
  - why: Illustrates the necessity of community and social cooperation as foundational to human well-being.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“the land belongs to us all”**
  - sentence: …Mathias has remembered an ancient truth: that the land belongs to us all.…
  - why: Invokes the ancient, pre-enclosure understanding of land as shared heritage.

### [Max Weber on “Politics as a Vocation”](https://www.progress.org/articles/max-weber-on-politics-as-a-vocation/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“the rentier is dispensable”**
  - sentence: …Weber stated that “the rentier is dispensable.”…
  - why: Weber explicitly separates the role of the passive landowner from the entrepreneur, arguing the former contributes nothing, which contradicts the claim that land is merely capital.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Soviets have had to accept”**
  - sentence: …The “Soviets have had to accept again absolutely all the things that Bolshevism had been fighting as bourgeois class institutions.”…
  - why: Weber argues that the Soviet revolution failed to eliminate class institutions, countering the Marxist critique that Georgism merely preserves capitalist domination.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“unearned income”**
  - sentence: …“He is a man who receives completely unearned income.”…
  - why: Weber defines the rentier's income as 'completely unearned,' supporting the Georgist distinction between earned wages and unearned land rent.

### [Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani, Champion of the Landless](https://www.progress.org/articles/mayor-elect-zohran-mamdani-champion-of-the-landless/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“exorbitant rents”**
  - sentence: …Like George, Mamdani named the problem of exorbitant rents.…
  - why: The essay uses Mamdani's victory to illustrate the modern political salience of the land-rent crisis central to this narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“youth, a renter-heavy demographic”**
  - sentence: …The youth, a renter-heavy demographic who don’t see a path to land ownership, also broke heavily for Mamdani.…
  - why: The essay provides evidence that young voters are locked out of ownership and are mobilizing politically as a result.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Henry George , the political economist”**
  - sentence: …Mamdani’s campaign bore striking resemblance to that of Henry George , the political economist who, in 1886, ran for mayor on a platform confronting the same contradiction: a city bursting with progress yet mired in poverty.…
  - why: The essay draws a direct historical parallel between Mamdani's campaign and Henry George's 1886 mayoral run, illustrating the enduring relevance of the Single Tax narrative.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“bloodthirsty communists”**
  - sentence: …George and Mamdani’s opponents attacked and caricatured them in similar ways, by labeling them as naive utopians and bloodthirsty communists.…
  - why: The essay documents the recurring political charge that Georgist or land-focused policies are a form of socialism or communism.

### [Michigan’s Government Failure](https://www.progress.org/articles/michigans-government-failure/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“The local dictator thinks he knows best”**
  - sentence: …The local dictator thinks he knows best, but, as also pointed out by Hayek, these central planners lack the knowledge about the circumstances of time and place.…
  - why: The essay uses the failures of Michigan's emergency managers to illustrate the public-choice argument that central planners lack the necessary local knowledge and incentives to govern effectively.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replacement of state taxes with property taxes”**
  - sentence: …That requires the replacement of state taxes with property taxes that exempt buildings.…
  - why: The essay argues for replacing distortionary state taxes (like the gross receipts tax) with a property tax that exempts buildings (i.e., a land value tax) to improve economic efficiency.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“community rent payment would, by bringing down the price of land”**
  - sentence: …And after the transition, even landowners would have no tax burden, as the community rent payment would, by bringing down the price of land, replace the mortgage payment.…
  - why: The essay supports the mechanism of tax capitalization, explaining that a land value tax lowers land prices (replacing mortgage payments) rather than being passed on as higher costs.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“make enterprise, labor, and consumption tax-free”**
  - sentence: …That would make enterprise, labor, and consumption tax-free.…
  - why: The essay advocates for the core Georgist narrative of shifting the tax burden off labor and enterprise onto land values.

### [Microeconomics in One Lesson](https://www.progress.org/articles/microeconomics-in-one-lesson/)
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“tax cannot be passed on to tenants”**
  - sentence: …A tax on land rent or land value reduces the price of land, since it has no cost of production, and the tax cannot be passed on to tenants, since if it were, it would create vacancies, and the landlords would lower the rent back to equilibr…
  - why: explains the mechanism preventing tax shifting to tenants
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“no deadweight loss”**
  - sentence: …When the supply is vertical or completely inelastic, as with spatial land, there is no deadweight loss.…
  - why: establishes the efficiency of taxing inelastic land supply
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“Traffic congestion can be avoided with tolls”**
  - sentence: …Traffic congestion can be avoided with tolls just high enough to let traffic flow.…
  - why: advocates for pricing to manage scarce road capacity
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Public goods such as streets and parks increase land rent”**
  - sentence: …Public goods such as streets and parks increase land rent.…
  - why: states the capitalization of public goods into land values

### [Milton Friedman (1912-2006)](https://www.progress.org/articles/milton-friedman-1912-2006/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“least bad tax”**
  - sentence: …Milton Friedman stated, "the least bad tax is the property tax on the unimproved value of land, the Henry George argument of many, many years ago" ("An Interview with Milton Friedman,” Human Events 38 [46], November 18, 1978, p. 14.)…
  - why: Friedman's endorsement of the land value tax as the 'least bad' option supports the claim that it is superior to other taxes due to its lack of excess burden.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“greatest advocate of freedom”**
  - sentence: …More importantly, though, Friedman explained to the world that freedom matters, and he is best known to the public from his works, Free to Choose.…
  - why: By highlighting Friedman—a staunch free-market advocate—as a supporter of land value taxation, the essay counters the objection that the tax is a form of socialism.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“not a Georgist”**
  - sentence: …He was not a Georgist, and said so.…
  - why: The essay notes that while Friedman was not a Georgist and did not advocate land rent as a major revenue source, he still recognized it as the 'least bad tax,' countering the objection that it is insu
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“absence of the excess burden”**
  - sentence: …Friedman's Chicago-school approach seemed to only appreciate the absence of the excess burden with land, and not the fundamental role played by land by capitalizing progress and benefits into higher rent and site values, and by overly optim…
  - why: The text explicitly states that Friedman appreciated the 'absence of the excess burden' with land taxation, which is the core efficiency argument for taxing land rather than labor.

### [Minimize This!](https://www.progress.org/articles/minimize-this/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“union tyrants”**
  - sentence: …Nevertheless, partisan democrats, welfare-state liberals, pseudo-progressives, union tyrants, and others who think government can create reality are pushing hard for a minimum wage.…
  - why: The essay satirizes the 'welfare-state liberals' and 'union tyrants' who believe government can create reality, mocking the socialist-style central planning that Georgists argue against.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“government can create reality”**
  - sentence: …Nevertheless, partisan democrats, welfare-state liberals, pseudo-progressives, union tyrants, and others who think government can create reality are pushing hard for a minimum wage.…
  - why: The essay serves as a reductio ad absurdum of the belief that legislation can override economic laws, illustrating the public-choice critique of government failure.

### [Mobile Home Parks, Harberger Auctions, and The Hold-Up Problem](https://www.progress.org/articles/mobile-home-parks-harberger-auctions-and-the-hold-up-problem/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“insecurity of tenure”**
  - sentence: …Although the Harberger tax system is an elegant solution for domains such as maritime trade, the insecurity of tenure it introduces is a real problem when you're not just trading goods, you're living in them.…
  - why: The essay argues that Harberger taxes (a form of self-assessed LVT) create insecurity of tenure, which counters the objection that LVT is useless without zoning reform by highlighting a distinct flaw 
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“classic rent-seeking behavior”**
  - sentence: …This is classic rent-seeking behavior.…
  - why: The essay uses the acquisition of mobile home parks by private equity firms to extract ground rents as a concrete example of rent-seeking that harms economic welfare.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“surrender your home”**
  - sentence: …In mobile home parks, residents face an impossible choice: surrender your home or pay more rent.…
  - why: The mobile home park scenario illustrates the 'asset-rich, cash-poor' problem, where residents face losing their immobile asset due to inability to pay increased charges.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“self assessment”**
  - sentence: …The premise is to allow people to assess their own property according to what they believe it is worth, rather than the more conventional method of employing professional property assessors (government employed or otherwise).…
  - why: The essay discusses self-assessment as a mechanism to avoid the corruption or bias of professional assessors, countering the objection that LVT requires corruptible assessors.

### [Money to the People!](https://www.progress.org/articles/money-to-the-people/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“the inevitable real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …Once again, the real estate interests and their financial symbiants will get rescued from the folly of ignoring the inevitable real estate cycle.…
  - why: The essay argues that the 2008 crisis was driven by a real estate cycle that bailed out landed interests.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“lenders who use land as collateral”**
  - sentence: …This indicates the real purpose of government: to protect and subsidize the landed interests, including lenders who use land as collateral.…
  - why: The essay identifies the financial crisis as a collapse of credit secured by land, necessitating a bailout.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“the very system of land royalty also controls education”**
  - sentence: …But the very system of land royalty also controls education, so few will learn the right lesson from the great real estate crash of 2008.…
  - why: The essay concludes that the 'land royalty' controls education to prevent the public from learning the true cause of the crash.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“public collection of all the economic rent”**
  - sentence: …A government of the people rather than of the landed royalty would require either anarchism, so that all state subsidies to landed interests cease, or else the public collection of all the economic rent, and its equal distribution to the pe…
  - why: The essay advocates for the public collection of economic rent to replace punitive taxation and eliminate poverty.

### [Monopoly was Invented to Demonstrate the Shortcomings of Capitalism](https://www.progress.org/articles/monopoly-was-invented-to-demonstrate-the-shortcomings-of-capitalism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“communally created value of its surroundings”**
  - sentence: …Because much of land’s value comes not from what is built on the plot but from nature’s gift of water or minerals that might lie beneath its surface, or from the communally created value of its surroundings: nearby roads and railways; a thr…
  - why: The essay explicitly attributes land value to communal factors like infrastructure and economy, which is the core of this claim.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“tax receipts should be invested on behalf of all”**
  - sentence: …And he argued that the tax receipts should be invested on behalf of all.…
  - why: The essay describes Henry George's proposal to tax land and use the revenue for public benefit, illustrating the Single Tax narrative.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“practical demonstration of the present system of land grabbing”**
  - sentence: …The purpose of the dual sets of rules, said Magie, was for players to experience a "practical demonstration of the present system of land grabbing with all its usual outcomes and consequences" and hence to understand how different approache…
  - why: The essay uses the Landlord's Game to demonstrate how monopolistic land grabbing leads to failure, illustrating the dynamics of land speculation.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“twisted version of her game”**
  - sentence: …It’s a maxim that would certainly serve you well in a game of Monopoly, the bestselling board game that has taught generations of children to buy up property, stack it with hotels, and charge fellow players sky-high rents for the privilege …
  - why: The essay describes how the original Georgist message of the game was suppressed and replaced by a pro-monopoly version, supporting the narrative of corruption.

### [Montenegro Becomes Independent](https://www.progress.org/articles/montenegro-becomes-independent/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“quantum leap in economic growth”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Montenegro can achieve what economist Mason Gaffney has called a "quantum leap" in economic growth by not just reducing but totally replacing taxes on income, value added, and transfers, with the tapping of the economic surplus that arises …
  - why: argues replacing distortionary taxes with land rent capture would cause a massive surge in growth
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“tapping of the economic surplus”**
  - sentence: …Montenegro can achieve what economist Mason Gaffney has called a "quantum leap" in economic growth by not just reducing but totally replacing taxes on income, value added, and transfers, with the tapping of the economic surplus that arises …
  - why: proposes replacing all major taxes (income, VAT, corporate) with public revenue from land rent
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“totally replacing taxes on income”**
  - sentence: …Montenegro can achieve what economist Mason Gaffney has called a "quantum leap" in economic growth by not just reducing but totally replacing taxes on income, value added, and transfers, with the tapping of the economic surplus that arises …
  - why: advocates shifting the tax burden off labor and transactions onto land to eliminate economic tyranny
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“eliminate the burden of taxation”**
  - sentence: …Using public revenue from land rent would eliminate the burden of taxation, as land rent is a surplus that does not shrink, hide, or flee when tapped for public revenue.…
  - why: explains that land rent is a surplus that can be taxed without the negative economic effects of other taxes

### [Moral Realism](https://www.progress.org/articles/moral-realism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“no inherent basis”**
  - sentence: …The equality premise is based on the common observation that there is in human nature no inherent basis for one group of persons to be superior masters over a second, inferior, group.…
  - why: The essay's Lockean argument against inherent master/slave relationships supports the narrative that existing land titles based on such supremacy are illegitimate.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“biological independence of individual”**
  - sentence: …The premises of natural moral law are the biological independence of individual thinking and feeling, the moral equality of persons, and the existence of a personal ethic in each person’s mind.…
  - why: By grounding morality in biological independence and individual equality, the essay counters the objection that land value capture is a form of socialism or collectivism.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“moral equality of persons”**
  - sentence: …The premises of natural moral law are the biological independence of individual thinking and feeling, the moral equality of persons, and the existence of a personal ethic in each person’s mind.…
  - why: The essay's premise of moral equality counters the Marxist critique that Georgism ignores class conflict, by asserting a universal ethic that rejects class supremacy.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“theft is evil”**
  - sentence: …For example, if a theft takes place, the individual moral value is that it is evil, and since the theft is an invasion, a coercive harm, this individual value becomes a universal ethic value.…
  - why: The essay's classification of theft as an evil invasion supports the moral narrative that capturing unearned increments (theft of community value) is objectively wrong.

### [More Energy, Less Waste](https://www.progress.org/articles/more-energy-less-waste/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“levy on emissions”**
  - sentence: …But a levy on emissions is not only within the market but required for a truly free market.…
  - why: The essay argues that charging for emissions is essential for a free market and corrects the subsidy of polluters.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“green-tax shift”**
  - sentence: …A green-tax shift, replacing destructive taxes with land-value taxation, would improve both the environment and their economies.…
  - why: The essay advocates for shifting taxes to capture environmental costs (pollution) alongside land value.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“improve both the environment and their economies”**
  - sentence: …A green-tax shift, replacing destructive taxes with land-value taxation, would improve both the environment and their economies.…
  - why: The essay claims that replacing destructive taxes with land-value taxation boosts economic growth.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replacing destructive taxes”**
  - sentence: …A green-tax shift, replacing destructive taxes with land-value taxation, would improve both the environment and their economies.…
  - why: The essay advocates replacing arbitrary taxes on wages with levies on land value to remove subsidies.

### [Must We Subsidize Genetic Flaws?](https://www.progress.org/articles/must-we-subsidize-genetic-flaws/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“forcing the public to pay”**
  - sentence: …In May 2008, the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act was enacted.…
  - why: The essay critiques government mandates that socialize individual costs, countering the objection that Georgist policies are uniquely socialistic by applying the same logic to existing interventions.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Governments often fail”**
  - sentence: …Governments often fail to do what is logical and efficient, so the U.S. federal government instead is now forcing the public to pay for genetic medical costs via higher insurance payments for everybody.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that government action often deviates from logical and efficient outcomes, supporting the public-choice critique of government failure.

### [My Own Private Basic Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/my-own-private-basic-income/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“serve the whims of rentiers”**
  - sentence: …I receive a high salary because I was lucky enough to be in the position to serve the whims of rentiers – that is, people who own resources and the stuff we make out of them.…
  - why: The essay argues that high salaries are often rewards for serving rentiers rather than productive work, supporting the claim that rent-seeking distorts rewards.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“My wife and I don’t have enough property income”**
  - sentence: …My wife and I don’t have enough property income to put us in the one percent, and at our age, it probably won’t get there while we’re alive.…
  - why: The author describes how a modest stream of property income acts as a private basic income, evidencing the feasibility and poverty-reducing potential of rent dividends.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“South Bend is a fabulous place”**
  - sentence: …South Bend is a fabulous place for small investors to get into real estate.…
  - why: The author notes that high returns on real estate are possible in South Bend due to low purchase prices despite existing zoning, countering the idea that zoning alone determines land economics.

### [Naming Things Right](https://www.progress.org/articles/naming-things-right/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“Capital could be the most confusing”**
  - sentence: …Capital could be the most confusing one of all.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes capital from land and wealth to refute the conflation of land with capital.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Henry George defined his terms carefully”**
  - sentence: …The Progressive Era political economist Henry George defined his terms carefully in advance, and sticks consistently with his definitions.…
  - why: The essay validates the Georgist reliance on precise definitions to understand political economy.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“The entire material universe exclusive”**
  - sentence: …LAND The entire material universe exclusive of people and their products.…
  - why: The essay provides a clear, inclusive definition of land that counters the objection that land value is indistinct.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“What's the point of taxation”**
  - sentence: …What's the point of taxation, anyway, and who ought to pay it?…
  - why: The essay prompts a fundamental inquiry into the proper basis of taxation, supporting the shift to land.

### [Natural Justice](https://www.progress.org/articles/natural-justice/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“reduction of society to a production machine”**
  - sentence: …Our age makes little advance towards social and economic justice because the reduction of society to a production machine continues to rule economic enquiry.…
  - why: The essay argues that mechanistic and materialist philosophies corrupted economic science, reducing society to a machine and severing it from natural justice.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“exploitation of nature corresponds exactly”**
  - sentence: …The exploitation of nature corresponds exactly with the exploitation of man and society.…
  - why: The essay draws a direct parallel between the exploitation of natural resources and social injustice, grounding the Georgist principle of ecological rent in natural law.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“land to mere resource”**
  - sentence: …Human nature was diminished to mere labour, land to mere resource, and capital to a mere tool for making a monetary return.…
  - why: The essay critiques the reduction of land to a mere resource or capital, arguing instead that it is part of a teleological natural order.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“exploitation of nature corresponds exactly”**
  - sentence: …The exploitation of nature corresponds exactly with the exploitation of man and society.…
  - why: By linking the exploitation of nature to the exploitation of man, the essay supports the narrative that unearned increments from land are unjust and violate natural harmony.

### [Nature’s Droughts Reined in by Natural Law](https://www.progress.org/articles/natures-droughts-reined-in-by-natural-law/)
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“use natural resources sparingly”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Using natural resources sparingly would become so ingrained, it’d be second nature for both rural growers and urban residents.…
  - why: The essay argues that charging for the use of nature (water and land) incentivizes conservation, which is the core definition of ecological rent.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“pay us members of society”**
  - sentence: …We’d pay our society for the nature we take, then society’s agent—government—would pay us members of society an equal share of the collected payments.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a system where collected land and resource rents are distributed equally to society as a dividend.
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“take more than they need”**
  - sentence: …Not having to pay for what they take, the more grasping among us take more than they need.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that unfettered ownership leads to efficient use, arguing instead that lack of payment for natural resources leads to hoarding and waste.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“funds went to construct”**
  - sentence: …California, for example, earned the moniker of “breadbasket of the world” once large landowners had to start paying for their ownership of land—funds went to construct the irrigation system at the end of the 19th century.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how public investment (irrigation) creates value that should be captured by the community via land rent.

### [Net Neutrality 3.0](https://www.progress.org/articles/net-neutrality-3-0/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“capture the land value resource rent”**
  - sentence: …A policy to capture the land value resource rent could also help fund Internet service initiatives.…
  - why: The essay explicitly proposes using land value capture as a funding mechanism for public internet infrastructure.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“Internet is increasingly becoming”**
  - sentence: …The Internet is increasingly becoming an essential resource, and more akin to a public utility.…
  - why: The essay frames the internet as a community-created essential resource, analogous to the public goods that generate land value.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“monopolistic companies would want”**
  - sentence: …It's easy to see why monopolistic companies would want to control the game.…
  - why: The essay describes how monopolistic control of infrastructure stifles open access and commerce, illustrating the drag of rent-seeking.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“giant media companies have built”**
  - sentence: …The reason that Net Neutrality is much of a concern at all is because the giant media companies have built so much of the infrastructure of the Internet.…
  - why: The essay highlights how entrenched corporate ownership of essential infrastructure creates a rentier economy.

### [New Book: The Quest to Know Earth’s Worth](https://www.progress.org/articles/new-book-the-quest-to-know-earths-worth/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“The sum of all rents”**
  - sentence: …The sum of all rents in a region—both natural and governmental—is so great that the dividend becomes mightily huger than an assistance to help meet basic needs.…
  - why: The essay argues that total rents are sufficient to fund significant public dividends, supporting the claim that land rent could fund a large share of government.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Rent shares paid to all”**
  - sentence: …Rent shares paid to all residents vastly beat the much talked about Basic Income, which does not exist anywhere while rent dividends do, such as the Alaska oil dividend.…
  - why: The essay explicitly contrasts rent dividends with basic income and cites the Alaska Permanent Fund as evidence that such dividends reduce poverty and exist in practice.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“One of their institutions”**
  - sentence: …One of their institutions rules the discipline—the Federal Reserve, which is neither federal nor a reserve.…
  - why: The essay argues that economic institutions deliberately ignore land value to protect rentier interests, echoing the narrative that economics was corrupted to bury Georgist ideas.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifted their property tax”**
  - sentence: …That is, they shifted their property tax off buildings, onto locations.…
  - why: The essay describes jurisdictions that shifted taxes onto land and subsequently saw improved development and land use, illustrating the benefits of split-rate taxation.

### [New Reports: Land Value Taxes in College Towns](https://www.progress.org/articles/new-reports-land-value-taxes-in-college-towns/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifts burden from productive uses”**
  - sentence: …LVT shifts burden from productive uses of land to vacant and underutilized property.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing land more heavily than buildings creates market pressure to develop vacant lots, supporting the claim that split-rate taxation encourages construction.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“tax shift is progressive”**
  - sentence: …The tax shift is progressive.…
  - why: The essay provides data showing that lower-income neighborhoods would see tax reductions while higher-income areas see increases, evidencing the progressivity of LVT.
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Universities create immense value”**
  - sentence: …Universities create immense value in their surrounding communities, but much of this benefit gets captured by private landowners rather than the public.…
  - why: The essay uses Notre Dame and Princeton University as specific examples of how public amenities capitalize into nearby land values, illustrating the Henry George Theorem.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“decreases taxes on rental properties”**
  - sentence: …The tax shift decreases taxes on rental properties.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that a split-rate tax significantly lowers taxes on rental properties, arguing this would ease rent increases and support affordability.

### [New York Fed Studies NYC Land Values](https://www.progress.org/articles/new-york-fed-studies-nyc-land-values/)
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“the price of land”**
  - sentence: …When real estate prices are rising rapidly, it is really the land value that rises.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that rising housing prices are driven by land value increases rather than construction costs.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“land values can be calculated”**
  - sentence: …A great bonus from this study is the evidence that land values can be calculated apart from the value of buildings.…
  - why: The study provides empirical evidence that land values can be accurately separated from building values.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“speculators were betting”**
  - sentence: …The latter is a restrained scholarly way of saying that land speculators were betting on ever increasing site values.…
  - why: The essay links the rapid rise in land values to speculative behavior that leads to booms and crashes.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“benefits of the neighborhood”**
  - sentence: …The authors note that the market value of land depends on the benefits of the neighborhood as well as anticipated changes.…
  - why: The study confirms that land value is determined by surrounding community factors and location.

### [Norway's Resource Rent Sharing Fund Reaches 1 Trillion USD](https://www.progress.org/articles/norways-resource-rent-sharing-fund-reaches-1-trillion-usd/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“The Fund has reached”**
  - sentence: …Norway's The Fund has reached a remarkable milestone in September 2017.…
  - why: The essay documents the massive financial success of Norway's high-rate capture of petroleum rent.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“benefit of future generations”**
  - sentence: …The Fund was established only 20 years ago as a means for Norway to store and invest the oil and gas revenues in a responsible manner for the benefit of future generations.…
  - why: The essay describes the fund's mission to invest resource wealth for the long-term benefit of the population.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“responsible, equitable, and just”**
  - sentence: …These funds are examples of responsible, equitable, and just natural resource management policies to be replicated around the world.…
  - why: The essay presents Norway's model as a moral example of sharing natural resource wealth.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“climate change, and water management”**
  - sentence: …The Fund focuses their investments in areas dealing directly with corporate transparency and disclosure on environmental and social issues.…
  - why: The essay highlights the fund's ethical focus on environmental issues like climate change.

### [Norway's Sovereign Wealth Fund](https://www.progress.org/articles/norways-sovereign-wealth-fund/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“massively taxing its extraction”**
  - sentence: …The practical upshot of Farouk's plan was that Norway should massively subsidize oil exploration, while massively taxing its extraction.…
  - why: The essay details Norway's successful 78% tax on oil profits as a prime example of high-rate resource rent capture.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“going directly into the Government Pension Fund”**
  - sentence: …But when you finally start pumping the oil, most of it goes to the Norwegian people (by going directly into the Government Pension Fund).…
  - why: It illustrates the durability of capturing resource rents and distributing them via a sovereign wealth fund.
- **counters** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“saving the Kingdom of Norway from the dreaded 'resource curse'”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Originally published on Progress and Poverty on April 5, 2022. Republished on Progress.org with permission. Meet Farouk Al-Kasim, a Norwegian-Iraqi petroleum geologist, Knight of the Royal Norwegian Order of Saint Olav, and the man widely c…
  - why: The essay uses Norway's avoidance of the resource curse to counter the claim that public rent collection inevitably leads to corruption.
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“avoided both the Scylla of corporatist privatization and the Charybdis of state socialism”**
  - sentence: …Al-Kasim's solution was to craft a private-public arrangement that avoided both the Scylla of corporatist privatization and the Charybdis of state socialism.…
  - why: It argues against state ownership (nationalization) as the solution, showing that a tax-based partnership with private firms yielded better results.

### [Ode to Liberty](https://www.progress.org/articles/ode-to-liberty/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Liberty means Justice”**
  - sentence: …For Liberty means Justice, and Justice is the natural law — the law of health and symmetry and strength, of fraternity and co-operation.…
  - why: The essay equates liberty with justice and argues that true liberty requires equal access to nature's bounty, forming the moral core of the argument to tax land rather than labor.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“turns the blessings of material progress into a curse”**
  - sentence: …It is this that turns the blessings of material progress into a curse.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that private land ownership converts technological and material progress into poverty and suffering, vindicating the claim that rising land costs drive poverty.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“monopolize the bounty of the Creator”**
  - sentence: …And land, being private property, the classes that now monopolize the bounty of the Creator would monopolize all the new bounty.…
  - why: The essay frames private land ownership as the theft of the Creator's gifts (nature's bounty), supporting the narrative that land titles constitute a robbery of common heritage.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“extracting from the masses the fruits of their weary toil”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …This is the subtle alchemy that in ways they do not realize is extracting from the masses in every civilized country the fruits of their weary toil…
  - why: The essay describes how landowners extract the value produced by the labor of others without working themselves, illustrating the concept of the unearned increment.

### [On Objective Public Finance](https://www.progress.org/articles/on-objective-public-finance/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“government does not initiate force”**
  - sentence: …Therefore government does not initiate force when it taps the land rent for public revenue, since the title holder only properly has the rights of possession.…
  - why: The essay argues that funding government through land rent is consistent with Objectivist non-aggression principles because land value is unearned.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed equally to the people”**
  - sentence: …The rent should be distributed equally to the people in cash and in the public services of a limited government.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for distributing collected land rent as a cash dividend to the populace.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“what man’s mind and labor does not produce”**
  - sentence: …Land rent is a surplus, and equality of rights implies an equal ownership of what man’s mind and labor does not produce: the benefit of land, which is its rent.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes land rent from produced wealth, implying it is a social surplus rather than a product of individual effort.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“government does not initiate force”**
  - sentence: …Therefore government does not initiate force when it taps the land rent for public revenue, since the title holder only properly has the rights of possession.…
  - why: The essay counters the charge that land value taxation is socialist by framing it as compatible with free-market Objectivism and private possession rights.

### [OpenAVMKit: a Free and Open Source mass appraisal library](https://www.progress.org/articles/openavmkit-a-free-and-open-source-mass-appraisal-library/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“mass appraisal”**
  - sentence: …Today we are launching OpenAVMKit , a new free and open source library for real estate mass appraisal .…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete tool and methodology to perform accurate land valuation, directly countering the objection that land value cannot be assessed.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“Test the quality of existing assessments”**
  - sentence: …Test the quality of existing assessments Remember all those tests we talked about in Mass Appraisal for the Masses?…
  - why: By enabling the public to statistically test official assessments against open data, the tool reduces the potential for corrupt or inaccurate assessments to go unnoticed.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“open data portal”**
  - sentence: …If you go and check your own local government’s website, there’s a better than even chance that there will be an “open data” portal posted there, with surprisingly high quality and well organized public data.…
  - why: The essay highlights the increasing availability of high-quality public property data, supporting the narrative that land is a transparent and unavoidable tax base.
- **counters** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“reproduce the same statistical tests”**
  - sentence: …So if you were curious about how your local government’s results stack up, we give you all the tools you need to be able to reproduce the same statistical tests that your state oversight body will likely run against them.…
  - why: Providing the public with the tools to audit government assessments addresses the public-choice concern that government assessors will act without accountability or oversight.

### [Organic Societies](https://www.progress.org/articles/organic-societies/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“equal dividend”**
  - sentence: …In agriculture, for example, the dysfunctional effects of the current farm subsidy system could be replaced by giving all farmers equal dividends from a pool of funds created from levies on land rent as well as on soil and water pollution.…
  - why: proposes replacing subsidies with dividends funded by levies
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“resource rent and dividend”**
  - sentence: …This means shifting from a taxes and subsidies system to a resource rent and dividend one.…
  - why: advocates shifting from taxes to a rent-funded dividend system
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“levies on land rent”**
  - sentence: …In agriculture, for example, the dysfunctional effects of the current farm subsidy system could be replaced by giving all farmers equal dividends from a pool of funds created from levies on land rent as well as on soil and water pollution.…
  - why: includes pollution levies alongside land rent in the funding pool
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“artificially-imposed bureaucracies”**
  - sentence: …But some social thinkers (for example, see Henry George’s The Science of Political Economy ) view society as being inherently organic in nature; they claim that since artificially-imposed bureaucracies are contrary to the organic nature of …
  - why: argues that bureaucratic structures (rent-seeking) harm organic society

### [Oshan Cook: A Call for Presidential Clemency](https://www.progress.org/articles/oshan-cook-a-call-for-presidential-clemency/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“shine light at a corrupt system”**

### [Pandemonium in Pakistan](https://www.progress.org/articles/pandemonium-in-pakistan/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“public revenue source”**
  - sentence: …The public revenue source most suitable to local government is land value, some of which would be kept by the local government and the rest passed up.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value is the optimal revenue base for local government to support decentralized democracy.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“power and public revenues”**
  - sentence: …With both power and public revenues originating in local communities, government would have been more closely aligned with the people.…
  - why: The essay posits that aligning government with the people requires that public revenues (specifically land value) originate locally.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“focal point for power contention”**
  - sentence: …Something is very wrong with the political system when a politician like Benazir Bhutto becomes a focal point for power contention.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the instability and dysfunction caused by rent-seeking behavior and centralized power struggles.

### [Plan C for Iraq](https://www.progress.org/articles/plan-c-for-iraq/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“distributed directly to the people”**
  - sentence: …All profits from oil production would be distributed directly to the people in equal shares.…
  - why: proposes distributing oil profits as a citizen dividend to incentivize peace
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“equal benefits from the natural resources”**
  - sentence: …Nevertheless, without the principle of equal benefits from the natural resources of Iraq, there can be no lasting peace.…
  - why: argues that capturing resource rent is necessary to resolve conflict
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“oil of Iraq properly belongs to all”**
  - sentence: …If the constitution of Iraq and the nations of the world recognize that the oil of Iraq properly belongs to all its residents in equal shares, and this is enforced, then much of the incentive for conflict will recede.…
  - why: uses the Iraq context to illustrate the narrative of common wealth
- **counters** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“fear that the same principle”**
  - sentence: …The nations of the world have not sought to recognize equality in the oil wealth in Iraq because they fear that the same principle could then be applied to them also.…
  - why: suggests resistance to rent capture stems from fear of the principle spreading, not corruption

### [Politicians take care of their own](https://www.progress.org/articles/politicians-take-care-of-their-own/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“granting immunity to state officials”**
  - sentence: …By granting immunity, the state has created an aristocracy above the law.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of government officials using power to exempt themselves from laws, illustrating the public-choice concern that government actors act in self-interest.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“politicians take care of their own”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Politicians take care of their own…
  - why: The essay exemplifies the broader narrative of systemic corruption and privilege-seeking by the ruling class, which aligns with the theme of institutional corruption described in the narrative.

### [Pope Benedict XVI says private enterprise should correct government failure](https://www.progress.org/articles/pope-benedict-xvi-says-private-enterprise-should-correct-government-failure/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“governments have assumed the role”**
  - sentence: …Regarding pollution and other environmental destruction, since the atmosphere and oceans have no private owners, they are owned by the people, and governments have assumed the role of agents for the people.…
  - why: The essay argues that failing to charge for pollution creates a subsidy that distorts market outcomes, illustrating the concept of rent-seeking and government failure.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“subsidize real estate”**
  - sentence: …Governments control the money supply, push interest rates around, and subsidize real estate, thus creating the booms and busts.…
  - why: The essay identifies government subsidies for real estate as a primary driver of economic cycles, aligning with the claim that public intervention capitalizes into land values.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“profit of the earth”**
  - sentence: …Why not let the profit of the earth go directly to the people in the first place?…
  - why: The essay invokes Henry George's moral argument that the earth's profit (land rent) belongs to everyone, supporting the narrative of the unearned increment.

### [Population in Africa: Whirlwind, or Just Spin?](https://www.progress.org/articles/population-in-africa-whirlwind-or-just-spin/)
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“fertility drives growth”**
  - sentence: …Yikes! Yes, fertility drives growth.…
  - why: The essay disputes the Malthusian view that population growth causes poverty, arguing instead that poverty is driven by other factors.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“Botswana's response to this crisis”**
  - sentence: …However, Botswana's response to this crisis has exemplified effective, responsive governance; it began in 2002 to implement a program to provide nationwide access to anti-HIV drugs, and now 95% of its people who need such treatments are rec…
  - why: Botswana is presented as a positive example of a nation managing natural resources effectively to improve welfare, supporting the claim that resource rent capture works where institutions are strong.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“Africa is only a tiny bit”**
  - sentence: …Yet with its current population density of 25 people per square kilometer, Africa is only a tiny bit more densely populated than North America (which includes all the open expanses of Canada, Alaska and the Western US), at 23.…
  - why: The essay uses low population density data to counter the argument that overpopulation is the cause of African poverty.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“sold off, in alarmingly large amounts”**
  - sentence: …There is also abundant land available to be sold off, in alarmingly large amounts, to foreign buyers.…
  - why: The essay describes the large-scale sale of African land to foreign buyers, illustrating the narrative of land dispossession and robbery.

### [Population U.S.A. 300 Million](https://www.progress.org/articles/population-usa-300-million/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Total land rent is about 16 percent of GDP”**
  - sentence: …Total land rent is about 16 percent of GDP, so about $2 trillion, or $6600 per person, enough to pay for all the goods and services provided by all levels of government if we exclude transfer payments such as Social Security, medical progra…
  - why: The essay provides a specific calculation that total US land rent is sufficient to fund government services.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“congestion charges to reduce the environmental damage”**
  - sentence: …One can roughly figure that pollution and congestion charges to reduce the environmental damage to an optimal, where the cost of more damage equals the cost of reducing more damage, at around 10 percent of GDP, or $4200 per person, paid for…
  - why: The essay argues for the economic viability and revenue potential of congestion charges.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“pollution and congestion charges”**
  - sentence: …Thus pollution and congestion charges plus land rent plus voluntary user fees would provide all current government revenue, enabling the elimination of all taxes on wages, interest, profits from operations, sales of goods, and produced prop…
  - why: The essay advocates for replacing distortionary taxes with pollution charges, supporting the principle of carbon pricing.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“elimination of all taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …Thus pollution and congestion charges plus land rent plus voluntary user fees would provide all current government revenue, enabling the elimination of all taxes on wages, interest, profits from operations, sales of goods, and produced prop…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land rent and user fees can replace taxes on labor and capital.

### [Possibility-Space, Location Rents, and Copyright Law](https://www.progress.org/articles/possibility-space-location-rents-and-copyright-law/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“possibility space fits into”**
  - sentence: …Accordingly, we can now see how the possibility space fits into a Georgist framework: it is part of what Georgists refer to as “economic land” or the natural world as a factor of production…
  - why: The essay argues that ideas and the possibility space are non-produced natural resources, countering the claim that land is just capital.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“extract rents through restrictions”**
  - sentence: …By having a monopoly over the copyrighted work, an owner of a copyright is able to extract rents through restrictions on competition.…
  - why: The essay uses copyright as a prime example of how monopoly privileges allow rent extraction from the community.
- **counters** [`objections/taxing-quasi-rents-kills-innovation`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-quasi-rents-kills-innovation/) — anchor: **“function of the producer is to discover”**
  - sentence: …The function of the producer is to discover , not create them.…
  - why: The essay reframes artistic production as discovery rather than creation, implying that taxing the resulting rents does not penalize genuine innovation.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“draws its exchange-value from obligation”**
  - sentence: …Just like title to a real estate lot, a copyright is a form of privilege disguised as true private property and draws its exchange-value from obligation rather than production .…
  - why: The essay illustrates the moral argument that copyright value comes from legal obligation (privilege) rather than productive effort.

### [Poverty is Prevented by Progressive Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/poverty-is-caused-by-regressive-taxes-and-prevented-by-progressive-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“progressive tax is one”**
  - sentence: …Stated differently, a regressive tax is one that can be shifted onto ordinary consumers and the poor, and a progressive tax is one that stays put when levied on a taker of unearned income.…
  - why: The essay defines progressive taxation as taxes that cannot be shifted, specifically identifying taxes on unearned income (like land value) as progressive.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“tax incidence”**
  - sentence: …Tax scholar Edwin Seligman also referred to George’s tax shiftability concerns as the “tax incidence,” and if you’re interested in exploring further, he well-explained the concept in “The Shifting and Incidence of Taxation” (1899).…
  - why: The essay centers on tax incidence and shiftability, arguing that taxes on unearned income (land) stay put with the owner rather than shifting to tenants.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“rent-seekers are “nature’s tax collectors””**
  - sentence: …Shearman’s basic idea was that certain kinds of people (rent-seekers) were “nature’s tax collectors,” and if government failed to heavily tax (really, retrieve or reclaim) their unearned takings (while leaving their wages untaxed), a grossl…
  - why: The essay uses Shearman's concept to illustrate how rent-seekers intercept value that should fund public services, creating an unfair tax burden.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“preventing the taxation of wages”**
  - sentence: …In addition to preventing the taxation of wages, Henry George was obsessed with the overall fairness of a tax system.…
  - why: The essay advocates for shifting the tax burden off labor and wages onto unearned income, which is the core of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.

### [Principles of an Optimal Tax Policy](https://www.progress.org/articles/principles-of-an-optimal-tax-policy/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“minimizes the excess burden”**
  - sentence: …In other words, the best tax minimizes the excess burden or deadweight loss, caused by a reduction in goods, labor, and investment.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxes on income, sales, and buildings create deadweight loss while land value tax does not, supporting the claim that shifting taxes to land improves welfare.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“promotes growth by pushing”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on income, sales, and buildings impede growth by creating a deadweight loss, while a land value tax promotes growth by pushing land to its best use.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that LVT promotes economic growth and efficiency by encouraging the best use of land, directly evidencing this claim.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“tax on land value cannot be evaded”**
  - sentence: …A tax on land value cannot be evaded.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the evasion of income and sales taxes with the impossibility of evading a land value tax, illustrating the administrative simplicity and unavoidability of LVT.
- **supports** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“replaces mortgage interest”**
  - sentence: …By reducing the purchase price, the tax replaces mortgage interest, and so, after the transition, a tax on land value does not even burden the landowner.…
  - why: The essay explains that LVT capitalizes into lower purchase prices and replaces mortgage interest, supporting the economic mechanism that prevents the tax from being passed on to tenants.

### [Private Property?](https://www.progress.org/articles/private-property/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“Land rent is created”**
  - sentence: …Land rent is created by the existence of the community and is therefore public property, because it hasn’t been earned by any individual title holder.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value is generated by the community rather than individual effort.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxing labour and capital”**
  - sentence: …The economic distortions created by the failure to capture land rent mount daily.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the taxation of labor and capital (real private property) with the failure to tax land rent.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“failing economies”**
  - sentence: …Otherwise, we end up with failing economies and social fracture such as we’re now experiencing.…
  - why: The essay links the failure to capture rent (allowing rent-seeking) to economic failure and social fracture.

### [Privilege versus Detriment](https://www.progress.org/articles/privilege-versus-detriment/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“favors to those who get subsidies”**
  - sentence: …We can also then better deal with the real privileges, favors to those who get subsidies from government because of political clout.…
  - why: The essay defines 'real privileges' as government subsidies granted via political clout, which aligns with the narrative that land titles and economic advantages are often derived from state-granted f
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“natural right to be free”**
  - sentence: …No, one has a natural right to be free of aggression.…
  - why: The essay's argument that baseline rights (like freedom from aggression) are natural rights, not privileges, supports the Georgist distinction between common rights to the earth and exclusive state-gr
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“gain is unrelated to merit”**
  - sentence: …One obtains a privilege, and is privileged, when a person or group is advantaged, benefitted, pleased, or favored relative to other groups, when the gain is unrelated to merit.…
  - why: The essay's definition of privilege as an advantage 'unrelated to merit' parallels the Georgist concept of the unearned increment, where land value increases are gains unrelated to the owner's product

### [Profits are People](https://www.progress.org/articles/profits-are-people/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“public goods provided by government”**
  - sentence: …The public goods provided by government—streets, highways, schools, security, transit, and parks—generate higher rent.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that government-provided public goods generate higher land rents, which is the core claim of this page.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“gains from higher rent”**
  - sentence: …Such is the case with gains from higher rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay argues that what appears to be corporate profit is often actually economic rent derived from land value, supporting the distinction between genuine profit and rent.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“generate higher rent”**
  - sentence: …The public goods provided by government—streets, highways, schools, security, transit, and parks—generate higher rent.…
  - why: By attributing the rise in land value to public goods and government services, the essay supports the narrative that the community creates land value.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“gains from higher rent and land value”**
  - sentence: …Such is the case with gains from higher rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes genuine entrepreneurial profits from gains derived from land value, implicitly rejecting the idea that land is just a form of capital that generates legitimate returns.

### [Progress and Poverty: Towards A New Georgist Dialogue](https://www.progress.org/articles/progress-and-poverty-towards-a-new-georgist-dialogue/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“common ownership of economic rents”**
  - sentence: …To solve this economic and social problem , Henry George promoted a program based on the common ownership of economic rents through a land value tax .…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames George's solution as a program of common ownership via LVT, which is the core definition of the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“extract wealth from society”**
  - sentence: …The possession of land allows classes of people to extract wealth from society in the form of rents, by charging for access to land or taking its rents for themselves, without contributing to the economy at all.…
  - why: This passage describes the mechanism of a rentier economy where wealth extraction occurs through monopolization rather than production.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“Every improvement in the circumstances”**
  - sentence: …Adam Smith noted this dynamic in the Wealth of Nations: Every improvement in the circumstances of the society tends either directly or indirectly to raise the real rent of land, to increase the real wealth of the landlord, his power of purc…
  - why: The essay cites Adam Smith to explain the 'unearned increment' concept—that societal progress increases landlord wealth without landlord effort.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“common right of mankind”**
  - sentence: …It is a society in which the common right of mankind to the universe and its natural materials, forces, and opportunities is recognized, a society in which economic success can be achieved only by positive contribution to the community, a s…
  - why: The essay outlines a property ethic based on the common right to natural resources, which aligns with the justice narrative regarding land and natural wealth.

### [Protesting the G-8 Summit](https://www.progress.org/articles/protesting-the-g-8-summit/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“Chancellor Merkel has been pushing”**
