  - sentence: …Chancellor Merkel has been pushing president Bush to bring the U.S. into a global agreement to reduce pollution, and made some progress at the summit.…
  - why: The essay notes progress toward a global pollution agreement, supporting the claim that pricing or regulating emissions is a viable political goal.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Real anarchists believe that there can be harmony”**
  - sentence: …Real anarchists believe that there can be harmony without imposed government.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes between violent 'anarcho-socialists' and 'individualist anarchists who favor peace and personal liberty,' countering the conflation of Georgist or radical market reforms with v

### [Public Works — the Bridge to Economic Nowhere](https://www.progress.org/articles/public-works-the-bridge-to-economic-nowhere/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replaced taxes on labor”**
  - sentence: …The economy would jump start if the government replaced taxes on labor and investment returns with taxes on pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates shifting the tax burden from labor and capital onto land value to stimulate the economy.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“make the economy more productive”**
  - sentence: …In that case, the resources do not crowd out alternatives, because the works make the economy more productive in the long run.…
  - why: The essay argues that public investment is only justified if it enhances productivity, a principle it applies to its advocacy for land value taxation.
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“The way to quickly revive”**
  - sentence: …The way to quickly revive the economy is to stimulate general demand, not specific demand.…
  - why: The essay critiques public works for distorting the economy, implicitly acknowledging that such works create specific land values rather than general prosperity.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“increase the barriers that crush”**
  - sentence: …Government chiefs are inconsistent when they claim to want to stimulate the economy, yet they increase the barriers that crush investment and enterprise.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing tax barriers (like shifting to LVT) is sufficient to revive the economy, countering the view that zoning reform must precede tax shifts.

### [Radical Democracy](https://www.progress.org/articles/radical-democracy/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“seeking of subsidies”**
  - sentence: …Economists use the odd term “rent seeking” for the seeking of subsidies, privileges, and protection from competition.…
  - why: The essay defines rent-seeking as the pursuit of subsidies and privileges, aligning with the wiki's claim that this behavior diverts effort from wealth creation.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“financing comes from special interests”**
  - sentence: …While individuals do send contributions to parties and candidates, much of the financing comes from special interests such as corporations, labor unions, lawyers, and the financial and real estate industries.…
  - why: The essay argues that mass democracy is inherently corrupted by the need for campaign funds from special interests, illustrating the mechanism of political corruption.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“Subsidies to special interests”**
  - sentence: …Subsidies to special interests are economic rents.…
  - why: The essay identifies subsidies as economic rents, supporting the narrative that a significant portion of the economy is driven by rent extraction rather than production.

### [Rangel's Mangled Tax Tangle](https://www.progress.org/articles/rangels-mangled-tax-tangle/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“shifting taxes from labor to land values”**
  - sentence: …These WFLs could decrease income inequality without any excess burden by shifting taxes from labor to land values, as much of today's inequality come from unequal land tenure.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes from labor to land values reduces inequality without the economic waste (excess burden) caused by income taxes.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“don't like it when entrepreneurs to make a lot of money”**
  - sentence: …My guess is that welfare-state liberals (WFLs) don't like it when entrepreneurs to make a lot of money.…
  - why: The essay critiques welfare-state liberals for rejecting efficient taxes like LVT, implying their opposition is driven by animus toward successful entrepreneurs rather than genuine socialist goals.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“levy taxes on all pollution”**
  - sentence: …An alternative to an increase in marginal tax rates would be for the federal government to levy taxes on all pollution.…
  - why: The essay advocates for pollution taxes as a superior alternative to income tax increases because they correct social costs without deadweight loss.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“waste caused by taxes”**
  - sentence: …The waste caused by taxes, which economists call the 'deadweight loss' or 'excess burden,' increases by much more than the increase in the tax as the total tax amount rises.…
  - why: The essay explains how high taxes on enterprise create deadweight loss that stifles investment and productivity, aligning with the claim that inefficient taxation drags economic growth.

### [Real Capitalism?](https://www.progress.org/articles/real-capitalism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“profit from the contributions of others”**
  - sentence: …When people and institutions profit from land, they profit from the contributions of others.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value increases are created by the community's hard work and ingenuity, which landowners then extract.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“treat all gifts of nature—including land—as capital goods”**
  - sentence: …Our current form of capitalism does not distinguish the gifts of nature from capital goods such as machines and other human-made products.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the conflation of land with capital, arguing that failing to distinguish them prevents 'real capitalism'.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“extract money from society without adding any corresponding value”**
  - sentence: …Our current form of capitalism allows some of us to extract money from society without adding any corresponding value to society.…
  - why: The essay characterizes land ownership as a form of rent-seeking where value is extracted rather than created.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“live off the lives of others”**
  - sentence: …Instead, we’ve had crony capitalism —the kind that allows some to live off the lives of others.…
  - why: The essay describes an economy where landowners extract unearned income, defining the rentier dynamic.

### [Real Estate Cycle on Schedule as Manufacturing Falls](https://www.progress.org/articles/real-estate-cycle-on-schedule-as-manufacturing-falls/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“18-year real estate boom-bust cycle”**
  - sentence: …The 18-year real estate boom-bust cycle has been running on schedule.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for the existence and predictability of an 18-year real estate cycle driven by speculation.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“powerless to prevent it”**
  - sentence: …The U.S. economy has become so politicized that the monetary and fiscal authorities can alter the timing of the next recession, but they are nevertheless powerless to prevent it, as ultimately markets are more powerful than governments.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that government intervention can stop the cycle, arguing they can only delay it.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Fiscal policy penalizes production and subsidizes land speculation”**
  - sentence: …Fiscal policy penalizes production and subsidizes land speculation, which combined with credit gyrations, induces speculative real estate booms followed by the decline.…
  - why: The essay argues that while credit is involved, land speculation subsidized by fiscal policy is the root cause of the cycle.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“tapping land values for public revenue”**
  - sentence: …So only a radical shift — to free-market money and tapping land values for public revenue — will eliminate the cycle.…
  - why: The essay concludes that capturing land rent is necessary to eliminate the boom-bust cycle.

### [Real Estate in 2020](https://www.progress.org/articles/real-estate-in-2020/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“replaced by a single tapping”**
  - sentence: …The complexity of income and sales taxes no longer benefits anybody, so these antiquated taxes are scrapped and replaced by a single tapping of site values, along with user fees and charges for congestion and pollution.…
  - why: The essay's conclusion explicitly depicts the replacement of complex taxes with a single levy on site values.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“fueled the speculative boom”**
  - sentence: …The failure of central banking, which fueled the speculative boom which then collapsed, is now also recognized.…
  - why: The essay attributes the 2008 crash to a speculative boom fueled by central banking, aligning with the Georgist narrative that land speculation drives cycles.
- **illustrates** [`benefits/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants`](https://progress.org/wiki/landlords-cannot-pass-lvt-to-tenants/) — anchor: **“Santa Clara Land Rent Trust”**
  - sentence: …The Santa Clara Land Rent Trust has a contract with the House Leasing Company to collect the land rents and distribute the rents as dividends to its share holders.…
  - why: The separation of land rent collection into a trust illustrates the mechanism of capturing rent directly from the landowner rather than the tenant.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“own shares of corporations”**
  - sentence: …Those seeking to profit from real estate could instead own shares of corporations or partnerships which own or develop real estate or which own mortgages.…
  - why: The essay describes the financialization of real estate into shares and derivatives, exemplifying the shift toward a rentier economy where income is derived from asset ownership.

### [Real Estate USA 2015](https://www.progress.org/articles/real-estate-usa-2015/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …The two variables to watch during the real estate cycle are construction and selling prices.…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the US economy around an 18-year real estate cycle driven by speculation, predicting a future boom and crash.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“capitalized into ever higher land rent”**
  - sentence: …Governmental public works, civic services, and subsidies become capitalized into ever higher land rent as they are paid for mostly by taxes on labor and capital.…
  - why: The essay argues that public goods and services increase land rent, which is then captured by landowners rather than the public.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“prosperity tax shift”**
  - sentence: …The only remedy that will prevent the next depression is a prosperity tax shift, the replacement of all taxes with levies on land values.…
  - why: The essay concludes that shifting taxes from labor and capital to land values is the only solution to prevent the boom-bust cycle and promote growth.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“None of the financial regulations”**
  - sentence: …None of the financial regulations confront the basic problem: massive subsidies to land values.…
  - why: The essay dismisses current financial regulations as ineffective, arguing they fail to address the root cause of economic instability.

### [Reclaiming Our Land and Water: Michigan and the World](https://www.progress.org/articles/reclaiming-our-land-and-water-michigan-and-the-world/)
- **illustrates** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“implemented reforms that made”**
  - sentence: …In the 1960s, in the town of Southfield, the Detroit suburb where this conference will take place, a forward-thinking Mayor, James Clarkson, and an expert Assessor (Ted Gwartney, who will be one of our featured speakers) implemented reforms…
  - why: cites Southfield's rapid growth following tax reforms as a case study
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“viable Third Way policy solutions”**
  - sentence: …The Council of Georgist Organizations looks beyond the ideological limits of “Left” or “Right” to explore viable Third Way policy solutions that can move society toward greater equality, without sacrificing prosperity.…
  - why: frames Georgist policy as a non-ideological path to equality and prosperity
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“move society toward greater equality”**
  - sentence: …The Council of Georgist Organizations looks beyond the ideological limits of “Left” or “Right” to explore viable Third Way policy solutions that can move society toward greater equality, without sacrificing prosperity.…
  - why: asserts the possibility of achieving equality without sacrificing prosperity

### [Redistribution: an Unnecessary Evil](https://www.progress.org/articles/redistribution-an-unnecessary-evil/)
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“benefits of natural resources”**
  - sentence: …As George argued, the benefits of natural resources belong to humanity in equal shares.…
  - why: The essay explicitly invokes Henry George's moral argument that natural benefits are a common birthright.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“state-provided public goods”**
  - sentence: …Even most free-market libertarians are in favor of implicit redistribution, the increase in land rent and land value due to state-provided public goods not paid for by the landowners, amounting to a forced redistribution of wealth from tena…
  - why: The essay describes the mechanism by which government spending increases land values, effectively redistributing wealth to landowners.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“land cannot hide, shrink, or flee”**
  - sentence: …If the land rent is directly taxed, there is no deadweight loss, because land cannot hide, shrink, or flee when taxed.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the efficiency of taxing land against the distortions of taxing labor and capital.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“politics in general redistributes”**
  - sentence: …The coming high inflation will redistribute wealth from creditors to borrowers, and politics in general redistributes from the general public to special interests such as lawyers, unions, and the chiefs of corporations.…
  - why: The essay argues that political redistribution acts as rent-seeking that transfers wealth to special interests at the expense of the economy.

### [Regulation of the Financial Industry](https://www.progress.org/articles/regulation-of-the-financial-industry/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate cycles”**
  - sentence: …Moreover, there have been real estate cycles for two hundred years, with periodic downturns of land value.…
  - why: The essay argues that the financial crisis is driven by a recurring land value cycle, not by deregulation.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Trillions of dollars of land value”**
  - sentence: …Trillions of dollars of land value back up real estate mortgages and packages of loans derived from real estate mortgages.…
  - why: The essay identifies land value as the primary collateral backing the mortgage securities that caused the crisis.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“The deep problem is not in the financial industry”**
  - sentence: …The deep problem is not in the financial industry, but in the real side of the economy, in real estate.…
  - why: The essay explicitly rejects the view that credit or financial regulation is the root cause, arguing instead for land values.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“The gains from land speculation would vanish”**
  - sentence: …If these taxes were shifted to land value, the price of land would fall to a tiny amount just high enough to facilitate the sale of land.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes to land value would eliminate the speculative gains that drive boom-bust cycles.

### [Religion, Philosophy, and Land Rights](https://www.progress.org/articles/religion-philosophy-and-land-rights/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“equal rights to land”**
  - sentence: …Leviticus 25 describes how each family is to have equal rights to land.…
  - why: The essay uses biblical scripture to argue that land rights are fundamentally equal and common, supporting the narrative that land titles should reflect shared ownership rather than exclusive private 
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“equal sharing of products”**
  - sentence: …Unlike land, which no person produced, products made by people involve incentives, which is why compulsory equal sharing of products, as in pure communism, leads to problems, including a lower standard of living for everyone.…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes between land (common property) and products (private property), arguing against the conflation of land value capture with communism or the forced redistribution of l
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“two basic kinds of property”**
  - sentence: …It's significant that religion, philosophy, and economics, with each tradition starting from different premises, each can arrive at the view that the two basic kinds of property need to be treated differently: property that is produced by h…
  - why: The essay articulates the core Georgist distinction between land and labor, which is the foundational narrative justifying the Single Tax.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“property in land and its natural resources”**
  - sentence: …It's significant that religion, philosophy, and economics, with each tradition starting from different premises, each can arrive at the view that the two basic kinds of property need to be treated differently: property that is produced by h…
  - why: The essay grounds the moral claim that land is distinct from human-made products in religious and philosophical tradition, supporting the justice narrative that land access is a right rather than a pr

### [Rent Control: A Case Study](https://www.progress.org/articles/rent-control-a-case-study/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“creation of higher rentals”**
  - sentence: …The biggest subsidy to land values is the creation of higher rentals and land values from the public goods provided by government, paid for mostly from taxes on, or at the expense of, wages.…
  - why: The essay argues that public goods funded by taxes on wages are capitalized into higher land rents, driving unaffordability.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“Shift all taxes to land values”**
  - sentence: …The remedy is the eliminate the cause, to stop the pushing and pulling. Shift all taxes to land values, eliminating taxes on wages, goods, enterprise profits, and interest.…
  - why: The essay proposes shifting taxes to land values as the fundamental remedy to cure housing unaffordability.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“rental of the land”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …We can understand "rent control" by dividing the rental paid to landlords into the rent of the land and the rental of the building and management services.…
  - why: The essay frames the housing crisis as a function of land rent, arguing that price controls distort the market for land rather than solving the crisis.
- **counters** [`objections/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap/) — anchor: **“rental-recipient control”**
  - sentence: …“Rent control” is better called the “rental-recipient control,” because government cannot directly control housing rentals.…
  - why: The essay disputes the efficacy of rent control (a form of intervention), arguing it fails to address the root cause of high costs, unlike the Georgist alternative.

### [Rent-Sharing Farm Trusts: A Commons-Based Way](https://www.progress.org/articles/rent-sharing-farm-trusts-a-commons-based-way/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“paying equal dividends”**
  - sentence: …However, whereas CLTs only charge nominal land rent while maintaining re-sale restrictions, a rent-sharing farm trust would charge full market rent while paying equal dividends to each farmer in the trust.…
  - why: The trust model demonstrates a practical, micro-scale application of distributing common wealth via equal dividends.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“did not create the land”**
  - sentence: …But some may realize it makes more sense to share the proceeds of these payments equally rather than see them go to a landlord or banker who, like the farmers, also did not create the land or its value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value is not created by the owner, justifying its capture and redistribution.
- **supports** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“land is hugely expensive”**
  - sentence: …For young people wanting to start family farms, land is hugely expensive to buy, as much as $10,000 per acre or more in some states.…
  - why: The essay cites high land prices as a primary barrier preventing young people from entering farming.

### [Reparations - Going All the Way](https://www.progress.org/articles/reparations-going-all-the-way/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“massive theft of their labor”**
  - sentence: …Clearly there was massive theft of their labor and liberty during slavery, and if a debt is owed to the slaves, then it is not unreasonable for that compensation to be inherited.…
  - why: The essay's opening premise that wealth was stolen from specific groups supports the narrative that existing land titles descend from theft and conquest.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“most tribes are owed reparations”**
  - sentence: …Starting with the Native American Indians, most tribes are owed reparations for the loss of their lands, culture, and violations of their treaties.…
  - why: The essay provides a detailed illustration of the specific injustices faced by Indigenous peoples that the land justice page seeks to address.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-and-the-black-white-wealth-gap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-and-the-black-white-wealth-gap/) — anchor: **“African-Americans are calling for reparations”**
  - sentence: …Some African-Americans are calling for reparations to compensate for past slavery.…
  - why: The essay engages directly with the topic of reparations for slavery, which is the central intersection of land justice and the Black-white wealth gap.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Equal benefits from land”**
  - sentence: …Equal benefits from land would be the most feasible and beneficial reparation.…
  - why: The essay concludes that equalizing land-value benefits is the best way to rectify past injustice, which is the core argument for a citizen's dividend.

### [Replace Confederate Chiefs with Soldier Memorials](https://www.progress.org/articles/replace-confederate-chiefs-with-soldier-memorials/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“forcibly moving American Indians”**
  - sentence: …Personally, I favor removing statues of president Andrew Jackson, because of his policy of forcibly moving American Indians.…
  - why: The essay cites Andrew Jackson's forced relocation of Indigenous peoples as a moral failing relevant to monument removal, intersecting with the page's discussion of land dispossession.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“preserve the evil institution of slavery”**
  - sentence: …The lines are subjective, but a reasonable line is to distinguish those who sought to create a better society, and those who sought to preserve the evil institution of slavery.…
  - why: The essay frames the Confederacy's defense of slavery as a moral evil, illustrating the narrative that existing titles and honors often rest on unjust foundations.

### [Replace Minimum Wages with the Earned Income Tax Credit](https://www.progress.org/articles/replace-minimum-wages-with-earned-income-tax-credit/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replacement of taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …With the replacement of taxes on wages with taxes on rent, not only would labor be paid its economic contribution to output, but the income from land would become equalized, reducing economic inequality not by investment-hampering redistrib…
  - why: The essay argues that eliminating taxes on wages (labor) and replacing them with taxes on rent removes the disincentive to hire and invest, directly supporting the claim that shifting tax bases improv
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“gains from trade and technological progress”**
  - sentence: …Henry George in his book Protection or Free Trade recognized that much of the gains from trade and technological progress are captured by higher land rent, so while national income rises, wages might not receive the benefits, due to both th…
  - why: The essay cites Henry George to explain how capturing existing wealth (rent) prevents workers from benefiting from productivity growth, illustrating the drag on economic welfare caused by rent-seeking
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“captured by higher land rent”**
  - sentence: …Henry George in his book Protection or Free Trade recognized that much of the gains from trade and technological progress are captured by higher land rent, so while national income rises, wages might not receive the benefits, due to both th…
  - why: By asserting that modern economic gains are absorbed by land rent rather than passed to workers, the essay counters the objection that land is no longer a significant economic factor.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“eliminate taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …The best long-run solution to the problem of both unemployment and low-wages is to completely eliminate taxes on wages.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for removing taxes on labor in favor of other revenue sources, which is the central thesis of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.

### [Resolving San Francisco’s Class Warfare](https://www.progress.org/articles/resolving-san-franciscos-class-warfare/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land values are community-generated”**
  - sentence: …Yet, that value is not shared: property owners claim those land values for themselves, even though human beings alone don’t make land valuable—land values are community-generated.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value derives from the community rather than the owner, which is the core thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“land is exceptionally valuable”**
  - sentence: …In the case of San Francisco, land is exceptionally valuable, since there’s a lot of wealth crammed into a relatively small area.…
  - why: The essay identifies the scarcity and privatization of land as the root cause of the housing crisis and inequality in San Francisco.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal stake in San Francisco land”**
  - sentence: …Imagine if all residents of San Francisco had an equal stake in San Francisco land!…
  - why: The essay proposes sharing land value with all residents as a solution to class warfare, illustrating the citizens dividend concept.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“economic system we are currently living in”**
  - sentence: …It also helps us realize that the economic system we are currently living in is deeply responsible for the poverty we have amidst ourselves today—the kind of poverty that is especially present in big cities such as San Francisco, California…
  - why: The essay argues that the current economic system's treatment of land creates poverty amidst wealth, supporting the claim that rising land costs drive poverty.

### [Resource Taxation for Developing Countries](https://www.progress.org/articles/resource-taxation-as-an-alternative-method-for-diversifying-the-revenue-of-developing-countries/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“source of economic rent”**
  - sentence: …Governments of resource-rich countries have an incentive to obtain revenues dominated by inflows from natural resources, as they are a source of economic rent that can generate earnings even in the absence of political stability or wider ec…
  - why: The essay identifies natural resource inflows as economic rent that can generate revenue without broader growth, supporting the claim that capturing resource rent is a viable fiscal strategy.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“taxation of use of natural resources”**
  - sentence: …Such characteristics would suggest that the taxation of use of natural resources could be an effective alternative to income and consumer taxation, producing significant fiscal benefits, among others.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting the tax base from value added (labor/capital) to resource throughput, supporting the claim that taxing rents rather than income improves efficiency.
- **counters** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“This vicious circle is a threat”**
  - sentence: …This vicious circle is a threat to civil society and human rights and increases the risk of corruption.…
  - why: The essay argues that relying on resource rents without taxing citizens creates a vicious circle of corruption, countering the objection that public rent collection itself is the primary driver of cor
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“taxation of resource throughput”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The idea of natural resource taxation and shifting the tax base from value added (labor and capital) to resource throughput (depletion and pollution) is not new.…
  - why: The essay explicitly supports the Green Georgist narrative of taxing resource throughput (depletion and pollution) rather than value added.

### [Restoring Communities](https://www.progress.org/articles/restoring-communities/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“share the monetary value”**
  - sentence: …While it’s infeasible in practical terms for us to share every aspect of nature with one another, it’s entirely possible for us to share the monetary value that human beings assign to nature.…
  - why: The essay argues for distributing the financial value of nature (land) to everyone, which is the core definition of a citizens' dividend.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“benefit enormously from the communities”**
  - sentence: …Property owners—and all those with a vested interest in properties, including, and perhaps even especially , financial institutions—benefit enormously from the communities in which their properties are located.…
  - why: The text explicitly states that land value derives from the community, justifying the capture of that value for public benefit.
- **illustrates** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“every resident of Alaska”**
  - sentence: …In other examples, today every resident of Alaska receives a relatively modest Basic Income from the value of oil.…
  - why: The essay cites Alaska's Permanent Fund as a concrete example of successfully sharing natural resource value with residents.
- **supports** [`objections/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap/) — anchor: **“Hong Kong’s leasehold model”**
  - sentence: …Yet even though Hong Kong’s leasehold model represents a step in the right direction, it remains flawed since land-value assessments are not updated annually to reflect the current market value of land; leasehold revenues therefore bear lit…
  - why: The essay acknowledges that despite capturing land value, Hong Kong still suffers from poverty and high costs, illustrating the claim that capture alone doesn't guarantee affordability.

### [Restructuring the Police](https://www.progress.org/articles/restructuring-the-police/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“propagates social problems”**
  - sentence: …The solution to the problem of police misconduct requires a radical restructuring, not just of the police, but also of the political and economic infrastructure that propagates social problems.…
  - why: The essay frames the current political and economic infrastructure as a system that propagates social problems, aligning with the claim that rent-seeking and structural inefficiencies drag on social w
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“generation of land rent”**
  - sentence: …The mother of all subsidies is the generation of land rent from the public goods and welfare provided by government.…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies the generation of land rent from public goods as a massive subsidy, directly supporting the claim that public goods create land value.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“divided among the people equally”**
  - sentence: …Justice requires that this ground rent be divided among the people equally, either for public goods or as an individual basic income.…
  - why: The essay argues that dividing ground rent equally among the people is a requirement of justice and a remedy for poverty, supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“wage needs to be tax-free”**
  - sentence: …To enable a worker to have an income that covers his basic needs, his wage needs to be tax-free, including the portion of the taxes on wages paid by the employer.…
  - why: The essay advocates for removing taxes on wages to allow workers to afford basic needs, which is the core argument for shifting the tax burden onto land.

### [Review of Confessions of an Economic Hit Man, by John Perkins](https://www.progress.org/articles/review-of-confessions-of-an-economic-hit-man-by-john-perkins/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Corruption of Economics”**
  - sentence: …If he reads Mason Gaffney's book, The Corruption of Economics , he will understand better how the discipline of economics was deliberately altered a century ago to serve those same corporate interests.…
  - why: The essay explicitly references Mason Gaffney's thesis that neoclassical economics was corrupted to serve corporate interests, validating the narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“birthright of all humanity”**
  - sentence: …DeSoto is now the foremost accomplice and apologist for the use of land titles to leverage natural capital, resources which from time immemorial have been the birthright of all humanity, not the license of a few.…
  - why: The essay argues that natural resources and land are the common birthright of humanity, supporting the justice narrative against private enclosure.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“mortgage such nations' economic dependency”**
  - sentence: …There was a conscious strategy to mortgage such nations' economic dependency to US policy and corporate business interests in a way that they would be hamstrung ever to raise significant challenges.…
  - why: The essay describes how capturing resource rents (via debt for infrastructure) is used as a tool of control, illustrating the power and mechanism of rent capture.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“enriches only a few”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …He compares the classic British empire's mercantile arrangements and the contemporary corporatocracy. each is based on ' a lie ... where the truth is that the system enrich[es] only a few at the expense of the many. '…
  - why: The essay critiques a system where economic growth primarily benefits asset owners and elites at the expense of the many, defining the rentier economy.

### [Riding the Upswing of the Real Estate Cycle: 2012 - 2022](https://www.progress.org/articles/riding-the-upswing-of-the-real-estate-cycle-2012-2022/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“basis of the real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …The basis of the real estate cycle is the fixed supply of land, which causes land rent to capture much of the gains from economic expansion.…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies the fixed supply of land and rent capture as the fundamental cause of the recurring 18-year real estate cycle.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Cheap credit is a financial subsidy”**
  - sentence: …Cheap credit is a financial subsidy added to the fiscal subsidy that fuels the real estate expansion and bubble.…
  - why: The essay argues that low interest rates and credit expansion are subsidies that fuel the real estate bubble, linking finance to land values.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Government’s public works, services, and subsidies”**
  - sentence: …Government’s public works, services, and subsidies make locations more attractive, and the increased demand to be located there generates higher land rent.…
  - why: The essay explains how government spending and services increase location value and land rent, validating the capitalization effect.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“If land rent were used for public revenue”**
  - sentence: …If land rent were used for public revenue instead of taxes on labor and goods, wages would be higher, real estate prices lower, the gains from growth would benefit all the public, and the real estate cycle would be replaced by sustainable g…
  - why: The essay concludes by advocating for shifting the tax burden from labor to land rent to achieve sustainable growth and higher wages.

### [Rights and Privileges](https://www.progress.org/articles/rights-and-privileges/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxes on wages violate natural rights”**
  - sentence: …Since people have a natural right to engage in labor for wages, taxes on wages violate natural rights and therefore the Constitutional rights recognized by the 9th Amendment.…
  - why: The essay argues that labor is a natural right protected by the 9th Amendment, making taxes on wages immoral, which supports the Georgist narrative of shifting taxes from labor to land.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“where there is no creator, the benefits belong to the people”**
  - sentence: …In my judgment, the moral law of property is, “To the creator belongs the creation, and where there is no creator, the benefits belong to the people in equal shares.”…
  - why: This articulates the moral foundation for the unearned increment narrative: that because land is not created by human effort, its benefits (rent) belong to the community rather than the individual own
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“the only source left is nature’s resources, land”**
  - sentence: …Since human exertion and its gains are a natural right, the only source left is nature’s resources, land.…
  - why: The essay concludes that because taxing labor violates natural rights, land rent is the only legitimate source of government revenue, supporting the claim that land rent can fund government.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“the rent belongs to the people”**
  - sentence: …Therefore, the rent belongs to the people, and by natural moral law, the individual right of the possession of land is conditional on paying the rent to the rightful owners, the people.…
  - why: The essay asserts that land rent belongs to the people, supporting the narrative that land value is socially created and should be returned to the community.

### [Ruffling More Than Feathers](https://www.progress.org/articles/ruffling-more-than-feathers/)
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“it was not capital but land”**
  - sentence: …A few geonomists pointed out that actually, it was not capital but land—usually referred to sloppily as “real estate”—that was the father of enduring fortunes.…
  - why: The essay directly challenges Piketty's capital thesis, arguing that the observed wealth accumulation is driven by land appreciation rather than capital returns.
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“declaring land to be “irrelevant today””**
  - sentence: …Piketty’s response is typical of people who use “capitalists” and “the rich” as synonyms, declaring land to be “irrelevant today”.…
  - why: The essay identifies and critiques the specific objection that land is no longer economically significant, attributing it to critics like Piketty.
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“What’s rising in value is the land”**
  - sentence: …Wouldn’t they want to know that, actually, the cost can not go up since the housing has already been built. What’s rising in value is the land.…
  - why: The essay argues that rising housing costs are fundamentally a function of rising land values, distinguishing the location from the structure.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Academia dismisses land as a major factor”**
  - sentence: …The press reports hi-tech minting billionaires, and previously oil doing the same, but not prime sites enriching a handful of owners. Academia dismisses land as a major factor in generating income.…
  - why: The essay describes the systemic neglect of land in economic discourse and media, illustrating the narrative that economic orthodoxy obscures the role of rent.

### [Russia's North-Pole Land Grab](https://www.progress.org/articles/russias-north-pole-land-grab/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“aboriginal Eskimos”**
  - sentence: …If any nation has a claim to the North Pole area, it is the aboriginal Eskimos.…
  - why: The essay uses the Arctic dispute to illustrate the tension between state claims and indigenous land rights.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed to humanity in equal shares”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The geoist position would let individuals claim possession of unoccupied land, but the title holders would have to pay natural rent of the land to an organization that represents humanity and which would distribute the rent to humanity in e…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a global system where natural resource rents are collected and distributed equally to all people.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“collect the economic rent”**
  - sentence: …That rent would then be distributed down the council chain to the people in equal per-capita amounts.…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific governance structure to capture and distribute the economic rent of oil and gas.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“morally absurd”**
  - sentence: …No human being made the oil and gas, and to open it up to a land rush and have the first person to poke a stick in the ground claim to own the oil is likewise morally absurd.…
  - why: The essay argues that claiming exclusive ownership of naturally occurring resources is morally indefensible.

### [Saudi Arabians Not Allowed to See the Cat](https://www.progress.org/articles/saudi-arabians-not-allowed-to-see-the-cat/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Abolish the income tax”**
  - sentence: …6) Abolish the income tax, value added tax, and sales tax.…
  - why: The essay explicitly lists the abolition of income tax as a priority reform to avoid Western economic imperialism.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“economies have been made catatonic”**
  - sentence: …It is a global catastrophe that economies have been made catatonic by the cauterizing effects of cataractic taxation.…
  - why: The essay uses the metaphor of 'catatonic' economies to illustrate the damaging effects of conventional taxation, aligning with the narrative that economic confusion obscures the truth.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“see the economic cat”**
  - sentence: …Saudi Arabia must let people sell cats and own cats, regardless of breed, not just because it is well within Islamic tradition, but so that just maybe it will help them to see the economic cat as well.…
  - why: The essay uses the 'cat' as a metaphor for economic clarity, implying that seeing the 'economic cat' is the goal of the reform.

### [Save Energy, Get Punished](https://www.progress.org/articles/save-energy-get-punished/)
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“charge tolls on congested roads”**
  - sentence: …California should charge tolls on congested roads, levies on polluting activity, and assessments on land rent or land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for tolls on congested roads as part of a shift away from taxing productive activity.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“levies on polluting activity”**
  - sentence: …California should charge tolls on congested roads, levies on polluting activity, and assessments on land rent or land value.…
  - why: The essay argues for shifting revenue to levies on polluting activity to avoid punishing beneficial environmental efforts.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Landowners get higher rent”**
  - sentence: …Landowners get higher rent and site values from public works and civic services, so to avoid this subsidy, their land value should be tapped for public revenue.…
  - why: The essay explains that public works increase land values, justifying the capture of that rent.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“shift public revenue to pollution”**
  - sentence: …To truly defend liberty and human rights, the state should abolish the tax on fuel and shift public revenue to pollution, congestion, and benefits received.…
  - why: The essay argues for replacing taxes on fuel (labor/enterprise) with taxes on land and pollution, embodying the tax-land-not-labor principle.

### [Scenarios for George's “Interest on Capital”](https://www.progress.org/articles/scenarios-for-georges-interest-on-capital/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“tax capital would discourage”**
  - sentence: …Claiming that to tax capital would discourage the incentive to invest in productive activity, he viewed capital as aiming to produce wealth, not to aggrandize itself by predatory, exploitative practices (apart from monopolies).…
  - why: The essay distinguishes productive capital (tools, savings) from monopoly rent, countering the claim that land is economically indistinguishable from capital.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“no corporate profits”**
  - sentence: …All are land users, but the landowner "as owner" is using his land ownership/monopoly powers to underpay the four employees.…
  - why: The essay argues that after deducting wages and the normal return on capital, remaining corporate profits are unearned rent, supporting the taxation of excess returns.
- **counters** [`objections/marshall-single-tax-objection`](https://progress.org/wiki/marshall-single-tax-objection/) — anchor: **“not “interest on capital.””**
  - sentence: …And since the plantation owner also actively manages the land, he is also a land user, like his four slaves.…
  - why: The essay explicitly separates the return to labor/management from the residual net income, countering Marshall's view that land rent functions as a necessary incentive.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“No tax on anyones wages”**
  - sentence: …Result: No tax on anyones wages, and no tax on their “interest on capital.”…
  - why: The scenarios illustrate the Single Tax narrative by exempting wages and true capital returns from taxation while targeting unearned monopoly income.

### [Sharing the Value of Land: The Promise of Land-Use Rights](https://www.progress.org/articles/sharing-the-value-of-land-the-promise-of-land-use-rights/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“Land values are community-generated”**
  - sentence: …Land values are community-generated: hospitals, schools, businesses, and so forth make nearby properties more valuable.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that location value is produced by public investment and the surrounding community.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“hospitals, schools, businesses”**
  - sentence: …Land values are community-generated: hospitals, schools, businesses, and so forth make nearby properties more valuable.…
  - why: The essay cites specific public goods and services as the source of increased land value.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“Land values should therefore belong”**
  - sentence: …Land values should therefore belong to those communities that create that value.…
  - why: The essay makes the moral case that rising land values are created by the community, not the owner.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“drastically lower the selling price”**
  - sentence: …Land-use rights drastically lower the selling price of land; buyers would require little financing, if any, to purchase properties tied to strong land-use rights.…
  - why: The essay argues that capturing land value lowers the upfront cost of land, making property acquisition more affordable.

### [Should Hate Speech be Banned?](https://www.progress.org/articles/should-hate-speech-be-banned/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“The law should prohibit”**
  - sentence: …The law should prohibit coercive harm to others regardless of which group categories someone belongs to.…
  - why: The essay argues for a minimal state that only prohibits coercive harm, rejecting the expansion of state power into regulating speech, which counters the objection that Georgism is a form of socialism
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“coercive harm to others”**
  - sentence: …The law should prohibit coercive harm to others regardless of which group categories someone belongs to.…
  - why: The essay's reliance on Locke's natural moral law to define the limits of state action supports the Georgist narrative that taxation should be limited to recovering value created by harm or exclusion,
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“arbitrary whims and beliefs”**
  - sentence: …If offensive speech or behavior is prohibited, then the law is based on the arbitrary whims and beliefs of anyone.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice objection by showing how laws based on subjective offense rather than objective harm lead to arbitrary and contradictory government enforcement.

### [Should there be demand-based recurring fees on ENS domains?](https://www.progress.org/articles/should-there-be-demand-based-recurring-fees-on-ens-domains/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“speculators do not actually”**
  - sentence: …Problem 2: speculators do not actually create efficient markets…
  - why: The essay uses auction theory to argue that profit-maximizing speculators often withhold assets to maximize revenue, reducing social welfare rather than improving allocation.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“reliance on secondary markets”**
  - sentence: …In fact, there are deep economic reasons to believe that reliance on secondary markets makes domains more expensive than a well-designed in-protocol mechanism.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates how capturing existing wealth (domain squatting) pays better than creating new value, leading to inefficient allocation and higher costs.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“time-unlimited allocation of a finite resource”**
  - sentence: …This is a two-line mathematical theorem, and it feels too simple to be saying anything important. But it actually gets at a crucial truth: time-unlimited allocation of a finite resource is incompatible with fairness across long time horizon…
  - why: The essay applies the Georgist concept of the unearned increment to digital assets, arguing that permanent ownership of finite resources inevitably leads to unfairness over time.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“fundamental tradeoff between strength”**
  - sentence: …Problem 1: there is a fundamental tradeoff between strength of property rights and fairness…
  - why: The essay supports the Georgist view that absolute property rights conflict with fairness, suggesting that recurring fees (like LVT) are necessary to balance these competing demands.

### [Singapore: Economic Prosperity through Innovative Land Policy](https://www.progress.org/articles/singapore-economic-prosperity-through-innovative-land-policy/)
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“Electronic Road Pricing”**
  - sentence: …Electronic Road Pricing (ERP) charges drivers each time they pass through gantries on heavily-used roads, with prices being higher during rush periods.…
  - why: describes Singapore's ERP system as a successful application of Georgist analysis that maintains traffic speeds
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“shifting the tax base off workers”**
  - sentence: …Thus, we observe that shifting the tax base off workers & their savings can free labour and capital to be put to their most productive use, generating prosperity for the nation as whole.…
  - why: argues that capturing land rent allows Singapore to maintain low taxes on labor and capital, boosting productivity
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“no private landowner should benefit”**
  - sentence: …Lee Kuan Yew explained that the above policies were explicitly intended to prevent landowners receiving unearned windfalls: “ First, that no private landowner should benefit from development which had taken place at public expense...…
  - why: cites Lee Kuan Yew's policy goal to prevent private owners from capturing value created by public development
- **counters** [`objections/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap/) — anchor: **“subsidised housing”**
  - sentence: …We will look at the way in which Singapore’s land was restored into public hands, deployed to build wealth, and redistributed through a near-universal program of subsidised housing.…
  - why: uses Singapore's universal subsidized housing program to rebut the objection that land capture fails to make housing affordable

### [Slaves to the System](https://www.progress.org/articles/slaves-to-the-system/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Greek banks are stricken”**
  - sentence: …The government has been forced to slash spending at the same time Greek banks are stricken with non-performing loans.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the systemic impact of bank credit conditions on the economy, specifically how non-performing loans and tight credit cause depression.
- **supports** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“Banks create the money supply”**
  - sentence: …Banks create the money supply when they extend loans because they create credit out of nothing.…
  - why: The essay explicitly supports the credit-school view by arguing that economic growth and cycles are driven by bank credit creation rather than land speculation.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“protect the status quo”**
  - sentence: …They protect the status quo that is enriching their clients and cronies.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative that powerful institutions (central banks) act to protect entrenched interests and suppress beneficial alternatives.

### [Smart Talk with Ed Dodson & Fred Harrison](https://www.progress.org/articles/smart-talk-with-ed-dodson-fred-harrison/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“rent-seeking behavior is on full display”**
  - sentence: …Harrison also points out that rent-seeking behavior is on full display by the current American president, which should be continually brought to attention and explained on a daily basis by Georgists in the news media, blogs, social media, a…
  - why: The essay cites Harrison identifying contemporary political rent-seeking as a key issue Georgists should highlight.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“disenchantment with mainstream economics”**
  - sentence: …According the Fred Harrison, not as much as it should and the reason is to be found not in the relevance of the message but in the way it is being articulated (by the followers of Henry George).…
  - why: The essay references the public's loss of faith in mainstream economics, validating the narrative that the current economic paradigm is failing.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“Georgist philosophy making new inroads”**
  - sentence: …Is the Georgist philosophy making new inroads in public policy?…
  - why: The essay opens by questioning the policy adoption of Georgism, directly addressing the lack of widespread implementation.

### [So You Want to Abolish Property Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/so-you-want-to-abolish-property-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/property-tax-raises-welfare-developing`](https://progress.org/wiki/property-tax-raises-welfare-developing/) — anchor: **“Property taxes (best)”**
  - sentence: …An OECD report ranks different taxes by which are the most harmful to growth: Corporate taxes (worst) Personal income taxes Consumption/sales taxes Property taxes (best)…
  - why: The essay cites OECD rankings to argue property taxes are the least harmful tax for economic growth compared to alternatives like sales and income taxes.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“Alaska permanent fund”**
  - sentence: …The other red state with an Oil & Gas fund, Alaska, has Texas beat in this regard: The Alaska permanent fund pays out a proper citizen’s dividend to all Alaskans, regardless of class or home ownership status.…
  - why: The essay uses Alaska's Permanent Fund as a successful example of distributing resource rents to citizens, contrasting it with Texas's approach.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“Property taxes (best)”**
  - sentence: …An OECD report ranks different taxes by which are the most harmful to growth: Corporate taxes (worst) Personal income taxes Consumption/sales taxes Property taxes (best)…
  - why: The essay argues that property taxes are economically superior to sales and income taxes, countering the objection that land value taxes (a form of property tax) are not distinct or beneficial.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“pays out a proper citizen’s dividend”**
  - sentence: …The other red state with an Oil & Gas fund, Alaska, has Texas beat in this regard: The Alaska permanent fund pays out a proper citizen’s dividend to all Alaskans, regardless of class or home ownership status.…
  - why: The essay advocates for distributing resource rents equally to all citizens as a fairer alternative to selective tax relief, supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce inequality.

### [Socialism vs. The Single Tax, Pt. 1](https://www.progress.org/articles/socialism-vs-the-single-tax-pt-1/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Individualism and socialism are in truth”**
  - sentence: …He believed in both individualism and socialism, a synthesis that is the very heart of his work: “Individualism and socialism are in truth not antagonistic but correlative.…
  - why: The essay explicitly contrasts the Single Tax with Socialism, quoting George's argument that they are complementary rather than opposed, countering the objection that Georgism is a form of socialism.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“abolition of all taxes on production”**
  - sentence: …The abolition of all taxes on production (i.e. all taxes) it is claimed, would permanently raise demand for workers, ending unemployment.…
  - why: The text outlines the core Single Tax mechanism—shifting taxes off production to solve unemployment—illustrating the central narrative of the movement.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“Panic of 1893 was one of a succession”**
  - sentence: …The Panic of 1893 was one of a succession of crashes punctuating the 'Long Depression' of 1873-1896.…
  - why: The essay cites the recurring crashes of the Long Depression as the historical context for the labor question, illustrating the Georgist narrative of land-driven cycles.
- **illustrates** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“choice has to be made between out and out Socialism”**
  - sentence: …Others were not convinced - “the choice has to be made between out and out Socialism and mere tinkering with taxation”.…
  - why: The essay documents the socialist rejection of the Single Tax as insufficient, illustrating the Marxist critique that Georgism fails to address class conflict.

### [Socialism vs. the Single Tax, Pt. 2](https://www.progress.org/articles/socialism-vs-the-single-tax-pt-2/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“The Single Tax is simply”**
  - sentence: …The Single Tax is simply the crowning of capitalism.…
  - why: The essay provides a verbatim historical record of the Marxist critique that Georgism merely patches capitalism rather than abolishing class exploitation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“The Single Tax is progressive”**
  - sentence: …The Single Tax is progressive.…
  - why: Post explicitly distinguishes the Single Tax from Socialism, arguing it is a progressive reform rather than a revolutionary state takeover.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“remove all incentive to hold land out of use”**
  - sentence: …As you take away land values you remove all incentive to hold land out of use.…
  - why: Post argues that removing land values via taxation eliminates the incentive to withhold land from productive use.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“remove all taxation from industry”**
  - sentence: …One effect of taxing land value would be to remove all taxation from industry.…
  - why: The text advocates shifting the tax burden off industry and labor onto land value to stimulate demand for labor.

### [Socialize the Rent](https://www.progress.org/articles/socialize-the-rent/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“socialize their rents”**
  - sentence: …We don’t need to socialize land or privatize natural resources, but we do need to socialize their rents which rightfully belong—equally—in the US to all the people of the USA, and to the people of all other countries.…
  - why: The essay argues for capturing resource rents for the public benefit, framing it as a necessary alternative to traditional socialism.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“public capture of economic rent”**
  - sentence: …For the sake of justice, and to put an end to economic imperialism, the USA must come to acknowledge the need for the public capture of economic rent.…
  - why: The essay contends that capturing economic rent is essential to check capitalism and economic imperialism.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“creation of money for rent-seekers”**
  - sentence: …That capitalism has now morphed completely into the creation of money for rent-seekers needs to be addressed and remedied urgently.…
  - why: The essay identifies the dominance of rent-seeking as a cause of economic retrogression that must be remedied.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“bring banking, rent-seeking private companies”**
  - sentence: …It would also bring banking, rent-seeking private companies and tax havens to heel.…
  - why: The essay describes an economy dominated by rent-seeking institutions that need to be reined in.

### [Some Initial Thoughts on Abundance](https://www.progress.org/articles/some-initial-thoughts-on-abundance/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“land value tax shifts”**
  - sentence: …In Pittsburgh , Allentown, Harrisburg and more, economic research has shown that land value tax shifts lead to significant increases in development .…
  - why: The essay cites specific cities where LVT shifts led to more development, evidencing the claim.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“Reward development, discourage blight”**
  - sentence: …We can decrease taxes on buildings and offset that with an increased tax on land.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes onto land rewards development and discourages blight, supporting the mechanism by which LVT improves affordability.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Abundance is incomplete without land value tax”**
  - sentence: …Abundance is incomplete without land value tax es.…
  - why: The essay asserts that LVT is a necessary component of the abundance agenda even alongside zoning reform, countering the objection that LVT is useless without it.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“shifting taxes off buildings onto land”**
  - sentence: …The abundance movement needs to include shifting taxes off buildings onto land.…
  - why: The essay frames the housing solution around correcting tax incentives on land, aligning with the narrative that the crisis is fundamentally a land issue.

### [Some Thoughts On Using Auctions For Land Valuation](https://www.progress.org/articles/some-thoughts-on-using-auctions-for-land-valuation/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“difficulty of assessing land values”**
  - sentence: …However, some people argue that the difficulty of assessing land values makes land value tax ation impractical.…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific auction mechanism to solve the valuation problem, directly countering the objection that accurate assessment is impossible.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“without any need for land value assessors”**
  - sentence: …For one, I’ll show that these auctions achieve reasonably-accurate valuations of the land without any need for land value assessors.…
  - why: The proposal aims to eliminate the role of government assessors, thereby removing the potential for corrupt or politically pressured assessments.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“fix the holdout problem”**
  - sentence: …This rule is required to fix the holdout problem.…
  - why: The essay argues that automatically increasing valuations prevents owners from holding land with artificially low taxes, thereby dampening speculative holding.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“people can upper-bound what taxes they will pay”**
  - sentence: …Third, people can upper-bound what taxes they will pay every year, there are no surprises due to changes in land assessment.…
  - why: The essay highlights that the auction system allows owners to reset their valuation, offering a mechanism to control tax burdens during a transition.

### [Speculation and the Price of Oil](https://www.progress.org/articles/speculation-and-the-price-of-oil/)
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“Install toll charges on all crowded streets”**
  - sentence: …Install toll charges on all crowded streets and highways just high enough to eliminate the traffic congestion.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for congestion pricing to eliminate traffic, mirroring the claim's central thesis.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“Taxes on fuel would be replaced with pollution charges”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on fuel would be replaced with pollution charges.…
  - why: The essay argues for replacing fuel taxes with direct pollution charges to address environmental externalities, supporting the mechanism of carbon pricing.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“urban trains and busses raise land rent”**
