  - sentence: …If they are useful, urban trains and busses raise land rent, and so they should be paid for by tapping the site rents of the communities.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the Henry George Theorem by noting that public transit increases land value, which should be captured to fund the service.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“economic rent of oil, the gains beyond normal costs”**
  - sentence: …Proclaim to the world that the economic rent of oil, the gains beyond normal costs, should be shared worldwide rather than go to the pockets of those who inherited conquered oil lands.…
  - why: The essay advocates for capturing the economic rent of oil resources for public benefit rather than private gain, aligning with the claim.

### [Spray! BAM! Thank you, Uncle Sam!](https://www.progress.org/articles/spray-bam-thank-you-uncle-sam/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“allocated $500,000 to pay”**
  - sentence: …The CDFA does pay attention to the view of the public, so it has allocated $500,000 to pay a public relations firm to spread propaganda among the public on the benefits of the spraying.…
  - why: illustrates government using funds to manipulate public perception rather than respond to it
- **illustrates** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“contributed to political campaigns”**
  - sentence: …The owner of the product, perhaps by sheer coincidence, has contributed to political campaigns, including $144,000 to the governor in his most recent political campaign, and money to members of the state assembly’s agricultural committee.…
  - why: shows how government spending programs invite rent-seeking and political influence
- **illustrates** [`objections/symmetry-decrement-objection`](https://progress.org/wiki/symmetry-decrement-objection/) — anchor: **“difficult to sue for damages”**
  - sentence: …Unfortunately, when the state does the spraying and it causes health and environmental problems, it is difficult to sue for damages, since government authorities are immune from prosecution in the conduct of their official tasks.…
  - why: demonstrates the asymmetry where the state can inflict harm without compensating victims

### [Spreading But Not Eliminating Risk](https://www.progress.org/articles/spreading-but-not-eliminating-risk/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“secondary market in mortgages”**
  - sentence: …The US government has subsidized residential real estate loans by promoting a secondary market in mortgages.…
  - why: The essay details how government-sponsored enterprises expanded the financial system specifically through mortgage credit, evidencing the claim that modern banking growth is tied to land.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“artificially pumps up land values”**
  - sentence: …What financial commentators are missing is that the whole tax and money system artificially pumps up land values to unsustainable heights.…
  - why: The essay argues that government subsidies and tax breaks inflate land values unsustainably, leading to systemic financial tremors, which supports the narrative that land speculation drives boom-bust 
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“spreads the risk among many players”**
  - sentence: …This secondary market spreads the risk among many players, but it does not eliminate the risk, and because the risk becomes deeply embedded in the international bond and stock markets, when there are massive defaults, the entire financial s…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that financial speculation on land is useful or stabilizing, showing instead that spreading risk via mortgage securities amplifies systemic instability.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“stopped the panic for now”**
  - sentence: …This action has stopped the panic for now, but there is a more fundamental problem than loose lending policies and uninformed buyers.…
  - why: The essay dismisses temporary government interventions like bailouts as insufficient to address the fundamental structural problems driving the crisis.

### [Stamps for Half of Forever](https://www.progress.org/articles/stamps-for-half-of-forever/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Government guarantees are never absolute”**
  - sentence: …Government guarantees are never absolute, so it is possible that in some distant day, if the US switches to the metric system for postage weights, the forever stamps would no longer be valid for the current postage rate.…
  - why: The essay uses the potential invalidation of 'forever' stamps to illustrate the risk of government changing rules, countering the idea that policy tools like LVT are useless without prior structural c
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“Any devaluation or inflation is a tax”**
  - sentence: …Any devaluation or inflation is a tax on money holdings, a cruel tax that wipes out savings and unfairly rewards going into debt.…
  - why: The essay argues that inflation acts as a hidden tax on money holders, supporting the narrative that shifting to non-fiat, service-backed currencies could avoid this unavoidable tax.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“wipes out savings and unfairly rewards”**
  - sentence: …Any devaluation or inflation is a tax on money holdings, a cruel tax that wipes out savings and unfairly rewards going into debt.…
  - why: By highlighting how inflation destroys savings, the essay supports the Georgist argument for capturing rent to fund dividends that protect citizens from such monetary instability.

### [Status Update, 2016: the real estate boom and bust](https://www.progress.org/articles/status-update-2016-the-real-estate-boom-and-bust/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“18-year real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …Those interested in the 18-year real estate cycle often ask for a status report.…
  - why: The essay explicitly references and tracks the timing of the recurring real estate cycle.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pumping up land rent”**
  - sentence: …The main intervention is the public goods paid mostly from taxing labor, which depresses net wages while pumping up land rent and value.…
  - why: The essay argues that public spending on goods, funded by labor taxes, increases land values.
- **supports** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“fueled by cheap credit”**
  - sentence: …Every real estate boom in the USA has been fueled by cheap credit, and interest rates continue to be very low, by the policy of the Federal Reserve.…
  - why: The essay emphasizes that cheap credit is the primary fuel for real estate booms.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“day of reckoning is coming”**
  - sentence: …The day of reckoning is coming, but it is still a decade away, as the real estate cycle is on its historic track.…
  - why: The author predicts a future downturn based on cycle timing, contradicting the view that bubbles cannot be identified in advance.

### [Stop incentivizing surface parking lots](https://www.progress.org/articles/stop-incentivizing-surface-parking-lots/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“parking lots that steadily drain”**
  - sentence: …Walking Syracuse in person, I experienced the pervasiveness of parking lots that steadily drain economic opportunity away from the city’s most valuable locations.…
  - why: The essay uses Syracuse surface lots as a prime example of land underuse persisting in high-demand areas due to distorted incentives.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“leaving the lot empty is easy”**
  - sentence: …The reason is because leaving the lot empty is easy–the parking lot owner generates steady revenue, and all the while the land underneath the parking lot continues to rise in value, yielding speculative gains.…
  - why: The essay argues that low holding costs (specifically property taxes) encourage owners to hold land idle for speculative gain rather than developing it.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“property taxes are far lower”**
  - sentence: …Most importantly, their property taxes are far lower than if they developed the lot, as the property tax taxes both land and improvements.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the tax burden on developed vs. undeveloped land to explain why current property taxes discourage construction.
- **illustrates** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“actively subtracts value”**
  - sentence: …Because in economic terms, a parking lot doesn’t simply fail to add value; it actively subtracts value.…
  - why: The essay quantifies the 'net contribution' of land uses, showing that idle land in prime locations reduces overall economic output.

### [Strategy for Tax Reform: Convert the Chiefs](https://www.progress.org/articles/strategy-for-tax-reform-convert-the-chiefs/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“replacing all taxes with”**
  - sentence: …For those who have not yet been introduced, Henry George was the economist associated with the explanation and advocacy of replacing all taxes with a single levy on land rent, which includes property taxes on land value or rent, tapping the…
  - why: The essay explicitly defines Henry George's proposal as replacing all taxes with a single levy on land rent, which is the core definition of the Single Tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal distribution of land rent”**
  - sentence: …Some Georgists reject the term “tax,” because the public collection and equal distribution of land rent is not an arbitrary levy, but a proper initial distribution, which would provide many economic benefits, including a greater equality of…
  - why: The essay describes the geoist policy of collecting and distributing land rent equally to end poverty, directly supporting the citizens dividend narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“prosperity tax shift”**
  - sentence: …Send them three documents, both as emails and on paper. a one-page summary explanation of a prosperity tax shift and its social benefits; a one-page legislative strategy for implementing the tax shift; a one-page explanation of why the tax …
  - why: The essay advocates for a 'prosperity tax shift'—shifting taxes to land—as the central message for reform, aligning with the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“convert the chiefs of government”**
  - sentence: …An effective strategy for tax reform could be to seek to convert the chiefs of government.…
  - why: The essay proposes a strategy of converting political elites to enact reform, which parallels the historical narrative of Georgism's influence during the Progressive Era.

### [Subsidies, Politics, and Pollution](https://www.progress.org/articles/subsidies-politics-and-pollution/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“land always takes the gains”**
  - sentence: …This being an immutable law of economics, shows further the folly of the scheme in the first place: Not only do subsidies distort markets and provide improper signals to producers and consumers, they also, ultimately, fail to help the inten…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete case study of how a subsidy intended for farmers was entirely captured by landlords through increased rents.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“using public funds to buy an election”**
  - sentence: …As a means of using public funds to buy an election, this is as blatant as it gets.…
  - why: The essay describes a government subsidy scheme designed to secure political power rather than create economic value, leading to fiscal loss and instability.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“rice-pledging scheme around $400 USD$40 USD”**
  - sentence: …A local paper reported in 2013: rice-pledging scheme around $400 USD$40 USD, or 10%1 rai = 1600 sq. m…
  - why: The text demonstrates that land values are observable and responsive to market changes, as evidenced by the reported rise in rents following the subsidy.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“Environmental destruction”**
  - sentence: …Environmental destruction Having to pay higher rents, farmers were forced to use any means necessary to improve yields.…
  - why: The essay links the failure to capture economic rent (which went to landlords) to environmental degradation, as farmers resorted to heavy chemical use to afford the rent.

### [Superbugs in Indian Chicken…and Global Property](https://www.progress.org/articles/superbugs-in-indian-chickenand-global-property/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“swift transmission from market to market”**
  - sentence: …We can think of the global property market in a similar way to the superbug problem afflicting chickens. First the rise with incredible demand, then the swift transmission from market to market, and then sickness, as it goes right over the …
  - why: The essay explicitly compares the global property boom to a contagion, illustrating the narrative that land speculation spreads and creates instability.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“the only thing that changes is access to finance”**
  - sentence: …Most of these real estate deals are swapping an existing asset. It’s a round robin, where the only thing that changes is access to finance.…
  - why: The essay argues that the real estate boom is driven primarily by credit expansion and debt rather than productive investment, supporting the claim that finance growth is land credit.
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“already at extremely high prices for locals”**
  - sentence: …On the frontline of this wave are the cities you already know about: Sydney, Vancouver, Hong Kong and London. These are already at extremely high prices for locals.…
  - why: The essay cites specific cities where property prices have skyrocketed due to global demand, evidencing the claim that housing unaffordability is driven by land values.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“pointing to a massive boom generating worldwide”**
  - sentence: …And, as we keep saying, everything we see is pointing to a massive boom generating worldwide.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies the 'New Era' thinking narrative by predicting a massive, sustained global boom while warning it cannot last indefinitely.

### [Sustainability Squared](https://www.progress.org/articles/sustainability-squared/)
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“collecting resource rents”**
  - sentence: …The Georgist remedy of collecting resource rents in lieu of taxing production is central to this shift.…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the Georgist remedy of collecting resource rents as the central mechanism for shifting to a sustainable, labor-intensive economy.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“new development sprawls”**
  - sentence: …Yet these days, while new development sprawls into farmland and woodland, large areas of older central cities decay and empty out.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the inefficiency of sprawl with the sustainability of compact cities, implicitly supporting the Georgist argument that land value taxation discourages sprawl.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“encourages development of new products”**
  - sentence: …Such specialization allows enormous gains in productivity, and encourages development of new products and new technology.…
  - why: The essay argues that dense cities foster productivity and innovation through the division of labor, supporting the claim that efficient land use (encouraged by taxing land) raises productivity.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxing production is central”**
  - sentence: …The Georgist remedy of collecting resource rents in lieu of taxing production is central to this shift.…
  - why: The essay advocates for shifting the tax burden off production (labor) and onto resource rents (land), which is the core definition of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.

### [Take “The Donald” Seriously? Yes, and Here’s Why.](https://www.progress.org/articles/take-the-donald-seriously-yes-and-heres-why/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“the real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …But besides Donald Trump, it’s got absolutely everything in it to help you learn about the real estate cycle.…
  - why: The essay uses Trump's career and the documentary to exemplify the mechanics and dynamics of the real estate cycle.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“control of an asset”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The real estate game is now much more about the ‘control’ of an asset, not ownership.…
  - why: This passage illustrates the rentier economy concept by highlighting how power and profit derive from controlling assets rather than productive ownership.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“Use your political connections”**
  - sentence: …As Trump himself says about the task of real estate work: No money down. Juggle the facts where necessary. Use your political connections to the utmost.…
  - why: Trump's described methods exemplify rent-seeking behavior—using political influence and manipulation to extract wealth rather than creating value.

### [Tax Reformers Propose Fiscal Suicide for California](https://www.progress.org/articles/tax-reformers-propose-fiscal-suicide-for-california/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“efficiency tax shift”**
  - sentence: …If the Commission were true to its aims, it should have proposed an efficiency tax shift, the replacement of the state’s income, sales, and property taxes with three truly 21st century revenue sources: a pollution tax, a levy on land value,…
  - why: The essay argues that replacing distortionary taxes (income, sales) with non-distortionary ones (land value, pollution) would eliminate deadweight loss and boost growth.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“dare not propose”**
  - sentence: …Why did the geniuses of the Commission on the 21st Century Economy propose a net receipts tax? Because they dare not propose to reform the state’s dysfunctional property tax.…
  - why: The essay identifies political resistance to property tax reform (Prop 13) as the reason for adopting a harmful alternative, implying that land value tax is politically difficult but economically supe
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“volcanic eruption of growth”**
  - sentence: …These would have no deadweight loss, and the quantum leap of that tax reform would be a volcanic eruption of growth, employment, and higher wages.…
  - why: The essay claims that shifting taxes to land value would result in a massive increase in economic growth and employment.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“levy on land value”**
  - sentence: …If the Commission were true to its aims, it should have proposed an efficiency tax shift, the replacement of the state’s income, sales, and property taxes with three truly 21st century revenue sources: a pollution tax, a levy on land value,…
  - why: The essay advocates replacing taxes on transactions and income (labor/capital) with a levy on land value as a superior fiscal policy.

### [Taxation by Citation](https://www.progress.org/articles/taxation-by-citation/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“shatters trust between residents”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …He wrote that excessive citations and fines “shatters trust” between residents and the police.…
  - why: The essay describes how governments using fines for revenue rather than safety creates predatory policing and erodes social trust, exemplifying how rent-seeking harms the social fabric.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replaces taxes on productive work”**
  - sentence: …Only a tax reform that replaces taxes on productive work with taxes on government subsidies will provide the revenue that eliminates the financial incentive for taxation by citation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that shifting the tax base from labor to land rent would eliminate the perverse incentives that drive revenue-motivated policing.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“public revenue from land rent”**
  - sentence: …In plain language, public revenue from land rent should replace punitive revenue, whether from excessive traffic fines or taxes imposed on labor and goods.…
  - why: The essay posits land rent as a viable alternative revenue source capable of replacing current distortionary taxes and fines.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“local governments were making up”**
  - sentence: …Safety concerns had not changed; local governments were making up for lost revenue with high fines for revenue, when the fines had previously been set to promote safety.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of government failure—using police power for revenue extraction—which supports the public-choice objection that government actors act in self-interest.

### [Teach Natural Law](https://www.progress.org/articles/teach-natural-law/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“Evil has roamed the earth”**
  - sentence: …Evil has roamed the earth since ancient times, as empires conquer and extinguish more peaceful and less powerful tribes.…
  - why: The essay's historical account of conquest and dispossection supports the narrative that land titles originate in force and theft.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“an equal share of natural benefits”**
  - sentence: …What people do have a right to is the wage and wealth of their own labor, and an equal share of natural benefits, as measured by what people pay for natural opportunities, namely land rent.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for a moral right to an equal share of natural benefits (land rent), which is the core of the citizens dividend narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“wage and wealth of their own labor”**
  - sentence: …What people do have a right to is the wage and wealth of their own labor, and an equal share of natural benefits, as measured by what people pay for natural opportunities, namely land rent.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes between earned wealth from labor and unearned natural benefits, supporting the moral case for the unearned increment.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“extinguish more peaceful and less powerful tribes”**
  - sentence: …Evil has roamed the earth since ancient times, as empires conquer and extinguish more peaceful and less powerful tribes.…
  - why: The essay's opening examples of conquest and extermination illustrate the historical injustices at the heart of the land justice narrative.

### [Ten Things That Would Happen If We Shared the Value of Land](https://www.progress.org/articles/ten-things-that-would-happen-if-we-shared-the-value-of-land/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“insanely profitable—and highly speculative—mortgage business”**
  - sentence: …Banks would no longer be able to participate in the insanely profitable—and highly speculative—mortgage business.…
  - why: The essay argues that modern banking is dominated by speculative mortgage lending, which would be curtailed by land value capture.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“money would no longer flow into speculative real estate”**
  - sentence: …The economy would experience a tremendous boost as money would no longer flow into speculative real estate assets (i.e. land) and instead flow into productive capital.…
  - why: The essay claims that sharing land value would divert capital away from land speculation and into productive investment.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“existing land would be used far more efficiently”**
  - sentence: …Urban sprawl would come to an end as existing land would be used far more efficiently.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value sharing would end sprawl by incentivizing efficient use of existing land.

### [Territocracy](https://www.progress.org/articles/territocracy/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“hidden in economic thought”**
  - sentence: …Land is paradoxically visible in nature but hidden in economic thought by neoclassically merging it with “capital,” homogenized into a symbol, a big capital K.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that neoclassical economics obscures the unique nature of land by merging it with capital, directly supporting the narrative of the 'Corruption of Economics'.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“rule by the big landowners”**
  - sentence: …Territocracy means “rule by the big landowners.”…
  - why: The essay defines and describes 'territocracy'—the control of government by landowners—which substantively supports the claim that the economy is ruled by rentiers.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“abolition of taxes on labor and capital”**
  - sentence: …The state-based remedy is the abolition of taxes on labor and capital, both in income and sales, replaced by a tapping of land rentals or land values, exclusive of any improvements or labor services.…
  - why: The essay advocates for replacing taxes on labor and capital with land value collection to eliminate deadweight loss, directly supporting this benefit claim.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tapping of land rentals”**
  - sentence: …The state-based remedy is the abolition of taxes on labor and capital, both in income and sales, replaced by a tapping of land rentals or land values, exclusive of any improvements or labor services.…
  - why: The essay's core proposal to shift the tax burden from labor and capital onto land values is the central thesis of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.

### [That Big Stock Market Drop](https://www.progress.org/articles/that-big-stock-market-drop/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …I have been forecasting a recession around 2008, based on the real estate cycle.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the business cycle is driven by the real estate cycle, predicting a recession because real estate peaked in 2006.
- **supports** [`objections/stock-crash-caused-great-depression`](https://progress.org/wiki/stock-crash-caused-great-depression/) — anchor: **“Every peak in real estate”**
  - sentence: …Every peak in real estate has been followed by a major depression.…
  - why: The essay posits that peaks in real estate are the historical precursors to major depressions, countering the view that stock crashes alone cause economic downturns.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“forecasting a recession”**
  - sentence: …I have been forecasting a recession around 2008, based on the real estate cycle.…
  - why: The author claims to have successfully forecasted the recession using the real estate cycle, contradicting the Greenspan doctrine that bubbles cannot be identified in advance.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“no way to avoid”**
  - sentence: …So long as these are in place, there is no way to avoid the next recession, which will take most people by surprise, but not you!…
  - why: The essay asserts that deep structural factors make the next recession inevitable, countering the notion that government intervention can permanently tame the business cycle.

### [The "Gore Tax"](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-gore-tax/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“taxing emissions is superior”**
  - sentence: …They show how taxing emissions is superior to pollution permit trading.…
  - why: The essay cites Laffer Associates' argument that carbon taxes are more efficient than cap-and-trade systems.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“green tax shift”**
  - sentence: …If Al Gore had made climate change the single top priority during his Vice Presidency, he would have promoted the green tax shift, and then the "Gore tax" would have been a tax on polluting emissions.…
  - why: The essay advocates for shifting taxes onto pollution (ecological rent) as a primary solution to climate change.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“fully offset by a static”**
  - sentence: …They propose that pollution taxes be "fully offset by a static dollar-for-dollar across-the-board reduction in marginal income tax rates."…
  - why: The essay endorses the economic argument that shifting taxes from income to pollution (a form of rent) improves efficiency.

### [The 100-year Flood](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-100-year-flood/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“subsidy is capitalized into”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Any subsidy for insurance is capitalized into higher land values.…
  - why: The essay argues that government subsidies for flood control or insurance are ultimately captured by landowners through higher land values, illustrating the capitalization of public benefits.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“separate values for the land”**
  - sentence: …Also, since only the building is insured and not the land, the insurance company will appraise the real estate with separate values for the land and for the vulnerable improvements.…
  - why: The essay provides a market-based example where insurance companies necessarily separate land value from improvement value, countering the objection that land value cannot be assessed.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“subsidy to landownership”**
  - sentence: …What looks like subsidized flood insurance is in reality a subsidy to landownership.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that public interventions (like subsidies) transfer value to landowners rather than serving the intended recipients, highlighting the dynamics of value creation and ca
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“capitalized into lower land rents”**
  - sentence: …The higher insurance costs are capitalized into lower land rents and land values.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that market forces (insurance costs) automatically adjust land values without zoning changes, countering the idea that land value mechanisms are secondary to or dependent on zon

### [The 125-Year Conspiracy](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-125-year-conspiracy-against-government-ownership-and-management-of-utilities-and-transportation-networks/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“opposition quickly infiltrated the movement”**
  - sentence: …Anyway, the opposition quickly infiltrated the movement, and right under Henry George's nose, and against his wishes, had the words 'managed by' removed at the second Single Tax Conference, held at the Columbian Exposition in Chicago on Aug…
  - why: provides a specific historical example of internal movement sabotage
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“repeated called George a socialist”**
  - sentence: …The monopolists found it acceptable that government 'maintain and control' (see their revised wording below) the utilities with occasional regulation, and by providing services at the governments' expense, but THEY wanted to retain the powe…
  - why: documents the historical use of the 'socialist' charge against George
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Platform of the Single Tax League”**
  - sentence: …Henry George, Paragraph 11, Platform of the Single Tax League of the Untied States, September 1890.…
  - why: cites the primary platform document defining the Single Tax movement
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“monopolists found it acceptable”**
  - sentence: …The monopolists found it acceptable that government 'maintain and control' (see their revised wording below) the utilities with occasional regulation, and by providing services at the governments' expense, but THEY wanted to retain the powe…
  - why: shows rent-seekers manipulating policy to retain control of public utilities

### [The 18-Year Pattern Predicting 2027's Market Crash](https://www.progress.org/articles/18-6-year-real-estate-cycle/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“boom and bust cycle”**
  - sentence: …It’s a boom and bust cycle driven by our economic relationship to land, called a real estate cycle for simplicity.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that recurring market crashes are driven by a land-based cycle rather than general business cycles.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“bank mortgage lending”**
  - sentence: …Josh Ryan-Collins and colleagues ( Rethinking Land and Housing , 2017) added the credit channel: demonstrating that bank mortgage lending — not just speculative appetite — is the primary modern amplifier…
  - why: The essay cites Ryan-Collins to support its argument that the expansion of banking is primarily mortgage lending against land.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“The cause is rising land prices”**
  - sentence: …The cause is not interest rates, inflation, or political mismanagement. Those are symptoms. The cause is rising land prices — driven by speculation and amplified by bank lending — becoming unsustainable for productive economic activity…
  - why: The essay directly disputes the objection that credit cycles alone drive crashes, asserting land prices are the root cause.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“contradiction itself became evidence”**
  - sentence: …After World War II, Hoyt claimed the cycle was dead — yet simultaneously invested heavily in rising post-war land values, contradicting his own claim. The contradiction itself became evidence that the cycle had not died.…
  - why: The essay uses the Hoyt/Harrison anecdote to refute the claim that government intervention or modern policy has eliminated the land cycle.

### [The 1890 Georgist “Constitution”](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-1890-georgist-constitution/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“raising all public revenues”**
  - sentence: …To carry out these principles we are in favor of raising all public revenues for national, state, county and municipal purposes by a single tax upon land values, irrespective of Improvements, and of the abolition of all forms of direct and …
  - why: The platform explicitly defines the Single Tax proposal to replace all other taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“value which the growth”**
  - sentence: …Therefore, no one should be permitted to hold natural opportunities without a fair return to all for any special privilege thus accorded to him, and that value which the growth and improvement of the community attach to land should be taken…
  - why: The text asserts the core moral claim that community-created land value belongs to the public.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“make it impossible for speculators”**
  - sentence: …It would thus make it impossible for speculators and monopolists to hold natural opportunities unused or only half used, and would throw open to labor the illimitable field of employment which the earth offers to man.…
  - why: The platform argues that taxing land value prevents the profitable holding of idle land.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no tax should be levied”**
  - sentence: …We hold that each man is entitled to all that his labor produces.…
  - why: The platform establishes the ethical and economic distinction between taxing labor (products) and land.

### [The 2007 US Economic Slowdown](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-2007-us-economic-slowdown/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“speculative boom that afflicted”**
  - sentence: …But the same speculative boom that afflicted residential real estate has affected commercial properties.…
  - why: The essay argues that a speculative boom in real estate is the primary driver of the economic slowdown and predicts a commercial crash will follow the residential one.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“credit created by excessive”**
  - sentence: …Prices have zoomed up, fueled by the credit created by excessive monetary expansion.…
  - why: The essay explicitly links the rise in real estate prices to excessive credit expansion, illustrating the mechanism of finance growth being tied to land.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“increases costs for enterprise”**
  - sentence: …High rentals and purchase prices for commercial real estate increases costs for enterprise, which reduces its profits, and thus results in less economic investment.…
  - why: The essay details how high commercial land costs reduce profits and investment, thereby suppressing economic productivity.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“crumbling of the residential sector”**
  - sentence: …The news media have focused on the crumbling of the residential sector, with big declines in sales, a fall in average prices, and growing mortgage defaults especially in the subprime loans.…
  - why: The essay provides a contemporaneous account of the 2007 crisis, illustrating the central role of residential land and construction collapse in the broader economic downturn.

### [The 4 Major Land-Gain Income Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-4-major-land-gain-income-taxes-that-developed-from-georges-two-general-categories-in-the-1890-national-platform/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“calling land “capital” is forbidden”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …we call land-gain income tax #2 the “capital gains tax,” but calling land “capital” is forbidden in Georgist theory, so it should really be called the “land gains tax” or “land sale gains tax:”…
  - why: The essay explicitly rejects the classification of land as capital, arguing that land gains are distinct from capital gains.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“tax on profits from hiring laborers”**
  - sentence: …the tax on profits from hiring laborers is today recognized as the tax on corporate profits, on excessive salaries taken by upper level employees, and on dividends paid out to passive shareholder;…
  - why: The essay categorizes corporate profits and dividends as economic rents derived from land monopoly, supporting the claim that corporate profits increasingly reflect rents.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“bank net profits from mortgage lending”**
  - sentence: …Thus, almost all of bank net profits from mortgage lending is unearned, and someday should be heavily taxed for the general welfare.…
  - why: The essay identifies mortgage lending as a mechanism for extracting unearned income from land, supporting the claim that modern banking growth is tied to land credit.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“not a tax on land”**
  - sentence: …“The single tax we propose is not a tax on land, and therefore would not fall on the use of land and become a tax on labor.”…
  - why: The essay quotes Henry George's distinction between a tax on land and a tax on land value to refute the idea that the Single Tax is merely a standard property tax.

### [The American Common Market - Gone!](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-american-common-market-gone/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“tap the land value”**
  - sentence: …If state governments seek more revenue, the optimal policy is to tap the land value of the state rather than interfere with interstate commerce.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value is the superior source of state revenue compared to sales taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“subsidize land value while imposing”**
  - sentence: …It is of course a political decision to subsidize land value while imposing higher costs on worker-consumers.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the burden on consumers with the potential to tax land, advocating for shifting the tax base.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“optimal policy is to tap”**
  - sentence: …If state governments seek more revenue, the optimal policy is to tap the land value of the state rather than interfere with interstate commerce.…
  - why: The essay presents land value capture as a viable revenue solution without preconditions, countering the idea that it is useless without other reforms.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“optimal policy is to tap”**
  - sentence: …If state governments seek more revenue, the optimal policy is to tap the land value of the state rather than interfere with interstate commerce.…
  - why: The essay suggests land value is sufficient to replace sales tax revenue, countering the objection that it cannot raise enough funds.

### [The American Infrastructure](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-american-infrastructure/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“additional land rent generated”**
  - sentence: …The benefit of a road can be measured by how much extra people are willing to pay to use locations served by the road, i.e. the additional land rent generated by the road.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the benefit of public infrastructure is measured by the land rent it generates.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“pay for a road”**
  - sentence: …The best way to pay for a road and its bridge is from the land rent that gets generated by that infrastructure.…
  - why: The essay advocates funding public works specifically through the land rent they create.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“tolls just high enough”**
  - sentence: …There should be tolls on all streets, highways, and bridges which vary by time and day, with tolls just high enough to prevent congestion.…
  - why: The essay proposes variable tolls to manage congestion, mirroring the claim's mechanism.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land rent that gets generated”**
  - sentence: …The best way to pay for a road and its bridge is from the land rent that gets generated by that infrastructure.…
  - why: By attributing the value of roads to the rent they generate, the essay implies that community infrastructure creates land value.

### [The Austrian School of Economics](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-austrian-school-of-economics/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“Austrian-school analysis shows”**
  - sentence: …Austrian-school analysis shows that only in a pure free market in money and banking can the natural rate of interest emerge to equalize savings and investment and allocate all production between consumption and investment.…
  - why: The essay details the Austrian School's theoretical framework, including the business cycle theory and skepticism of central planning, which forms the basis of their objection to LVT.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“distorting the price structure”**
  - sentence: …The excessive issuance of new money not only raises prices, but also changes relative prices, distorting the price structure, like land values rising much more than the prices of consumer goods or of wages.…
  - why: The essay describes the mechanism by which credit expansion distorts land values and triggers a boom-bust cycle, directly supporting the narrative that land speculation causes economic cycles.
- **supports** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“monetary authority injects excessive new money”**
  - sentence: …But when the monetary authority injects excessive new money into the banking system, to the banks it is like additional savings, as they lower the interest rate to loan out the extra funds.…
  - why: The essay argues that the root cause of the cycle is the injection of new money (credit) by the monetary authority rather than land speculation itself, aligning with the credit-school objection.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“not been assimilated into neoclassical”**
  - sentence: …The conception of capital goods in the Austrian school has not been assimilated into neoclassical thought.…
  - why: The essay highlights a fundamental theoretical divergence between Austrian and neoclassical economics regarding capital structure, illustrating the narrative that mainstream economics has marginalized

### [The Basic Income Guarantee and Tautological Libertarianism](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-basic-income-guarantee-and-tautological-libertarianism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“force of the legal system”**
  - sentence: …Over a long history, property owners use the force of the legal system to force, coerce, or interfere with other people, establishing “property rights” without the consent of or compensation for the people they thereby force into a state of…
  - why: The essay argues that current property rights were established historically by force and interference, stripping the propertyless of their natural liberty to use the earth.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Basic Income Guarantee”**
  - sentence: …The right-libertarian journal, Cato Unbound , has published a 4-party debate on Basic Income Guarantee (BIG) this month.…
  - why: The essay defends a Basic Income Guarantee as a means to relieve the propertyless from forced labor and improve their bargaining power.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“stalking-horse for socialism”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Critics from Frank Knight to apartheid-era South African free-marketeers have branded the single tax a stalking-horse for socialism or Soviet-style collectivism — a charge Georgist…
  - why: The essay critiques right-libertarian definitions that label any redistribution or interference with property rights as 'socialism' or 'slavery'.

### [The Bear Will Get You](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-bear-will-get-you/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“distortions caused by its past policy”**
  - sentence: …But regardless of what the Fed does, the distortions caused by its past policy and indeed by the very existence of money manipulation will inevitably cause a recession.…
  - why: The essay argues that monetary manipulation creates artificial booms and inevitable busts, specifically citing real estate speculation.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“waste of resources cause by investments and speculations such as in real estate”**
  - sentence: …The problem is not just inflation, but a waste of resources cause by investments and speculations such as in real estate, induced by artificially low interest rates that later get choked off when interest rates rise to stop the inflation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies real estate speculation as the central waste of resources driving the cycle, countering the view that credit alone is the driver.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“The bears are coming home”**
  - sentence: …The bears are coming home, but in the real world, there is no where to run.…
  - why: The essay mocks the Fed's attempt to fine-tune the economy, arguing that a recession is inevitable regardless of policy choices.
- **counters** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“The Fed is playing Goldilocks”**
  - sentence: …The Fed is playing Goldilocks, trying to fine-tune the economy to balance inflation and recession, to have an economy that is not too cold or too hot, but in the Goldilocks story, when the bears returned, she ran away.…
  - why: The essay uses the Goldilocks metaphor to ridicule the belief that policymakers can permanently avoid the cycle.

### [The BIG Misunderstanding About the Cost of Universal Basic Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-big-misunderstanding-about-the-cost-of-universal-basic-income/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“eliminate poverty costs”**
  - sentence: …I found that a UBI large enough to eliminate poverty costs on $539 billion per year–less than 16% of its often-mentioned but not-very-meaningful gross cost ($3.415 trillion), less than 25% of the cost of current U.S. entitlement spending, l…
  - why: provides a specific cost estimate for a poverty-eliminating UBI
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“UBI is a negative tax”**
  - sentence: …It fact, it’s not a cost in any meaningful sense, because UBI is a “tax rebate” or “a refundable tax credit.” That is, UBI is a negative tax.…
  - why: frames UBI as a tax rebate rather than a gross expenditure
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“real redistributive cost”**
  - sentence: …A calculation of real redistributive cost of UBI requires subtracting all of that taking-and-giving-back to focus on the net increase in taxes on contributors (or net cuts in other spending) that will be necessary to support the net benefit…
  - why: distinguishes between gross expenditures and net fiscal burden

### [The Blessings of Cash](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-blessings-of-cash/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“cutting marginal tax rates”**
  - sentence: …What is needed is the ultimate supply-side policy, cutting marginal tax rates on labor and investment yields to zero, with a prosperity tax shift, replacing all other taxes with a single tax on land value.…
  - why: The essay argues for eliminating taxes on labor and investment to unshackle the economy, replacing them with a land value tax.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“push land to its most productive use”**
  - sentence: …The land-value tax would also push land to its most productive use, stimulating productive investment and employment.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that a land value tax would force land into its most productive use, thereby stimulating investment.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replacing all other taxes with a single tax on land value”**
  - sentence: …What is needed is the ultimate supply-side policy, cutting marginal tax rates on labor and investment yields to zero, with a prosperity tax shift, replacing all other taxes with a single tax on land value.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting the tax burden entirely off labor and capital onto land value.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“The main resource that cannot hide is land”**
  - sentence: …The main resource that cannot hide is land.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value taxation is the only unevadable tax, contrasting it with the evasion enabled by cash.

### [The Blindspot that has Plagued Humanity for Eons](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-blindspot-that-has-plagued-humanity-for-eons/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“the difference between things of value”**
  - sentence: …Now, due to that blindspot, the difference between things of value that are gifts of nature and things of value that are products of labor and capital carries no weight in our consciousness.…
  - why: The essay argues that society fails to distinguish between nature's gifts and human-made products, which is the core moral argument of the Great Land Robbery narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“the single largest stream in the GDP”**
  - sentence: …It comes as a surprise to most that the latter sort of payment is the single largest stream in the GDP and that it drives the business cycle.…
  - why: The essay asserts that payments for land and resources constitute the largest part of the economy, supporting the narrative that the unearned increment is a dominant, yet overlooked, economic force.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“drives the business cycle”**
  - sentence: …It comes as a surprise to most that the latter sort of payment is the single largest stream in the GDP and that it drives the business cycle.…
  - why: The essay explicitly claims that land payments are the driver of the business cycle, directly supporting the Georgist narrative on land speculation and cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“compensating those whom we exclude”**
  - sentence: …That we pay an absentee owner for land feels fine while the notion of compensating those whom we exclude from our land feels strange.…
  - why: The essay highlights the blindness to the idea that land value belongs to the community (those excluded), contrasting it with the current norm of paying absentee owners.

### [The Burma Slaves](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-burma-slaves/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“military dictators have enslaved”**
  - sentence: …The military dictators have enslaved the entire country, and recently brutally repressed protests led by Buddhist monks.…
  - why: illustrates the concept of dispossession and tyranny associated with the enclosure of common wealth
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“The people are proudly Burmese”**
  - sentence: …The people are proudly Burmese.…
  - why: highlights the conflict between imposed state authority (Myanmar) and the people's identity (Burma)

### [The Case Against the Case Against the Single Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-case-against-the-case-against-the-single-tax/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base/) — anchor: **“value kernel of landed property”**
  - sentence: …Johnson correctly stated that a tax on the entire rent of land would bring the purchase price down to zero, but he expressed it as: "the value kernel of landed property will have been seized by the state."…
  - why: The essay addresses the objection that a full land tax destroys its own base by clarifying that the rental value base remains intact even if the selling price falls to zero.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“propaganda for the universal confiscation”**
  - sentence: …Johnson continues his attack by calling the single tax on land value "propaganda for the universal confiscation of land."…
  - why: The essay rebuts the charge that the Single Tax is socialism or confiscation by arguing that rent belongs to the people and collection is merely the state acting as an agent for the proper owners.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“revenue would cover the costs of government”**
  - sentence: …This despite the statement of George that "I do not propose either to purchase or to confiscate private property in land." Land-value taxation would not disturb private titles; it would not alter private control and possession.…
  - why: The essay disputes the objection that LVT cannot raise enough revenue, noting that total land rent could theoretically cover government costs and be redistributed to the people.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Johnson, along with other economists”**
  - sentence: …Johnson, along with other economists who were turning the classical theories of the 1800s into the neoclassical doctrines of the 1900s, generalized "rent" from the yield of land into any surplus above opportunity cost, i.e. above the cost n…
  - why: The essay illustrates the narrative that early neoclassical economists deliberately obscured the distinct economic nature of land to undermine Henry George's proposals.

### [The Casino Economy](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-casino-economy/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“our economy seems to make”**
  - sentence: …Here's a possible explanation: these days, our economy seems to make no particular connection between effort and reward.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies the rentier economy by describing a system where rewards are disconnected from productive effort.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“rewards those who work”**
  - sentence: …That's the key to creating a dynamic economy that rewards those who work, create, innovate -- not those who pad, skim and rob.…
  - why: The essay contrasts productive work with 'padding, skimming, and robbing,' supporting the claim that rent-seeking drags growth by diverting reward from creation.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“difference between earned and unearned”**
  - sentence: …Our course, Understanding Economics , explores the difference between earned and unearned wealth.…
  - why: The essay explicitly centers on the moral and economic distinction between earned and unearned wealth.

- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“bad policy creates shortages”**

  - sentence: …The market-based pricing of water is often higher than the cost of extraction, and that difference constitutes “economic rent.”…

  - sentence: …If we believe that human beings have equal natural rights, the implication is that the rent of material natural resources as well as spatial land should be shared equally.…

  - sentence: …The effects of market-based water pricing would cascade throughout the economy.…

### [The Commonwealth City](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-commonwealth-city/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“publicly created but privately captured”**
  - sentence: …Urban land values are publicly created but privately captured.…

- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Distributed directly back to residents”**

  - sentence: …Invested in new public goods for city residents - parks, infrastructure, transit, etc.…
  - why: The essay outlines using ground rent to fund public goods, supporting the claim that public investment creates land value that can be captured.

  - sentence: …First, there is no upfront land cost.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing upfront land costs (capitalized rent) benefits developers by eliminating the risk of being underwater, supporting the incidence argument that taxes on land value are bor

### [The Conceit of Climate Control](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-conceit-of-climate-control/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“reduce pollution”**
  - sentence: …The solution to climate change is to reduce pollution rather than counter its effects.…
  - why: The essay advocates for reducing pollution via green tax shifts as the correct solution to climate change.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“pollution charges”**
  - sentence: …A green tax shift would both reduce pollution and enhance prosperity as pollution charges bring in revenue that is used to reduce taxes on goods and wages.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for charging for pollution (ecological rent) to fund government and reduce other taxes.

### [The Concept of Profit](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-concept-of-profit/)
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“monopoly profit”**
  - sentence: …Second is monopoly profit, the economic profit that comes from a price greater than a competitive price, such as the profit from holding a patent.…
  - why: The essay defines monopoly profit as economic profit derived from prices above competitive levels, which aligns with the wiki's claim that rising corporate profits reflect economic rents rather than c
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“land has no opportunity cost”**
  - sentence: …Land has an individual opportunity cost for the owner, but for the economy, land has no opportunity cost.…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes land from other factors of production by stating it has no opportunity cost for the economy, directly countering the objection that land is merely a form of capital.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“Gains from force and fraud”**
  - sentence: …True profit means that the gain came from voluntary enterprise and legitimately owned assets; gains from force and fraud are not economic profit.…
  - why: By defining true economic profit as distinct from gains obtained through force or fraud, the essay supports the distinction between productive wealth creation and rent-seeking behavior.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“normal returns on assets”**
  - sentence: …But zero economic profit implies just enough accounting profit to pay for all costs, including normal returns on assets values.…
  - why: The essay's definition of zero economic profit as covering 'normal returns on assets' provides the theoretical foundation for tax systems that target only above-normal returns (rents) rather than tota

### [The Conservation of the Laws of Science](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-conservation-of-the-laws-of-science/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“The profit of the earth”**
  - sentence: …The profit of the earth is for all in equal shares.…
  - why: The essay explicitly lists this principle as the third immutable law of prosperity, echoing the Georgist single-tax narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“To the creator belongs”**
  - sentence: …To the creator belongs the creation.…
  - why: The essay posits this as the first law of prosperity, supporting the Georgist justice narrative that land value belongs to its creator (the community/nature) rather than the title holder.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“There are economic laws”**
  - sentence: …There are economic laws that prescribe prosperity, and these laws do not change.…
  - why: The essay frames economic prosperity as governed by unchanging natural laws, providing a philosophical foundation for the specific economic law of taxing land rather than labor.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“The profit of the earth”**
  - sentence: …The profit of the earth is for all in equal shares.…
  - why: By asserting that the earth's profit belongs to all, the essay supports the narrative that land value is a common product to be shared rather than privately captured.

### [The Copenhagen Conference a Victory for Polluters](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-copenhagen-conference-a-victory-for-polluters/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“Denmark also has a carbon tax”**
  - sentence: …Denmark also has a carbon tax, which has reduced per-capita emissions by reducing the use of coal.…
  - why: The essay cites Denmark's carbon tax as evidence that pricing emissions successfully reduces pollution.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“reduction in taxes on low-pollution industries”**
  - sentence: …Any country can implement this on its own, but the U.S. administration has rejected environmental taxes, since industry prefers the permits, which become for them a profitable asset that can be manipulated to reduce competition from new fir…

- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“A complete green tax shift”**
  - sentence: …A complete green tax shift would replace all other taxes with levies on pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay advocates for the Georgist 'Green Georgism' principle of replacing distortionary taxes with levies on pollution and land.
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“Billions of dollars will go to the chiefs”**
  - sentence: …Billions of dollars will go to the chiefs of the less developed economies, with little benefit to the people of these economies, while much deforestation will continue anyway.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the objection that handing large rent revenues to governments leads to corruption and misallocation.

### [The Corn is as High as the Subsidy's Aye](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-corn-is-as-high-as-the-subsidys-aye/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“economic waste”**
  - sentence: …Many economists conclude that ethanol made from corn is an economic waste, that the production of ethanol requires more energy than it provides.…
  - why: The essay uses ethanol subsidies as a prime example of rent-seeking—political clout diverting resources to inefficient production—that hurts the broader economy.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Any good economist”**
  - sentence: …Any good economist will tell you that ethanol does not and cannot drive economic development.…
  - why: The author appeals to economic consensus against subsidies to argue that political interests distort economic reality, aligning with the narrative that economics is often corrupted by special interest
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“owners of farmland”**
  - sentence: …The main beneficiaries are the owners of farmland.…
  - why: The essay explicitly invokes Ricardo to distinguish land rent from capital or labor returns, arguing that subsidies capitalize into land values rather than benefiting farmers or workers.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“pay the full social costs”**
  - sentence: …Charge car owners for the pollution, congestion, and collision costs they impose on others.…
  - why: The essay advocates for internalizing externalities like congestion and pollution as the most effective policy, which is the core mechanism of congestion pricing.

### [The Cruelty of Capitalism](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-cruelty-of-capitalism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“restricts freedom”**
  - sentence: …Those who criticize today’s economies should learn the difference between voluntary market action and intervention that restricts freedom, and then clearly say which one they think is the problem: is it freedom, or the restriction of freedo…
  - why: The essay argues that social problems like poverty are caused by government intervention (taxes and restrictions) rather than voluntary market exchange, advocating for a shift away from taxing labor.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“triumph of coercively socialist propaganda”**
  - sentence: …The Pope is only reflecting the triumph of coercively socialist propaganda, which brilliantly exploits the ambiguity of the term “capitalism” to condemn markets, with no analysis of whether it is government intervention that causes these pr…
  - why: The author explicitly rejects the conflation of free markets with socialism or state coercion, arguing that critics who blame 'capitalism' are actually falling for socialist propaganda that ignores th
- **counters** [`objections/land-no-longer-matters`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-no-longer-matters/) — anchor: **“protects land monopoly”**
  - sentence: …If a pure free market has peace, prosperity, and harmony, then the absence of these today must have its cause in government intervention, which creates war, subsidizes pollution, protects land monopoly, depresses wages with taxes and restri…
  - why: The essay identifies government protection of 'land monopoly' as a primary cause of poverty and social disharmony, contradicting the view that land is no longer a significant economic factor.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“no reason for poverty”**
  - sentence: …The most important statement ever made in economics was written by the 19th-century American economist Henry George in Social Problems: “There is in nature no reason for poverty.”…
  - why: By quoting Henry George's assertion that nature provides no reason for poverty, the essay directly counters the Malthusian claim that overpopulation or natural scarcity is the root cause of poverty.

### [The curious case of Qingdao, China's land value tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-curious-case-of-qingdao-chinas-land-value-tax/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“land speculation with all its consequences”**
  - sentence: …It was our firm conviction from the outset that land speculation with all its consequences, as we experienced them in other East-African coastal areas, had to be made impossible.…
  - why: The essay cites the German governor's explicit motivation for implementing LVT was to prevent the disastrous land speculation seen in other colonies.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“What would a maximalist Georgist regime”**
  - sentence: …What would a maximalist Georgist regime look like in practice?…
  - why: The essay uses Qingdao—a case where LVT was implemented without modern zoning reform—to investigate how a Georgist regime functions in practice, implying LVT can work prior to or independent of zoning
