- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“How accurate do land assessments have”**
  - sentence: …How accurate do land assessments have to be for LVT to work?…
  - why: The essay frames Qingdao as a historical case study that helps answer the practical question of assessment accuracy requirements for a functional LVT system.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“no income or rent tax”**
  - sentence: …No income or rent tax, but rather a relatively high real estate tax.…
  - why: The essay details Qingdao's tax structure—minimizing other taxes to rely chiefly on land value—as a concrete historical approximation of the Single Tax ideal.

### [The Dalai Lama on Capitalism and Inequality](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-dalai-lama-on-capitalism-and-inequality/)
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“Much of the inequality”**
  - sentence: …Much of the inequality in income and wealth throughout the world comes from having title to land, and that system of land tenure is not ‘capital’-ism…
  - why: The essay explicitly attributes global inequality and poverty to land tenure systems rather than capital ownership.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“benefits from the public goods”**
  - sentence: …Economic analysis also tells us that the benefits from the public goods provided by government generate higher land rentals and site values, and these gains become a subsidy to landowners when the provision is paid by taxes on wages rather …
  - why: The essay argues that government spending on public goods increases land values, effectively subsidizing landowners.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxes only on pollution and land value”**
  - sentence: …The remedy is a truly free market with taxes only on pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay concludes that the solution to economic problems is shifting taxes off labor and onto land value.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Marxism implies income redistribution”**
  - sentence: …If Marxism implies income redistribution or government ownership of industry, this treats the symptoms, not the causes.…
  - why: The essay critiques the Marxist approach to inequality as treating symptoms rather than the root cause of land tenure.

### [The Dark Matter in Economics](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-dark-matter-in-economics/)
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“Economists, however, not being as candid”**
  - sentence: …Economists, however, not being as candid as astronomers, call this mysterious substance "capital."…
  - why: The essay argues that the growth attributed to 'capital' is actually land rent, supporting the claim that the rise in capital's share is land.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“revenue from selling goods is much higher”**
  - sentence: …Yet the revenue from selling goods is much higher than the costs of production for many firms.…
  - why: The essay identifies the 'producer surplus' in corporate revenue as hidden economic rent, supporting the claim that profits increasingly reflect rents.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“lack of recognition of economic dark matter”**
  - sentence: …Mason Gaffney has shown that lack of recognition of economic dark matter was deliberate, as the landed interests did not want the public to know that rent has been soaking up the economic gains.…
  - why: The essay explicitly cites Mason Gaffney's argument that landed interests deliberately obscured land rent by merging it into capital.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“So they merged land into "capital"”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …So they merged land into "capital," where rent hides in "returns to capital," and indeed they call the whole system "capitalism."…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that land is just capital, arguing instead that land was deliberately misclassified as capital to hide rent.

### [The Depression of 2008-2009](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-depression-of-2008-2009/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“colossal collapse of real estate”**
  - sentence: …All the signs of depression are here - record high unemployment compensation, big bankruptcies, the crash of the financial sector, and the colossal collapse of real estate prices and construction.…
  - why: The essay identifies the real estate boom-bust as the primary driver of the depression, arguing that recovery depends on land values resetting.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“loans go into default”**
  - sentence: …As land values collapse, loans go into default, banks reduce their lending, both because they have fewer funds to lend, and because of the greater uncertainty about the ability of borrowers to pay back the loans.…
  - why: The essay explicitly links the collapse of land values to the banking crisis and credit contraction, illustrating the mechanism of land-based finance.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“quickly push land into productive use”**
  - sentence: …The effective supply-side policy is a quick and radical tax shift, eliminating all taxes on labor, goods, interest, and dividends, replacing these with a tax that collects the potential rent of land, as if the land were being used in its mo…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes to land value would force productive use of land and end the depression, supporting the claim that taxing land raises productivity.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“usual demand-side policies fail”**
  - sentence: …During the recession, the usual demand-side policies fail to generate an economic recovery.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that standard government interventions (monetary expansion, bailouts) can stop the cycle, arguing they are futile compared to land value taxation.

### [The Depression of 2026](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-depression-of-2026/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“fundamental cause of the boom and bust cycle”**
  - sentence: …There has been one fundamental cause of the boom and bust cycle: massive subsidies to land values.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land subsidies drive the recurring 18-year business cycle.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“generation of land rent by government’s public goods”**
  - sentence: …The biggest subsidy is the generation of land rent by government’s public goods.…
  - why: The essay details how public goods like transit and schools increase land value.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“deep fiscal and monetary structures”**
  - sentence: …The deep fiscal and monetary structures of the US economy, which have not changed in 200 years, will generate the next boom and bust just as they have done so in the past.…
  - why: The essay rejects the idea that government intervention can prevent the cycle, predicting another crash.
- **supports** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“scoff at this prediction”**
  - sentence: …Almost all economists, financial analysts, journalists, pundits, bloggers, and armchair cynics will also scoff at this prediction of a severe recession and depression in 2026.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the recurring 'New Era' fallacy where experts ignore historical cycles.

### [The Economy and Happiness of Bhutan](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-economy-and-happiness-of-bhutan/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“eliminates taxes on income”**
  - sentence: …Bhutan’s economy and national happiness would be greatly enhanced by a tax reform that eliminates taxes on income, exchanges, goods, and buildings, and replaces them with a tax on land value that taps about 85 percent of the ground rent, an…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that replacing distortionary taxes with a land value tax would enhance the economy and national happiness.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“prevent speculation”**
  - sentence: …Bhutan’s National Land Commission has been revising the land tax rates to obtain revenue, prevent speculation, and reduce the concentration of land holdings.…
  - why: The essay cites the Bhutanese government's use of land taxes specifically to prevent speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“reduce the concentration of land holdings”**
  - sentence: …Bhutan’s National Land Commission has been revising the land tax rates to obtain revenue, prevent speculation, and reduce the concentration of land holdings.…
  - why: The essay notes that land tax policy is used to reduce the concentration of land holdings, implying a progressive redistribution of wealth.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“obtains substantial revenue from hydropower”**
  - sentence: …The government of Bhutan obtains substantial revenue from hydropower.…
  - why: The essay identifies the capture of resource rents (hydropower) as a key source of government revenue.

### [The EMP Threat](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-emp-threat/)
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“political dynamic is reactive”**
  - sentence: …Unfortunately the political dynamic is reactive, not proactive.…
  - why: The essay argues that government fails to address existential threats like EMP because it is reactive rather than proactive, countering the claim that government intervention can effectively tame or p
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“government is meddling into affairs”**
  - sentence: …Instead, government is meddling into affairs that don’t harm others, and spends treasure to repair deprivation done by its tax and regulatory policies.…
  - why: The essay supports the public-choice critique by asserting that government misallocates resources to trivial meddling rather than fulfilling its prime responsibility to protect citizens from existenti

### [The End of the Great Recession](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-end-of-the-great-recession/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“financed the real estate bubble”**
  - sentence: …Governments have gone all-out to bail out the financial firms that financed the real estate bubble.…
  - why: The essay identifies the bailout of financiers who fueled the real estate bubble as a key feature of the crisis, illustrating the narrative that land speculation drives economic cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“The big gainers are the landed interests”**
  - sentence: …The big gainers are the landed interests and their financial allies in banking and insurance.…
  - why: The essay argues that despite the general economic depression, the primary beneficiaries of government policy are landed interests and their financial allies, supporting the claim that the economy inc
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Mother nature is willing to pay”**
  - sentence: …Mother nature is willing to pay all the expenses of government, yet people shun her offer and instead inflict costs on human action.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that government should be funded by nature (land rent) rather than inflicting costs on human action (labor and capital), directly supporting the narrative to tax land not l
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“pollution taxes offset by lower taxes”**
  - sentence: …Pollution should indeed be curbed, but it should be done with a “green tax shift,” with pollution taxes offset by lower taxes on wages and capital.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a 'green tax shift' where pollution charges replace taxes on labor and capital, supporting the narrative of ecological rent.

### [The Ethics of Land and Liberty](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-ethics-of-land-and-liberty/)
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“natural rent”**
  - sentence: …The homesteader may claim possession of a natural resource, but not the natural rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that while individuals may possess land, the economic rent generated by it belongs to the community, supporting the moral claim that land value increments are unearned by the title ho
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“public works”**
  - sentence: …Much of the land rental in developed communities comes not from nature but from the public works and civic services…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that public works and civic services generate land rent, illustrating the mechanism of public investment capitalizing into land values.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“paying back the value”**
  - sentence: …and so a payment of the rentals to government would be justified as paying back the value generated by these works.…
  - why: The essay justifies capturing land rent to fund government services as a repayment for the value those public goods create.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal per-person shares”**
  - sentence: …and then the global association distributes the natural rent in equal per-person shares back down the governing tiers.…
  - why: The essay proposes distributing collected natural rent to humanity in equal shares, directly supporting the citizens' dividend narrative.

### [The Evil of Taxing the Rich](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-evil-of-taxing-the-rich/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“about $3trillion in annual land rent”**
  - sentence: …Land rent being about 20 percent of national income, there is in the $14 trillion US economy about $3trillion in annual land rent, which about equal to the total US budget.…
  - why: The essay quantifies US land rent to argue it could replace total federal revenue.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pump up and rent and land value”**
  - sentence: …Public works and civic services pump up and rent and land value, and landowners pay only a tiny portion of the bill in property taxes on land.…
  - why: The essay argues public spending creates a hidden subsidy by increasing land values.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“directly tax the rent, which would eliminate”**
  - sentence: …Since taxes come from rent anyway, the government could directly tax the rent, which would eliminate the deadweight loss.…
  - why: The essay claims shifting taxes to land rent removes the economic waste of other taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxing pollution and land instead”**
  - sentence: …When politicians call for higher taxes on the rich, they reject the better alternatives of taxing pollution and land.…
  - why: The essay advocates taxing land and pollution rather than income or wealth.

### [The Evolutionary Blueprint](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-evolutionary-blueprint/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Economics as an analytical social science”**
  - sentence: …Economics as an analytical social science was perverted by the rent seekers of the late 19th century.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that neoclassical economists, hired by landed interests, suppressed the classical distinction between land and capital to protect rent-seeking.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“services provided by society”**
  - sentence: …Rent, the revenue associated with “land”, is an income that originates with the services provided by society, as well as nature.…
  - why: The essay argues that land rent is a composite value derived from both nature and the collective labor and services of the community.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“land-grabbing nobility began to privatise”**
  - sentence: …When, 500 years ago, the land-grabbing nobility began to privatise rent for their personal use, the social status of those resources had to be revised.…
  - why: The essay frames the historical privatization of rent by the nobility as a structural injustice that required a distortion of collective consciousness.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“reframe the unique qualities represented by”**
  - sentence: …They hired professors to reframe the unique qualities represented by the words “land” and “rent”, which became sub-categories of “capital”.…
  - why: The essay disputes the economic conflation of land and capital, characterizing it as a deliberate perversion of classical economics to obscure rent.

### [The Fallacy of Excessive Premise Extension](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-fallacy-of-excessive-premise-extension/)
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“yield of land”**
  - sentence: …Henry George, who separated the rights of possession from the rights to the yield of land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly validates the Georgist distinction between possessing land and owning its economic yield.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“generated by the local population”**
  - sentence: …Land rent generated by the local population and commerce remains with the local community, while rent generated by natural conditions (climate, soil, wildlife, minerals, water) ideally is owned in equal shares by humanity.…
  - why: The essay argues that land rent originates from the community and nature, justifying public capture.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“originated in conquest”**
  - sentence: …One problem with allodial ownership is that most land ownership today originated in conquest.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that current land titles are often rooted in theft and conquest.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“collect the land rent”**
  - sentence: …Henry George proposed that the government, as the agent of the people, collect the land rent, and use it for public revenue.…
  - why: The essay endorses the proposal that government can and should collect land rent for public purposes.

### [The Folly of 'Cap and Trade' Approach to Pollution Reduction](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-folly-of-cap-and-trade-approach-to-pollution-reduction/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“punish the polluters with a stiff charge”**
  - sentence: …The solution is so simple, that a 5th grader would have no trouble understanding it. Just punish the polluters with a stiff charge equal to the cost of the damage.…
  - why: The essay argues that directly charging polluters for the damage they cause is the most efficient way to reduce emissions.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“pollution taxes do not have this excess burden”**
  - sentence: …In contrast, pollution taxes do not have this excess burden, since the tax shift removes the social cost of the pollution, in effect removing the subsidy to polluters.…
  - why: The essay explains that shifting taxes from income (which has deadweight loss) to pollution (which corrects an externality) benefits the economy.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“pollution control is mandatory if humanity is to survive”**
  - sentence: …Pollution control is mandatory if humanity is to survive, but the way that governments world-wide are going about it is astonishingly insane.…
  - why: The essay frames pollution as an unpaid taking of natural wealth that must be charged for to prevent catastrophe.

### [The Foundations of Religion](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-foundations-of-religion/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“values are subjective”**
  - sentence: …This concept is in accord with a basic premise of economics, that values are subjective, that things have value only because people have an interest in them, a value that comes from the mind, and not from any inherent aspect of the object.…
  - why: The essay explicitly references the economic premise that value is subjective and socially instilled rather than intrinsic, which is the foundational logic for the Georgist claim that community activi
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“natural moral law”**
  - sentence: …The world will never be at peace until most people believe that aside from their religious and cultural beliefs, there is an ethic universal to all persons, a natural moral law that is the imperative and foundation for proper governance.…
  - why: The essay argues that a universal natural moral law derived from reason is necessary for peace and proper governance, supporting the narrative that economic and social policy should be grounded in uni
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“imposing implicitly supremacist costs”**
  - sentence: …Every thief implicitly denies the equality of his victim, and that includes people who think they are doing good by imposing implicitly supremacist costs and restrictions on honest and peaceful but disfavored human actions.…
  - why: The essay identifies the imposition of costs on peaceful individuals as a form of supremacism and ethical failure, illustrating the public-choice concern that actors (including governments) will use p

### [The Futility of Not Tapping Land Value](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-futility-of-not-tapping-land-value/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“land cannot hide, flee, or shrink”**
  - sentence: …But land cannot hide, flee, or shrink when tapped for civic revenue.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land is uniquely inescapable as a tax base compared to mobile factors like income and sales.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Civic works pump up land values”**
  - sentence: …Civic works pump up land values as the charges on property owners only pays for part of these services.…
  - why: The essay notes that public infrastructure increases land values, creating a subsidy for landowners who do not pay the full cost.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“exempt buildings and other improvements”**
  - sentence: …It would have been much simpler and less costly to exempt buildings and other improvements from the property tax and simply levy a charge based on the current market value of land.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing land alone is superior to the current complex mix of property-based levies, distinguishing land value taxation from standard property taxes.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“tapping of land value for civic goods”**
  - sentence: …Californians could spare themselves these excess burdens by repealing Proposition 13 and replacing it with a constitutional law that replaces all taxes with truly voluntary user fees as well as levies on pollution and the tapping of land va…
  - why: The essay concludes that shifting the tax base entirely onto land value is a viable and superior alternative to current complex revenue systems.

### [The Gaffney Quantum Leap Effect](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-gaffney-quantum-leap-effect/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“shift to land-value taxation”**
  - sentence: …The only way to eliminate the Gaffney quantum leap effect is to directly tax rent instead of indirectly taxing it via taxes on revenue and profits.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes from capital and sales to land value eliminates the 'quantum leap' efficiency losses caused by current tax systems.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“quantum leap up to dazzling productivity”**
  - sentence: …With the elimination of taxes on wages, sales, buildings, and entrepreneurial profits, the economy would take a quantum leap up to dazzling productivity.…
  - why: The essay claims that removing distortionary taxes in favor of land value taxation would result in a massive increase in economic productivity.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“reduction in output”**
  - sentence: …There is a quantum leap down to much less production, because the land use has shifted to one which has less output.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how tax-induced distortions cause land to shift to less productive uses, thereby dragging down economic growth.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“taxes on gross revenues”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on gross revenues are especially destructive, as they squeeze out the profit more than taxes on income, which is gains minus costs.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes the destructive effects of taxing revenue (capital) from the proposed solution of taxing land rent, implying land is not just capital.

### [The Game of Monopoly versus Equopoly](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-game-of-monopoly-versus-equopoly/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“The Landlord’s Game”**
  - sentence: …The game was invented by Elizabeth Magie Phillips, a follower of Henry George, as The Landlord’s Game to illustrate how land monopolies create inequality and economic woes.…
  - why: The essay recounts the origin story of Monopoly (The Landlord's Game) to demonstrate the Georgist critique of land monopoly.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distributed to the players equally”**
  - sentence: …If the game is changed so that the land rent is collected periodically, and distributed to the players equally, there would still be a game, but now there is no more gain from a land monopoly, and the players end up in a more equal financia…
  - why: The essay uses the game modification to argue that sharing land rent equally prevents inequality and bankruptcy, supporting the concept of a citizens' dividend.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“land monopolies create inequality”**
  - sentence: …The game was invented by Elizabeth Magie Phillips, a follower of Henry George, as The Landlord’s Game to illustrate how land monopolies create inequality and economic woes.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the mechanics of a rentier economy where landlords accumulate wealth at the expense of tenants through monopoly control of land.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“no more gain from a land monopoly”**
  - sentence: …If the game is changed so that the land rent is collected periodically, and distributed to the players equally, there would still be a game, but now there is no more gain from a land monopoly, and the players end up in a more equal financia…
  - why: The essay argues that the unearned gains from land ownership should be shared rather than privately captured, aligning with the moral case against the unearned increment.

### [The GDP Illusion](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-gdp-illusion/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“the fundamental boom-bust real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …The demand-side policy of greater government spending will not solve the fundamental boom-bust real estate cycle.…
  - why: The essay argues that the recession is driven by a real estate cycle that government stimulus cannot fix.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“remove the subsidy from land values”**
  - sentence: …When the economy recovers, the next real estate boom-bust cycle will occur unless governments replaces punitive taxation with public revenue from land rent to remove the subsidy from land values and land speculation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for land value taxation as the solution to end the boom-bust cycle.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“propped up by borrowing from abroad”**
  - sentence: …The economy is artificially being propped up by borrowing from abroad.…
  - why: The essay describes the economy as sustained by foreign borrowing, linking the financial bubble to the underlying land cycle.
- **counters** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“economic reality will hit home”**
  - sentence: …Maybe that game can continue until the November elections, but eventually economic reality will hit home and the revised data will show lower real growth.…
  - why: The essay dismisses the idea that government intervention can permanently suspend the economic cycle.

### [The German Land Policy Success and Failure](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-german-land-policy-success-and-failure/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“private appropriation of the rent”**
  - sentence: …Germany in the 1920s "provided a striking example supporting the theory that the private appropriation of the rent of land is the fundamental cause of industrial depression and of distress among those who labor in the production of wealth -…
  - why: The essay explicitly cites the 1920s German economy as evidence for Henry George's theory that land rent appropriation causes industrial depression.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Pfandbriefe, jointly backed by”**
  - sentence: …The Landschaften issued bonds, Pfandbriefe, jointly backed by the members’ land in addition to the individual plots of the members.…
  - why: The essay details the historical creation of mortgage-backed securities (Pfandbriefe) secured by land, illustrating the deep link between banking growth and land values.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“generously built roads and railways”**
  - sentence: …Taxes were extraordinarily high because the state and the city had to redeem the costs of dearly bought land and generously built roads and railways...…
  - why: The essay argues that public infrastructure investments raised land values, enriching landowners rather than the public.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“landlords bought Nazidom”**
  - sentence: …As communists and socialists threatened the landed interests, the "landlords bought Nazidom with part of the money they obtained from the Republic both in cash subsidies and through the rise in land values.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the political power of landed interests (Junkers) to capture government policy and subsidies, aligning with the narrative of economic corruption by landowners.

### [The Global Debt Catastrophe](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-global-debt-catastrophe/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“using the economic surplus that is land rent”**
  - sentence: …The only way to avoid economic catastrophe is to raise the income of workers by eliminating taxes on wages and goods, and using the economic surplus that is land rent to finance government.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting the tax base to land rent is the only viable solution to the massive government debt crisis.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replace market-hampering taxes with market-enhancing levies”**
  - sentence: …The only possible way to induce high economic growth is with an efficiency tax shift--replace market-hampering taxes with market-enhancing levies on pollution and land value, plus voluntary user fees.…
  - why: The essay claims that replacing distortionary taxes with land value taxes is the specific mechanism required to achieve the rapid growth needed to solve the debt problem.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“funding for policy institutes derives from the profits”**
  - sentence: …Much of the funding for policy institutes derives from the profits of the landed interests, and that influences policy positions.…
  - why: The essay explicitly attributes the failure of major think tanks to consider land value taxation to their funding sources and the influence of landed interests.

### [The Global Debt Catastrophe in Real-Time](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-global-debt-catastrophe-in-real-time/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“impending debt catastrophe”**
  - sentence: …In this article, we will take a look at what geo-libertarians think of the impending debt catastrophe, how we can track the growth of national debt, and what we can expect from the global economy in the future.…
  - why: The essay frames the unsustainable accumulation of government debt as a drag on future economic stability and growth, aligning with the Georgist view that rent-seeking (here, deficit spending) harms t
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“next financial crisis”**
  - sentence: …Geo-libertarian economist Fred Foldvary claims that the large amounts of debt accrued by governments around the world will be highly problematic in the next financial crisis.…
  - why: The essay references Fred Foldvary's prediction of a crisis driven by debt, which is a core component of his Georgist land-cycle theory linking financial busts to speculative bubbles.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“disaster is impending”**
  - sentence: …Therefore he takes the position that disaster is impending.…
  - why: Foldvary's conclusion that disaster is inevitable counters the objection that government intervention or policy can successfully prevent or tame economic cycles.

### [The Government's War Against Healthy Food](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-governments-war-against-healthy-food/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“the hidden hand of special interests”**
  - sentence: …One does not have to be that cynical to see the hidden hand of special interests.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies rent-seeking by arguing that agri-business uses political clout to destroy competitors and secure subsidies, dragging down the organic food sector.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Government serves the big-money interests”**
  - sentence: …Government serves the big-money interests and the political pressure groups, and the agri-business that get billions of dollars of federal subsidies also uses its clout to destroy threats to its profits…
  - why: The essay supports the public-choice critique by depicting government intervention as a tool for entrenched interests to harm competitors rather than serve the public.

### [The Government's War on Teenagers](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-governments-war-on-teenagers/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“instinct of government official”**
  - sentence: …The instinct of government official is to maximize power.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies the public-choice critique by arguing that officials prosecute teens to 'maximize power' despite the inefficiency and cruelty.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“Government’s clumsy and cruel response”**
  - sentence: …Government’s clumsy and cruel response to textnuding will clog the legal system and create far worse problems than the teenage foolishness.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that government intervention is a necessary or effective solution to social problems, countering arguments that rely on state capacity.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Government chiefs are not just cruel”**
  - sentence: …Government chiefs are not just cruel, but more foolish than the silly teenagers.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that government actors are fundamentally flawed or corrupt, aligning with the critique of establishment institutions.

### [The Great American Automobile Crash](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-great-american-automobile-crash/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“governments skew the market”**
  - sentence: …But governments skew the market outcomes with taxes and restrictions, making labor more expensive while reducing the take-home pay of a worker.…
  - why: The essay argues that government interventions (taxes and subsidies) distort market outcomes, specifically by inflating labor costs and subsidizing pollution, which aligns with the narrative that gove
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“governments have subsidized the pollution”**
  - sentence: …It is not a car’s fault that governments have subsidized the pollution and congestion.…
  - why: The essay counters the idea that automobiles are inherently the cause of urban problems like pollution and congestion, attributing them instead to government subsidies that fail to price externalities
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“power of a labor union”**
  - sentence: …The power of a labor union comes from its ability to strike, which depends on its having a labor monopoly.…
  - why: The essay identifies labor unions using monopoly power to extract above-market wages and pensions as a drag on the industry, illustrating how rent-seeking behavior hinders economic efficiency and grow
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“industry still has political clout”**
  - sentence: …The industry still has political clout because it is still important in Michigan and Ohio, states which can swing the coming congressional and presidential elections.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice critique by showing how the auto industry seeks a bailout not through economic merit but by leveraging its political power in swing states.

### [The Great Crash of 2008](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-great-crash-of-2008/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“endogenous cycle keeps returning”**
  - sentence: …Yet, the endogenous cycle keeps returning, as soon as we find peace, and economic life returns to its even tenors.…
  - why: The essay argues that economic history is defined by a recurring internal land cycle, not external shocks.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“taking as security liens on new buildings and on lands”**
  - sentence: …Outside money flows into growth areas, taking as security liens on new buildings and on lands.…
  - why: The essay identifies the expansion of credit secured by land as the mechanism that drives the boom and subsequent bust.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“latest boom tracks the events that Hoyt recorded”**
  - sentence: …It is uncanny how the latest boom tracks the events that Hoyt recorded and generalized.…
  - why: The essay counters the idea that bubbles are unidentifiable by demonstrating that the 2008 crash followed a predictable historical pattern.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“speculation in rising land values”**
  - sentence: …In the process most actors lose sight of the original cause, speculation in rising land values, and the stage is set to begin the next cycle.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the view that credit alone drives cycles, identifying land speculation as the root cause.

### [The Great Inflation of 2009](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-great-inflation-of-2009/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“Land speculation will be back”**
  - sentence: …Land speculation will be back, as buyers take advantage of low-priced land.…
  - why: The essay predicts that inflationary monetary policy will revive land speculation, illustrating the cyclical link between credit expansion and land bubbles.
- **supports** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“vast expansion of money by the Federal Reserve”**
  - sentence: …This vast expansion of money by the Federal Reserve and the banking system is a monetary inflation that then creates price inflation.…
  - why: The essay attributes the coming inflation and asset price changes primarily to monetary expansion and credit creation, aligning with the credit-driven view of cycles.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“seek to buy land as an inflation hedge”**
  - sentence: …The inflation will bring the fall in US real estate prices to a halt, as people seek to buy land as an inflation hedge.…
  - why: It highlights how land values are driven by speculative financial dynamics rather than just housing utility, reinforcing the land-centric view of the crisis.

### [The Growth "Imperative"](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-growth-imperative/)
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“people who grow the crops”**
  - sentence: …And why? Because the people who grow the crops do not own the land.…
  - why: The essay argues that poverty persists despite GDP growth because land ownership is concentrated, forcing workers to produce export crops rather than food for themselves.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“consolidates resources in the hands”**
  - sentence: …Too often, we're told that economic growth is a process that consolidates resources in the hands of the wealthy, creating damage that everyone has to pay for.…
  - why: The essay critiques the current model of growth as one that benefits asset owners at the expense of the public, aligning with the rentier economy narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“satisfaction of more human desires”**
  - sentence: …Rather, economic growth is the satisfaction of more human desires.…
  - why: By defining growth as the satisfaction of desires rather than the physical accumulation of stuff, the essay implicitly counters Malthusian views that resource scarcity inevitably causes poverty.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“do not own the land”**
  - sentence: …And why? Because the people who grow the crops do not own the land.…
  - why: The essay highlights the injustice and economic inefficiency resulting from the dispossession of land from those who work it.

### [The Hidden Key to a New Paradigm Economy](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-hidden-key-to-a-new-paradigm-economy/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“the community has made”**
  - sentence: …In short: the community has made this land more valuable.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value increases are caused by the community's addition of goods and services, not the owner's labor.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“locational value of the property”**
  - sentence: …If I buy a property for $100,000 and sell it a year later for $120,000 (with no work done to it), the locational value of the property has increased by $20,000.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how economic growth and community services capitalize into higher land values, benefiting owners rather than the broader economy.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“redistributed to all community members”**
  - sentence: …When the local community is reimbursed and when the funds are redistributed to all community members via a Universal Basic Income (including those who do not own land), everyone benefits.…
  - why: The essay advocates for capturing land rent and distributing it as a universal basic income to benefit all members of society.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“with no work done to it”**
  - sentence: …If I buy a property for $100,000 and sell it a year later for $120,000 (with no work done to it), the locational value of the property has increased by $20,000.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of an unearned increment, where profit arises solely from locational value appreciation rather than owner effort.

### [The High Cost of Food](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-high-cost-of-food/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-farmers`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-farmers/) — anchor: **“The economic cost of farmland”**
  - sentence: …The economic cost of farmland is the value of the forests and wild grasslands that would otherwise be there.…
  - why: The essay defines farmland value via opportunity costs, implying low rural land values, which counters the objection that LVT would crush farmers.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“tax land value with no deadweight loss”**
  - sentence: …That means we would be over ten percent richer if not for the waste caused by taxes. This is not a necessary waste, since we could tax land value with no deadweight loss, as land does not hide, shrink, or flee when taxed.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that taxing land value avoids the deadweight loss inherent in taxing goods, labor, and business profits.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxes on goods, labor, and business profits”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on goods, labor, and business profits create an excess burden, a misallocation and waste of resources, also called a “deadweight loss.”…
  - why: The essay contrasts the waste caused by current taxes on labor and capital with the efficiency of taxing land, supporting the narrative to shift the tax base.

### [The Homevoters and the Haut Precariat](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-homevoters-and-the-haut-precariat/)
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“increase in capital’s share”**
  - sentence: …Some economists argue that the entire increase in capital’s share of income over the past few decades is due to increases in the value of land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly cites the economic argument that the rise in capital's share is attributable to land.
- **supports** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“homevoters and their supply restrictions”**
  - sentence: …But thanks to the homevoters and their supply restrictions, it’s hard for the haut precariat to afford a rental in San Francisco or NYC or Los Angeles — or, increasingly, in anywhere else they might find worth living.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the 'homevoter' hypothesis, detailing how existing homeowners use political power to restrict housing supply.
- **supports** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“shut out of the wealth escalator”**
  - sentence: …So they’re shut out of the wealth escalator, and unless they know how to invest in stocks, they may end up wasting much of their savings by holding it in cash or just consuming it because they don’t know what else to do with it.…
  - why: The essay describes the 'haut precariat' as a class of young people excluded from the wealth accumulation typically provided by homeownership.
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“shift to knowledge industries created a boom”**
  - sentence: …how the shift to knowledge industries created a boom in demand for urban living that created a huge windfall for strategically located homeowners…
  - why: The essay links housing unaffordability to the value of land in strategic locations driven by demand from knowledge industries.

### [The Housing Crisis as a Land Crisis](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-housing-crisis-as-a-land-crisis/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“housing crisis is a land crisis”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …We are in a housing crisis, and more specifically, a land crisis.…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the housing affordability crisis as a crisis of land scarcity in desirable locations rather than a general shortage of housing.
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“housing has simply become more expensive to produce”**
  - sentence: …A natural explanation for rising housing costs is that housing has simply become more expensive to produce, or that we stopped building enough of it overall.…
  - why: The essay argues that rising construction costs and aggregate housing shortages do not explain the crisis, isolating land scarcity as the driver.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“demand and supply cannot meet”**
  - sentence: …When demand and supply cannot meet, prices rise–not because there’s not enough housing anywhere in the United States, but because not enough housing is built on scarce urban land, where people desire it the most.…
  - why: The essay identifies the misallocation of labor and housing due to constraints on scarce urban land as the root of rising prices.
- **counters** [`objections/planning-restrictions-cause-high-prices`](https://progress.org/wiki/planning-restrictions-cause-high-prices/) — anchor: **“deliberate urban renewal policies”**
  - sentence: …However, the decline in urban density over the 20th century was driven just as much by top-down policy as it was by invention and market forces.…
  - why: The essay acknowledges that zoning and policy restrictions drove de-densification, but frames this as a historical cause of the current land crisis rather than a complete alternative explanation.

### [The Housing Ladder's Broken Promise](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-housing-ladders-broken-promise/)
- **supports** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“young working families”**
  - sentence: …This is the so-called “housing ladder,” which young working families are increasingly unable to climb, precisely because housing has become an investment, and therefore unaffordable.…
  - why: The essay describes how the housing-as-investment model prevents young families from buying homes.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“housing has become an investment”**
  - sentence: …This is the so-called “housing ladder,” which young working families are increasingly unable to climb, precisely because housing has become an investment, and therefore unaffordable.…
  - why: The essay frames the affordability crisis as a result of treating housing as an investment vehicle rather than shelter.
- **supports** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“protective of people that already own a house”**
  - sentence: …Last week, President Trump gave a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, where he said housing affordability stands in direct opposition to preserving the property values of existing homeowners (emphasis mine): I am very protective of…
  - why: The essay cites a political leader explicitly prioritizing existing homeowners' property values over affordability, illustrating the Homevoter Hypothesis.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“Imagine America in 2008”**
  - sentence: …The Big Bad Crash Imagine America in 2008 , but worse.…
  - why: The essay uses the 2008 crash as a primary example of the inevitable bust resulting from unsustainable housing price inflation.

### [The Implementation of Land-Value Taxation](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-implementation-of-land-value-taxation/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“board of assessors”**
  - sentence: …Therefore there would be a county-level board of assessors of, for example, nine members.…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific institutional structure (a multi-level board of assessors) to address the practical objection that land value cannot be accurately assessed.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“treatment of retired folks”**
  - sentence: …One problem that always comes up with LVT is the treatment of retired folks with low cash incomes, or a person who loses a source of income and has no savings.…
  - why: The essay directly addresses the 'asset-rich, cash-poor' objection by proposing solutions like LVT insurance and deferred payment plans.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“incentive is to pass on”**
  - sentence: …Since the county is passing on a large amount of the revenues to the state, the incentive is to pass on as little as possible, and therefore to under-assess the land value.…
  - why: The essay acknowledges the incentive for corrupt under-assessment and proposes a federal/state/local appointment structure for the board of assessors to mitigate it.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“distributed in money”**
  - sentence: …Some of the LVT revenue should be distributed in money to all the residents in equal shares.…
  - why: The essay advocates for distributing a portion of LVT revenue as a citizen's dividend, supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty.

### [The Internet Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-internet-tax/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tax on capacity”**
  - sentence: …This would be a tax on capacity, rather than action.…
  - why: The essay argues for shifting taxes from transactions (labor/internet) to capacity (land), which is the core thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“deadweight loss”**
  - sentence: …Economics calls this a “deadweight loss” and an “excess burden.”…
  - why: The essay explains that taxing mobile transactions causes deadweight loss, implying that taxing immobile land avoids it, supporting the efficiency claim.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“delay the payment”**
  - sentence: …On political if not humanitarian grounds, the tax collecting agency could allow homeowners to delay the payment of additional taxes, plus interest on the debt, until the property is transferred.…
  - why: The essay explicitly proposes deferring tax payments to address the concern that rising land values hurt homeowners with fixed incomes.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“no speculative demand”**
  - sentence: …If most of the land rent is tapped for public revenue, then there is no speculative demand for land that pushes up the price and the tax.…
  - why: The essay argues that capturing land rent eliminates the speculative demand that drives up prices, directly supporting this claim.

### [The Jobs Summit](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-jobs-summit/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“Subsidies to landowners, including cheap credit”**
  - sentence: …Subsidies to landowners, including cheap credit, generated the real estate bubble that burst and made workers lose their jobs.…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies subsidized land and credit as the cause of the real estate bubble and subsequent job losses.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“shifting taxes from wages to land rent”**
  - sentence: …Instead of preventing the next boom and bust by shifting taxes from wages to land rent, the jobs summit will propose low-interest loans to business, and limited tax credits.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting the tax burden from labor to land is the correct method to prevent economic cycles and unemployment.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“the possessors compensate the rest of society”**
  - sentence: …We cannot now have unlimited access to natural resources, but we can have the possessors compensate the rest of society for exclusive access.…
  - why: The essay proposes a mechanism for compensating society for exclusive land access, implying that such a value can be determined and collected.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“It goes back to conquest”**
  - sentence: …It goes back to conquest, as the land was taken by the war chiefs and then the peasants had to pay rent plus taxes.…
  - why: The essay traces the loss of free access to land and the origin of land titles to historical conquest and dispossession.

### [The Landownership Cycle](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-landownership-cycle/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value is created by the community”**
  - sentence: …While land can certainly be privately used, its value is created by the community and therefore belongs to the community.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value derives from community activity and should belong to the community.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“extract this value by making money”**
  - sentence: …As communities become more attractive to live in, some property owners—but mostly the financial institutions that finance them—then extract this value by making money from real estate—money that in truth belongs to everyone—, and this extra…
  - why: The essay identifies the extraction of community-created land value by owners and financiers as a driver of inequality and economic instability.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“root causes of economic recessions”**
  - sentence: …This privatization of nature is one of the root causes of economic recessions, ecological destruction, as well as social and cultural decline—even in a world of plenty.…
  - why: The essay links the privatization of land (and the resulting extraction of value) directly to the causation of economic recessions.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“redistributed to all community members”**
  - sentence: …And finally, these contributions would then have to be redistributed to all community members in the form of a Universal Basic Income to prevent gentrification, reduce wealth inequality, and create a truly fair economy for all participants.…
  - why: The essay proposes redistributing land value contributions as a Universal Basic Income to reduce inequality and gentrification.

### [The Landscape #4: Wins aren’t always legislative, though legislative wins are on the horizon](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-landscape-4-wins-arent-always-legislative-though-legislative-wins-are-on-the-horizon/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“vacant lot holding in Baltimore”**
  - sentence: …If SDAT carries through with the fixes, then vacant lot holding in Baltimore will be disincentivized and development will occur.…
  - why: The essay argues that correcting assessments to tax vacant land at its true value will discourage holding it idle.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shift taxes from buildings to land”**
  - sentence: …We are hopeful that the House will see the benefits of allowing Baltimore to shift taxes from buildings to land.…
  - why: The essay advocates for Maryland legislation enabling split-rate taxation to encourage reinvestment in housing stock.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“reclaim the narrative on the ability”**
  - sentence: …Lars has been doing great work to reclaim the narrative on the ability to value land.…
  - why: The essay highlights efforts to demonstrate that land can be accurately valued within existing systems.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“cut taxes for most homeowners”**
  - sentence: …Their panel included Josie Faass, Stephen Hoskins, and Sen. Hinrichsen, and showed how an LVT would cut taxes for most homeowners and renters, shift the burden onto vacant land and parking lots, and support equitable growth.…
  - why: The essay cites a panel explaining that LVT reduces the tax burden on typical residents while shifting it to vacant land owners.

### [The Leviathan: Property Taxes in the Big Apple](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-leviathan-property-taxes-in-the-big-apple/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“punishes productive land use”**
  - sentence: …This is a particularly valuable exercise for property taxes, which in many places are a tangled mess that hides the true tax burden and punishes productive land use.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates how NYC's conventional property tax actively penalizes density and rewards speculation, contrasting this with the Georgist ideal of taxing land alone.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“reducing or removing taxes on building values”**
  - sentence: …More comprehensively, we propose offering a carrot to developers by reducing or removing taxes on building values, offsetting lost revenues with a land value tax.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a split-rate approach (shifting taxes off buildings and onto land) to stimulate construction and fix the city's housing supply issues.
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“vacant land enjoying relatively low tax bills”**
  - sentence: …And finally, productive land use is penalized & speculation is rewarded , with vacant land enjoying relatively low tax bills and are penalized with a sharp increase in taxes if they choose to develop.…
  - why: The essay provides a concrete example of a Manhattan block left vacant for decades due to low tax bills, illustrating how current systems encourage land banking.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“penalized with a sharp increase in taxes”**
  - sentence: …And finally, productive land use is penalized & speculation is rewarded , with vacant land enjoying relatively low tax bills and are penalized with a sharp increase in taxes if they choose to develop.…
  - why: The essay highlights that NYC's current tax system actively discourages development even where demand is high, countering the idea that zoning is the only barrier to supply.

### [The London Land Levy](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-london-land-levy/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“shifting the burden of tax”**
  - sentence: …When we do this, we are more comprehensively shifting the burden of tax from earned income to unearned wealth.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for replacing income and capital taxes with a land levy to improve efficiency.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“wealth that is created through public investment”**
  - sentence: …Effectively, it captures a proportion of that wealth that is created through public investment in order to fund services.…
  - why: The essay identifies unearned land wealth as the product of public investment, justifying its capture.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“returns are high without having to do any work”**
  - sentence: …In turn, this has attracted investors to sink their money into property in London as the returns are high without having to do any work.…
  - why: The essay critiques the current system for rewarding passive speculation, implying a land levy would address this.

### [The Lone Star State Needs Land Value Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-lone-star-state-needs-land-value-taxes/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“punishes productivity and efficiency”**
  - sentence: …However, this analysis punishes productivity and efficiency by focusing on what currently exists rather than what could exist.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the punitive nature of taxing improvements with the efficiency of taxing land, arguing that LVT aligns incentives against waste.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“encourages development and supports”**
  - sentence: …Thus, where the property tax punishes the homeowner who builds an addition and the business owner who renovates their property, the land value tax encourages development and supports thriving communities.…
  - why: The text explicitly argues that removing the tax penalty on improvements (via LVT) encourages development, which is the core mechanism of the split-rate benefit.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value generated collectively should”**
  - sentence: …Privately generated value should remain private, and value generated collectively should be used collectively.…
  - why: The essay articulates the ethical and economic principle that land value arises from community effort and should therefore be captured for public use.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“replacing property tax with a land value tax”**
  - sentence: …Neither party has yet embraced the solution — replacing property tax with a land value tax.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes the current property tax system (which it critiques as regressive and inefficient) from the proposed land value tax, countering the idea that they are functionally the same.

### [The LVT Landscape #1: Movement Across the U.S.](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-lvt-landscape-1-movement-across-the-u-s/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“encouraging development by taxing”**
  - sentence: …A land value tax shift would allow Spokane to more effectively utilize its limited property taxing authority, generating necessary revenue while simultaneously encouraging development by taxing unimproved land at higher rates than developed…
  - why: The essay cites Spokane's interest in LVT specifically to encourage development by taxing unimproved land higher, which is the core mechanism of the split-rate effect.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“land around transit is”**
  - sentence: …The land around transit is the most valuable and should be the most developed.…
  - why: The essay argues that land near rail is most valuable and should be developed, supporting the Georgist claim that LVT reduces sprawl by incentivizing density in high-value locations.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“most regressive tax environments”**
  - sentence: …This creates a system where lower-income residents typically pay a much higher percentage of their income in taxes than wealthy residents, cementing the state's position as one of the nation's most regressive tax environments.…
  - why: The essay contrasts Washington's regressive sales tax reliance with the potential for LVT to fix this, implicitly supporting the claim that LVT can be progressive.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“generating necessary revenue while”**
  - sentence: …A land value tax shift would allow Spokane to more effectively utilize its limited property taxing authority, generating necessary revenue while simultaneously encouraging development by taxing unimproved land at higher rates than developed…
  - why: Spokane's pursuit of LVT to encourage development counters the objection that LVT is useless without prior zoning reform, as they seek the tax to spur development directly.

### [The Mad Policy Hurting Bank Recovery](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-mad-policy-hurting-bank-recovery/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“long tail of the 2007”**
  - sentence: …This is the long tail of the 2007 subprime collapse still wagging after all these years.…
  - why: The essay identifies the lingering foreclosure inventory as the aftermath of the 2007 subprime collapse, illustrating the long duration of the land/property cycle.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“negative interest rates suggests”**
  - sentence: …The fact that central banks are using negative interest rates suggests another agenda — one of their own.…
  - why: The essay argues that central bank credit policies (negative rates) are actively harming the banking system and recovery, countering the view that credit manipulation is the solution to the cycle.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“Bank of Japan’s decision to use”**
  - sentence: …The Bank of Japan’s decision to use negative rates will most likely drive the country into recession again.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that government intervention (specifically negative interest rates) can tame the cycle, arguing instead that it drives recession.

### [The Man Who Used Liberty Dollars](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-man-who-used-liberty-dollars/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“The Feds were mighty bad”**
  - sentence: …When it came to truth and real legality, The Feds were mighty bad!…
  - why: The essay dramatizes the state's suppression of alternative currencies, illustrating the narrative that established powers enforce their interests.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Why do you hate America”**
  - sentence: …“Why do you hate America?” they yelled.…
  - why: The agents' accusation frames alternative monetary systems as un-American, illustrating the objection that Georgist or radical reforms are conflated with socialism.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“Silver keeps it real”**
  - sentence: …But Jake just answered calm and steady, “Silver keeps it real.”…
  - why: The protagonist's defense of silver coins illustrates the narrative that tangible assets (like land) offer a 'real' value that fiat systems cannot evade.

### [The Marxist Distribution of Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-marxist-distribution-of-income/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“complete community collection”**
