  - sentence: …Given that the amount of land rent is substantial, about a third of national income (according to Australian studies), the efficient initial distribution of income is zero taxes on labor and capital, and the complete community collection of…
  - why: The essay argues that land rent is substantial enough to replace all taxes on labor and capital.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“wrongly analyzed, such as mixing”**
  - sentence: …Economists today ignore land because it is excluded from most textbooks and economics classes, and when it is taken into account, it is usually wrongly analyzed, such as mixing in returns to capital goods and entrepreneurship into land rent…
  - why: The essay critiques the conflation of land with capital goods and entrepreneurship.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“land rent is substantial”**
  - sentence: …Given that the amount of land rent is substantial, about a third of national income (according to Australian studies), the efficient initial distribution of income is zero taxes on labor and capital, and the complete community collection of…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that rent is too small to fund government, citing it as a third of national income.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“almost all economists since then”**
  - sentence: …That can be solved with a classical two-factor model that uses land and labor, but Karl Marx told everybody that Das Kapital is the key to economics, and almost all economists since then, even the free-marketeers, have been, in that sense, …
  - why: The essay argues that modern economics is corrupted by a Marxist framework that excludes land.

### [The Modern Georgism of Respected Economists Part 1/3: Joseph Stiglitz](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-modern-georgism-of-respected-economists-part-1-3-joseph-stiglitz/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“Henry George Theorem”**
  - sentence: …The interview later mentions Stiglitz’ work on the Henry George Theorem : “Your academic work led you to formulate what you called "The Henry George Theorem."…
  - why: The essay details Stiglitz's formulation of the theorem, which proves that efficient public spending generates land rent sufficient to fund it.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“tax natural resource rents”**
  - sentence: …That is why it also makes sense, from an efficiency point of view, to tax natural resource rents at as close to 100% as possible .…
  - why: Stiglitz argues for high-rate taxation of inelastic natural resources like spectrum and minerals to capture rent without distorting supply.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“distinction between true capital”**
  - sentence: …The key to these confusions lies in recognizing a key distinction of Georgism: the distinction between true capital and spurious capital.…
  - why: The essay uses Stiglitz's critique of Piketty to refute the conflation of land and monopoly privileges with productive capital goods.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxing as many resource rents”**
  - sentence: …Stiglitz himself supports both halves of the synthesis that lies at the heart of Georgist tax policy: taxing as many resource rents as we can while untaxing productive things like labor.…
  - why: The essay cites Stiglitz's advocacy for shifting the tax burden from labor to inelastic resources like land and pollution.

### [The Modern Georgism of Respected Economists Part 2/3: Harry Gunnison Brown](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-modern-georgism-of-respected-economists-part-2-3-harry-gunnison-brown/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“victims of a legacy of bias”**
  - sentence: …He reviewed the treatment accorded the single tax on land values in several texts and was criticized by one commentator for the causticity of his criticism of them.…
  - why: Brown argued that contemporary economists were biased against the single tax, treating it as a sacred cow they could not objectively evaluate.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“stop the detrimental effects of land speculation”**
  - sentence: …For example, he believed that LVT would stop the detrimental effects of land speculation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly notes Brown's view that LVT would halt the negative impacts of land speculation.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“chief source, even though not necessarily the only source”**
  - sentence: …But that the annual rental value of land should be, eventually, almost entirely appropriated by the public in taxation and that it should be the chief source, even though not necessarily the only source, of public revenue, I am thoroughly c…
  - why: Brown argued that land rent should be the primary source of public revenue, even if not the sole source.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“not stressing any important distinction between sites and natural resources”**
  - sentence: …Does it not seem a bit incongruous to find socialists, "parlor pinks," "liberals" (post-war style) and hidebound conservatives, all in agreement in not admitting or, anyhow, in not stressing any important distinction between sites and natur…
  - why: Brown criticized the conflation of land (natural resources) with constructed capital, arguing that income from land is unearned permission rather than a reward for production.

### [The Modern Georgism of Respected Economists Part 3/3: Leon Walras](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-modern-georgism-of-respected-economists-part-3-3-leon-walras/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no right to any property”**
  - sentence: …It has no right to any property other than the rent of land.…
  - why: Walras explicitly argues the state has no right to tax labor or capital, only land rent.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“lack of taxes”**
  - sentence: …“The collective property of land, and the lack of taxes, which would be its consequence, are not only two acts of justice, they are acts of essential interest for a nation that wishes to live.…
  - why: Walras claims that state ownership of land allows for the elimination of other taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“state could use this revenue”**
  - sentence: …The Georgist solution was that the state could use this revenue on societies behalf instead, allowing it to eliminate other taxes.…
  - why: The essay summarizes the core Georgist narrative of replacing all taxes with land rent revenue.
- **counters** [`objections/symmetry-decrement-objection`](https://progress.org/wiki/symmetry-decrement-objection/) — anchor: **“justice required partial compensation”**
  - sentence: …Firstly, Walras believed justice required partial compensation for landowners.…
  - why: The essay notes Walras supported compensating landowners, countering the objection that Georgism ignores the decrement in land value.

### [The Money-Making Ethic](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-money-making-ethic/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“making money as if”**
  - sentence: …We venerate work and denigrate leisure. We value time spent making money as if work represents our entire contribution to society; as if work is what gives our lives meaning; as if work is who we are.…
  - why: The essay critiques the societal elevation of money-making over genuine contribution, illustrating how a 'money-making ethic' misallocates effort toward rent-seeking rather than productive value.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“value time spent making money”**
  - sentence: …We venerate work and denigrate leisure. We value time spent making money as if work represents our entire contribution to society; as if work is what gives our lives meaning; as if work is who we are.…
  - why: The essay argues that society overvalues labor (making money) relative to other meaningful activities, implicitly supporting the Georgist shift to tax land (passive value) rather than labor (active ef
- **counters** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“evaluated by their job”**
  - sentence: …People usually find themselves evaluated by their job (or by their spouse's job).…
  - why: The essay challenges the rentier economy's underlying status hierarchy by arguing that economic productivity (job status) is a poor proxy for a person's actual contribution to society.

### [The Most Socially Just Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-most-socially-just-tax/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land value is created by man”**
  - sentence: …Consequently, much of the land value is created by man within his society, by his need and ability to reach it and take from it materials, plants and live creatures, as well as the opportunities it provides for working space near to people.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land value derives from community presence and infrastructure, not nature.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“Corruption of Economics”**
  - sentence: …Speculation in land values and its rental earnings are encouraged by the law, in treating a site of land as personal or private property—as if it were an item of capital goods, although it is not, see Prof. Mason Gaffney's and Fred Harrison…
  - why: The essay cites Gaffney and Harrison to argue that economic theory has been corrupted to treat land as capital.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“lack of opportunity of access”**
  - sentence: …The most basic cause of our continuing poverty is the lack of properly paid work and the reason for this is the lack of opportunity of access to the land on which the work must be done.…
  - why: The essay claims that land speculation and monopoly suppress productivity and cause poverty by restricting access.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“single tax on land values”**
  - sentence: …In “Progress and Poverty”, (4), Henry George proposed a single tax on land values without other kinds of tax on earnings, sales of produce, services, capital-gains etc.…
  - why: The essay outlines Henry George's proposal for a single tax on land values to replace all other taxes.

### [The Multiplier Myth](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-multiplier-myth/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“shift to taxing the productive capacity of land”**
  - sentence: …Instead of taxing production and consumption, government could shift to taxing the productive capacity of land.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes from output to land stimulates production rather than stifling it.
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“funds from savings and money creation have been misallocated”**
  - sentence: …Like in the years after 2001, funds from savings and money creation have been misallocated into the purchase of assets - stocks and real estate.…
  - why: The essay disputes the utility of speculation by arguing that capital flowing into land and stocks does not generate real growth.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate bubble of the 1980s”**
  - sentence: …Likewise Japan spent a fortune on infrastructure and welfare after its real estate bubble of the 1980s, with no multiplier response in the economy.…
  - why: The essay cites Japan's post-bubble stagnation as evidence that demand-side stimulus fails following a land bubble.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“deadweight loss of ever greater restrictions”**
  - sentence: …The deadweight loss of ever greater restrictions and imposed costs have stifled economic growth.…
  - why: The essay identifies government-imposed costs and restrictions as the primary factor stifling economic growth.

### [The Myth of the Spending Multiplier](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-myth-of-the-spending-multiplier/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“taxing the location value”**
  - sentence: …Second, push land to its most productive use by taxing the location value of optimal use regardless of current use.…
  - why: The essay proposes taxing land at its optimal-use value, directly addressing the objection that land value cannot be accurately separated from current use or assessed.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“push land to its most productive use”**
  - sentence: …Second, push land to its most productive use by taxing the location value of optimal use regardless of current use.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing location value forces land to its highest productivity, supporting the claim that shifting taxes onto land increases economic output.
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“taxing the location value of optimal use regardless of current use”**
  - sentence: …Second, push land to its most productive use by taxing the location value of optimal use regardless of current use.…
  - why: By advocating for a tax based on optimal use rather than current use, the essay counters the idea that holding land for speculative gain serves a useful economic function.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“push land to its most productive use”**
  - sentence: …Second, push land to its most productive use by taxing the location value of optimal use regardless of current use.…
  - why: The essay's proposal to tax land to force optimal use implies that such a tax would prevent land from being held idle for speculation, aligning with the claim that LVT dampens speculation.

### [The Natural Minimum Wage](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-natural-minimum-wage/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“natural minimum wage”**
  - sentence: …The pure free market has its own natural minimum wage.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing taxes on labor and shifting revenue to land rent establishes a 'natural minimum wage' that maximizes employment and wages.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“abolish all taxes”**
  - sentence: …A natural minimum wage can only be attained if we abolish all taxes and arbitrary restrictions on labor and enterprise, with public revenue instead coming from the rental yield of land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the Georgist policy shift of removing taxes on labor to fund government via land rent.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“margin of production”**
  - sentence: …Collecting the land rent for community revenue would also prevent the margin of production, and wage level, from being pushed to a lower level by those holding land for speculative gains due to expanding commerce and public works.…
  - why: The essay explains that land speculation pushes the margin of production down, suppressing wages, which aligns with the claim that high land rents suppress productivity.
- **counters** [`objections/land-speculation-is-productive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-is-productive/) — anchor: **“holding land for speculative gains”**
  - sentence: …Collecting the land rent for community revenue would also prevent the margin of production, and wage level, from being pushed to a lower level by those holding land for speculative gains due to expanding commerce and public works.…
  - why: The essay argues that holding land for speculative gains actively harms the economy by depressing wages, countering the objection that such speculation is useful.

### [The Optimal Distribution of Income](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-optimal-distribution-of-income/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“optimal productivity is obtained with no taxes on earned income”**
  - sentence: …Since talent is connected to education and labor, optimal productivity is obtained with no taxes on earned income, as that allows individuals to maximize utility based on their preference for leisure versus goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing wages creates disincentives to work and invest, implying that shifting taxes to unearned income (land rent) maximizes welfare.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Fair’s lump-sum tax is equivalent to a continuous tax on all the land rent”**
  - sentence: …Fair’s lump-sum tax is equivalent to a continuous tax on all the land rent, since a tax on a plot of land is a fixed cost to the taxpayer.…
  - why: The essay equates the theoretically optimal lump-sum tax with a tax on land rent, suggesting land rent is a sufficient base for government revenue.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“absence of any tax or arbitrary restriction on labor”**
  - sentence: …In conclusion, the optimal distribution of income requires the equalization of the benefits from land, and the absence of any tax or arbitrary restriction on labor.…
  - why: The essay concludes that the optimal system taxes unearned land rent while leaving earned income (labor) untaxed.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Ethical optimality is achieved by an equal distribution of unearned income”**
  - sentence: …Ethical optimality is achieved by an equal distribution of unearned income, either in cash or in wanted goods, since the ethics of natural moral law presumes human equality.…
  - why: The essay argues for the ethical and efficient distribution of unearned land rent to the population, which is the core of the citizens' dividend narrative.

### [The Origins of Evil](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-origins-of-evil/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“seeking more than one”**
  - sentence: …Specifically, evil arises from ignorance about ethics, a lack of sympathy or empathy for others, and seeking more than one morally deserves.…
  - why: The essay defines evil as seeking more than one morally deserves, which substantively supports the narrative that land titles based on enclosure and conquest constitute a 'great land robbery' or unjus
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“moral equality of human beings”**
  - sentence: …The cognitive independence and moral equality of human beings makes all our values individually subjective, and therefore the concept of the “greater good” is fallacious.…
  - why: The essay grounds its ethical argument in the moral equality of human beings, which is the foundational premise for the intersection of Georgism and Indigenous land justice.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“seeking more than one”**
  - sentence: …Specifically, evil arises from ignorance about ethics, a lack of sympathy or empathy for others, and seeking more than one morally deserves.…
  - why: The essay identifies the pursuit of unearned gain as the root of evil, which directly supports the moral narrative that capturing the unearned increment (land rent) is an ethical imperative.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-and-the-black-white-wealth-gap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-and-the-black-white-wealth-gap/) — anchor: **“equality with respect to”**
  - sentence: …Thus, slowly, and after long struggles, the concept of equality can make headway, so that we can extend the concept of moral equality yet further, to recognize our equality with respect to natural opportunities and to self-ownership.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for extending moral equality to include equal access to natural opportunities, which is the core ethical argument used to address wealth disparities rooted in land access.

### [The Panic of 1907](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-panic-of-1907/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land speculation had caused”**
  - sentence: …Land speculation had caused a previous panic, in 1893, followed by the severe depression of 1893.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land speculation was the main driver of the 1907 panic and identifies a recurring 18-year cycle of land value peaks preceding depressions.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“loans used real estate”**
  - sentence: …Many of their loans used real estate and stocks as collateral.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how the financial crisis was rooted in credit extended against real estate, showing the link between banking growth and land values.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pumped up land values”**
  - sentence: …The great expansion of state-subsidized infrastructure such as canals, railroads, and highways, along with other government services, pumped up land values, subsidizing land ownership.…
  - why: The essay describes how public infrastructure investment increases land values, which then fuels speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“land-value tappation”**
  - sentence: …The effective policy to end panics and depressions is free banking and land-value tappation, the tapping of land value and rent for public revenue, which would prevent speculative excess.…
  - why: The essay concludes that capturing land rent is the necessary remedy to prevent the speculative excesses that cause financial panics.

### [The People’s Endowment](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-peoples-endowment/)
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-dividends-work`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-dividends-work/) — anchor: **“Alaska Permanent Fund”**
  - sentence: …One SWF, the Alaska Permanent Fund (APF) pays a regular dividend, called the Permanent Fund Dividend (PFD), to citizen-residents of Alaska.…
  - why: The essay cites the Alaska Permanent Fund Dividend as a successful example of distributing resource rents to citizens.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“dividend in the form”**
  - sentence: …The Proposal Governments should start to build up a permanent endowment of publicly held assets, both financial and physical, lease at least some of them out to private industry, and use the revenue for two purposes: half for government spe…
  - why: The essay advocates for a universal dividend funded by public assets to address poverty and inequality.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“recognition of their shared ownership”**
  - sentence: …The Proposal Governments should start to build up a permanent endowment of publicly held assets, both financial and physical, lease at least some of them out to private industry, and use the revenue for two purposes: half for government spe…
  - why: The essay frames the dividend as a moral recognition of shared ownership of common resources.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“lease resources at market prices”**
  - sentence: …Otherwise, it should lease resources at market prices, leaving private agents free to decide how to use them.…
  - why: The essay argues that governments should lease public resources at market rates to capture rent for the public endowment.

### [The Police Versus the People](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-police-versus-the-people/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“tax and regulatory policies”**
  - sentence: …The government’s tax and regulatory policies impose obstacles to enterprise and employment.…
  - why: The essay argues that government obstacles to enterprise drive youth into unproductive rent-seeking (drug trade) rather than productive work.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“police themselves have been a lobby”**
  - sentence: …The police themselves have been a lobby in favor of the war on drug possession, and industries such as alcohol and tobacco would have more competition.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice critique by showing how police agencies lobby for policies (drug prohibition) that expand their power and budgets.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“industries such as alcohol and tobacco”**
  - sentence: …The police themselves have been a lobby in favor of the war on drug possession, and industries such as alcohol and tobacco would have more competition.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that established interests (alcohol/tobacco) corrupt policy to suppress competition (drugs), aligning with the theme of regulatory capture.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“obstacles to enterprise and employment”**
  - sentence: …The government’s tax and regulatory policies impose obstacles to enterprise and employment.…
  - why: The essay implicitly supports the Georgist narrative that taxing labor and enterprise (regulatory obstacles) harms the economy, contrasting with the need to remove such burdens.

### [The Pope on Climate Change](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-pope-on-climate-change/)
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“globally enacted levy on all environmental destruction”**
  - sentence: …The recognition that all human beings are properly the owners of natural resources, whose benefits should benefit everyone equally, points to the most effective policy solution: a globally enacted sharing of natural benefits as measured by …
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for charging for environmental destruction as a form of rent capture.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“efficient way to prevent pollution”**
  - sentence: …Economists have mostly agreed since the analysis of Arthur Pigou in 1920 that the generally efficient way to prevent pollution and other negative external effects is a payment of the social cost by the firm and its customers.…
  - why: The essay cites Pigou to argue that charging for pollution is the efficient solution.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replacing their taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …All countries could enact a prosperity-enhancing tax shift by replacing their taxes on wages, enterprise, value added, and goods, with levies on pollution and land value.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting the tax burden off labor and onto land/pollution.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“the earth is essentially a shared inheritance”**
  - sentence: …The Pope also believes that all human beings are owners of natural resources.…
  - why: The essay invokes the moral principle that natural resource benefits belong to everyone.

### [The Powerful Opponents to Geonomics Throughout History](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-powerful-opponents-to-geonomics-throughout-history/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“wrote land out of the economics discipline”**
  - sentence: …Accepting the funds, professors wrote land out of the economics discipline.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that early economics departments, funded by land-owning Robber Barons and universities, intentionally removed land from the discipline to protect their interests.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“contain Marxism because it doesn’t work”**
  - sentence: …Mid last century, a justice of the US Supreme Court is reported to have confided that his elite class felt they could always contain Marxism because it doesn’t work but secretly worried about Georgism because “that was the one reform that h…
  - why: The essay cites a Supreme Court Justice's reported view to distinguish Georgism from Marxism, noting that elites feared Georgism as a uniquely effective reform rather than dismissing it as unworkable 
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“willing to wage war”**
  - sentence: …One reason why the idea of sharing Earth by sharing her worth did not catch on is that its opponents were rich and powerful. And willing to wage war.…
  - why: The essay provides a historical explanation for why LVT has not been widely adopted, attributing its failure to violent suppression and political machinations by powerful elites.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“The Anti-Henry-George Elite”**
  - sentence: …The Anti-Henry-George Elite When Henry George, famous for his Single Tax on land value, campaigned in Great Britain, he had to flee for his life from thugs hired to murder him.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice critique by detailing how entrenched interests (landowners, the Pope, Tammany Hall) used violence, fraud, and political power to block Georgist reforms.

### [The Pre-Existing Land Value Problem](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-pre-existing-land-value-problem/)
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“pre-existing land value problem”**
  - sentence: …The major obstacle to shifting public finances from economy-crushing to prosperity-boosting is pre-existing land value.…
  - why: The essay explicitly defines and analyzes the transition shock problem as the primary obstacle to LVT.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-destroys-its-own-tax-base/) — anchor: **“selling price of land would plunge”**
  - sentence: …To some degree, the decrease in land value is offset by an increase in land rent due to greater production and growth, but if that extra rent is also tapped for public revenue, the selling price of land would plunge to near zero.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the mechanism by which a full LVT capitalizes rent into price, driving the selling price to zero.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“government would give the title holder bonds”**
  - sentence: …In this example, the government would give the title holder bonds that pay interest at $5,000 per year.…
  - why: The essay proposes compensation via bonds as a solution for landowners who lose asset value and income.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“abolition of taxes on these earnings”**
  - sentence: …The abolition of taxes on these earnings would offset the increase in taxes on their land holdings.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing taxes on wages and capital offsets the burden of LVT, supporting the efficiency shift claim.

### [The Privatization of the Moon is Coming](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-privatization-of-the-moon-is-coming/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“mine and retrieve minerals”**
  - sentence: …Ultimately, Moon Express hopes to mine and retrieve minerals.…
  - why: Illustrates the expansion of resource extraction to new frontiers, raising questions about charging rent for natural wealth.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“our current property right system”**
  - sentence: …That will mean our current property right system being exported to outer space.…
  - why: Highlights the extension of terrestrial enclosure and property claims to extraterrestrial commons.
- **illustrates** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“mine and retrieve minerals”**
  - sentence: …Ultimately, Moon Express hopes to mine and retrieve minerals.…
  - why: Provides a contemporary example of the high-value resource rents that Georgist policy aims to capture.

### [The Property Tax Patchwork Impeding Our Local Governments](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-property-tax-patchwork-impeding-our-local-governments/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“workaround to paper over”**
  - sentence: …Baltimore’s patchwork is not a strategy, it is a workaround to paper over the contradictions of a property-tax system that punishes investment and then tries to claw back exemptions to encourage it.…
  - why: The essay argues that the current property tax system is inherently flawed because it punishes investment, necessitating a complex web of exemptions that a land value tax would avoid.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“structure itself is corrupting”**
  - sentence: …But even when corruption is not present, the structure itself is corrupting.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how the discretionary, case-by-case nature of current property tax incentives creates opportunities for favoritism and backdoor deals, validating the objection that complex asses
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifting tax burden from buildings to land”**
  - sentence: …In analyzing this web of distortions and carve-outs, I observe that many of them rely on principles which, if taken to their logical conclusion, point clearly towards shifting tax burden from buildings to land.…
  - why: The essay notes that the patchwork of exemptions effectively attempts to reduce taxes on improvements, implying that a systematic shift to land value taxation would achieve these goals more efficientl
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“access and privilege determine”**
  - sentence: …It creates a system where access and privilege determine who gets the break, and where rules are bent to fit the deal.…
  - why: The essay provides concrete evidence of public-choice failures, showing how local governments engage in rent-seeking and favoritism through tax abatements rather than applying neutral rules.

### [The Pure Free Market: When We Respect Our Rights as Equals](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-pure-free-market-when-we-respect-our-rights-as-equals/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no tax on labor”**
  - sentence: …This implies that all acts and only those acts which are evil are prohibited or penalized.…
  - why: The essay argues that a pure free market requires the absence of taxes on peaceful human action, specifically labor, capital, and exchange.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“generated rentals and site values”**
  - sentence: …Capital goods such as streets, security, transit and parks make sites more productive and attractive, and the generated rentals and site values can be tapped to pay for these goods.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that public infrastructure and civic goods increase site values, which can then be captured to fund those goods.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“benefits of natural resources”**
  - sentence: …Equality implies the equal ownership of the benefits of natural resources.…
  - why: The essay argues that because land is a creation of nature, the economic benefits (rent) derived from it belong equally to all humanity.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“charge equal to the social cost”**
  - sentence: …On-going pollution and other environmental destruction beyond those that can be remedied by negotiation can be remedied with a periodic charge equal to the social cost.…
  - why: The essay proposes treating pollution as a trespass compensated by a charge on social cost, aligning with the Green Georgist principle of charging for environmental use.

### [The Pursuit of Accord](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-pursuit-of-accord/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Basic Income plays”**
  - sentence: …Basic Income plays a significant role in the minimization of negative interference and other aspects of JPA.…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies Basic Income as a key mechanism for minimizing negative interference within its theory of justice.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“minimization of negative interference”**
  - sentence: …Basic Income plays a significant role in the minimization of negative interference and other aspects of JPA.…
  - why: The essay frames Basic Income as a tool to protect dissenters from the ruling coalition, aligning with the narrative of a dividend as a safeguard for individual autonomy.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“A privileged majority”**
  - sentence: …A privileged majority can never speak for an underprivileged minority.…
  - why: The essay critiques the legitimacy of ruling coalitions claiming to represent outsiders, countering the objection that Georgist rent-sharing is merely socialism by framing it as a necessary check on i
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“A privileged majority”**
  - sentence: …A privileged majority can never speak for an underprivileged minority.…
  - why: The essay's core thesis that majorities cannot authentically represent minorities supports the narrative that land justice requires addressing historical dispossession and power imbalances.

### [The Real Lessons in Greek for Leftists](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-real-lessons-in-greek-for-leftists/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“cannot be avoided”**
  - sentence: …None of these forms of taxation, unlike taxes on income or sales, can be driven offshore—income taxes drive income offshore, while taxes on sales drive sales offshore.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value taxes are impossible to offshore, directly supporting the narrative that land is the hardest tax base to avoid.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“raise trillions in America”**
  - sentence: …The Land Value Tax, at least in this country, could raise trillions in America, as I document in my new book, “ America is Not Broke! ”…
  - why: The essay asserts that a land value tax could raise trillions, substantiating the claim that land rent can fund a large share of government.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“community demand that created”**
  - sentence: …It is also Fair (#4) because it is the community demand that created the value of the location in the first place.…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that community demand creates location value, which is the core thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“Tax the large landowners”**
  - sentence: …Tax the large landowners with a Land Value Tax.…
  - why: The essay proposes taxing large landowners as a solution, illustrating the progressive nature of the tax where the burden falls on those with significant land holdings.

### [The Real Rebirth of the American Economy Starts Now](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-real-rebirth-of-the-american-economy-starts-now/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land prices going up”**
  - sentence: …And land prices going up after 2009 were a sure thing.…
  - why: The essay argues that land prices follow a predictable cycle that sophisticated investors exploit, evidencing the claim that land speculation drives boom-bust dynamics.
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“prices across the US”**
  - sentence: …Bloomberg reported last week that builders replenishing land holdings are finding that prices across the US have jumped 57% since the bottom in 2009.…
  - why: The essay attributes rising housing costs and affordability pressures directly to the rapid appreciation of land prices.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“credit expansion will kick”**
  - sentence: …Why do you care? Soon the credit expansion will kick into full gear in the US.…
  - why: The essay links the anticipated economic recovery and housing market activity directly to the expansion of credit and lending.
- **supports** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“next collapse or depression”**
  - sentence: …Let’s reflect here on what’s dominated the headlines for the last five years. And that’s been mostly why the next collapse or depression is just around the corner.…
  - why: The essay references the widespread fear of an imminent collapse that kept capital on the sidelines, illustrating the recurring 'new era' thinking that dismisses cycle risks.

### [The Resources of Nature Belong to Everybody](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-resources-of-nature-belong-to-everybody/)
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“unearned increment of profit”**
  - sentence: …That represents an unearned increment of profit—a profit which comes to a mere handful of lucky citizens and which is denied to the vast majority.…
  - why: The essay explicitly cites FDR to define the injustice of private individuals capturing value created by the community.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land value is a function”**
  - sentence: …Thus, land value is a function exclusively of community and not one’s own labor.…
  - why: The essay argues that location value derives from public services and community density, justifying public capture.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“predict economic crashes”**
  - sentence: …We can even predict economic crashes through the real estate cycle.…
  - why: The essay links land speculation and the real estate cycle to systemic economic instability.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“fruits of the Earth belong to all”**
  - sentence: …Beware of listening to this impostor; you are lost if you forget that the fruits of the Earth belong to all and that the Earth belongs to no one.…
  - why: The essay uses Rousseau to argue that private land titles are illegitimate appropriations of a common inheritance.

### [The Revolt Against the American Empire](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-revolt-against-the-american-empire/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“stop taxing wages and goods”**
  - sentence: …They cannot be blamed, because nobody is paying attention to the remedies proposed by Henry George: stop taxing wages and goods, and follow Ecclesiastes 5:9, "The profit of the earth is for all."…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the Georgist remedy of shifting taxes off labor and goods.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“years of economic stagnation”**
  - sentence: …The barbarians today, in the streets and in the farms and in government, are reacting to years of economic stagnation and growing inequality, but they fail to understand the implicit reality.…
  - why: The essay attributes social unrest and economic stagnation to systemic economic injustice, aligning with the rent-seeking narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“every new consumer creates jobs”**
  - sentence: …But every immigrant is a consumer as well as a potential producer, and every new consumer creates jobs.…
  - why: The essay argues that population growth (immigration) does not cause unemployment, countering the Malthusian objection.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“Massive taxation plus minimum wages”**
  - sentence: …Massive taxation plus minimum wages make labor artificially more expensive, and that drives companies to substitute capital goods for labor.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing labor reduces employment and productivity, implying the efficiency benefit of shifting taxes to land.

### [The Right to Arbitrarily Discriminate](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-right-to-arbitrarily-discriminate/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“freedom of association”**
  - sentence: …Since it is a violation of liberty for government to select one’s friends and business associates, it would therefore also be a violation of liberty for government to force shopkeepers, employers, or landlords to associate with certain grou…
  - why: The essay argues that forcing private business owners to associate with others violates liberty, countering the objection that Georgism (or LVT) is socialism by defining a strict non-interventionist b
- **counters** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“refusing to issue marriage licenses”**
  - sentence: …In the famous case of a county official refusing to issue marriage licenses to homosexual couples , the official was in the wrong: the marriage license should not be denied to couples legally-able to marry.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes between government obligations (which must be non-discriminatory) and private rights, countering narratives that might conflate state justice with private association rules rel
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“government of a free and just society”**
  - sentence: …The government of a free and just society has a moral obligation to serve all equally, and thus avoid arbitrary discrimination—such as the apartheid that took place in South Africa, or the racial segregation of the old South.…
  - why: The essay acknowledges that government actors can fail moral obligations (citing apartheid and segregation), supporting the public-choice critique that government intervention is not automatically ben

### [The Right-wing schism over property taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-right-wing-schism-over-property-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“taxing the land underneath”**
  - sentence: …We can do that by ending the taxation of buildings and improvements and just taxing the land underneath for everybody.…
  - why: The essay advocates shifting the tax burden off buildings (capital) onto land to improve efficiency and incentives.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“incentivizing landowners and landlords”**
  - sentence: …This allows people to improve their homes without fear of long-term tax implications, while also incentivizing landowners and landlords to develop or offload underutilized properties.…
  - why: The text argues that removing the tax on improvements encourages development and prevents land hoarding.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“safeguards need to be put”**
  - sentence: …In that episode, Kolvet brought up the issue that some safeguards need to be put in place for old people to ensure their security.…
  - why: The essay addresses the concern that LVT hurts seniors by noting that safeguards (like tax deferrals) are a solved problem.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“give-away to the wealthy landowner”**
  - sentence: …And so I’m concerned more specifically at a very high level that this is largely a give-away to the wealthy landowner class, which yes, also disproportionately happens to be much older...…
  - why: The essay argues that abolishing property taxes benefits the wealthy landowning class, implying that taxing land value would be more equitable.

### [The Rules of Reality: How Societies Change](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-rules-of-reality-how-societies-change/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“rent is common wealth”**
  - sentence: …Instead, if you really mean that the earth is round, that space is flexible, and that rent is common wealth, then, heck come out and say it.…
  - why: The essay explicitly lists 'rent is common wealth' as a core truth that advocates must boldly articulate to shift paradigms.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“rent is common wealth”**
  - sentence: …Instead, if you really mean that the earth is round, that space is flexible, and that rent is common wealth, then, heck come out and say it.…
  - why: By framing 'rent is common wealth' as a fundamental truth akin to the earth being round, the essay reinforces the moral narrative that economic rent belongs to the community.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“rent is common wealth”**
  - sentence: …Instead, if you really mean that the earth is round, that space is flexible, and that rent is common wealth, then, heck come out and say it.…
  - why: The essay encourages advocates to clearly state the Georgist principle that rent is common wealth, which is the central tenet of the Single Tax narrative.

### [The Sales Tax: History of Its Fallen Champions](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-sales-tax-history-of-its-fallen-champions/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“replace them with heavier taxes”**
  - sentence: …One of their main causes was to rid France of its internal sales taxes and replace them with heavier taxes on property, especially land values.…
  - why: The essay frames the Physiocratic struggle against sales taxes as a fight to shift the tax burden onto land values, illustrating the core Georgist principle of taxing land rather than labor or commerc
- **illustrates** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“Jefferson, Madison and Monroe”**
  - sentence: …These Virginians were heavily imbued with Physiocracy.…
  - why: The essay provides historical evidence that early U.S. leaders like Jefferson, Madison, and Monroe were influenced by Physiocratic (Georgist) ideas, supporting the narrative of Georgism's influence on
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“rights attached to the person”**
  - sentence: …It urged the King to maintain the rights of property, and also to preserve “rights attached to the person and those which derive from the prerogatives of birth and Estate.”…
  - why: The essay documents how entrenched landowning interests (the Parlement de Paris) used ideology to resist land taxation, illustrating the political resistance and class dynamics that Gaffney argues cor
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“subjecting them to the uniform yoke”**
  - sentence: …The Rémonstrance warns against “mixing all the orders of the state together by subjecting them to the uniform yoke of a land tax.”…
  - why: The historical opposition to a 'uniform yoke of a land tax' highlights that land taxation was viewed as distinct from other property taxes, countering the objection that LVT is merely a standard prope

### [The Shape of Rent](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-shape-of-rent/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“new bridge or light rail”**
  - sentence: …Then the new bridge or light rail or even an amphitheater elevates the surrounding land values.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that public infrastructure projects raise nearby land values, which is the core claim of this page.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“self-financing, not needing”**
  - sentence: …Each one could be self-financing, not needing one penny of subsidy.…
  - why: The essay argues that public improvements generate enough land value to cover their costs, directly supporting the claim that public goods can be funded from the rent they create.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“products of the public”**
  - sentence: …Most of these are products of the public, of everyone, not of the individual owner of the location.…
  - why: The essay attributes land value to public amenities and community presence rather than individual owner effort, illustrating the narrative that community creates land value.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“extra income for everyone”**
  - sentence: …Every place could create an extra income for everyone, and they should.…
  - why: The essay advocates for distributing collected land rents to all residents, similar to the Alaska Permanent Fund, which is the central example of this narrative.

### [The Siberian Solution for Refugees](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-siberian-solution-for-refugees/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“higher taxes on land value”**
  - sentence: …A mistake in its policy is to include lower real estate property taxes; the ADT should have higher taxes on land value, and no taxes on buildings, as well as the elimination of taxes on wages and profits.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the Russian Far East's development policy should shift to higher land value taxes and no building taxes to correct current mistakes.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“no taxes on buildings”**
  - sentence: …A mistake in its policy is to include lower real estate property taxes; the ADT should have higher taxes on land value, and no taxes on buildings, as well as the elimination of taxes on wages and profits.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a tax structure that removes penalties on construction (no building tax) while taxing land value, which is the mechanism by which LVT encourages development and discourages spe
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“elimination of taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …A mistake in its policy is to include lower real estate property taxes; the ADT should have higher taxes on land value, and no taxes on buildings, as well as the elimination of taxes on wages and profits.…
  - why: The essay proposes replacing taxes on wages and profits with land value taxation to foster a free-market environment where the region would thrive.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“elimination of taxes on wages”**
  - sentence: …A mistake in its policy is to include lower real estate property taxes; the ADT should have higher taxes on land value, and no taxes on buildings, as well as the elimination of taxes on wages and profits.…
  - why: The essay argues for shifting the tax burden off labor and capital (wages and profits) onto land value to improve economic outcomes in the Russian Far East.

### [The Slowdown Slows Down](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-slowdown-slows-down/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“the economic cycle”**
  - sentence: …People pay attention to the peaks and bottoms of the economic cycle, but the more important turning points are the change in the rate of growth or decline.…
  - why: The essay analyzes the turning points of the business cycle using derivatives to predict the end of the recession, illustrating the mechanics of the cycle.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“Government chiefs will take credit”**
  - sentence: …Government chiefs will take credit for the end of the recession.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the idea that government intervention was the cause of the recovery, arguing it would have happened sooner without it.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“push people to make their land productive”**
  - sentence: …the replacement of punitive income and sales taxation with economy-stimulating land value taxes that would push people to make their land productive while pulling them up by the incentive of tax-free gains.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes to land values would stimulate the economy and encourage productive use of land.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“tapping the rent beneath our feet”**
  - sentence: …the economist who realized that true free trade means the abolition of all tax barriers, which implies lowering taxes to the ground, tapping the rent beneath our feet.…
  - why: The essay advocates for Henry George's specific remedy of abolishing other taxes to collect ground rent.

### [The Social Security Non-Multiplier](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-social-security-non-multiplier/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“spending multiplier is a myth”**
  - sentence: …In a normal economy, the spending multiplier is a myth, and without Social Security, private spending would more than replace the spending from Social Security.…
  - why: The essay disputes the Keynesian multiplier logic often used to justify government intervention, countering the objection that Georgist policies (like LVT) require prior government fixes to work.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“savings is spent for investment”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …When it is recognized that savings are spent for investment, the model’s spending multiplier disappears.…
  - why: The essay argues that private savings are channeled into productive investment, supporting the Georgist claim that shifting resources from public consumption to private investment (or untaxed capital)

### [The Story of Dancing Matilda](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-story-of-dancing-matilda/)
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“confiscated, and the land given”**
  - sentence: …The law requires all golf courses in Venezuela to be confiscated, and the land given to the poor.…
  - why: The story depicts arbitrary state confiscation of land leading to injustice and violence, illustrating the failure of nationalization to solve land problems equitably.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“The law requires all golf courses”**
  - sentence: …The law requires all golf courses in Venezuela to be confiscated, and the land given to the poor.…
  - why: The confiscation of Matilda's home under the guise of redistribution exemplifies the narrative that land titles are vulnerable to state seizure rather than secure property.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“wages were very low”**
  - sentence: …During the depression, the demand for sugar had fallen, and wages were very low.…
  - why: The story highlights the economic desperation of the Depression era where low wages drove the characters' desperate attempts to secure land for survival.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“bought a house and land”**
  - sentence: …I bought a house and land with the money, and now, it has just been confiscated.…
  - why: Matilda's loss of her home and livelihood upon the confiscation of her land illustrates the vulnerability of asset-rich individuals when their property is targeted.

### [The Subprime Mortgage Freeze](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-subprime-mortgage-freeze/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“collapsing real estate and credit bubbles”**
  - sentence: …The main problem is not the sub-prime mortgages but the collapsing real estate and credit bubbles.…
  - why: The essay attributes the crisis to a land-fueled boom-bust cycle, arguing that speculation and credit bubbles drive the turmoil.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pump up land values”**
  - sentence: …Public works and civic services pump up land values, as these goods are mostly paid from taxes on wages.…
  - why: It explicitly argues that public investment and services increase land values, creating a subsidy for landowners.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“band-aids on the crumbling”**
  - sentence: …As is typical with government, the U.S. federal government chiefs want to put band-aids on the crumbling real estate market.…
  - why: The essay dismisses government intervention (mortgage freezes) as ineffective 'band-aids' that fail to address the root cause of the cycle.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“manipulation of the interest rate”**
  - sentence: …The roots of the real estate problem are the manipulation of the interest rate by the Federal Reserve system and the refusal by governments to tap land values for public revenue.…
  - why: While acknowledging credit's role (Fed rates), it argues the cycle persists fundamentally because governments fail to tax land values.

### [The Ten Commandments of Geoism](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-ten-commandments-of-geoism/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“distribute the natural land rent”**
  - sentence: …Thou shalt distribute the natural land rent to all persons in equal shares when possible, otherwise distribute the rent to the members of the relevant community.…
  - why: The essay explicitly mandates distributing natural land rent equally to all persons, which is the core definition of a citizen's dividend.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“site rentals generated by population”**
  - sentence: …Thou shalt distribute the site rentals generated by population and commerce in equal shares to the members of the communities that generate them.…
  - why: This passage identifies that site rents are generated by the community (population and commerce), justifying the return of that value to the community.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“rentals generated by civic works”**
  - sentence: …Thou shalt pay the rentals generated by civic works and services to the agent that generates them, whether in the governmental or the private sectors.…
  - why: The essay argues that the value created by civic works should be captured to pay for those services, which is the principle of self-financing public goods.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no tax on any transaction”**
  - sentence: …Therefore there shall be no tax on any transaction, nor on the possession of produced wealth, and there shall be true free trade.…
  - why: The essay advocates for the abolition of taxes on transactions and produced wealth, which implies shifting the tax burden onto land (the only remaining source mentioned for revenue).

### [The Theory of Pure Theory](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-theory-of-pure-theory/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“The Austrian school”**
  - sentence: …The Austrian school of economic thought, especially Ludwig von Mises, bases some of its theory on the concept of human action.…
  - why: The essay outlines the Austrian methodological framework of praxeology and pure theory which underpins the Austrian school's critique of LVT.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“Pure microeconomic theory”**
  - sentence: …Pure microeconomic theory is derived from premises such as scarcity, diminishing returns, unlimited human desires, the tendency to want goods sooner rather than later, economizing behavior, subjective values, and the uncertainty of the futu…
  - why: By defining the axioms of pure theory, the essay implicitly distinguishes land (scarcity) from capital (produced means), countering the neoclassical conflation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“true in theory but false in practice”**
  - sentence: …When some say that an explanation is true in theory but false in practice, they are defining “theory” as a false conjecture rather than a warranted proposition.…
  - why: The essay explicitly refutes the common objection that LVT is theoretically sound but practically unworkable.

### [The Threat of an Electromagnetic Bomb](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-threat-of-an-electromagnetic-bomb/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“Israeli settlers would pay rent”**
  - sentence: …For some mysterious reason, the obvious solution is not being discussed, namely, a confederation in which the Israeli settlers would pay rent in exchange for remaining in the West Bank.…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific rent-based settlement for the West Bank, illustrating a Georgist approach to land justice.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“overthrowing the elected government”**
  - sentence: …The Iranians have a legitimate grievance against the US for its overthrowing the elected government of Mohammad Mossadegh in 1953.…
  - why: The essay cites the 1953 coup as a root cause of conflict, illustrating the narrative that external intervention corrupts political and economic stability.

### [The Transition to Land-Value Taxation](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-transition-to-land-value-taxation/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“land-value reduction stirs up opposition”**
  - sentence: …But this land-value reduction stirs up opposition to the tax shift.…
  - why: The essay directly addresses the wealth shock objection by analyzing the transition's impact on land prices and arguing that most landowners are net winners.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“compensation for net losses”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …While compensation is not morally required, a payment for net losses would make the transition more politically feasible.…
  - why: The essay discusses the 'asset-rich, cash-poor' scenario (Case #2) and proposes compensation mechanisms to resolve it.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“removal of market-hampering taxes”**
  - sentence: …And as the removal of market-hampering taxes would result in greater employment, income, and growth, over time, the government debt that paid for the one-time compensation could be paid off.…
  - why: The essay argues that replacing other taxes with LVT boosts economic growth and employment, supporting the claim of efficiency gains.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“public goods generate higher rent”**
  - sentence: …The typical landowner has been receiving an implicit subsidy from the government, as public goods generate higher rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay justifies the transition by citing the principle that public investment creates land value, which belongs to the community.

### [The Truce and the Noose](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-truce-and-the-noose/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“the land be made common property”**
  - sentence: …The path to peace has to be based on deep equality, which requires that the land be made common property, implemented by each title holder paying the rent of that land to the whole community.…
  - why: The essay argues that peace requires treating land as common property by collecting rent for the community, which is the core definition of this narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“use that rent for its expenses”**
  - sentence: …The confederation government would use that rent for its expenses, and then send the remaining rent to all the residents, each receiving an equal share.…
  - why: The essay proposes a specific political structure where a confederation funds its operations entirely through collected land rent.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“send the remaining rent to all”**
  - sentence: …The confederation government would use that rent for its expenses, and then send the remaining rent to all the residents, each receiving an equal share.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for distributing surplus land rent equally to all residents as a mechanism for peace.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“equality with respect to both self-ownership”**
  - sentence: …How can any reasonable person not agree that equality with respect to both self-ownership and land ownership is the deepest justice?…
  - why: The essay frames the resolution of the Israel-Palestine conflict as a matter of deep justice regarding land ownership, illustrating the broader application of land justice principles.

### [The Two Ultimate Religions](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-two-ultimate-religions/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“persons who have conquered land”**
  - sentence: …Another supremacist view is that persons who have conquered land, and their heirs, when there are no other documented claimants or survivors, have a superior status.…
  - why: The essay identifies the belief that conquest confers superior land rights as a supremacist view, implicitly endorsing the egalitarian counter-claim that such titles are unjust.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal share of the economic benefit”**
  - sentence: …Equality does not require equality in possession, which is not possible, but an equal share of the economic benefit, as measured by what people are willing to pay to use land.…
  - why: The essay argues that true equality regarding land means an equal share of its economic value (rent), which is the foundational logic of the citizens' dividend.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“equal share of the economic benefit”**
  - sentence: …Equality does not require equality in possession, which is not possible, but an equal share of the economic benefit, as measured by what people are willing to pay to use land.…
  - why: By defining the egalitarian share of land as the economic benefit people are willing to pay, the essay substantiates the claim that this rent is a distinct, quantifiable fundable resource.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no restriction or imposed cost”**
  - sentence: …If we take human equality to its ultimate logical conclusion, each person has an equal natural right to live, work, own property, and completely direct his life, with no restriction or imposed cost on his peaceful and honest actions.…
  - why: The essay posits that equality implies removing imposed costs on peaceful action, supporting the Georgist shift of taxation off labor (action) and onto land.

### [The Tyranny of Billionaire Monopolists](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-tyranny-of-billionaire-monopolists/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“land rent belongs to the people”**
  - sentence: …One model is geolibertarianism, in which land rent belongs to the people in equal shares.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for the geolibertarian model where land rent is distributed equally, which is the core definition of the citizens' dividend.
