- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“prevents firm owners from exploiting workers”**
  - sentence: …When the land rent is distributed equally among the people, and when there is no legal restriction or imposed cost on peaceful and honest enterprise, nor on the consumption of goods, then a basic income from rent, plus the easy ability to b…
  - why: The essay argues that a basic income derived from distributed land rent prevents exploitation and housing tyranny, directly supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty and inequality.
- **counters** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“monopolies would dominate the economy”**
  - sentence: …Critics of free markets claim that in a pure market economy, monopolies would dominate the economy, and billionaires would dominate families.…
  - why: The essay disputes the claim that monopolies and billionaires would inevitably dominate a free market, countering the narrative that the economy is structurally doomed to rentier domination.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“confusing capitalism as a free market”**
  - sentence: …The case for landlord and company tyranny by billionaires collapses when closely examined. But the critics cannot do such analysis, as they keep confusing capitalism as a free market with capitalism as today’s mixed economies.…
  - why: The essay critiques the conflation of free markets with mixed economies, implicitly defending the free-market nature of Georgist land value capture against the charge that it is a form of socialism.

### [The Tyrant in the Train](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-tyrant-in-the-train/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“tapping the land values”**
  - sentence: …The efficient way to make rail transit free is to pay for it by tapping the land values of the communities served by the system.…
  - why: The essay argues that public transit should be funded by the land values it generates, which is the core mechanism of land value capture described on this page.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“pay for it by tapping”**
  - sentence: …The efficient way to make rail transit free is to pay for it by tapping the land values of the communities served by the system.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates funding a specific public good (rail transit) via land rent to avoid the inefficiency of other taxes like sales tax.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“efficient and equitable source”**
  - sentence: …We need to pay for mass transit with the efficient and equitable source of public revenues, land rent or land value.…
  - why: The essay characterizes land value as an 'efficient and equitable' revenue source, contrasting it with the regressive and distortionary effects of sales taxes and fines.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land values of the communities”**
  - sentence: …The efficient way to make rail transit free is to pay for it by tapping the land values of the communities served by the system.…
  - why: The essay implies that the transit system creates value for the surrounding communities, justifying the capture of that community-generated land value to fund the service.

### [The U.S. Government's Power to Confiscate](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-us-governments-power-to-confiscate/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“government almost unlimited powers”**
  - sentence: …Such government acts stems from a war mentality that allows government almost unlimited powers during times of war or warlike circumstances.…

- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“power to confiscate or freeze”**
  - sentence: …Thus there seems to be no Constitutional authority for such a taking.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice objection by documenting how the U.S. government utilizes broad emergency powers to seize assets, validating fears that government powers will expand beyond the
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“power to confiscate financial property”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The United States of America is currently under a state of emergency, so the U.S. government could confiscate financial property at any time.…
  - why: The essay supports the corruption objection by demonstrating that the government already possesses and is willing to use sweeping authority to seize financial assets, implying that large rent revenues

### [There Will Always Be Labor](https://www.progress.org/articles/there-will-always-be-labor/)
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“triumph of landowners over labor”**
  - sentence: …It would be a triumph of landowners over labor, because as wages fall, land rent would rise.…
  - why: The essay argues that gains from automation are captured by landowners rather than capital owners, supporting the claim that rising capital shares are actually land rents.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“land rent or land value would be collected”**
  - sentence: …Also, since public works and services increase land rent, to avoid a subsidy, almost all the land rent or land value would be collected for public revenue.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a system where land rent funds public revenue, illustrating the claim's feasibility.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“distributed to the public in equal shares”**
  - sentence: …With government works and services also automated, most of the funds would be distributed to the public in equal shares.…
  - why: The essay proposes distributing collected land rent as a basic income to prevent impoverishment from automation, supporting the dividend narrative.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“ultimately left with land”**
  - sentence: …Tools are produced from land, labor, and capital goods, so if labor is no longer being used, we are ultimately left with land.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes land from capital goods to argue that automation benefits landowners, countering the claim that land is merely a form of capital.

### [Tiny Democracy for Michigan and Florida](https://www.progress.org/articles/tiny-democracy-for-michigan-and-florida/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“The party demagogs need to keep the system”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The party demagods need to keep the system in place to maintain their control.…
  - why: The essay exemplifies public-choice theory by arguing that party chiefs reject efficient, low-cost voting methods to preserve their own power.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“democracy run amok”**
  - sentence: …It is democracy run amok.…
  - why: The essay critiques the inefficiency and complexity of the current political system, echoing the Georgist narrative that existing institutions are dysfunctional and require fundamental reform.

### [To Confront a Bubble](https://www.progress.org/articles/to-confront-a-bubble/)
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“never found a way”**
  - sentence: …The Wall Street Journal on September 7, 2007, reported the former chairman of the Federal Reserve System as saying, "The human race has never found a way to confront bubbles."…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes Greenspan's assertion that bubbles cannot be identified or confronted, citing predictive works on the real estate cycle.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …Evidently he did not read the works of Fred Harrison and others on the real estate cycle that has been affecting economies for 2000 years, and which in the US had already peaked out in 2006, and with recessions following these peaks.…
  - why: The essay argues that the business cycle is fundamentally a recurring real estate cycle driven by land speculation, supporting the narrative claim.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“real estate bubble is caused”**
  - sentence: …The real estate bubble is caused by bad policy and can be prevented by good policy.…
  - why: While acknowledging the role of credit, the essay identifies the subsidy of landowners and land value capture as the root cause, countering the view that credit alone drives cycles.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“Collecting most of the land rent”**
  - sentence: …Collecting most of the land rent would avoid subsidizing landowners who get a pumped up rental and land value due to public works and economic expansion.…
  - why: The essay advocates for land value taxation as a necessary policy to prevent the land bubble and stop the boom-bust cycle.

### [To Subsidize is to Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/to-subsidize-is-to-tax/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“gain in land rent”**
  - sentence: …The biggest subsidy is the gain in land rent and land value caused by governmental public works and civic services.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that public works generate higher land rent, identifying this as a form of subsidy.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“tapping of site value”**
  - sentence: …The tapping of site value to pay for mass transit is likewise a room charge that efficiently pays for the transport.…
  - why: The essay illustrates that public goods like transit can be efficiently funded by capturing the land value they generate.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“pollution charge”**
  - sentence: …Without a pollution charge equal to its social cost, the buyers of goods that create pollution get a subsidy, as they don’t pay the full social cost of their purchase.…
  - why: The essay argues that pollution charges remove the deadweight loss of implicit subsidies by ensuring buyers pay full social costs.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“tapping the economic rent”**
  - sentence: …Instead of taxing the buyers, governments can avoid the deadweight loss by tapping the economic rent or surplus of extracting oil, gas, and other ground resources when they are extracted.…
  - why: The essay advocates for capturing resource rent as an efficient alternative to taxation that avoids deadweight loss.

### [Transcending National Sovereignty](https://www.progress.org/articles/transcending-national-sovereignty/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“sanction the economic institution”**
  - sentence: …And yet, today's nations do not do that.…
  - why: The essay argues that true national sovereignty requires administering territory, but states fail this by sanctioning private landownership, supporting the narrative that land titles are illegitimate 
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“administer its territory”**
  - sentence: …Whatever else a nation-state may do -- join in trade agreements, engage in military buildups, persecute ethnic minorities, manipulate currencies -- Job #1 for every nation-state is to administer its territory.…
  - why: The essay posits that the state's primary job is to administer land rules, implying that the value and order of land derive from the community's political structure rather than private owners.
- **supports** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“sanction the economic institution”**
  - sentence: …And yet, today's nations do not do that.…
  - why: The essay critiques the current system of private landownership as a failure of state sovereignty, implicitly supporting the objection that state ownership (nationalization) is the necessary remedy to
- **supports** [`narratives/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-justice-and-indigenous-reconciliation/) — anchor: **“Individuals, or corporations, are allowed”**
  - sentence: …Individuals, or corporations, are allowed to buy and sell territory, to use it for their own profit, to exclude people from it, and to pollute it, in many cases, at will.…
  - why: The essay highlights the injustice of private entities having the right to exclude and pollute territory, which aligns with the narrative that current land titles are unjust and must be reconciled.

### [Trickle Up? Trickle Down? Or Should Surplus Trickle Out?](https://www.progress.org/articles/trickle-up-trickle-down-or-should-surplus-trickle-out/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“Unlike owners, they do create its value”**
  - sentence: …Like owners, neighbors did not create the land.…
  - why: The essay argues that land value is generated by community presence and demand rather than by owners or physical development.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“distribute recovered rents”**
  - sentence: …Some governments already distribute recovered rents: Aspen CO, Singapore, and Alaska with its oil royalties.…
  - why: The essay cites Alaska and other jurisdictions as successful examples of governments distributing recovered rents to the public.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifted their property tax off buildings”**
  - sentence: …So they shifted their property tax off buildings, onto locations, set the rate at a market amount so it did not gouge anybody, and "the rest was history";…
  - why: The essay uses the historical example of Johannesburg to illustrate that shifting tax onto land spurred redevelopment.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“sharing the worth of Earth”**
  - sentence: …That is, the sharing the worth of Earth, before any elite or state has a chance to capture society's spending for the nature it uses.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a system of zero taxes on labor and capital funded solely by sharing Earth's worth.

### [Trickle-Down Economics](https://www.progress.org/articles/trickle-down-economics/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“zero marginal tax rate”**
  - sentence: …The ultimate supply-side policy is a marginal tax rate of zero, so that taxes are fixed cost per year, regardless of one’s income, spending, or personal property.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting to a fixed-cost land value tax eliminates marginal tax rates on labor and capital, maximizing production and employment.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Governmental public works”**
  - sentence: …Governmental public works and services make locations more productive and attractive, which increases the demand to be located in those locations, and therefore increase the land rent and land value if the landowners do not pay for these be…
  - why: The essay explains that public investment increases land values, creating an implicit subsidy for landowners when they do not pay for the benefits they receive.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“speculative demand adds”**
  - sentence: …The speculative demand adds to the demand for actual use, and, as happened in 2006-2007, real estate becomes unaffordable, and then we get a severe recession as in in 2008-2009.…
  - why: The essay attributes the 2008 recession to land speculation driven by rising land values, arguing this misdirects economic expansion.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“geyser of economic growth”**
  - sentence: …Land value taxation, and the elimination of other taxes, would result in a geyser of economic growth.…
  - why: The essay claims that replacing distortionary taxes with land value taxation would unleash significant economic growth by removing barriers to production.

### [Trump Says: Let the Lords of Finance Rule](https://www.progress.org/articles/trump-says-let-the-lords-of-finance-rule/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“property cycle that underpins”**
  - sentence: …Over at Cycles, Trends and Forecasts we often write about the property cycle that underpins the US economy.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the economy is driven by a recurring property cycle, which is the core claim of this narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“property market and banks”**
  - sentence: …If you had to boil it down to two factors, it’s the property market and banks of course.…
  - why: The essay identifies the property market and banking as the two fundamental drivers of the economic cycle, supporting the claim that finance growth is tied to land.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“ensure ‘another financial panic”**
  - sentence: …That’s the Act brought in after 2008 to better regulate the banks and ensure ‘another financial panic would never happen again’.…
  - why: The essay cites the failure of the Dodd-Frank Act to prevent the next cycle, countering the objection that government intervention can successfully tame the land/property cycle.
- **supports** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“biggest boom of all time”**
  - sentence: …Cycles, Trends and Forecasts has said the same thing since it launched: the world is on track for the biggest boom of all time.…
  - why: The essay promotes the idea that the current cycle will result in an unprecedented boom, illustrating the 'new era' thinking described in this narrative.

### [Two States Pass Land Value Tax Enabling Legislation -- Landscape 8](https://www.progress.org/articles/two-states-pass-land-value-tax-enabling-legislation-landscape-8/)

  - sentence: …In Falls Church, Councilmember Justine Underhill was making the case publicly before the ink was dry, arguing that soaring Northern Virginia land values need to translate into affordable housing, not just windfalls for landowners.…

- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“translate into affordable housing”**
  - sentence: …In Falls Church, Councilmember Justine Underhill was making the case publicly before the ink was dry, arguing that soaring Northern Virginia land values need to translate into affordable housing, not just windfalls for landowners.…
  - why: cites a policymaker arguing LVT enables affordability rather than windfalls
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“We’d like to see more development go downtown”**
  - sentence: …“We’ve gotten to a little bit of a breaking point where the people that live in the suburban areas of the city are saying, ‘We don’t want any more development. We’d like to see more development go downtown,’” Owen said.…
  - why: shows LVT is supported to direct development into urban centers
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“land value tax enabling bill”**
  - sentence: …Big news today: it’s been many years since a land value tax enabling bill has passed in a state legislature, until this past week, where it’s just happened twice; once in Virginia last week and once in Kentucky yesterday.…
  - why: documents recent legislative progress toward the Single Tax goal

### [Two Undeniable and Two Weak Arguments for a Land Value Tax](https://www.progress.org/articles/two-undeniable-and-two-weak-arguments-for-a-land-value-tax/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“natural rights argument”**
  - sentence: …This is a “natural rights argument” similar to the “homesteading” principle worshipped by many libertarians: They insist that a person should be allowed to own exclusively anything that they have first taken into use (i.e., homesteaded)–inc…
  - why: The essay critiques the ethical argument that land ownership is a natural right, framing it as an arbitrary rule that does not guarantee efficiency.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“tax that cannot be avoided”**
  - sentence: …Many people would rather minimize the amount of money on which the public sector can get its hands. For them, it would be a catastrophe if the state had in its toolbox a tax that (a) could not be avoided and, “worse” yet, (b) one that would…
  - why: The essay explicitly discusses the administrative feature that LVT cannot be avoided, though it notes the political controversy of this fact.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“incentive to underutilize their resources”**
  - sentence: …Real estate owners have in fact a significant incentive to underutilize their resources. Firstly, there are significant risks involved in leasing out an apartment or facility to a tenant.…
  - why: The essay argues that untaxed land ownership creates incentives to withhold land from productive use, which LVT corrects.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“Unnecessarily lowered population density”**
  - sentence: …Unnecessarily lowered population density decreases the efficiency and potential of the whole urban economy (see Conclusions).…
  - why: The essay links inefficient land use (underutilization) to lower density and reduced economic efficiency, supporting the anti-sprawl argument.

### [Type I Civilization Issues](https://www.progress.org/articles/type-i-civilization-issues/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“providing everyone with a base level of support”**
  - sentence: …We claim exclusive ownership of the land and natural resources that we all need to survive and then we force each other to labor to acquire the money in order to use the same resources that we all need to survive instead of sharing the abun…
  - why: The essay argues for sharing natural resource abundance to support everyone, which is the core moral and economic argument of the citizens dividend narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“destroy and pollute the resources”**
  - sentence: …We destroy and pollute the resources that we all need to survive instead of striving for cleanliness and purity.…
  - why: The essay critiques the destruction and pollution of natural resources, aligning with the Green Georgist narrative of charging for ecological use to prevent harm.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“admire those that have acquired the most money”**
  - sentence: …We admire those that have acquired the most money and material possessions even if by plundering, manipulating, and destroying instead of admiring the achievements of scientific, technological, and peacemaking breakthroughs.…
  - why: The essay criticizes the social admiration for wealth extraction via 'plundering' over productive creation, illustrating how rent-seeking behavior drags societal progress.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“claim exclusive ownership of the land”**
  - sentence: …We claim exclusive ownership of the land and natural resources that we all need to survive and then we force each other to labor to acquire the money in order to use the same resources that we all need to survive instead of sharing the abun…
  - why: The essay frames exclusive land ownership as a mechanism that forces labor and restricts access to survival resources, supporting the narrative that land titles are a form of robbery or enclosure.

### [Tyrants Exploit Income Inequality](https://www.progress.org/articles/tyrants-exploit-income-inequality/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“land was dropped as an input factor”**
  - sentence: …Unfortunately, when the classical economics of the 1800s turned into the neoclassical doctrines of the 1900s, both by design (in opposition to the Georgist remedy of taxing land value) and for mathematical convenience, land was dropped as a…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that neoclassical economics intentionally excluded land to oppose the Georgist remedy.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“It is illogical that land rent gets included”**
  - sentence: …It is illogical that land rent gets included in the distribution of income in the return on K, but excluded on the production side, as the models are based only on the two inputs, labor L and capital goods K.…
  - why: The essay critiques the mainstream economic model for conflating land rent with capital returns.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“land rent captures the gains from economic progress”**
  - sentence: …Henry George pointed out that the surplus from production is not in wages, nor in business profits, but in land rent, which is a pure surplus, since land has no cost of production.…
  - why: The essay argues that land rent, not labor or capital, is the primary source of unearned income and inequality.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Marx and his followers focused on the conflict”**
  - sentence: …Marx and his followers focused on the conflict between labor and capital rather than the inequality.…
  - why: The essay contrasts Marx's labor-capital focus with George's land-rent focus to identify the true source of inequality.

### [U.S. Government Demands Congestion Subsidy](https://www.progress.org/articles/us-government-demands-congestion-subsidy/)
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“fee has indeed reduced traffic congestion”**
  - sentence: …The government of London enacted a congestion charge on cars that enter the city center area.…
  - why: The essay cites the London congestion charge as a successful example of reducing traffic.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“shift the public finance of London”**
  - sentence: …It would shift the public finance of London from taxes on production to taps on the site values.…
  - why: The essay argues for replacing taxes on production with site value charges.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“housing would be more affordable”**
  - sentence: …And housing would be more affordable, as land values would not be so pumped up by the civic services.…
  - why: The essay claims that funding services via site values prevents land price inflation and improves affordability.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“higher rental and land value received”**
  - sentence: …Landowners as members of the LCA would no longer be subsidized, as the higher rental and land value received from the city services would be returned to pay for them.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how public services increase land values and advocates for recapturing that value.

### [U.S. Government Voiding the First Amendment](https://www.progress.org/articles/us-government-voiding-the-first-amendment/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“effectively voids the first amendment”**
  - sentence: …The subjective perception of any offense, regardless of the picture or intent, is now punishable, and effectively voids the first amendment.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the public-choice failure where government agencies, specifically the EEOC, expand their power to regulate speech and behavior in ways that infringe on liberty.
- **illustrates** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“subjective perception of any offense”**
  - sentence: …The subjective perception of any offense, regardless of the picture or intent, is now punishable, and effectively voids the first amendment.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates how government control over standards—here, defining harassment—can lead to arbitrary, subjective enforcement that suppresses liberty.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“revolutionary spirit of liberty”**
  - sentence: …The fact that the US government is even considering banning “DONT TREAD ON ME” shows how, effectively, the revolutionary spirit of liberty has already been lost.…
  - why: The essay narrates a departure from the original principles of the American Revolution, paralleling the narrative that core economic and political concepts have been corrupted or lost over time.

### [U.S. Judges Kill the Ninth Amendment](https://www.progress.org/articles/us-judges-kill-the-ninth-amendment/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“actual governments are not really created”**
  - sentence: …Of course actual governments are not really created to protect life, property, or liberty, but to seize and use power.…
  - why: The essay explicitly endorses the public-choice view that government actors seek power rather than the public good.
- **supports** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“use of government to murder, steal”**
  - sentence: …But morally, the use of government to murder, steal, and destroy is no different from individuals doing this.…
  - why: It argues that government power is inherently prone to abuse and immorality, supporting the critique that state control of revenue leads to corruption.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“judicial branch of government having the last say”**
  - sentence: …But the way the U.S. government works is by the judicial branch of government having the last say, overruling laws that violate the Constitution.…
  - why: The essay illustrates that the judiciary can nullify rights, countering the objection that legal barriers like zoning are the primary obstacle to reform.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“rule of men who can decide”**
  - sentence: …We no longer have a real rule of law, but a rule of men who can decide what portions of the constitution to live by and which to disparage.…
  - why: It exemplifies the narrative that systemic corruption and the abandonment of principle have subverted the intended legal order.

### [U.S. Mint Seeks Monopoly](https://www.progress.org/articles/us-mint-seeks-monopoly/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“U.S. Mint issued a warning”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …But on September 14, 2006, the United States Mint issued a warning that "the use of NORFED's 'Liberty Dollar' medallions violates 18 USC § 486, (enacted in 1864) and is a crime."…
  - why: Illustrates how state actors use regulatory power to suppress competition, analogous to how zoning rules might block LVT's supply-side benefits.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“mighty U.S. monetary authorities fear”**
  - sentence: …Is the U.S. dollar domination so fragile that the mighty U.S. monetary authorities fear the competition of this small operation?…
  - why: Illustrates the narrative that established powers fear and suppress alternatives to protect their monopoly.
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“warning is bombast that taints”**
  - sentence: …If they are not engaged in such prosecution, then this warning is bombast that taints what should be the grandeur of a United States of America that stands for liberty and justice for all.…
  - why: Illustrates the public-choice critique that government actions may be driven by power preservation rather than justice or efficiency.

### [UBI advocates should watch South Korea](https://www.progress.org/articles/ubi-advocates-should-watch-south-korea/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Taxing South Korean land values”**
  - sentence: …Taxing South Korean land values could generate an enormous amount of revenue, and a modest LVT could easily raise enough for a meaningful UBI with no risk of evaporating the tax base.…
  - why: provides specific evidence that land rent is sufficient to fund major government programs
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“taxing the speculative extraction”**
  - sentence: …This would directly drive affordability in two ways: provide direct financial support to struggling Korean citizens, while taxing the speculative extraction that makes housing unaffordable in the first place.…
  - why: links high housing costs directly to land speculation and land value
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“The land is already valued”**
  - sentence: …Technically feasible: The land is already valued The next objection to a UBI+LVT proposal is griping about how hard it is to value land.…
  - why: demonstrates that technical valuation is feasible and already implemented
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“UBI directly addresses financial hardship”**
  - sentence: …LVT attacks land speculation and boosts housing supply to address the housing shortage, while UBI directly addresses financial hardship.…
  - why: argues that rent dividends (UBI) funded by land value can alleviate poverty

### [Understanding Land Speculation](https://www.progress.org/articles/the-dog-in-the-manger/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“Not ready, or willing”**
  - sentence: …Not ready, or willing, to use the land they hold, they deny its use to others, and the whole community suffers.…
  - why: The essay opens with the 'dog in the manger' metaphor to illustrate how land speculators withhold land from productive use, which is the core problem described on this page.
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“labor and capital must go elsewhere”**
  - sentence: …The land speculator stands to benefit from long-term increase in land value — but all the while, labor and capital must go elsewhere, farther from the centers of population and exchange.…
  - why: This passage explicitly argues that speculation forces labor and capital away from optimal locations, reducing overall production and supporting the claim that high land rents suppress productivity.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“As the speculative bubble grows”**
  - sentence: …As the speculative bubble grows, people borrow more money to buy the more-expensive land, and even more money to build larger buildings (which will keep land values rising).…
  - why: The essay describes how credit expansion is driven by land speculation and construction, supporting the claim that the growth of modern banking is largely mortgage credit against land.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“epidemic of suburban sprawl”**
  - sentence: …The most visible effect of land speculation is the epidemic of suburban sprawl.…
  - why: By identifying land speculation as the primary driver of suburban sprawl and urban blight, the essay supports the Georgist claim that taxing land (to curb speculation) would reduce sprawl.

### [Undoing Gentrification in San Francisco](https://www.progress.org/articles/san-francisco-prosperity-how-to-prevent-gentrification-in-san-francisco/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“As more people live closer together”**
  - sentence: …As more people live closer together, demand for a location increases.…
  - why: The essay explains that rising land values are driven by community density and demand, not owner effort.
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“Not a very efficient use of space”**
  - sentence: …Not a very efficient use of space—especially given the housing shortage in San Francisco.…
  - why: The essay uses a specific parking lot example to demonstrate how land speculation leads to inefficient use of space.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“redistributed this wealth to all San Franciscans”**
  - sentence: …If the City of San Francisco were to receive the full rental value of its land and redistributed this wealth to all San Franciscans via a Universal Basic Income , we could stop gentrification in San Francisco.…
  - why: The essay argues that capturing land rent and distributing it as a UBI would reduce inequality and social tensions.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“not encouraged to put it to efficient use”**
  - sentence: …Because property owners currently don’t have to pay much money to the City for their ownership of land, they are not encouraged to put it to efficient use.…
  - why: The essay claims that the lack of a land value tax encourages owners to hold land idle rather than develop it.

### [Unemployment is Unnecessary](https://www.progress.org/articles/unemployment-is-unnecessary/)
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“elimination of taxes on labor”**
  - sentence: …Full employment requires the elimination of taxes on labor, exchange, production, and consumption.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing taxes on labor (and capital) is necessary for full employment, and that land value revenue can replace them.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“promoting the productive use of land”**
  - sentence: …Public revenue from land rent or land value could replace all these labor-hampering taxes, while promoting the productive use of land which would further increase wages.…
  - why: The essay claims that shifting taxes to land value promotes the productive use of land and increases wages.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“shift in taxation from labor to land”**
  - sentence: …Furthermore, a shift in taxation from labor to land would both increase employment and increase wages, while letting the worker keep his wage.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates shifting the tax burden from labor to land to improve employment and wages.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Public revenue from land rent”**
  - sentence: …Public revenue from land rent or land value could replace all these labor-hampering taxes, while promoting the productive use of land which would further increase wages.…
  - why: The essay asserts that public revenue from land rent is sufficient to replace existing distortionary taxes.

### [Uniformity Does Not Preclude Land Value Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/uniformity-does-not-preclude-land-value-taxes/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“tax buildings less so we can build more”**
  - sentence: …The pitch is simple: let’s tax buildings less so we can build more.…
  - why: The essay concludes with this pitch to city council members, directly linking the policy mechanism (taxing buildings less) to the desired outcome (building more) which is the core claim of the page.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“taxing land differently from improvements is justified”**
  - sentence: …Under this analysis, taxing land differently from improvements is justified because external, public factors drive land’s value, while improvements reflect the owner’s personal investment.…
  - why: The essay argues that land and improvements are fundamentally different economic subjects, countering the objection that a land value tax is merely a standard property tax and demonstrating why differ
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“uniformity clauses do not need to be overturned”**
  - sentence: …Regardless, the takeaway is that uniformity clauses do not need to be overturned to implement land value taxes.…
  - why: The essay addresses a common legal and political roadblock (uniformity clauses) often cited as the reason LVT is not adopted, and argues that these barriers are surmountable, thereby countering the ob
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“cities to implement land value taxes successfully”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …This precedent has allowed cities across the state to implement land value taxes successfully.…
  - why: By citing successful implementations in cities like those in Pennsylvania, the essay provides evidence that the policy is workable in practice, supporting the broader benefit claims associated with LV

### [Unreasoned Induction](https://www.progress.org/articles/unreasoned-induction/)
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“government itself does not follow”**
  - sentence: …A major problem of teaching moral logic is that government itself does not follow these principles.…
  - why: The essay argues that government failures (supremacism, prohibition, taxation) undermine the teaching of moral logic, illustrating the public-choice critique that government actors are not immune to t
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“unreasoned induction”**
  - sentence: …The problem of unreasoned induction has deep origins in the culture, as it inflicts the great majority of persons.…
  - why: The essay provides a broad sociological account of how 'unreasoned induction' (bias, prejudice, overgeneralization) pervades culture and institutions, serving as a conceptual illustration of the syste
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“taxes and excessive restrictions imposed”**
  - sentence: …As another example, the taxes and excessive restrictions imposed by government on employment destroy job opportunities to persons at the margins...…
  - why: The essay cites taxes and restrictions as causes of economic marginalization, implicitly countering the objection that regulatory interventions (like zoning) are benign solutions, by highlighting thei

### [Updating Henry George’s Meaning of “Single Tax”](https://www.progress.org/articles/updating-henry-georges-meaning-of-single-tax/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“The Single Tax, of course”**
  - sentence: …The Single Tax, of course, was not literally a single tax, but a tax system.…
  - why: The essay explicitly redefines the historical 'Single Tax' concept for a modern context, detailing its scope as a system rather than a literal single levy.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“tax on unearned income”**
  - sentence: …The most accurate modern meaning of “Single Tax” would refer to a single global uniform tax on unearned income, broadly interpreted to include all forms of income that are unearned, but would not refer to any tax on wages.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes unearned income (rent, interest, dividends) from wages, countering the marginalist view that treats all returns to factors of production as equivalent.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-targeting-taxes-reduce-debt-bias/) — anchor: **“All of the corporation’s capital goods”**
  - sentence: …All of the corporation’s capital goods, equipment, land improvements, etc. are assumed to be accurately depreciated, and where applicable, research and development costs are also deducted from gross corporate income.…
  - why: The essay advocates for a corporate tax base that allows full expensing of capital and R&D (deducting the 'normal' return), which is the defining feature of rent-targeting taxes like ACE/CFT.
- **supports** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“pure profits or economic surplus”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …This is what Seligman called “pure profits” or “economic surplus.”…
  - why: The essay frames corporate net profits as 'pure unearned income' or economic rent, supporting the claim that a significant portion of corporate profits are rents rather than returns to productive capi

### [US Agriculture Will Collapse](https://www.progress.org/articles/us-agriculture-will-collapse/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“mining the water rather than harvesting it”**
  - sentence: …US farming is unsustainable, as farmers are mining the water rather than harvesting it.…
  - why: The essay describes how the incentive to capture existing common wealth (water) destroys the resource base, illustrating the drag on growth caused by rent-seeking behavior.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“users pay the price that keeps the quantity demanded”**
  - sentence: …Equal benefits can be implemented by having the users pay the price that keeps the quantity demanded equal to the average annual quantity supplied from rain and snow.…
  - why: The essay argues for charging for the use of natural resources (water) to prevent depletion, supporting the Green Georgist principle of charging rent for ecological use.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“Water has a common pool problem”**
  - sentence: …Water has a common pool problem.…
  - why: The essay attributes agricultural collapse to the mismanagement of a common resource (water) rather than population pressure, countering the Malthusian claim that overpopulation is the primary driver 
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“infrastructure - canals, roads, and bridges - will crack”**
  - sentence: …As the ground falls, infrastructure - canals, roads, and bridges - will crack and break.…
  - why: The essay details how the degradation of the land (subsidence) destroys public infrastructure, illustrating the negative externalities that arise when land rent is not properly managed.

### [US GDP First Quarter 2009](https://www.progress.org/articles/us-gdp-first-quarter-2009/)
- **illustrates** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“huge inventory of empty houses”**
  - sentence: …With the huge inventory of empty houses, construction will continue to decline.…
  - why: The essay documents the bust phase of a land cycle, where speculative overbuilding leaves a surplus of empty housing that depresses construction.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“shift from punitive taxation to economy-boosting land value taxation”**
  - sentence: …Unfortunately, most folks do not understand the first fundamental law of science, that correlation is not causation... only a radical cure, a shift from punitive taxation to economy-boosting land value taxation, can save us, but that requir…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that replacing standard taxes with land value taxation is the necessary 'radical cure' to save the economy from debt and stagnation.
- **illustrates** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“Commercial real estate mortgages are in trouble”**
  - sentence: …The banks are still suffering from loan defaults. Commercial real estate mortgages are in trouble as enterprises cut back and shut down.…
  - why: The essay highlights how the banking sector's distress is tied to real estate mortgages, illustrating the link between finance growth and land credit.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“Government officials will correlate their rescue policies”**
  - sentence: …Unfortunately, most folks do not understand the first fundamental law of science, that correlation is not causation. Government officials will correlate their rescue policies with the coming economic recovery, and few people will demand tha…
  - why: The essay disputes the effectiveness of government intervention, arguing that officials will falsely claim credit for a natural recovery rather than proving their policies caused it.

### [Valence in Economics](https://www.progress.org/articles/valence-in-economics/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“optimize the valence”**
  - sentence: …A land-value tax that replaces taxes on transactions would optimize the valence of all factors.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes from transactions (labor) to land value optimizes the complementarity (valence) of factors, which aligns with the claim that taxing land raises productivity.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replaces taxes on transactions”**
  - sentence: …A land-value tax that replaces taxes on transactions would optimize the valence of all factors.…
  - why: The essay contends that replacing transaction-based taxes (which stifle turnover and employment) with a land-value tax eliminates economic inefficiencies, supporting the claim of shifting tax bases wi
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“stuck in low-valence uses”**
  - sentence: …Much real estate is then stuck in low-valence uses such as vacant lots or one-story buildings in a neighborhood of taller buildings while the land value appreciates.…
  - why: The essay describes how current tax policies cause land to remain underused (low valence), which substantiates the claim that high land rents or mispriced land suppress productivity.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“tax based on current land value”**
  - sentence: …A tax based on current land value pushes the owners to high-valence uses.…
  - why: The essay contrasts a tax based on current land value (which encourages productive use) with the existing acquisition-based system (Prop 13), implicitly countering the objection that LVT is functional

### [Values are Subjective](https://www.progress.org/articles/values-are-subjective/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“value of the land rent”**
  - sentence: …Menger showed how the reverse happens. The value of the land rent and labor used in production comes from the market value of the product, which comes from the subjective values of the buyers.…
  - why: The essay uses Menger's subjective value theory to argue that the value of inputs like land rent is derived from the value of consumer goods, countering the neoclassical view that land is merely a fac
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-austrian-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-austrian-critique/) — anchor: **“values are subjective”**
  - sentence: …One of the axioms or premises of economics is that values are subjective.…
  - why: The essay explicitly grounds its economic analysis in the Austrian axiom of subjective value, which is the foundational premise for Austrian critiques of land value taxation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Some socialists still believe”**
  - sentence: …In the past, economists thought that value came from the cost of production, or the amount of labor need to produce a good.…
  - why: The essay explicitly refutes the labor theory of value—a core tenet of socialism—by arguing that value is subjective and not determined by labor or production costs.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“value came from the cost of production”**
  - sentence: …In the past, economists thought that value came from the cost of production, or the amount of labor need to produce a good.…
  - why: By rejecting the labor theory of value in favor of subjective value, the essay undermines the Marxist economic framework used to critique Georgism.

### [Valuing Land: The Simplest Viable Method](https://www.progress.org/articles/valuing-land-the-simplest-viable-method/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“simplest viable method”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Valuing Land: The Simplest Viable Method…
  - why: The essay directly addresses the objection that land cannot be assessed accurately by proposing and validating a simplified, practical valuation method.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“Building is not punished”**
  - sentence: …The concrete, observable, testable, benefits we’re constantly touting about Land Value Tax boil down to two main things: Building is not punished Land speculation is punished…
  - why: The essay distinguishes LVT from conventional property tax by establishing criteria where improvements do not increase land tax, countering the claim that they are functionally identical.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“Land speculation is punished”**
  - sentence: …The concrete, observable, testable, benefits we’re constantly touting about Land Value Tax boil down to two main things: Building is not punished Land speculation is punished…
  - why: The essay identifies punishing speculation as a core feature of LVT and uses it as a primary test for the validity of its proposed valuation models.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“An LVT based on these land values”**
  - sentence: …We also have a simple, objective test that will tell us for sure when we’ve hit the mark: An LVT based on these land values will yield the benefits predicted by theory.…
  - why: The essay argues that accurate land valuation alone is sufficient to yield LVT's benefits, implying that zoning reform is not a strict prerequisite for the tax to function.

### [Want to Model a Land Value Tax Shift in Your City? Here's How.](https://www.progress.org/articles/want-to-model-a-land-value-tax-shift-in-your-city-heres-how/)
- **illustrates** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“shifting the building tax burden”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …Tax Burden Shift: Are you proposing a split-rate tax that shifts the building tax burden to land?…
  - why: The essay provides a practical methodology for modeling the split-rate tax policy discussed on this page.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“assessor's existing land valuations”**
  - sentence: …In this case we will use the assessor's existing land valuations.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that land value assessments are already available and usable for modeling, countering the objection that they cannot be accurately separated.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“traditional property taxes are split”**
  - sentence: …My reports typically assume a revenue-neutral policy where traditional property taxes are split into two components: one on buildings and one on land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes between traditional property taxes and a split-rate LVT, countering the claim that they are the same.
- **illustrates** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“revenue-neutral policy”**
  - sentence: …My reports typically assume a revenue-neutral policy where traditional property taxes are split into two components: one on buildings and one on land.…
  - why: The essay focuses on modeling a revenue-neutral shift from buildings (capital) to land, illustrating the mechanism of this benefit.

### [War on Drugs Expands to Catnip](https://www.progress.org/articles/war-on-drugs-expands-to-catnip/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“assistant to the Drug Czar”**
  - sentence: …The assistant to the Drug Czar for federal territory is Ima Tyrant, who was transferred to the ONDCP from the Federal Communications Commission's "Office of Philosophy and Economics," which has been enforcing a ban on philosophic and econom…
  - why: satirizes the expansion of bureaucratic power and the potential for government failure
- **illustrates** [`objections/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-revenue-breeds-corruption/) — anchor: **“Ima Tyrant sent dog patrols”**
  - sentence: …But Ima Tyrant sent dog patrols down the red-zone streets of Washington, hounds who were trained to detect minute particles of catnip.…
  - why: depicts the aggressive enforcement tactics and corruption associated with prohibition
- **illustrates** [`objections/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-capture-didnt-make-housing-cheap/) — anchor: **“makes it a federal crime to grow catnip”**
  - sentence: …The 2006 Prohibition of the Possession of Nepetalactone and Catnip Plants Act makes it a federal crime to grow catnip on one's land, even if the landowner does not know that the plants are present.…
  - why: demonstrates how state control over land use can lead to punitive outcomes
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“catnip SWAT teem stormed into the house”**
  - sentence: …The dogs howled at Dr. Felix's front door, and the catnip SWAT teem stormed into the house and went through all the closets, cabinets and shelves, dumping everything on the floor until they finally found the catnip.…
  - why: exemplifies the fear that state agents will abuse power to enforce assessments or bans

### [Water Creates Rent](https://www.progress.org/articles/water-creates-rent/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pumps up the market rent”**
  - sentence: …That water subsidy does not benefit a new buyer of the real estate, because the annual subsidy pumps up the market rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates how subsidized public infrastructure (water works) increases the value of nearby land, capitalizing the public investment into private land prices.
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“tapping economic rents for public revenue”**
  - sentence: …A policy of tapping economic rents for public revenue, replacing taxes on wages and goods, would include tapping the water rent.…
  - why: The essay argues for capturing the scarcity rent of water (a natural resource) to fund government, validating the principle that resource rent capture is workable and beneficial.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“price rationing of markets is more efficient”**
  - sentence: …then maybe people will realize that the price rationing of markets is more efficient, more equitable, and less vulnerable to political pressures.…
  - why: The essay advocates for pricing water at its social cost to prevent depletion, exemplifying the Georgist extension of charging rent for the use of natural commons.

### [Wealth and Capital](https://www.progress.org/articles/wealth-and-capital/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“Land, meaning natural resources, is also excluded”**
  - sentence: …Land, meaning natural resources, is also excluded from economic wealth, as it is not produced by human action.…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes land from economic wealth and capital goods because it is not produced by human action, directly countering the claim that land is just a form of capital.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“financial wealth includes economic wealth and also financial assets”**
  - sentence: …Economic wealth consists of produced goods with a market value, while financial wealth includes economic wealth and also financial assets such as money, bonds, and land.…
  - why: The essay clarifies the composition of financial wealth versus produced economic wealth, illustrating the conceptual distinction relevant to analyzing rentier income derived from non-produced assets l
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“the implicit reality that exists beneath the superficial appearances”**
  - sentence: …The main value of learning economics is to understand the implicit reality that exists beneath the superficial appearances of financial transactions.…
  - why: The essay argues that economic value lies in implicit factors like land rent and capital services rather than superficial financial transactions, supporting the view that land value can be separated f
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“rent which becomes wasted consumption”**
  - sentence: …The economic way of thinking will make you realize that a vacant plot of land still provides land services and rent which becomes wasted consumption if there is no tenant to make productive use of that land.…
  - why: The essay explains that vacant land generates rent that is wasted if unproductively held, substantiating the claim that speculative vacancy represents an economic waste.

### [What a Wonderful World, Unbeknownst to Many](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-a-wonderful-world/)
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“give full value to our natural resources”**
  - sentence: …We need to revise our economic thinking to give full value to our natural resources.…
  - why: The essay argues for a revised economic framework that values natural capital, which is the core thesis of the Green Georgism narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“importance of our collective health”**
  - sentence: …We must also educate ourselves on the importance of our collective health and well being within community where each individual plays a part.…
  - why: The essay emphasizes that individual well-being is tied to the community, supporting the narrative that social value is collectively created.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“directly affect other issues such as poverty and overpopulation”**
  - sentence: …Addressing the massive waste, plundering, exploitation, pollution, and overall inefficiency around our utilization of the natural resources that we all need to survive would also directly affect other issues such as poverty and overpopulati…
  - why: The essay suggests that poverty and overpopulation are symptoms of resource mismanagement and unjust systems rather than root causes, countering the Malthusian objection.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“optimizing resource management as a society”**
  - sentence: …Thankfully, we don't have to halt human activity in order to clean up the house. We just have to adopt more conscious strategies for optimizing resource management as a society.…
  - why: The essay advocates for systemic changes in resource management to support prosperity without halting economic activity, aligning with the efficiency argument for shifting tax burdens.

### [What Baltimore’s assessments taught me about cities, data, and fairness](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-baltimores-assessments-taught-me-about-cities-data-and-fairness/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“enriching the data with public data”**
  - sentence: …However, even with the lacking data, there is good news: enriching the data with public data sources can significantly help.…
  - why: The essay demonstrates that automated valuation models (AVMs) can effectively assess land values despite poor official data, countering the objection that accurate assessment is impossible.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“punish efficient land use”**
  - sentence: …The result is upside-down incentives: commercial assessments often punish efficient land use while rewarding speculation in the form of sprawling parking lots.…
  - why: The essay documents how current assessment methods (income approach) create perverse incentives that punish density and reward speculation, illustrating the risks of flawed assessment systems.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“bring idle land back”**
  - sentence: …In effect, they are a way to bring idle land back into productive use.…
  - why: The essay discusses the prevalence of vacant lots and structures, and the use of land banks to return idle land to productive use, evidencing the problem of speculative vacancy.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“assessments rely on sales data”**
  - sentence: …Assessments rely on sales data, but sales are not the only source of market data.…
  - why: The essay explores the technical challenges of relying on sparse sales data for assessments and the potential of continuous rental data, engaging with the administration of property taxation.

### [What Car Stacks in New York Reveal](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-car-stacks-in-new-york-reveal/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“lift the rent”**
  - sentence: …But once the owner of the site (the landlord) sees this, what’s he going to do?…
  - why: The essay describes how private improvements (car stacking) are absorbed by landlords via higher rents, illustrating the capitalization of value into land.
- **supports** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“corner blocks are vacant”**
  - sentence: …Actually, more than one-fifth of the entire city, excluding roads, is vacant land.…
  - why: The essay cites the prevalence of vacant land in high-demand New York as evidence of land underuse.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“no property tax”**
  - sentence: …The New York councils now offer new start-up businesses no sales taxes, no company tax and no property tax.…
  - why: The essay argues that removing property taxes (holding costs) worsens vacancy, countering the view that zoning alone restricts supply.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“cause of the cycle”**
  - sentence: …This is the cause of the cycle right in front of you.…
  - why: The essay identifies land speculation and tax-free holding costs as the direct drivers of economic cycles.

### [What Do You Know About Public Finance?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-do-you-know-about-public-finance/)
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“pushes land to its most productive use”**
  - sentence: …A land-value tax pushes land to its most productive use, increasing productivity and efficiency.…
  - why: The essay directly states that LVT increases productivity and efficiency by driving land to its highest use.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“increase in the land rent generated”**
  - sentence: …The rest of the cost is best paid for from the increase in the land rent generated by the transit.…
  - why: The essay argues that public goods like transit should be funded by the land rent they create.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“land rent is about a third of national income”**
  - sentence: …Also, empirical studies show that land rent is about a third of national income, more than sufficient to pay for public goods.…
  - why: The essay cites empirical evidence that land rent is sufficient to fund public goods.
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“charge or tax on pollution”**
  - sentence: …A charge or tax on pollution, based on its damage, is more effective than regulations and permits, and the funds can replace taxes that harm the economy.…
  - why: The essay argues that taxing pollution is more effective than regulation and can replace harmful taxes.

### [What don’t we know about LVT? Research that could advance Georgism.](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-dont-we-know-about-lvt-research-that-could-advance-georgism/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“how can we accurately assess”**
  - sentence: …The core research question is how can we accurately assess the value of land for vacant and improved parcels in a defensible way.…
  - why: The essay frames accurate land assessment as a solvable research challenge rather than an impossibility, directly countering the objection.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“What impact does LVT have”**
  - sentence: …What impact does LVT have on the type and quantity of housing that gets built?…
  - why: The essay lists determining LVT's effect on construction as a primary research goal, implying the claim is testable and significant.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“To what extent have Hawaii’s”**
  - sentence: …To what extent have Hawaii’s split-rate taxes discouraged development?…
  - why: The essay proposes studying Hawaii's inverse-LVT to see if tax structure alone discourages development, supporting the idea that zoning and tax policy interact.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-reduces-sprawl`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-reduces-sprawl/) — anchor: **“What impact does LVT have”**
  - sentence: …What impact does LVT have on density and urban sprawl?…
  - why: The essay explicitly identifies the impact of LVT on density and sprawl as a key unanswered research question.

### [What Georgism Is Not](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-georgism-is-not/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-socialism`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-socialism/) — anchor: **“Georgism Is Not Socialism”**
  - sentence: …Georgism Is Not Socialism One common assertion (or objection) is that georgism is simply a form of socialism.…
  - why: The essay explicitly refutes the objection that Georgism is a form of socialism by contrasting its support for private capital and labor with socialist nationalization.
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Marx is absolutely correct”**
  - sentence: …Marx is absolutely correct that under a georgist system, private ownership of capital would still exist.…
  - why: The essay addresses Marx's critique directly, acknowledging his point that Georgism preserves capitalist production while rejecting his conclusion that this makes it backward.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“Georgism is Not Just a Land Value Tax”**
  - sentence: …Georgism is Not Just a Land Value Tax Henry George’s signature proposal for economic reform, the land value tax , has in many ways come to be synonymous with georgism.…
  - why: The essay argues that Georgism is a broader political-economic philosophy and property ethic, not merely a tax swap, countering the reduction of the movement to a single fiscal policy.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“calling themselves “Single Taxers””** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The original members of the georgist movement are partially responsible for propagating this error by calling themselves “Single Taxers,” implying that the single tax—the LVT—was the whole of their political program.…
  - why: The essay discusses the historical 'Single Tax' narrative, noting how early Georgists' branding obscured the broader scope of the philosophy.

### [What happens when America's Monopoly board fills up?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-happens-when-americas-monopoly-board-fills-up/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth`](https://progress.org/wiki/young-locked-out-of-land-wealth/) — anchor: **“no chance of getting”**
