  - sentence: …In such a rigged game, you would have almost no chance of getting on the “property ladder” and would spend most of your time paying rent to other players until you inevitably go bankrupt.…
  - why: The Monopoly metaphor illustrates the intergenerational lockout caused by the exhaustion of frontiers.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“paradox of Progress and Poverty”**
  - sentence: …Henry George called this phenomenon of massive wealth marching right alongside massive want the paradox of Progress and Poverty , the title of his seminal work on the root cause of economic inequality .…
  - why: The essay cites George's core thesis that rising land values drive inequality and poverty.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“Monopoly game is the American housing market”**
  - sentence: …In case you couldn’t tell, this is a metaphor. The Monopoly game is the American housing market, and the player cohorts are generations.…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames the housing crisis as a land scarcity problem using the Monopoly analogy.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“end of the automobile frontier announced”**
  - sentence: …As the western frontier’s close heralded the first Gilded Age, so the end of the automobile frontier announced the second.…
  - why: The essay links the exhaustion of land frontiers to the recurrence of Gilded Age inequality and economic cycles.

### [What is a Governmental Intervention?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-is-a-governmental-intervention/)
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“economic solution for traffic congestion”**
  - sentence: …For example, the economic solution for traffic congestion is tolls just high enough to let the traffic flow smoothly.…
  - why: The essay argues that tolls are the efficient solution to congestion, contrasting them with restrictive interventions like high-occupancy lanes.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“levy on ground rent or land value”**
  - sentence: …But truly voluntary user fees are not interventions, and a levy on ground rent or land value is not an intervention, since it does not depend on any event, and the fee prevents what would otherwise be the implicit subsidy of the rent genera…
  - why: The essay argues that a levy on land value is not an intervention because it recovers the value generated by public goods, aligning with the claim that public goods can fund themselves via land rent.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Taxes on events, other than negative externalities”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on events, other than negative externalities, intervene into these events.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes between taxes on events (like labor) and levies on land value, supporting the narrative that we should tax land rather than labor to avoid intervention.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“a levy on ground rent or land value is not an intervention”**
  - sentence: …But truly voluntary user fees are not interventions, and a levy on ground rent or land value is not an intervention, since it does not depend on any event, and the fee prevents what would otherwise be the implicit subsidy of the rent genera…
  - why: The essay explicitly distinguishes a land value levy from standard taxes and interventions, countering the objection that LVT is merely another form of property tax or intervention.

### [What Is Geonomics And Why Is It Good To Know?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-is-geonomics-and-why-is-it-good-to-know/)
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“real beneficiaries have been landowners”**
  - sentence: …Bestselling author Thomas Piketty was mistaken to blame capital for the persistent wealth gap. Matt Rognlie (a former Portland neighbor though I didn’t know him) showed the real beneficiaries have been landowners.…
  - why: The essay cites Matt Rognlie's analysis to refute Piketty's capital thesis, arguing that landowners, not capitalists, are the primary beneficiaries of wealth accumulation.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“18-yr land-price cycle”**
  - sentence: …Paying attention to the 18-yr land-price cycle, geonomists can and do regularly.…
  - why: The essay asserts that geonomists can predict recessions by monitoring the 18-year land-price cycle, directly supporting the narrative that land speculation drives boom and bust cycles.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“community does generate its value”**
  - sentence: …While society does not make land, community does generate its value, by their mere presence!…
  - why: The essay explicitly states that land value is generated by the community's presence, which is the core argument for the community-created land value narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“disburse the revenue as dividends”**
  - sentence: …Then disburse the revenue as dividends to citizens.…
  - why: The essay advocates for recovering socially generated values and distributing them as citizen dividends, illustrating the citizens dividend narrative.

### [What is God, and How Does the Brain Handle Mystery?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-is-god-and-how-does-the-brain-handle-mystery/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“spontaneous order in society”**
  - sentence: …In economics, there is a spontaneous order in society and markets.…
  - why: The essay invokes the concept of spontaneous order to argue against central planning, aligning with the Georgist argument for natural economic coordination.
- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“People readily believe in propositions”**
  - sentence: …People readily believe in propositions that have no basis in logic and evidence, and often despite contrary evidence and logic.…
  - why: The essay observes that humans often act illogically and ignore evidence, supporting the public-choice critique that government actors are not rational optimizers.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“universal ethical imperative”**
  - sentence: …The universal ethic, which gives a pure free market its meaning, can be derived from human nature.…
  - why: The essay grounds economic ethics in human nature and natural order, paralleling the claim that community interaction creates value and ethical norms.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“inflict costs on the very economy”**
  - sentence: …The economic mystery is why people reject nature’s offer to pay for collective goods, and instead seek to inflict costs on the very economy that sustains them.…
  - why: The essay describes how people impose costs on the economy rather than cooperating, illustrating the destructive nature of rent-seeking behavior.

### [What Policy Makers and Opinion Formers Should Know](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-policy-makers-and-opinion-formers-should-know/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“The most efficient and equitable sources”**
  - sentence: …The most efficient and equitable sources of public revenue are land rent, pollution charges, and voluntary user fees.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues for shifting revenue sources from labor to land and pollution charges.
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“The most effective policy for pollution”**
  - sentence: …The most effective policy for pollution and congestion is a direct charge on these, compensating for the effect, rather than restrictions that do not let people respond according to their costs and benefits.…
  - why: The text advocates for charging for pollution as trespass, aligning with the Green Georgism narrative.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“policy for pollution and congestion”**
  - sentence: …The most effective policy for pollution and congestion is a direct charge on these, compensating for the effect, rather than restrictions that do not let people respond according to their costs and benefits.…
  - why: The essay supports direct charges on congestion as the most effective policy.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal benefit from the natural bounty”**
  - sentence: …Each person is properly an equal self-owner, and equality requires an equal benefit from the natural bounty, which in the economy becomes measured as land rent.…
  - why: The essay grounds the moral claim to land rent in the equal right to natural bounty.

### [What Should be Exempt from a Land Value Tax?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-should-be-exempt-from-a-land-value-tax/)
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“The market would still decide”**
  - sentence: …The market would still decide what the land is used for, and who gets the right to use it, but it would no longer be possible for a private individual to make money out of the land itself.…
  - why: The essay argues that LVT allows the market to determine land use, countering the objection that LVT is useless without zoning reform.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-causes-overdevelopment`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-causes-overdevelopment/) — anchor: **“The market would still decide”**
  - sentence: …The market would still decide what the land is used for, and who gets the right to use it, but it would no longer be possible for a private individual to make money out of the land itself.…
  - why: The essay asserts that LVT preserves market allocation of land use, countering fears that it forces development regardless of public benefit.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-asset-rich-cash-poor/) — anchor: **“The National Trust would clearly either be wiped out”**
  - sentence: …The National Trust would clearly either be wiped out under a full LVT, or would no longer be able to fulfil its mandate to conserve Britain’s heritage so effectively.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the objection that LVT can financially cripple asset-rich, income-poor owners like conservation charities.
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“land rent should be used for public benefit”**
  - sentence: …Georgists believe that land rent should be used for public benefit, and LVT is just a means to that end.…
  - why: The essay explicitly grounds the justification for LVT in the principle that land rent belongs to the public.

### [What Should Be Public, and What Should Be Private?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-should-be-public-and-what-should-be-private/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“private labor and wages”**
  - sentence: …A tax on wages socializes labor to that degree.…
  - why: The essay argues that wages should be tax-free to avoid socializing labor, supporting the claim to shift taxes off labor.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“equal share of rent”**
  - sentence: …If this is accepted, as was argued by the philosopher John Locke, then this implies that each person has a right to an equal share of benefits from nature, which economically is manifested in an equal share of the natural land rent.…
  - why: The essay argues that natural land rent belongs equally to individuals and should be paid as a resident's dividend.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“government has created that poverty”**
  - sentence: …But government has created that poverty in the first place by taxing wages and subsidizing landowners.…
  - why: The essay claims that government intervention—specifically taxing wages and subsidizing landowners—is the root cause of poverty.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“foundation on which the financial”**
  - sentence: …but not so evident is the foundation on which the financial edifice is built, the ground upon which it is all constructed, the land.…
  - why: The essay asserts that the financial industry is fundamentally built upon land.

### [What Troubles Disappear If We Share Rents?](https://www.progress.org/articles/what-troubles-disappear-if-we-share-rents/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Less Poverty”**
  - sentence: …Less Poverty Less embarrassment from putting groceries back at the checkout.…
  - why: The essay lists specific ways a Citizens Dividend alleviates poverty, such as affording quality goods and avoiding utility shut-offs.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“share of social surplus”**
  - sentence: …Only if the extra income—a “Citizens Dividend”—is a share of social surplus, mainly of location value and patent rent, then the healing of society and politics becomes permanent.…
  - why: The essay explicitly defines the Citizens Dividend as a share of social surplus (location value), illustrating the core narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“share of land value”**
  - sentence: …Getting a share of land value, people can always afford housing.…
  - why: The essay argues that capturing land value to fund a dividend directly addresses housing affordability and poverty.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“always afford housing”**
  - sentence: …Getting a share of land value, people can always afford housing.…
  - why: The essay posits that distributing land rent enables people to afford housing, supporting the link between land value capture and affordability.

### [What's the Best Way to Save Energy?](https://www.progress.org/articles/whats-the-best-way-to-save-energy/)
- **supports** [`benefits/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions`](https://progress.org/wiki/carbon-pricing-cuts-emissions/) — anchor: **“The efficient way to reduce pollution”**
  - sentence: …The efficient way to reduce pollution is with a periodic charge.…
  - why: The essay argues that pollution charges (carbon pricing) are superior to command-and-control regulation for reducing emissions.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“federal government would intrude”**
  - sentence: …But now the federal government would intrude into local governance with one-size-fits-all regulations.…
  - why: The essay critiques federal 'one-size-fits-all' mandates, implicitly supporting the Georgist preference for local governance and opposing centralized regulatory burdens.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“The costs of such government mandates”**
  - sentence: …The costs of such government mandates are in effect a tax.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the inefficiency of hidden regulatory costs compared to transparent taxes, supporting the narrative of shifting to efficient, visible taxes.

### [What's the Matter with Money?](https://www.progress.org/articles/whats-the-matter-with-money/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“fuels land speculation”**
  - sentence: …The pushed-down interest rate also induces the purchase of real estate, and fuels land speculation during an economic boom.…
  - why: The essay argues that artificially low interest rates from central banking specifically drive land speculation and real estate bubbles.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“business cycle would not be eliminated”**
  - sentence: …But if the money and banking system remained status quo, the business cycle would not be eliminated.…
  - why: The essay posits that monetary manipulation of interest rates is a primary driver of the business cycle and depressions.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“distortion of the interest rate”**
  - sentence: …The distortion of the interest rate and the rise in price inflation skews prices and distorts profits, creating the economic waste of misallocated resources.…
  - why: While acknowledging credit's role, the essay argues the cycle is caused by the distortion of interest rates affecting land and capital goods, not just credit volume alone.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“leave land values as the basis”**
  - sentence: …The elimination of stinky taxes - on wages, profits, value added, and sales - would leave land values as the basis for public finance, whether tapped by governments or by contractual civic associations and proprietary communities.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates replacing all distortionary taxes with public finance based on land values.

### [What’s the Cost of America?](https://www.progress.org/articles/whats-the-cost-of-america/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“how much is the worth”**
  - sentence: …The past few weeks, I’ve been pestering our official , public economists for a statistic absent from government websites—to wit: how much is the worth of Earth in America?…
  - why: The essay argues that quantifying total land value is a critical missing statistic necessary to establish the potential revenue base for government.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“BEA (the people that do the GDP)”**
  - sentence: …It’s my understanding that the BEA (the people that do the GDP) is working on including land in the National Balance Sheet, but they’re not there yet.…
  - why: The correspondence with economists indicates that official bodies are actively working on methodologies to assess and include land value in national accounts, countering the claim that it cannot be do
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“surplus—also known as “economic rent””**
  - sentence: …This article is part of a series by Jeffery J. Smith on the surplus—also known as “economic rent”—that exists in the economy.…
  - why: The essay frames the economy around the concept of economic rent, supporting the narrative that a significant surplus exists separate from productive value.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“once shared, this surplus could”**
  - sentence: …Currently, this surplus is hoarded; yet once shared, this surplus could generate undreamed of possibilities for the entire human population.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for sharing the economic rent surplus to benefit the population, which is the core of the citizens' dividend narrative.

### [When to Preserve? When to Develop?](https://www.progress.org/articles/when-to-preserve-when-to-develop/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“raise surrounding land values”**
  - sentence: …That is, if government is to green-light a project, or to pursue a project on its own, either way, the project would have to “geonomize”, to raise surrounding land values by an amount greater the project’s costs—all the costs.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a policy where projects are only approved if they increase surrounding land values enough to cover costs, directly supporting the claim that public investment capita
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“rise in value of bordering parcels”**
  - sentence: …And not just directly, but indirectly, too, off of any rise in value of bordering parcels?…
  - why: The essay argues that the value generated by preserving nature (eco-tourism, etc.) is reflected in the value of surrounding land, illustrating the principle that community activity and land use create
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“disbursed to residents as a dividend”**
  - sentence: …The rise in site value, once the bonds are paid off, could be disbursed to residents as a dividend, a la Alaska and Singapore.…
  - why: The essay proposes capturing the rise in site value to fund a citizen dividend, using Alaska and Singapore as examples to support the claim that rent dividends are workable and beneficial.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-causes-overdevelopment`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-causes-overdevelopment/) — anchor: **“profit from non-development”**
  - sentence: …Different from developers, however, is that the public need not develop to profit.…
  - why: The essay argues that a system of public rent capture allows the public to profit from preserving land (non-development) rather than forcing development for revenue, countering the objection that LVT 

### [Where A Tax Reform Has Worked](https://www.progress.org/articles/where-a-tax-reform-has-worked/)
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“taxing site values have 50% more built value”**
  - sentence: …Dr. Ken Lusht, visiting from Penn State, found towns taxing site values have 50% more built value per acre than those that tax both land and buildings (“Site Value Tax [SVT] & Residential Development Patterns”, Lincoln Monograph Series, 199…
  - why: Provides empirical evidence from Victoria, Australia, that taxing land alone results in higher development density.
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pay for them by taxing the resultant rise”**
  - sentence: …The state government passed the 1887 Wright Act, which allowed communities to create by popular vote irrigation districts to build dams and canals and pay for them by taxing the resultant rise in land value.…
  - why: Illustrates the principle of land value capture funding public infrastructure in California's Central Valley.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“levy prevented land speculation”**
  - sentence: …At 6%,this levy prevented land speculation, collected about half the land Rent, and funded government services until the Germans lost their colony at the outbreak of World War I.…
  - why: Cites the historical example of Kiaochow, China, where a land-value tax successfully curbed speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-value-tax-can-be-progressive/) — anchor: **“poorer sections of both cities bear no resemblance”**
  - sentence: …But the poorer sections of both cities bear no resemblance to the degrading slums of nearly all American cities (Woodruff, A. M. & Ecker-Racz, L. L., “Property Taxes and Land-Use Patterns in Australia and New Zealand,” in Land and Building …
  - why: Uses the absence of slums in Australian cities with land taxes as evidence of the equitable outcomes of the policy.

### [Where Does Rent Come From?](https://www.progress.org/articles/where-does-rent-come-from/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“surplus is land rent”**
  - sentence: …But we see here that the surplus is really land rent captured by the landlord.…
  - why: The essay argues that the surplus value generated by the enterprise is actually land rent derived from the scarcity of the space, supporting the narrative that location value (created by the community
- **counters** [`objections/marxist-critique-of-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/marxist-critique-of-georgism/) — anchor: **“Marxist-socialist view is that”**
  - sentence: …The Marxist-socialist view is that it is wages unjustly taken by capitalist profit.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the Marxist view that the surplus is wages stolen by capitalists, arguing instead that it is land rent paid to landlords.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“surplus is really land rent”**
  - sentence: …But we see here that the surplus is really land rent captured by the landlord.…
  - why: By distinguishing the surplus captured by the landlord from the normal return on capital goods, the essay counters the claim that land is just a form of capital.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“efficient and equitable solution”**
  - sentence: …Therefore the efficient and equitable solution is to collect the rent, and then divided it equally among the four waiters and the capitalist…
  - why: The essay concludes by advocating for the collection and equal distribution of land rent among the residents, illustrating the citizens' dividend narrative.

### [Which Is Natural: Poverty or Wealth?](https://www.progress.org/articles/which-is-natural-poverty-or-wealth/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxes were taken off of labor”**
  - sentence: …Poverty would be “extricated,” George said, pulled out by the roots, if taxes were taken off of labor and enterprise, and placed instead on the rent of land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly endorses Henry George's proposal to shift the tax burden from labor to land to abolish poverty.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“poverty is not caused by such deficiency”**
  - sentence: …If poverty is caused by nature, that implies that there are not enough natural resources in the world to keep everyone above subsistence. But in fact poverty is not caused by such deficiency, nor was this the case in the past.…
  - why: The essay argues against the Malthusian idea that natural resource scarcity causes poverty, attributing it instead to human institutions.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“inequality from much of the income”**
  - sentence: …Poverty is caused by the human action of imposing costs and restrictions on production and consumption, and, just as with the serfs, the inequality from much of the income of the economy being captured by the landed elites.…
  - why: The essay supports the narrative that poverty stems from the capture of income by landed elites, analogous to historical serfdom.
- **supports** [`problems/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rising-land-costs-drive-poverty/) — anchor: **“wages plus received rent are not sufficient”**
  - sentence: …The ultimate origins of income are labor and land, and therefore, the prevalence of poverty implies that for many, wages plus received rent are not sufficient to avoid economic deprivation.…
  - why: The essay analyzes poverty as a result of insufficient wages and rent, implicitly linking deprivation to the distribution of land returns.

### [Who benefits from bailouts?](https://www.progress.org/articles/who-benefits-from-bailouts/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real estate is cyclical”**
  - sentence: …But homebuilders are supposed to understand their business. They know that real estate is cyclical.…
  - why: The essay argues that real estate crashes are cyclical events driven by speculative peaks, supporting the narrative that land speculation causes boom and bust cycles.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“government services pump up land values”**
  - sentence: …Taxing the profits of house builders is a crazy way to get government revenue, so giving back some of those tax payments would not be so bad if not for the fact that the profits of the real estate boom included huge subsidies as government …
  - why: The essay explicitly states that government services increase land values, which builders then capture as private profit, evidencing the claim that public investment capitalizes into land.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“smooth out the real estate roller coaster”**
  - sentence: …The natural and simple solution to all this is a single tax on land value that would tap most of the land rent. That would smooth out the real estate roller coaster, and there would be no more bailouts, give-backs, and special favors.…
  - why: The essay argues that a single tax on land value would eliminate the boom-bust cycle and bailouts, supporting the claim that land value taxation dampens speculation.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“special interests are happy to supply”**
  - sentence: …The structure of mass democracy, where millions or many thousands elect a stranger to office, creates an inherent demand for campaign money, which special interests are happy to supply in exchange for favors.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the mechanism of political corruption where real estate interests buy policy favors, aligning with the narrative of how special interests influence government to protect rent-see

### [Who Made the Land Value?](https://www.progress.org/articles/who-made-the-land-value/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“value of land is not created”** ⚠️ *ungrounded anchor*
  - sentence: …The crucial point is this: land value is entirely created by nature, government, and society.…
  - why: The essay's central thesis is that land value derives from nature, government, and society rather than the owner, directly supporting this narrative.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“capitalizes directly into land values”**
  - sentence: …Access to public parks and lakes capitalizes directly into land values, and home buyers compete fiercely for properties within the zone of a high-performing school.…
  - why: The essay lists specific government actions and public goods that raise property prices, evidencing the capitalization of public investment.
- **supports** [`problems/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/housing-unaffordability-is-a-land-problem/) — anchor: **“most of the growth in property values”**
  - sentence: …But buildings are depreciating assets, and most of the growth in property values actually comes from land, which the landowner does not create.…
  - why: The essay argues that rising property values are driven by land appreciation rather than building improvements, supporting the decomposition claim.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-housing-crisis-is-a-land-crisis/) — anchor: **“relentless rise in urban land prices”**
  - sentence: …This explains the relentless rise in urban land prices around the world, much to the glee of homeowners in these locations.…
  - why: The essay explains the mechanism behind rising urban land prices and the resulting benefits to owners, illustrating the land-crisis narrative.

### [Who Owns Your Nation?](https://www.progress.org/articles/who-owns-your-nation/)
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“Those who own the country”**
  - sentence: …Two centuries ago, first Chief Justice of the US Supreme Court, John Jay, said, “Those who own the country ought to govern it.”…
  - why: The essay uses this quote to frame the argument that concentrated land ownership equates to political power and control.
- **supports** [`problems/capital-share-rise-is-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/capital-share-rise-is-land/) — anchor: **“It, not capital, is actually”**
  - sentence: …It, not capital, is actually the driver in growing fortunes, something Georgists have known forever and the rest of the world is catching up .…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that land, rather than capital, is the primary source of wealth concentration.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Demand a Citizen’s Dividend”**
  - sentence: …You are a citizen. Demand a Citizen’s Dividend. Become a “share-receiver”.…
  - why: The essay concludes by advocating for a Citizen's Dividend as the remedy for the unjust privatization of land value.
- **illustrates** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“Investing in government (bribing?) pays off”**
  - sentence: …Investing in government (bribing?) pays off astronomically.…
  - why: The essay illustrates rent-seeking by detailing how the FIRE sector uses political contributions to secure subsidies and privileges.

### [Who should manage land and natural resources?](https://www.progress.org/articles/who-should-manage-land-and-natural-resources/)
- **supports** [`narratives/community-creates-land-value`](https://progress.org/wiki/community-creates-land-value/) — anchor: **“collective goods that generate land rent”**
  - sentence: …In effect, communities such as land trusts, condominiums, and homeowners’ associations provide collective goods that generate land rent, and collect the rent as dues or assessments to pay for the goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that private communities successfully provide public goods which generate land rent, supporting the claim that community activity creates land value.
- **counters** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“government bureaucrats have a knowledge problem”**
  - sentence: …First of all, government bureaucrats have a knowledge problem; they lack sufficient information about what to regulate.…
  - why: The essay counters the public-choice objection by arguing that government management is inefficient due to knowledge problems, whereas private governance can overcome these issues.
- **counters** [`objections/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem`](https://progress.org/wiki/nationalization-solves-the-land-problem/) — anchor: **“privately organized communities often manage resources”**
  - sentence: …The Nobel prize committee described Ostrom’s findings as showing that privately organized communities often manage resources such as lakes and fish better than do governments.…
  - why: The essay disputes the idea that state ownership or nationalization is the solution, arguing instead that private communities manage resources better than governments.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“collect the rent as dues or assessments”**
  - sentence: …In effect, communities such as land trusts, condominiums, and homeowners’ associations provide collective goods that generate land rent, and collect the rent as dues or assessments to pay for the goods.…
  - why: The essay illustrates a practical mechanism for capturing land rent to fund public goods (via private communities), which parallels the Single Tax narrative of funding public services through land ren

### [Whole Phooey on Antimonopoly Action](https://www.progress.org/articles/whole-phooey-on-antimonopoly-action/)
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“governmental regulation of business”**
  - sentence: …The historian Gabriel Kolko in books such as The Triumph of Conservatism has argued that governmental regulation of business was mainly aimed at limiting competition rather than protecting the public.…
  - why: The essay cites Kolko to argue that antitrust regulation is a form of rent-seeking that protects competitors rather than the public.
- **illustrates** [`problems/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents`](https://progress.org/wiki/corporate-profits-increasingly-rents/) — anchor: **“protect its major competitors”**
  - sentence: …It makes me wonder if the real reason for this action is to protect its major competitors.…
  - why: The essay illustrates the claim that corporate profits reflect rents by suggesting the lawsuit is a tool to suppress competition and entrench established firms.

### [Why a Land Value Tax is Inevitable](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-a-land-value-tax-is-inevitable/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“Henry George Theorem”**
  - sentence: …This is the Henry George Theorem, which demonstrates how government spending increases land values by more than this expenditure.…
  - why: The essay explicitly invokes the Henry George Theorem to explain how public spending inflates land values.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“taxes on labour and capital”**
  - sentence: …Shedding taxes on labour and capital, while shifting the burden onto land (and perhaps other unearned income) is the only way out of the fiscal hole that many nations have dug themselves into.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting taxes from labor and capital to land is the only viable solution to fiscal deficits without causing economic harm.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“lights fire under the feet”**
  - sentence: …It lights fire under the feet of land speculators and those underusing large lots of valuable urban land, putting it to its highest and best use.…
  - why: The essay describes how LVT pressures speculators to stop holding land idle, thereby curbing speculation.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“making them more accessible”**
  - sentence: …It pushes more units of housing onto the market, making them more accessible and affordable.…
  - why: The essay claims that LVT increases housing supply, which directly improves affordability.

### [Why are We Still Serfs?](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-are-we-still-serfs/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“we’d all pay Land Dues”**
  - sentence: …This geonomic system—we’d all pay Land Dues in and get Rent Shares back—is both way fair and way efficient.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for a system where land rent is collected and redistributed as a dividend to all members of society.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“eat up the profit in land speculation”**
  - sentence: …Dues would eat up the profit in land speculation—rampant in cities like New York and San Francisco—so speculators would quit mucking with real estate.…
  - why: It argues that charging land dues eliminates the profit motive for speculation, causing speculators to exit the market.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“mortgages cut in half”**
  - sentence: …Home buyers would find mortgages cut in half, since buyers no longer would be buying the land, only the building on it.…
  - why: The essay claims that removing land value from the purchase price would significantly reduce mortgage costs.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“pay rent to the bank”**
  - sentence: …How many, in effect, pay rent to the bank, with the bank as landlord?…
  - why: The essay highlights the dominance of banks in land ownership through mortgages, framing homeowners as effectively paying rent to financial institutions.

### [Why Does Government Promote Home Ownership?](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-does-government-promote-home-ownership/)
- **supports** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“the little owners are”**
  - sentence: …So the big landed interests, those who own valuable commercial real estate, seek the widest possible home ownership, because the little owners are “useful idiots,” allies of the big owners in minimizing taxes on land.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that widespread home ownership is engineered by large landowners to create a voting bloc ('useful idiots') that opposes land taxation, directly supporting the Homevoter Hyp
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“pumping up land values”**
  - sentence: …Governmental works and services are paid for mostly from taxes on wages, goods, profits, and buildings, pumping up land values that capitalize benefits into higher rent and land value.…
  - why: The essay describes the mechanism by which public services, funded by other taxes, increase land values, illustrating the capitalization effect central to this claim.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“kept out of economics textbooks”**
  - sentence: …The capitalization of economic development and civic services into higher land value is kept out of economics textbooks to prevent students and the public from being more aware of it.…
  - why: The essay alleges a deliberate suppression of the concept of land value capitalization in education, which aligns with the narrative that economic teaching has been corrupted to obscure land's role.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“artificial secondary market”**
  - sentence: …Laws subsidizing low-income home ownership and prohibiting discrimination combine with the artificial secondary market for mortgages to push poor folk to buy houses.…
  - why: The essay identifies the creation of an artificial secondary mortgage market as a key government policy driving real estate speculation and ownership, supporting the claim that modern finance is heavi

### [Why Economies Are Failing](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-economies-are-failing/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“repetitive land price bubbles”**
  - sentence: …As a valuer, I’ve not been given the power granted economists to divorce these ills from a marriage which generates repetitive land price bubbles, economic recession, unemployment and myriad accompanying social pathologies – so I leave them…
  - why: The essay argues that land price bubbles directly cause economic recessions and unemployment, forming a cycle that could be eliminated by capturing land rent.
- **supports** [`benefits/taxing-land-raises-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/taxing-land-raises-productivity/) — anchor: **“elimination of bubbles also implies elimination of cycles”**
  - sentence: …I argued “ elimination of bubbles also implies elimination of cycles, and therefore of the retrogression in growth that occurs during each too-politely named ‘business cycle’.…
  - why: The author claims that eliminating land bubbles via land rent capture would have resulted in significantly higher GDP growth, supporting the link between land taxation and productivity.
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“equate the rent flowing from land”**
  - sentence: …Disclosures I’m not a fan of economists who are able to equate the rent flowing from land with the returns to manufactured capital without experiencing a frisson of self-reproach.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the conflation of land rent with capital returns, arguing they must be distinguished.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“mercilessly rip public rent”**
  - sentence: …This has seen wage levels stagnate or decline across the world as the 0.1% (including banks, CEOs and other aspirants) mercilessly rip public rent out of the economy.…
  - why: The essay describes a rentier economy where the wealthy extract public rent, causing wage stagnation and economic failure.

### [Why Georgism? Why Now?](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-georgism-why-now/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“fruits of their labor”**
  - sentence: …By George (get it?), people are entitled to make their own life choices, and to the fruits of their labor, but it doesn’t follow that they must then become atomized individuals who selfishly abandon the common good or the well-being of othe…
  - why: The essay explicitly frames Georgism as the synthesis of individualism and communitarianism, arguing people are entitled to the fruits of their labor while also owing the community for land value.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“getting rich without producing”**
  - sentence: …Henry George’s writing takes a clear moral stand (some people getting rich without producing anything of value, or actively harming society in the process, just feels intuitively wrong to most of us), while also resting on a strong conseque…
  - why: The essay cites the moral intuition that unearned income is wrong as a core strength of Georgism's appeal.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“raising enough tax revenue”**
  - sentence: …Think people should have maximal control over their own lives and be able to pursue their own happiness? Want to keep more of your own hard-earned money while also raising enough tax revenue to pay for basic public goods?…
  - why: The essay describes Georgism's ability to fund public goods (implying rent capture/dividends) while letting people keep their labor income.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“discouraging rent-seeking”**
  - sentence: …Henry George’s writing takes a clear moral stand (some people getting rich without producing anything of value, or actively harming society in the process, just feels intuitively wrong to most of us), while also resting on a strong conseque…
  - why: The essay highlights the consequentialist benefit of Georgism in discouraging rent-seeking and negative externalities.

### [Why Georgists use the 🔰 mark](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-georgists-use-the-mark/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“treating land and other non-produced assets”**
  - sentence: …The socialists and the capitalists were making the same mistake—of treating land and other non-produced assets as merely another species of capital—a fact we know that just ain’t so.…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes the economic conflation of land and capital, identifying it as a fundamental error shared by both socialists and capitalists.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“how much we think we know that just ain’t so”**
  - sentence: …we as a society have gotten it so wrong because of how much we think we know that just ain’t so.…
  - why: The essay echoes the narrative that modern economic understanding is fundamentally flawed regarding land, necessitating a return to first principles.
- **supports** [`narratives/progressive-era-georgism`](https://progress.org/wiki/progressive-era-georgism/) — anchor: **“the first Gilded Age, the rise of the labor movement”**
  - sentence: …And when it closed, we got the first Gilded Age, the rise of the labor movement, and a stark choice: Georgist reform, or Marxist revolution.…
  - why: The essay outlines the historical context of the first Gilded Age and the pivotal choice between Georgist reform and revolution, supporting the narrative of Georgism's historical significance.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“The Second Gilded Age”**
  - sentence: …The material conditions under which Georgism first became the necessary and obvious solution have reproduced themselves now, in this time, a time people outside our movement are already calling, “ The Second Gilded Age.”…
  - why: The essay argues that the material conditions of the original Gilded Age—characterized by the rentier problem—have re-emerged in the modern era.

### [Why I Am a Georgist](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-i-am-a-georgist/)
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“single tax on land value”**
  - sentence: …He is known chiefly for analyzing and promoting a single tax on land value, thus eliminating all other taxes.…
  - why: The essay explicitly endorses the Single Tax narrative by defining Henry George's core proposal and its goal of eliminating other taxes.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“no human being produced the land”**
  - sentence: …Terry Newland agreed, except for a levy on land value, as no human being produced the land, and it did not impose a cost on the economy.…
  - why: The author recounts the moral and economic argument for taxing land rather than labor or capital, which is the central thesis of this narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“role of real estate in the business cycle”**
  - sentence: …My term paper for macroeconomics was on the role of real estate in the business cycle.…
  - why: The essay describes the author's academic work synthesizing Georgist and Austrian theories to link real estate cycles to economic depressions, supporting the claim that land speculation causes cycles.
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“financing of private communities from land rent”**
  - sentence: …His grandfather, Spencer Heath, influenced by Henry George, wrote on the financing of private communities from land rent.…
  - why: The essay references Spencer Heath's work and the author's own dissertation demonstrating that public goods can be funded by the rent they generate.

### [Why Land Value Tax and Universal Basic Income Need Each Other](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-land-value-tax-and-universal-basic-income-need-each-other/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“funds for a Basic Income”**
  - sentence: …It is the most secure way to provide funding for a Basic Income.…
  - why: The essay argues that LVT provides the secure funding necessary for a Basic Income, which functions as a rent dividend to reduce poverty.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“share of the nation’s natural wealth”**
  - sentence: …To provide a citizen with anything less would be to rob him/her of her rightful inheritance.…
  - why: The essay frames the Basic Income as a citizen's rightful share of common wealth, directly supporting the moral narrative of a citizens' dividend.
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“provide sufficient funds for a Basic Income”**
  - sentence: …This would not only provide sufficient funds for a Basic Income to all adults at a reasonable level — £4.5k per year for those ages 21+, £7.2k for those over 65, £2.7k for 16–21 year olds — but also bring an end to private profiteering from…
  - why: The essay provides specific figures demonstrating that land rent and commons licenses can generate substantial revenue, supporting the claim that land rent can fund major public expenditures.
- **counters** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“average owner-occupier household”**
  - sentence: …As a result, the average owner-occupier household in the UK will be between £4,400 and £6,400 a year better off once both LVT and Basic Income have been taken into account.…
  - why: The essay directly counters the objection that homeowners will block LVT by arguing that the dividend distribution makes the average homeowner financially better off.

### [Why NIMBYs Are Not Enough](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-nimbys-are-not-enough/)
- **counters** [`objections/homevoters-will-block-lvt`](https://progress.org/wiki/homevoters-will-block-lvt/) — anchor: **“electoral systems that favor incumbent”**
  - sentence: …Specifically, he argues that, in cities, “electoral systems that favor incumbent landowners and homeowners.…
  - why: The essay directly addresses and disputes the objection that homeowner political capture makes LVT impossible.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“all of the arguments mustered by Tyler”**
  - sentence: …Even more damning, all of the arguments mustered by Tyler could apply equally to his favored method of urban reform: YIMBYism.…
  - why: The essay refutes the idea that LVT is useless without zoning reform by showing YIMBYism faces the same political barriers yet succeeds.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-not-widely-adopted`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-widely-adopted/) — anchor: **“georgism has never been adopted wholesale”**
  - sentence: …To be fair, georgism has never been adopted wholesale by any country.…
  - why: The essay confronts the lack of wholesale adoption as a political challenge rather than a refutation of the policy's merit.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-improves-housing-affordability`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-improves-housing-affordability/) — anchor: **“causes no economic waste or inefficiencies”**
  - sentence: …In fact, Tyler concedes that the economic arguments for the benefits of a land value tax—that it causes no economic waste or inefficiencies and stimulates new construction— are perfectly sound!…
  - why: The essay explicitly affirms the economic consensus that LVT stimulates construction and lacks inefficiencies.

### [Why People Are Unemployed](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-people-are-unemployed/)
- **supports** [`problems/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity`](https://progress.org/wiki/high-land-rents-suppress-productivity/) — anchor: **“Jobs are plentiful where land”**
  - sentence: …Jobs are plentiful where land is, too.…
  - why: The essay argues that affordable land is necessary for employers to hire and expand, linking high land costs directly to unemployment.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Pay everybody a share”**
  - sentence: …Try this. Pay everybody a share of all the rent that’s spent in the region.…
  - why: The essay proposes distributing land rent to residents to increase their bargaining power and reduce involuntary employment.
- **supports** [`benefits/split-rate-increases-construction`](https://progress.org/wiki/split-rate-increases-construction/) — anchor: **“exempt new structures while”**
  - sentence: …In the 1920s, construction workers were kept fully employed in New York because the state let the city exempt new structures while maintaining the property tax on lots.…
  - why: The essay cites 1920s New York as evidence that taxing land but not buildings stimulates construction and employment.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“owners don’t speculate but”**
  - sentence: …To pay the land rents, owners don’t speculate but keep their sites at highest and best use.…
  - why: The essay argues that recovering land rent forces owners to develop sites rather than hold them idle, thereby increasing labor demand.

### [Why People Get Fat](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-people-get-fat/)
- **illustrates** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“experts also sell products”**
  - sentence: …One problem in determining which expert is best is that often, the experts also sell products, so they have a financial interest in their advice beyond what is paid for the information.…
  - why: illustrates the public-choice concern that experts act in self-interest
- **illustrates** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“deadweight loss from taxation”**
  - sentence: …Just as in economics, there is a deadweight loss from taxation that reduces output and growth, in nutrition, there is a deadweight loss from eating junk food that makes you gain weight and will leave you dead sooner.…
  - why: uses the economic concept of deadweight loss as an analogy

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 1](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-1/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-is-just-capital`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-is-just-capital/) — anchor: **“destroy the concept of turnover”**
  - sentence: …There is irony in making this point here in the shadow of The Midway because Frank Knight, the granddaddy of Chicago economists, took great pains to destroy the concept of turnover and its reciprocal, the period of investment, and thus to e…
  - why: The essay explicitly disputes Frank Knight's attempt to erase the economic distinction between land and capital.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“refute by demonstration the claim”**
  - sentence: …For any government to place values on land, whatever the ostensible purpose, is to provide the infrastructure prerequisite to taxing it, and to refute by demonstration the claim that valuation is impracticable.…
  - why: The author argues that the act of valuing land for any purpose demonstrates that the practical objection to assessment is false.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“land tax is not like the income tax”**
  - sentence: …The land tax is not like the income tax a personal liability, but is just in rem, levied on the objective thing, not the person as such.…
  - why: The essay contrasts the intrusive nature of income tax with the limited, objective nature of land tax to support shifting the tax base.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“property tax on land garners that social surplus”**
  - sentence: …It dawns on some as quickly as a bursting tropical daybreak, and on others more like a boreal twilight, that a surplus may be taxed, and that the property tax on land garners that social surplus for the public treasury without impairing any…
  - why: The essay distinguishes the economic effects of taxing land (capturing surplus without disincentive) from general property taxation.

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 2](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-2/)
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“most of what banks have been monetizing”**
  - sentence: …Now as defaults accelerate and the system starts to crumble, we suddenly become aware that most of what banks have been monetizing in recent years is not federal debt at all but, rather -- real estate!…
  - why: argues banking collapses are driven by real estate credit cycles
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“banking collapse was an echo of the real estate collapse”**
  - sentence: …In 1933 Professor Herbert D. Simpson of Northwestern University told the American Economic Association that the banking collapse was an echo of the real estate collapse, and there was none to deny what none could hide, much as some wanted t…
  - why: cites historical evidence linking land booms to banking crises
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“The assessor is only doing for a public purpose”**
  - sentence: …But the assessor is only doing for a public purpose what corporate raiders like T. Boone Pickens and Carl Icahn do for private profit: ferreting out latent values in misallocated land, to make the market work better.…
  - why: defends assessors as necessary for discovering latent land values
- **illustrates** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“ferreting out latent values in misallocated land”**
  - sentence: …But the assessor is only doing for a public purpose what corporate raiders like T. Boone Pickens and Carl Icahn do for private profit: ferreting out latent values in misallocated land, to make the market work better.…
  - why: describes how assessment forces land to its highest use

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 3](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-3/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land value cycle whose amplitude”**
  - sentence: …Here we learn that there was a land value cycle whose amplitude, in the appropriate relative terms, far exceeds that of any other cycle of comparable weight.…
  - why: Cites Hoyt's data to argue that land value cycles are uniquely powerful drivers of economic instability.
- **supports** [`problems/finance-growth-is-land-credit`](https://progress.org/wiki/finance-growth-is-land-credit/) — anchor: **“monetization of land value collateral”**
  - sentence: …Hoyt also documents a social-psychological process of increasing credit financing of land purchases, and monetization of land value collateral, during the upswing of a cycle.…
  - why: Argues that banking expansion is driven by lending against land, creating systemic vulnerability.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“payoff from many public works”**
  - sentence: …The main payoff from many public works is their enhancement and sustenance of land values.…
  - why: States that the primary economic benefit of public infrastructure is the creation of land value.
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“Census of Governments has performed”**
  - sentence: …The U.S. Census of Governments has performed a signal service, since beginning in 1957 under the leadership of Allen Manvel, by collecting and publishing assessment/sales ratios, classified by jurisdiction and class of property.…
  - why: Cites the systematic collection of assessment/sales ratios as evidence that land value can be monitored and evaluated.

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 4](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-4/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“Major biases in land assessment”**
  - sentence: …II. Major biases in land assessment Assessors traditionally enjoy a certain latitude which may be used as de facto "industrial policy" (as the current fashion has it), and has been for decades past, to the degree that assessors must be or a…
  - why: The essay details specific, systemic biases in assessment practices, arguing that the difficulty lies in political interference rather than the technical impossibility of separating land value.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“responsive to local political pressures”**
  - sentence: …Assessors traditionally enjoy a certain latitude which may be used as de facto "industrial policy" (as the current fashion has it), and has been for decades past, to the degree that assessors must be or are responsive to local political pre…
  - why: The text provides evidence that assessors are vulnerable to political pressure and use their discretion to favor specific interests, validating the objection that LVT enables corruption.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-farmers`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-farmers/) — anchor: **“preferential assessment of farmland”**
  - sentence: …Up until 1955 or so the preferential assessment of farmland occurred in many jurisdictions without benefit of law, and the legislative movement kicked off by then-Assemblyman Spiro Agnew of Maryland merely formalized and reinforced, to meet…
  - why: The essay documents that farmland has historically received preferential treatment and underassessment, countering the claim that farmers would be uniquely hurt by accurate land valuation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“bias against owner-occupied homes”**
  - sentence: …Yet this did not prevent, and probably worsened the bias against owner-occupied homes, as aforementioned.…
  - why: By identifying a bias against residential property in favor of industrial or raw land, the text illustrates that current property taxes do not treat all land equally, distinguishing them from a theore

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 5](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-5/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“restrict your data sources”**
  - sentence: …But for technicians using assessment data the lesson is clear: restrict your data sources to the recent sales, then extrapolate from them yourself, the assessor isn't doing it for you.…
