  - sentence: …Some California assessors under Proposition 13 are claiming that real estate has been sold (and may therefore be reassessed) when some arbitrary percentage of the shares are sold. I wish them all success. The successful assessor must be res…
  - why: The essay illustrates the arbitrary and politically fraught nature of assessment rules, specifically the failed attempt to reassess corporate land based on stock sales, supporting the claim that asses
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-farmers`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-farmers/) — anchor: **“institutionalized underassessment of farmland”**
  - sentence: …We know about the institutionalized underassessment of farmland.…
  - why: The essay documents widespread legislative mandates that underassess farmland, contradicting the objection that farmers are uniquely burdened by property taxes.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“preferential assessment for timberland”**
  - sentence: …Fewer people know about preferential assessment for timberland.…
  - why: The essay describes how preferential tax treatment (TPZ) allows timber holders to pay near-zero taxes regardless of zoning or use, showing that tax policy itself drives land use outcomes independent o

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 6](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-6/)
- **illustrates** [`problems/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities`](https://progress.org/wiki/speculative-vacancy-wastes-cities/) — anchor: **“no intention of relinquishing”**
  - sentence: …It has no intention of relinquishing any part of either tract because they are tax free and the annual opportunity cost never appears in the budget.…
  - why: provides a concrete example of tax-free institutional land blocking needed development
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“annual opportunity cost never appears”**
  - sentence: …It has no intention of relinquishing any part of either tract because they are tax free and the annual opportunity cost never appears in the budget.…
  - why: argues that taxation itself creates the pressure to sell, independent of zoning
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“incentive is weak to give priority”**
  - sentence: …Some assessors keep values on exempt land which often are not current because the incentive is weak to give priority to work with no payoff.…
  - why: notes that lack of taxation leads to poor assessment quality
- **supports** [`narratives/the-tax-you-cant-dodge`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-tax-you-cant-dodge/) — anchor: **“assessments help to make markets”**
  - sentence: …Without assessments and land taxes, land markets turn to glue.…
  - why: asserts that taxation and assessment are necessary for liquid land markets

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 7](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-7/)
- **supports** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“Spectrum is a major omission”**
  - sentence: …Spectrum is a major omission from any full accounting of national assets.…
  - why: The essay argues that valuable natural resources like radio spectrum are currently excluded from assessment rolls, implying that capturing this rent could significantly increase government revenue.
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“severed, become intangible or invisible”**
  - sentence: …Sometimes valuable portions of the real estate bundle of rights are severed, become intangible or invisible, and escape valuation.…
  - why: The essay provides concrete examples of rights (like pollution rights and TDRs) that are difficult to assess, evidencing the objection that separating and valuing land components is technically challe
- **illustrates** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“valuable portions of the real estate bundle”**
  - sentence: …Sometimes valuable portions of the real estate bundle of rights are severed, become intangible or invisible, and escape valuation.…
  - why: The essay illustrates how economic rent is increasingly captured through intangible rights (e.g., spectrum, pollution permits) that escape traditional valuation, characteristic of a complex rentier ec
- **supports** [`benefits/resource-rent-capture-works`](https://progress.org/wiki/resource-rent-capture-works/) — anchor: **“limited natural resource that has the essential characteristics”**
  - sentence: …But all these signals travel through a limited natural resource that has the essential characteristics of real estate: natural origin; permanence; fixed location; appropriability; policeability; and now salability…
  - why: The text identifies radio spectrum as a scarce, appropriable natural resource suitable for rent capture, supporting the broader principle that capturing resource rents is feasible.

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 8](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-8/)
- **supports** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“problems with the capitalized income”**
  - sentence: …Here are some problems with the capitalized income approach to assessing farmland, which seems dominant in this area even where not mandated by law.…
  - why: The essay details specific failures of the capitalized income approach (ignoring amenities, mineral rights, tax shelters) to accurately assess land value.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“political organized strength”**
  - sentence: …More operationally effective, I suspect, is political organized strength, an increasing share of it coming from that vast gray area that separates the city from the country where farmers are speculators and speculators may be farmers and bo…
  - why: The author argues that political pressure from organized interests leads to under-assessment of farmland, illustrating how assessment can be gamed.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-hurts-farmers`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-hurts-farmers/) — anchor: **“value of farmland as a tax shelter”**
  - sentence: …The value of farmland as a tax shelter is not news, but perhaps never so dramatized as in a paper by Finis Welch and Robert Evenson finding that the farm income reported on the 1040s of farmers in certain states was about 2% of the respecti…
  - why: The essay notes that farmland values are inflated by tax shelter advantages and speculative mineral rights, implying assessments based on true income would be lower than feared.
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“let the land bubble of the 70s”**
  - sentence: …It was the lack of such a quick remedy that let the land bubble of the 70s soar so dangerously high above reality.…
  - why: The author links the failure to accurately assess speculative land values to the inflation of the 1970s land bubble.

### [Why Research Farmland Ownership And Values? Part 9](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-research-farmland-ownership-and-values-presented-paper-excerpt-9/)
- **counters** [`objections/land-cannot-be-assessed`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-cannot-be-assessed/) — anchor: **“No one should be allowed”**
  - sentence: …No one should be allowed to assess land without one.…
  - why: The essay argues that mapping is essential for accurate assessment, countering the objection that land value cannot be reliably separated or assessed.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-invites-assessment-corruption/) — anchor: **“The usefulness of cadastres presupposes”**
  - sentence: …The usefulness of cadastres presupposes a social psychology of objectivity about land, a cultural heritage from before the 16th Amendment with its in personam approach to public finance.…
  - why: The essay contends that mapping promotes objectivity and prevents the 'in personam' favoritism that leads to corruption, countering the fear that assessors would abuse the system.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-is-just-a-property-tax/) — anchor: **“The parcel is a disorderly”**
  - sentence: …The parcel is a disorderly and unsystematic basis for land records.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes land-based mapping from parcel-based ownership records to show why taxing land alone is fundamentally different and more systematic than standard property taxation.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“The fast-moving tax assessor”**
  - sentence: …The fast-moving tax assessor is a sobering, equilibrating force.…
  - why: The essay argues that assessors using maps can counteract market irrationality and resistance to density, suggesting LVT can work effectively even without prior zoning reform.

### [Why the Crash of 2008 was not a Market Failure](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-the-crash-of-2008-was-not-a-market-failure/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land values induce speculation”**
  - sentence: …Land values capture much of the gain from economic expansion, and then rising land values induce speculation that adds to the demand and pushes land values to such a high level that those who want to buy land to use it get squeezed out.…
  - why: The essay explicitly argues that the 2008 crash was caused by a land-value subsidy that fueled speculation, leading to a boom and bust cycle.
- **supports** [`problems/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-investment-capitalizes-into-land/) — anchor: **“government services and subsidies all increase”**
  - sentence: …Highways, streets, parks, security, schooling, and all the other government services and subsidies all increase the demand for the lands serviced by these works.…
  - why: The essay describes how public works and services increase land values, capitalizing into a subsidy for landowners.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-corruption-of-economics`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-corruption-of-economics/) — anchor: **“capitalization of public works”**
  - sentence: …Conventional economics ignores land and the capitalization of public works into site values — you will not find this in any commercial economics textbook.…
  - why: The essay claims that economic thought is corrupted by landed interests that ensure the exclusion of land value theory from textbooks and think tanks.
- **counters** [`objections/cycles-are-credit-not-land`](https://progress.org/wiki/cycles-are-credit-not-land/) — anchor: **“biggest governmental distortion is the”**
  - sentence: …The biggest governmental distortion is the implicit subsidy to land value.…
  - why: The essay counters the view that credit cycles are independent of land, arguing that the primary distortion driving the cycle is the subsidy to land value.

### [Why the Purpose of the “Single Tax” Changed](https://www.progress.org/articles/why-the-purpose-of-henry-georges-single-tax-changed-after-the-coinage-act-of-1965/)
- **counters** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“taxes on economic rent”**
  - sentence: …Stated differently, and from a Georgist view, after the Act of 1965, taxes on economic rent/surplus/waste (i.e., taxes on unearned income) are no longer needed to fund government, but to cancel or remove waste from the monetary/economic sys…
  - why: The essay explicitly revises the traditional Georgist 'Single Tax' narrative, arguing that while land value taxation remains vital, its purpose has shifted from funding government to removing economic
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“remove waste from the system”**
  - sentence: …As I’ve said in a prior post, if Congress neglects its duty to remove waste from the system (by heavily taxing unearned income and large estates), we’re essentially stuck living in a polluted economic environment.…
  - why: The essay frames unearned income and speculation as 'waste' or a 'tumor' that pollutes the economy, supporting the claim that rent-seeking drags down economic health.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-rentier-economy`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-rentier-economy/) — anchor: **“financial sector and the Donald Trumps”**
  - sentence: …"What you have today is the economy imagines that the financial sector and the Donald Trumps of the world, the real estate speculators, are part of the economy and part of GDP instead of being an overhead, a tumor."…
  - why: The essay cites Michael Hudson to argue that the economy misidentifies rent-seeking activities (finance, speculation) as productive growth, illustrating the rentier economy critique.
- **counters** [`problems/land-rent-could-fund-government`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-rent-could-fund-government/) — anchor: **“taxes on economic rent”**
  - sentence: …Stated differently, and from a Georgist view, after the Act of 1965, taxes on economic rent/surplus/waste (i.e., taxes on unearned income) are no longer needed to fund government, but to cancel or remove waste from the monetary/economic sys…
  - why: The essay disputes the necessity of using land rent to fund government operations in a fiat monetary system, arguing instead that its purpose is to cancel waste.

### [Wildlife Conservation](https://www.progress.org/articles/wildlife-conservation/)
- **supports** [`narratives/ecological-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/ecological-rent/) — anchor: **“simple economic solution”**
  - sentence: …He has dedicated his life to informing people who love nature that there’s a simple economic solution that can save wildlife at no cost to the taxpayer.…
  - why: The essay frames Georgist rent capture as the mechanism to protect the environment and prevent exploitation of the commons.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“at no cost to the taxpayer”**
  - sentence: …He has dedicated his life to informing people who love nature that there’s a simple economic solution that can save wildlife at no cost to the taxpayer.…
  - why: The essay promotes the Georgist argument that public revenue can be raised from land/nature rather than taxes on labor.
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“alleviate poverty, starvation”**
  - sentence: …If adopted worldwide, the solution would alleviate poverty, starvation, unjust wealth inequality, and even make a significant contribution to reducing war and terrorism.…
  - why: The essay claims the economic solution (implied to be rent capture/dividends) would resolve poverty and inequality.
- **supports** [`problems/rent-seeking-drags-growth`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-seeking-drags-growth/) — anchor: **“preventing the exploitation of the commons”**
  - sentence: …This solution will actually grow the economy, create jobs, and help create a more beautiful world by rewarding those who contribute while preventing the exploitation of the commons.…
  - why: The essay argues that the current system allows exploitation of the commons, implying that stopping this rent-seeking would improve economic outcomes.

### [Will T-bills become Risky?](https://www.progress.org/articles/will-t-bills-become-risky/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“18-year real estate cycle”**
  - sentence: …In 2008 the 18-year real estate cycle marched right on schedule, with a downturn 18 years after the last real-estate recession of 1990.…
  - why: The essay explicitly cites the 18-year real estate cycle as a predictable driver of economic downturns, supporting the narrative that land speculation causes boom and bust cycles.
- **counters** [`objections/government-can-tame-the-cycle`](https://progress.org/wiki/government-can-tame-the-cycle/) — anchor: **“treating the effects, not the cause”**
  - sentence: …Recent and coming legislation to reign in the speculative excesses are only treating the effects, not the cause of the boom-bust cycle.…
  - why: The essay argues that legislative attempts to regulate speculation fail because they address symptoms rather than the root cause, countering the objection that government intervention can successfully
- **counters** [`narratives/this-time-is-different`](https://progress.org/wiki/this-time-is-different/) — anchor: **“the next expansion will again”**
  - sentence: …If no great disaster interferes, the economy will recover and the next expansion will again become a speculative boom.…
  - why: The essay dismisses the idea that structural change or policy has ended the old pattern, predicting that the next expansion will inevitably become a speculative boom.
- **supports** [`objections/stock-crash-caused-great-depression`](https://progress.org/wiki/stock-crash-caused-great-depression/) — anchor: **“The next Great Depression”**
  - sentence: …The next Great Depression will most likely not be this time around but in the next downturn 18 years hence, when the unfunded liabilities come due.…
  - why: The essay predicts a future Great Depression driven by debt and land cycles rather than a stock crash, aligning with the view that the 1929 crash was a symptom of deeper land-based cycle issues.

### [Will the Housing Market Crash in 2026?](https://www.progress.org/articles/will-the-housing-market-crash-in-2026/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“land speculation plays a significant role”**
  - sentence: …Henry George argued that land speculation plays a significant role in economic cycles, and that land value taxation could dampen cycles by deterring such speculation.…
  - why: The essay explicitly validates the Georgist narrative that land speculation is the primary driver of boom-and-bust cycles.
- **counters** [`objections/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance`](https://progress.org/wiki/bubbles-cannot-be-identified-in-advance/) — anchor: **“predicting that the next peak”**
  - sentence: …Harrison applied the model prospectively, predicting that the next peak in land values would occur around 1990, followed by a recession.…
  - why: The essay cites Fred Harrison's successful prospective predictions as evidence that bubbles can indeed be identified in advance, countering the Greenspan doctrine.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-dampens-land-speculation`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-dampens-land-speculation/) — anchor: **“land value taxation could dampen cycles”**
  - sentence: …Henry George argued that land speculation plays a significant role in economic cycles, and that land value taxation could dampen cycles by deterring such speculation.…
  - why: The essay supports the claim that LVT mitigates economic volatility by deterring the speculative behavior that causes cycles.

### [William F. Buckley the Georgist](https://www.progress.org/articles/william-f-buckley-the-georgist/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“tapping land value for public revenue”**
  - sentence: …Taxes on labor, enterprise, and goods interfere with and hamper the market, whereas tapping land value for public revenue does not burden enterprise and even helps the economy.…
  - why: The essay explicitly contrasts the burdens of taxing labor versus land to argue for the efficiency of land value capture.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-not-enough-revenue`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-not-enough-revenue/) — anchor: **“not enough land value to tap”**
  - sentence: …Milton Friedman, for example, understood that the tax on land value was the “least worst,” as he put it, but did not speak or write about it.…
  - why: The essay cites Milton Friedman's specific objection that land value is insufficient to fund government needs.
- **illustrates** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“bring the price of land down”**
  - sentence: …His right-wing associates opposed taxing land, probably because they recognized that the tapping of land value for public revenue will bring the price of land down to ground-zero, and rich conservatives tend to be big landowners, or they ge…
  - why: The essay identifies the political resistance to LVT based on the wealth shock it inflicts on current landowners.
- **illustrates** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“truly free market requires public revenue”**
  - sentence: …Nock and Chodorov were free-marketeers, geolibertarians who recognized and wrote that a truly free market requires public revenue from land rent.…
  - why: The essay frames the Georgist 'Single Tax' philosophy as a necessary component of a genuine free-market system.

### [Wired Magazine Lionized Tom Paine](https://www.progress.org/articles/wired-magazine-lionized-tom-paine/)
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“Agrarian Justice proposed compensating”**
  - sentence: …Agrarian Justice proposed compensating the landless.…
  - why: The essay explicitly cites Paine's 'Agrarian Justice' as a key work, which is the foundational text for the citizens' dividend narrative.
- **supports** [`narratives/the-great-land-robbery`](https://progress.org/wiki/the-great-land-robbery/) — anchor: **“We have it in our power”**
  - sentence: …Paine’s bold declarations— “We have it in our power to begin the world over again”— put him in the forefront.…
  - why: The essay highlights Paine's revolutionary rhetoric and his challenge to the 'privileged' and 'feudal lords,' aligning with the narrative that land titles and power were unjustly seized.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“compensating the landless”**
  - sentence: …Agrarian Justice proposed compensating the landless.…
  - why: The essay references Paine's proposal to compensate the landless, which is the core mechanism of the unearned increment narrative—recovering value for those excluded from ownership.
- **supports** [`narratives/single-tax-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/single-tax-narrative/) — anchor: **“Agrarian Justice proposed compensating”**
  - sentence: …Agrarian Justice proposed compensating the landless.…
  - why: By identifying 'Agrarian Justice' as a major work alongside 'Common Sense' and 'Rights of Man,' the essay supports the historical narrative of Paine's economic proposals regarding land and rent.

### [Without Taxes](https://www.progress.org/articles/without-taxes/)
- **supports** [`problems/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-goods-fundable-from-land-rent/) — anchor: **“financed from the rental value”**
  - sentence: …The basic answer to how governance and collective goods can be financed without taxes is that they would be financed from the rental value of the space and the associated capital goods.…
  - why: The essay argues that the fundamental solution to financing public goods without taxation is capturing the rental value of space.
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“taxation in substance”**
  - sentence: …Secondly, we need to distinguish taxation in substance from taxation in form.…
  - why: The essay distinguishes between taxes on labor/capital (substance) and levies on land rent (form), supporting the shift to land-based financing.
- **supports** [`narratives/unearned-increment-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/unearned-increment-narrative/) — anchor: **“profit of the earth is equally for all”**
  - sentence: …The second moral law of the market is: “When there is no creator but nature, the profit of the earth is equally for all.”…
  - why: The essay invokes the moral principle that natural profits belong to the community, justifying the capture of land rent.
- **supports** [`benefits/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic`](https://progress.org/wiki/congestion-pricing-reduces-traffic/) — anchor: **“just high enough to prevent congestion”**
  - sentence: …Fees for transit, parking, and highways are most efficient when they are just high enough to prevent congestion.…
  - why: The essay explicitly advocates for user fees set at levels that prevent congestion as the most efficient method.

### [Would Money For Nothing Spoil You?](https://www.progress.org/articles/would-money-for-nothing-spoil-you/)
- **supports** [`benefits/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/rent-dividends-reduce-poverty/) — anchor: **“Citizen's Dividend”**
  - sentence: …This is particularly true with a Citizen's Dividend, as the amount of income given is largely dependent on the value of the improvements made by working people.…
  - why: The essay argues that a Citizen's Dividend motivates productivity and improves lives, directly supporting the claim that rent dividends reduce poverty and inequality.
- **supports** [`narratives/citizens-dividend-narrative`](https://progress.org/wiki/citizens-dividend-narrative/) — anchor: **“investment in humanity”**
  - sentence: …It’s effectual to think of a Universal Basic Income as an investment in humanity.…
  - why: The essay frames the dividend as a moral investment that empowers people and keeps the economy moving, illustrating the narrative of a dividend from common wealth.
- **counters** [`objections/overpopulation-causes-poverty`](https://progress.org/wiki/overpopulation-causes-poverty/) — anchor: **“Malthusian Darwinian theory”**
  - sentence: …We keep inventing jobs because of this false idea that everybody has to be employed at some kind of drudgery because, according to Malthusian Darwinian theory, he must justify his right to exist.…
  - why: The essay explicitly rejects the 'Malthusian' justification for forced labor, countering the objection that overpopulation or scarcity necessitates poverty or drudgery.

  - why: The essay characterizes the current system as a rentier economy that enforces wage slavery and unearned income, supporting the narrative that economic structures reward asset ownership over productive

### [“Constitutional Economics”](https://www.progress.org/articles/how-constitutional-economics-can-restore-order-justice-and-faith-in-government/)
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“labor income tax”**
  - sentence: …My first recommendation would be to either remove legal tender status from the Federal Reserve note and replace them with U.S. Notes, or to make the Federal Reserve note immediately redeemable on demand for current U.S. coin.…
  - why: The essay argues that current taxes on wages are actually regulatory fees on currency, implying that labor should not be taxed directly.

  - why: The author grounds land rights in Lockean common ownership to argue against monopolization, countering the claim that Georgism is equivalent to socialism.
- **counters** [`objections/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-needs-zoning-reform-first/) — anchor: **“excess holding of land”**

- **supports** [`objections/public-choice-critique`](https://progress.org/wiki/public-choice-critique/) — anchor: **“Leviathan government”**
  - sentence: …The late "Constitutional economist" James M. Buchanan (1919-2013) did not understand the legal arguments we advance here at Common Wealth Tax, nor was he a lawyer, but he did believe that strict adherence to the Constitution, as defined and…
  - why: The essay invokes Buchanan's public-choice theory regarding the risk of government tyranny to justify strict constitutional constraints.

### [“We are all Greeks”](https://www.progress.org/articles/we-are-all-greeks/)
- **supports** [`narratives/land-speculation-causes-cycles`](https://progress.org/wiki/land-speculation-causes-cycles/) — anchor: **“real-estate cycle is scheduled”**
  - sentence: …The most likely year of reckoning is 2026, when the real-estate cycle is scheduled to plunge the economy into the next big recession, 18 years after the Crash of 2008.…
  - why: The essay explicitly predicts a future economic crash based on an 18-year real-estate cycle, supporting the narrative that land speculation drives boom-and-bust cycles.
- **supports** [`benefits/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-can-replace-capital-taxes-without-efficiency-loss/) — anchor: **“replace VAT and all other taxes with LVT”**
  - sentence: …The only way out is radical tax reform: replace VAT and all other taxes with LVT—land value taxation.…
  - why: The essay argues that shifting from harmful taxes on production to a land value tax is the solution to economic stagnation, supporting the claim that LVT can replace other taxes without efficiency los
- **supports** [`narratives/tax-land-not-labor`](https://progress.org/wiki/tax-land-not-labor/) — anchor: **“Land does not flee, shrink, or hide”**
  - sentence: …Land does not flee, shrink, or hide when taxed.…
  - why: The essay uses this specific phrasing to argue for the efficiency of taxing land over mobile factors of production, which is the core of the 'Tax Land, Not Labor' narrative.
- **supports** [`objections/lvt-transition-wealth-shock`](https://progress.org/wiki/lvt-transition-wealth-shock/) — anchor: **“The only economic problem is the transition”**
  - sentence: …The only economic problem is the transition, a one-time cost more than compensated from the future benefits.…
  - why: The essay acknowledges that implementing LVT involves a difficult transition period, validating the objection regarding the transition shock while arguing it is worth overcoming.
